| Response Time | Fast (ms) | Medium (s) | Very Fast (ms) |
| Contact Type | Contact | Contact | **Non-contact** |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Best Use | Furnace / Boiler | HVAC / Lab | Moving / Molten objects |

| Parameter | Orifice | Venturi | Rotameter | Ultrasonic |
|:----------|:--------|:--------|:----------|:-----------|
| Pressure Loss | High | **Very Low** | Low | **Zero** (clamp-on) |
| $C_d$ | 0.60–0.65 | 0.95–0.98 | N/A (direct read) | 0.95–1.00 |
| Cost | Low | High | Low | High |
| Accuracy | ±2 % | ±1 % | ±2 % | ±0.5–1 % |
| Suitable For | Clean liquids/gases | All fluids | Transparent fluids | **Any** fluid (incl. dirty) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Power Analyzer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    currInput["Current Shunt or CT"] --> adc

```

**Caption:** Functional architecture of a modern 3-phase Power Analyzer. The two CT/PT channels feed a high-speed ADC; the DSP executes FFT and computes all derived parameters in real-time.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three instruments used in an electrical energy audit and state the parameter measured by each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Power Analyzer** → measures active power (kW), reactive power (kVAr), power factor, voltage, current, harmonics.
2. **Clamp Meter** → measures AC/DC current without breaking the circuit.
3. **Lux Meter** → measures illuminance level (in lux) for lighting audit compliance.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Multimeter for voltage, Megger for insulation resistance, Earth Tester for earth resistance.)* — **3 marks**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define emissivity. Why is it important while using an infrared pyrometer?**

**Model Answer:**
**Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** is the ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a perfect blackbody at the same temperature, for the same wavelength and temperature. It ranges from **0** (perfect reflector) to **1** (perfect blackbody).

**Importance:** The infrared pyrometer calculates temperature using Stefan-Boltzmann's law which contains $\varepsilon$ as a direct multiplier. If the user enters an incorrect emissivity, the pyrometer will read **inaccurate temperatures** — e.g., polished steel ($\varepsilon \approx 0.1$) vs. oxidized steel ($\varepsilon \approx 0.85$). — **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **digital power analyzer**. List the parameters it can measure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During an energy audit of a 3-phase motor, the power analyzer recorded: $V_L = 400 \text{ V}$, $I_L = 50 \text{ A}$, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, operating time = 12 hr/day, 300 days/year, unit cost = ₹8/kWh. Compute the **(i)** Active Power, **(ii)** Annual Energy Consumption, **(iii)** Annual Electricity Cost. Suggest **one** energy-saving measure.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

A digital power analyzer is a precision instrument that simultaneously samples voltage and current waveforms from all three phases.

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):**

The instrument has three **voltage input channels** through potential dividers and three **current input channels** through CTs/shunts. These are multiplexed into a high-speed **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** operating at sampling rates ≥ **1024 samples/cycle**.

The digitized signals are processed by a **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** which performs:

- RMS computation: $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum v_i^2}$
- Active power per phase: $P_p = \frac{1}{N} \sum v_i \cdot i_i$
- Total: $P = P_1 + P_2 + P_3$
- Apparent power: $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$
- Power factor: $\cos\phi = P/S$
- FFT analysis → harmonics (THD, individual harmonic magnitudes)
- Energy: $E = \int P \, dt$

**Parameters measured:** Voltage (RMS), Current (RMS), Active Power, Reactive Power, Apparent Power, Power Factor, Frequency, Energy (kWh), Demand (kVA), THD, individual harmonics up to 63rd order, voltage & current unbalance. — **7 marks**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**(i) Active Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi \\
  &= \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 50 \times 0.85 \\
  &= 1.732 \times 400 \times 50 \times 0.85 \\
  &= 29,444 \text{ W} \approx 29.44 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Annual Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 29.44 \times 12 \times 300 = 105,984 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Annual Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 105{,}984 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 8,47,872 / \text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Energy-saving measure:**
- Install a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** if the motor drives a variable-torque load (fan/pump). A 20% speed reduction saves ~50% power (cube law).
- OR install a **power factor correction capacitor bank** to improve $\cos\phi$ from 0.85 to 0.95 and avoid PF penalty from the utility.
- OR replace with a **IE3/IE4 premium efficiency motor**. — **7 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **thermocouple** and an **RTD**. Compare them in any **four** aspects.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A boiler flue gas analyzer gives: O₂ = 5 %, CO₂ = 10 %, stack temperature = 220 °C, ambient = 35 °C. Using Siegert's formula (A₁ = 0.65), compute the **(i)** % Excess Air, **(ii)** % Combustion Efficiency, **(iii)** Annual fuel cost loss in ₹ if boiler input is 500 kW and it operates 5000 hr/year at ₹6/kWh.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Thermocouple:**
Based on **Seebeck Effect** — when two dissimilar metals (e.g., Chromel & Alumel for Type K) are joined at a hot junction, an EMF proportional to the temperature difference between the hot and reference (cold) junctions is generated. The EMF (typically microvolts to millivolts) is measured by a **cold-junction-compensated digital indicator** and converted to temperature via NIST reference tables.

$$
E = \alpha \Delta T + \beta (\Delta T)^2
$$

**RTD (Pt100):**
Based on the **linear increase of resistance** of pure platinum with temperature. For a Pt100, $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C, with $\alpha = 0.00385\ \Omega/\Omega/°C$:

$$
R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha \Delta T)
$$

The resistance is measured by a **Wheatstone bridge or 4-wire Kelvin connection** to eliminate lead-wire errors.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Thermocouple | RTD |
|:-------|:-------------|:----|
| Principle | Seebeck EMF | Resistance vs. temperature |
| Range | −270 to +2300 °C | −200 to +850 °C |
| Accuracy | ±1–2 °C | ±0.1 °C (better) |
| Stability | Drifts over time | Very stable |
| Linearity | Non-linear (needs table) | Highly linear |
| Cost | Low | Higher (needs bridge/lead comp) |
| Response | Fast (ms) | Slower (s) |
| Best For | High-temp industrial | Precision laboratory |

— **7 marks**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**(i) % Excess Air:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%EA &= \frac{O_2}{21 - O_2} \times 100 \\
     &= \frac{5}{21 - 5} \times 100 \\
     &= \frac{5}{16} \times 100 \\
     &= 31.25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) % Combustion Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{comb}} &= 100 - \left[ A_1 \cdot \frac{T_F - T_A}{CO_2} \right] \\
                   &= 100 - \left[ 0.65 \cdot \frac{220 - 35}{10} \right] \\
                   &= 100 - \left[ 0.65 \cdot 18.5 \right] \\
                   &= 100 - 12.025 \\
                   &= 87.975\% \approx 88.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Annual Heat Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Loss} &= 100 - 88.0 = 12.0\% \\
\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}} &= 0.12 \times 500 = 60 \text{ kW} \\
E_{\text{loss, annual}} &= 60 \times 5000 = 300{,}000 \text{ kWh/year} \\
\text{Cost Loss} &= 300{,}000 \times 6 = \text{₹ } 18,00,000 / \text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

— **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units Mismatch:** Converting kW to W incorrectly — KTU deducts **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Sign of Reactive Power:** For lagging PF, Q is **positive** (inductive load). For leading PF, Q is negative. KTU often gives a twist in the question.
> 3. **Thermocouple EMF Sign:** Always mention **cold-junction compensation** (otherwise you lose 2 marks).
> 4. **Manometer Fluid:** Mercury (ρ = 13,600 kg/m³) for high pressure, water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) for low pressure. Mixing them up is a classic error.
> 5. **RTD Lead Compensation:** For long leads, mention **3-wire or 4-wire Kelvin connection** — KTU specifically tests this in 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Instruments for Energy Audit**

### **A. Electrical Instruments**
- ✅ **Power Analyzer** — most powerful single instrument; measures V, I, P, Q, S, PF, THD, kWh.
- ✅ **Clamp Meter** — for live circuit current without isolation.
- ✅ **Lux Meter** — for lighting audit; standards per IS 3646.

### **B. Temperature Instruments**
- ✅ **Thermocouple** — Seebeck effect, robust, wide range, **non-linear** (needs NIST tables).
- ✅ **RTD Pt100** — high accuracy (±0.1 °C), linear, limited to 850 °C.
- ✅ **Pyrometer** — non-contact, uses Stefan-Boltzmann law, depends on emissivity.

### **C. Combustion Instruments**
- ✅ **Flue Gas Analyzer** — gives O₂, CO, CO₂, NOₓ; computes excess air & efficiency.
- ✅ Siegert formula: $\eta = 100 - A_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_F - T_A}{CO_2}$
- ✅ Excess air formula: $\%EA = \dfrac{O_2}{21 - O_2} \times 100$

### **D. Pressure Instruments**
- ✅ **U-tube Manometer** — $P = \rho g h$
- ✅ **Bourdon Tube** — mechanical, robust, up to 1000 bar.
- ✅ **Differential Pressure Transmitter** — for orifice/Venturi flow.

### **E. Flow Instruments**
- ✅ **Orifice** — cheap, high pressure loss, $C_d \approx 0.62$.
- ✅ **Venturi** — efficient, $C_d \approx 0.96$.
- ✅ **Rotameter** — local indication, transparent fluids only.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic** — clamp-on, **zero pressure loss**, expensive.

### **F. Other Instruments**
- ✅ **Tachometer** — non-contact preferred; uses IR/optical sensing.
- ✅ **Stroboscope** — for visualizing rotating parts.
- ✅ **Sound Level Meter** — for compressed air leak detection.
- ✅ **Vibration Analyzer** — per ISO 10816 zones.
- ✅ **Hygrometer** — HVAC audits (45–55 % RH).

### **G. Universal Audit Equations**
- ✅ Annual Energy = Power × hours/day × days/year
- ✅ Annual Cost = Energy × Tariff
- ✅ Simple Payback = Capital Cost / Annual Savings
- ✅ % Savings = (Baseline − Proposed) / Baseline × 100

### **H. Key Standards & Codes**
- ✅ **IS 3646** — Indian Standard for indoor lighting
- ✅ **IEC 60751** — Industrial Pt100 RTD standard
- ✅ **ISO 50001** — Energy Management Systems
- ✅ **ISO 10816** — Mechanical vibration evaluation
- ✅ **BEE Star Rating** — Bureau of Energy Efficiency labelling

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If you remember only **three** formulas, remember:
> 1. $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ (Active Power)
> 2. $\dfrac{O_2}{21 - O_2}$ (Excess Air)
> 3. Siegert's Efficiency formula
>
> These three alone can fetch you **10–12 marks** in any KTU PEMRT746 exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit Report
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit Report

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Audit Report (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An *Energy Audit Report* is a formally structured, technically exhaustive, and verifiable document that systematically records the complete findings of an energy audit study. It serves as the **primary communication deliverable** between the energy auditor and the client organization's management. It documents the baseline energy scenario, identifies Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs), quantifies potential savings, recommends Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs), and provides a prioritized, cost-justified implementation roadmap aligned with the organization's financial and operational goals.

The report is governed by standards such as **IS 12615 (Part 1)**, **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) – India** guidelines under the **Energy Conservation Act 2001**, and internationally by **ASHRAE Level 2 & 3 audit protocols** and **ISO 50002**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Health Checkup Report":**
> Imagine a doctor performs a full body checkup. You walk in feeling fine, but the doctor runs blood tests, scans, and pressure readings. The **Energy Audit Report** is exactly like the doctor's final prescription report. It tells the factory/building owner:
> - *Where* they are losing energy (analogous to the patient's problem areas).
> - *How much* energy is being wasted (analogous to the diagnosis).
> - *What* to fix and *in what order* (analogous to the prescription).
> - *How much* it will cost vs. how much will be saved (analogous to treatment cost vs. recovery benefit).
>
> Without this written report, the entire audit exercise is just data collection with **zero actionable value**.

### 1.3 Purpose & Objectives

The Energy Audit Report fulfills **four mandatory KTU-listed objectives**:

1. **Documentation of Baseline Energy Performance** — Establishes the *Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)*, *Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)*, and the **Energy baseline (kWh/year, TOE/year)**.
2. **Quantification of Energy Savings Potential** — Calculates the *annual energy savings*, *monetary savings*, and the *reduction in CO₂ emissions* achievable through recommended ECMs.
3. **Financial Justification** — Provides economic feasibility metrics such as **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**.
4. **Implementation Roadmap** — Delivers a prioritized action plan with *responsibility matrix*, *M&V (Measurement & Verification) protocol*, and *training requirements*.

### 1.4 Mandatory Metrics (KTU Defined Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units Used in KTU Energy Audit Reports:**
> - **Energy:** **kWh**, **MJ**, **TOE** (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) where **1 TOE = 41.868 GJ**
> - **Power:** **kW**, **MW**, **HP** where **1 HP = 0.746 kW**
> - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** **kWh/unit product**, **MJ/tonne**
> - **Currency:** ₹/kWh (Indian Rupees), $ (International)
> - **CO₂ Emission Factor (India grid average):** **0.716 kg CO₂ / kWh** (CEA 2023 baseline)
> - **Discount Rate for KTU problems:** typically **r = 10% to 15%** unless stated otherwise
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of a Standard Energy Audit Report

A KTU-compliant Energy Audit Report follows this **mandatory section sequence**:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1.  Executive Summary (1-2 pages)           │
│ 2.  Audit Scope, Objectives & Boundaries    │
│ 3.  Facility Description & Process Overview │
│ 4.  Methodology & Instrumentation Used      │
│ 5.  Baseline Energy Analysis                │
│ 6.  Energy Consumption Breakdown            │
│ 7.  Identified Energy Conservation Measures│
│ 8.  Cost-Benefit Analysis of Each ECM       │
│ 9.  Prioritized Implementation Plan         │
│ 10. Monitoring & Verification (M&V) Plan    │
│ 11. Training & Awareness Recommendations    │
│ 12. Appendices (Data sheets, raw readings)  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2 Detailed Component Analysis

#### A. Executive Summary
- A **condensed management-level overview** containing: total annual energy cost, **% energy savings potential**, total investment required, average payback, and the **top 5 ECMs**.
- Designed for **decision-makers** who may not read the full 50-100 page report.

#### B. Audit Methodology Section
- States whether it is a **Walk-through (Level 1)**, **Detailed (Level 2)**, or **Investment-grade (Level 3)** audit.
- Lists all instruments used (power analyzer, lux meter, flue gas analyzer, IR thermometer) with calibration certificates.

#### C. Baseline Energy Analysis
- Establishes the **Energy Baseline (E_base)** — the reference against which future savings are measured.
- Uses **regression models** of the form:

$$
E_{base} = a \cdot P + b \cdot H + c
$$

where $P$ = production output, $H$ = heating degree days or operating hours, and $a, b, c$ are regression coefficients determined from historical data.

#### D. ECM Identification & Classification

| ECM Category | Description | Typical Payback |
|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **No-Cost / Low-Cost** | Housekeeping, operational tweaks | < 6 months |
| **Medium-Cost** | VFD installation, insulation | 1 - 3 years |
| **High-Cost** | Boiler replacement, cogeneration | > 3 years |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/unit |
| 2 | $S_{annual} = E_{saved} \times C_e$ | Annual Monetary Savings | ₹/year |
| 3 | $SPP = \dfrac{I_0}{S_{annual}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years |
| 4 | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Net Present Value | ₹ |
| 5 | $IRR \rightarrow NPV = 0$ | Internal Rate of Return | % |
| 6 | $CO_2\ Saved = E_{saved} \times 0.716$ | CO₂ Emission Reduction | kg/year |
| 7 | $E_{base} = aP + bH + c$ | Energy Baseline Regression | kWh |
| 8 | $PEI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{Floor\ Area}$ | Plant Energy Intensity | kWh/m² |
| 9 | $CUE = \dfrac{Energy\ Cost}{Production\ Value}$ | Cost of Utilized Energy | ₹/₹ |
| 10 | $\% Savings = \dfrac{E_{saved}}{E_{base}} \times 100$ | Percentage Energy Savings | % |

> **Critical Note for KTU Board Valuation:** When calculating NPV, always show the **discount factor table** explicitly. Examiners deduct marks if cash flow rows are missing.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Energy Audit Report is the **legal, financial, and engineering backbone** for:
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme compliance** under BEE for Designated Consumers (DCs).
- **Securing bank loans** for energy efficiency projects (report serves as due-diligence document).
- **Carbon credit registration** under CDM/VCS protocols.
- **ISO 50001 certification** as documented evidence of EnPI establishment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Comprehensive KTU Board Style Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **PROBLEM STATEMENT:**
> A cement plant consumes **8,50,000 kWh/year** of electrical energy and **2,400 tonnes/year** of coal (GCV = 5,500 kcal/kg). The audit identifies **4 ECMs** as detailed below. Electricity cost = **₹7.50/kWh**, coal cost = **₹9,000/tonne**. Discount rate **r = 12%**. Plant operates **300 days/year**. Compute: (a) Baseline TOE and CO₂ emission. (b) SPP, NPV (over 5 years), and IRR for **each ECM**. (c) Recommend prioritization.

| ECM | Description | Investment (₹) | kWh Saved/yr | Coal Saved (t/yr) |
|-----|-------------|----------------|--------------|-------------------|
| E1  | Replace old motors with IE3 | 4,00,000 | 60,000 | 0 |
| E2  | VFD on ID fan | 6,00,000 | 1,20,000 | 0 |
| E3  | Waste Heat Recovery | 15,00,000 | 0 | 350 |
| E4  | APH改造 (Air Preheater) | 8,00,000 | 50,000 | 200 |

### 3.2 Solution — Part (a): Baseline Calculation

**Step 1: Convert electrical energy to TOE**

Thermal equivalent of 1 kWh = **860 kcal** (standard conversion)

$$
E_{elec, kcal} = 8{,}50{,}000 \times 860 = 7.31 \times 10^{8}\ kcal
$$

Since **1 TOE = 41.868 × 10⁶ kcal**:

$$
TOE_{elec} = \dfrac{7.31 \times 10^{8}}{41.868 \times 10^{6}} = 17.46\ TOE
$$

**Step 2: Convert coal energy to TOE**

$$
E_{coal, kcal} = 2400 \times 1000 \times 5500 = 1.32 \times 10^{10}\ kcal
$$

$$
TOE_{coal} = \dfrac{1.32 \times 10^{10}}{41.868 \times 10^{6}} = 315.34\ TOE
$$

**Step 3: Total Baseline Energy**

$$
TOE_{total} = 17.46 + 315.34 = 332.80\ TOE
$$

**Step 4: Baseline CO₂ Emission**

For electricity: $E_{CO2,elec} = 8{,}50{,}000 \times 0.716 = 6{,}08{,}600\ kg$

For coal (assume emission factor **2.42 kg CO₂/kg of coal**):
$$
E_{CO2,coal} = 2400 \times 1000 \times 2.42 = 58{,}08{,}000\ kg
$$

$$
CO_{2,total} = 6{,}08{,}600 + 58{,}08{,}000 = 64{,}16{,}600\ kg = 6416.6\ tonnes
$$

### 3.3 Solution — Part (b): Economic Analysis Per ECM

**Helper formulas used throughout:**

$$
S_{annual} = (kWh_{saved} \times C_e) + (t_{coal,saved} \times C_{coal})
$$

$$
SPP = \dfrac{I_0}{S_{annual}}
$$

$$
NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \dfrac{S_{annual}}{(1.12)^t}
$$

Using **Present Worth Factor (PWF) at 12% for 5 years:**
$$
PWF_{12\%,5yr} = \dfrac{(1.12)^5 - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^5} = 3.6048
$$

**ECM E1 (IE3 Motors):**
- $S_{annual} = 60{,}000 \times 7.50 = ₹4{,}50{,}000$
- $SPP = 4{,}00{,}000 / 4{,}50{,}000 = \mathbf{0.89\ years}$
- $NPV = -4{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 = ₹12{,}22{,}160$

**ECM E2 (VFD on ID Fan):**
- $S_{annual} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 7.50 = ₹9{,}00{,}000$
- $SPP = 6{,}00{,}000 / 9{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{0.67\ years}$
- $NPV = -6{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6048 = ₹26{,}44{,}320$

**ECM E3 (Waste Heat Recovery):**
- $S_{annual} = 350 \times 9{,}000 = ₹31{,}50{,}000$
- $SPP = 15{,}00{,}000 / 31{,}50{,}000 = \mathbf{0.48\ years}$
- $NPV = -15{,}00{,}000 + 31{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 = ₹98{,}55{,}120$

**ECM E4 (Air Preheater + Electrical Savings):**
- $S_{annual} = (50{,}000 \times 7.50) + (200 \times 9{,}000) = ₹3{,}75{,}000 + ₹18{,}00{,}000 = ₹21{,}75{,}000$
- $SPP = 8{,}00{,}000 / 21{,}75{,}000 = \mathbf{0.37\ years}$
- $NPV = -8{,}00{,}000 + 21{,}75{,}000 \times 3.6048 = ₹70{,}40{,}440$

### 3.4 IRR Calculation — KTU Board Method

The **trial-and-error interpolation method** (as taught in KTU):

For E2 (VFD): Test r = 80%
$$
NPV_{80\%} = -6{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{(1.80)^5 - 1}{0.80 \times (1.80)^5} = ₹-12{,}125
$$

Test r = 75%:
$$
NPV_{75\%} = -6{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{(1.75)^5 - 1}{0.75 \times (1.75)^5} = ₹+95{,}893
$$

By linear interpolation:
$$
IRR \approx 75\% + \dfrac{95893}{95893 + 12125} \times (80\% - 75\%) = 79.88\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Do not confuse IRR with the discount rate *r*. The IRR is the rate that makes NPV = 0. Always perform **two trial values** on either side of zero NPV before interpolating.

### 3.5 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| ECM | I₀ (₹) | S_annual (₹) | SPP (yr) | NPV (₹) | IRR (%) | Priority |
|-----|--------|--------------|----------|---------|---------|----------|
| E1  | 4,00,000 | 4,50,000 | 0.89 | 12,22,160 | ~118% | 3 |
| E2  | 6,00,000 | 9,00,000 | 0.67 | 26,44,320 | ~80% | 2 |
| E3  | 15,00,000 | 31,50,000 | 0.48 | 98,55,120 | ~210% | 1 |
| E4  | 8,00,000 | 21,75,000 | 0.37 | 70,40,440 | ~272% | 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU Standard):**
> 1. Rank by **lowest SPP** first.
> 2. For ties, prefer **higher NPV**.
> 3. Verify **IRR > cost of capital (typically 12–15%)** for viability.
> 4. In this case: **E4 → E3 → E2 → E1** in priority order.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Audit Report — Structural Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Audit Commissioned] --> B[Phase 1: Planning & Scope]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Data Collection]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Site Survey & Measurement]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Data Analysis]
    E --> F[Phase 5: ECM Identification]
    F --> G[Phase 6: Economic Evaluation]
    G --> H[Phase 7: Report Drafting]
    H --> I[Phase 8: Client Review]
    I --> J[Phase 9: Final Report Delivery]
    J --> K[Phase 10: M&V Implementation]
    K --> L[END: Continuous Improvement]

    B --> B1[Define boundaries, objectives, KPIs]
    C --> C1[Utility bills, production data, logs]
    D --> D1[Instruments: power analyzer, lux meter, flue gas]
    E --> E1[SEC, EnPIs, baseline regression]
    F --> F1[No-cost, Low-cost, High-cost ECMs]
    G --> G1[SPP, NPV, IRR calculation]
    H --> H1[Executive summary + Technical sections]
    I --> I1[Stakeholder feedback incorporation]
    J --> J1[Approved actionable document]
    K --> K1[IPMVP plan, savings verification]
```

### 4.2 Information Flow Inside the Report

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAW[Raw Field Data] --> PROC[Data Processing]
    PROC --> BASE[Baseline Energy Model]
    BASE --> ECM_ID[ECM Identification Engine]
    ECM_ID --> COST[Cost-Benefit Module]
    COST --> EXEC[Executive Summary]
    BASE --> TECH[Technical Analysis Section]
    ECM_ID --> TECH
    COST --> TECH
    TECH --> FIN[Final Report Document]
    EXEC --> FIN
    COST --> FIN
    FIN --> CLIENT[Client Management Decision]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Report Sections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Energy Audit Report] --> S1[Section A: Executive Summary]
    R --> S2[Section B: Audit Methodology]
    R --> S3[Section C: Baseline Analysis]
    R --> S4[Section D: ECM Recommendations]
    R --> S5[Section E: Economic Analysis]
    R --> S6[Section F: Implementation Plan]
    R --> S7[Section G: M&V Protocol]

    S1 --> S1A[Total savings, top 5 ECMs, payback]
    S2 --> S2A[Audit level, instruments, ISO compliance]
    S3 --> S3A[SEC, EnPIs, regression baseline]
    S4 --> S4A[Categorized ECM list with technical details]
    S5 --> S5A[SPP, NPV, IRR, ROI per ECM]
    S6 --> S6A[Priority matrix, Gantt chart, responsibility]
    S7 --> S7A[IPMVP Option A/B/C/D selection]
```

### 4.4 ECM Decision Matrix Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is it operational/housekeeping?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Category 1: No-Cost ECM]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Investment < ₹5 Lakh?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Category 2: Low-Cost ECM]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Payback < 2 years?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Category 3: Quick-win ECM]
    Q3 -->|No| A4[Category 4: Strategic ECM]
    A1 --> REC[Add to Report]
    A2 --> REC
    A3 --> REC
    A4 --> REC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** What is an Energy Audit Report? List any **four** mandatory components of a KTU-compliant audit report.

**Model Answer:**
> An Energy Audit Report is a formal document that systematically records the methodology, findings, and recommendations of an energy audit study. The four mandatory components are:
> 1. **Executive Summary** – condenses key findings for top management.
> 2. **Baseline Energy Analysis** – establishes SEC, EnPIs, and energy baseline.
> 3. **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** – identifies energy-saving opportunities.
> 4. **Economic Analysis** – provides SPP, NPV, IRR for each ECM, plus a prioritized implementation plan.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **SPP** and **NPV**. State one limitation of SPP.

**Model Answer:**
> - **SPP (Simple Payback Period)** is the time (in years) required for cumulative savings to equal the initial investment. Formula: $SPP = I_0 / S_{annual}$.
> - **NPV (Net Present Value)** is the discounted sum of all future cash flows minus the initial investment. Formula: $NPV = -I_0 + \sum S_t / (1+r)^t$.
>
> **Difference:** SPP ignores the **time value of money** and cash flows beyond payback, while NPV accounts for the discount rate and full project lifecycle.
>
> **Limitation of SPP:** It does **not consider earnings after the payback period**, potentially rejecting highly profitable long-term projects.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> A process industry consumes **5,00,000 kWh/year** of electricity at ₹6.50/kWh. An audit identifies two ECMs:
> - **ECM-1:** Investment ₹3,00,000, saves 80,000 kWh/year.
> - **ECM-2:** Investment ₹5,00,000, saves 1,20,000 kWh/year, plus 100 tonnes/year of coal (₹8,000/tonne).
>
> **Compute:**
> **(a)** SPP and NPV (over 6 years, r = 10%) for each ECM. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Recommend the prioritization with justification and comment on the role of an Energy Audit Report in industrial decision-making. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Calculations:**

**ECM-1:**
- $S_{annual} = 80{,}000 \times 6.50 = ₹5{,}20{,}000$
- $SPP = 3{,}00{,}000 / 5{,}20{,}000 = \mathbf{0.577\ years}$
- $PWF_{10\%,6yr} = \dfrac{(1.10)^6 - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^6} = 4.3553$
- $NPV = -3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}20{,}000 \times 4.3553 = ₹19{,}64{,}756$

**ECM-2:**
- $S_{annual} = (1{,}20{,}000 \times 6.50) + (100 \times 8{,}000) = ₹7{,}80{,}000 + ₹8{,}00{,}000 = ₹15{,}80{,}000$
- $SPP = 5{,}00{,}000 / 15{,}80{,}000 = \mathbf{0.316\ years}$
- $NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}80{,}000 \times 4.3553 = ₹63{,}81{,}374$

**(b) Recommendation & Justification:**

| ECM | SPP | NPV | Recommendation |
|-----|-----|-----|----------------|
| ECM-1 | 0.577 yr | ₹19,64,756 | Implement SECOND |
| ECM-2 | 0.316 yr | ₹63,81,374 | Implement FIRST |

**Prioritization:** ECM-2 first (lower payback + higher NPV), then ECM-1.

**Role of Energy Audit Report:**
1. Acts as a **decision-making tool** for top management to allocate capital efficiently.
2. Provides **legal and financial documentation** for availing incentives under the BEE-PAT scheme.
3. Establishes a **baseline** against which actual savings are verified through M&V.
4. Ensures **structured communication** between auditors, engineers, and management.
5. Supports **ISO 50001 certification** and CSR/sustainability reporting.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]:**
> **(a)** Describe the structure and components of a comprehensive Energy Audit Report. Discuss the importance of the Executive Summary and the M&V section. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A boiler in a textile unit consumes 1,500 litres/day of furnace oil (GCV = 10,200 kcal/kg, density = 0.96 kg/L). An ECM proposes installing an economizer that recovers 12% of the flue gas heat. Compute: **(i)** daily fuel savings in kg, **(ii)** annual monetary savings at ₹75/kg, **(iii)** simple payback if investment is ₹6,00,000. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure of Energy Audit Report:**

The report has **12 standard sections** (as detailed in Section 2.1 of these notes): Executive Summary, Scope, Facility Description, Methodology, Baseline Analysis, Consumption Breakdown, ECMs, Cost-Benefit, Implementation Plan, M&V Plan, Training, Appendices.

**Importance of Executive Summary:**
- Provides a **one-page snapshot** for time-pressed senior management.
- Lists **top 5 ECMs** with payback and NPV.
- Highlights **total investment required** vs. **total annual savings**.
- Enables **instant go/no-go decision** for the project.

**Importance of M&V Section:**
- Ensures **post-implementation verification** of claimed savings using the **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** framework.
- Defines the **baseline**, **reporting boundary**, **measurement points**, and **responsibility matrix**.
- Protects both auditor and client against **disputes** regarding actual savings achieved.
- Supports **continuous improvement** and **future audits**.

**(b) Boiler Economizer Calculations:**

**Given:**
- Fuel consumption = 1,500 L/day
- Density = 0.96 kg/L → Mass consumption = 1,500 × 0.96 = **1,440 kg/day**
- GCV = 10,200 kcal/kg
- Heat recovery = 12%

**(i) Daily fuel savings:**
$$
Fuel_{saved} = 1{,}440 \times 0.12 = \mathbf{172.8\ kg/day}
$$

**(ii) Annual monetary savings (300 operating days):**
$$
S_{annual} = 172.8 \times 300 \times 75 = ₹38{,}88{,}000
$$

**(iii) Simple Payback Period:**
$$
SPP = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{38{,}88{,}000} = \mathbf{0.154\ years} \approx 56\ days
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students frequently **forget to multiply by operating days** (300) when computing annual savings — losing 2 marks.
> - The **density of fuel oil (0.96 kg/L)** is often ignored — students use 1500 kg directly instead of converting from litres.
> - **GCV is not used** in this problem since the question gives percentage heat recovery directly — do not unnecessarily convert to kcal and back.
> - Always state the **assumption of operating days** explicitly in your solution.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Energy Audit Report:**
>
> ✅ A report must follow the **12-section KTU standard structure** without omission.
>
> ✅ **Baseline energy (E_base)** must be established *before* identifying savings — this is the reference for all M&V.
>
> ✅ **TOE conversion:** 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 41,868 MJ = 11,630 kWh (electrical equivalent for thermal sources).
>
> ✅ **CO₂ emission factor (India grid 2023):** 0.716 kg CO₂/kWh — use this unless the problem specifies otherwise.
>
> ✅ **Simple Payback (SPP):** $SPP = I_0 / S_{annual}$ — ignores time value of money, easy to compute, but limited.
>
> ✅ **NPV** requires the discount factor table: $PWF = \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$ — must show this calculation explicitly.
>
> ✅ **IRR** is the rate *r* where NPV = 0 — always use **interpolation method** with two trial values bracketing zero.
>
> ✅ **Prioritization rule:** Lowest SPP → Highest NPV → IRR > Cost of Capital.
>
> ✅ **M&V Plan** uses IPMVP Options A/B/C/D — Option A (retrofitted isolation) and Option B (whole facility) are most common.
>
> ✅ **BEE & PAT compliance:** Reports for Designated Consumers must follow BEE's *Energy Audit Report Format* with mandatory *Annexure 1 to 6*.
>
> ✅ Always state **assumptions** (operating days, discount rate, escalation rate) clearly at the beginning of any numerical answer.
>
> ✅ For 14-mark questions, **show full working** — partial steps receive zero credit per KTU 2024 evaluation norms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Quality Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Quality Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Quality (PQ):** As per **IEEE Std 1159**, Power Quality refers to the concept of powering and grounding sensitive electronic equipment in a manner that is suitable to the operation of that equipment and compatible with the premise wiring system and other connected equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Quality Audit (PQA):** A systematic, measurement-based engineering assessment of an electrical installation that evaluates the quality of power delivered to and consumed by the loads, quantifies deviations from ideal sinusoidal waveforms, identifies sources of disturbances, and recommends corrective measures to ensure compliance with standards such as **IEEE 519**, **IEC 61000**, and **CBEMA/ITIC curves**.

The two governing attributes of ideal power quality are:
1. **Pure sinusoidal voltage and current waveforms** at the **nominal frequency (50 Hz in India)**
2. **Constant magnitude (RMS value)** equal to the **declared nominal voltage (e.g., 415 V / 230 V)**

Any sustained deviation from these conditions constitutes a **Power Quality Disturbance (PQD)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Pipeline"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Drinking Water Quality vs. Power Quality**

Imagine water flowing through your home pipeline. You expect three things:
- **Constant pressure** (analogous to **constant RMS voltage**)
- **Clean, clear flow** without mud or air bubbles (analogous to **pure sinusoidal waveform**)
- **Steady flow rate** without surges or drops (analogous to **constant frequency**)

Now imagine your neighbour installs a high-power pump that causes pressure drops (voltage sag), or someone throws stones into the overhead tank (transients/harmonics). Your tap water becomes unsuitable — this is exactly what happens in a power network when non-linear loads like variable frequency drives, UPS systems, or arc furnaces are connected. A **Power Quality Audit** is essentially a **"water quality test"** performed on the electrical network using specialized diagnostic instruments to identify the "contaminants" and suggest "filtration" equipment.

### 1.3 Categories of Power Quality Problems

| **Category** | **Spectral Content** | **Typical Duration** | **Magnitude (V)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Transients** (Impulsive/Oscillatory) | Very high frequency | < 50 ns to 5 ms | Variable |
| **Short-duration variations** (Sag/Swell/Interruption) | Fundamental (50 Hz) | 0.5 cycles – 1 min | 0.1 pu – 1.8 pu |
| **Long-duration variations** (Over/Under voltage, Sustained interruption) | Fundamental | > 1 minute | 0.8 pu – 1.2 pu |
| **Waveform Distortion** (Harmonics, Inter-harmonics, DC offset, Noise) | 0 – 9 kHz | Steady state | Variable |
| **Voltage Fluctuations & Flicker** | < 25 Hz modulation | Continuous | 0.1% – 7% |
| **Frequency Variations** | Fundamental | < 10 s | ±0.5 Hz |

### 1.4 Need for Power Quality Audit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The modern electrical ecosystem is dominated by **non-linear electronic loads** (SMPS, VFDs, LED drivers, IT loads) which generate **harmonics** and **distortion**. A Power Quality Audit is mandated by:
> - **Regulatory compliance** with CEA regulations and IEEE 519
> - **Equipment protection** — PQ issues cause 30%–40% of industrial equipment failures
> - **Energy cost reduction** — Poor PF and harmonics attract **penalty tariffs**
> - **Process reliability** — Voltage sags cause costly downtime in process industries
> - **Legal liability** — Utilities and consumers must mutually respect PQ limits

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Harmonic Theory — The Foundation of PQ Analysis

A **non-sinusoidal periodic waveform** can be decomposed using **Fourier Series Analysis** (Jean-Baptiste Joseph Fourier, 1822). Any periodic signal $v(t)$ with fundamental frequency $f_1$ can be expressed as:

$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_h \sin(h\omega_1 t + \phi_h)$$

Where:
- $V_0$ = DC component (ideally zero in AC systems)
- $V_h$ = RMS magnitude of the $h^{\text{th}}$ harmonic
- $h$ = Harmonic order (1 = fundamental, 3 = triplen, 5 = 5th harmonic, etc.)
- $\omega_1 = 2\pi f_1$ = Angular fundamental frequency

#### Step-by-Step Logic of Harmonic Generation:

1. **Non-linear load behavior** — Load impedance varies instantaneously with applied voltage (e.g., diode bridge rectifiers draw current only at voltage peaks)
2. **Current waveform distortion** — The non-sinusoidal current can be mathematically reconstructed as a sum of sinusoids at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency
3. **System impedance interaction** — These harmonic currents, when flowing through source impedance ($Z_s$), produce harmonic voltage drops ($V_h = I_h \cdot Z_h$)
4. **Voltage distortion at PCC** — The voltage at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC) becomes distorted, affecting all other consumers

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **all mandatory formulas** expected in KTU examinations for Power Quality Audit.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units / Remarks** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** — Voltage | $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%**, IEEE 519 limit: **5%** at PCC for V $\leq$ 1 kV |
| **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** — Current | $THD_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Depends on $I_{sc}/I_L$ ratio |
| **Individual Harmonic Distortion (IHD)** | $IHD_h = \dfrac{V_h}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Limit for each harmonic order |
| **Total Demand Distortion (TDD)** | $TDD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_L} \times 100\%$ | $I_L$ = Maximum demand load current |
| **Displacement Power Factor (DPF)** | $DPF = \cos(\phi_1)$ | $\phi_1$ = angle between $V_1$ and $I_1$ |
| **Distortion Power Factor** | $Distortion\,PF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$ | Always $\leq 1$ |
| **True Power Factor (TPF)** | $TPF = DPF \times Distortion\,PF$ | Used for penalty billing |
| **Crest Factor (CF)** | $CF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | Pure sine: $CF = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ |
| **Form Factor (FF)** | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ | Pure sine: $FF = \dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$ |
| **Voltage Sag Magnitude** | $Magnitude = \dfrac{V_{sag}}{V_{nominal}}$ | Often expressed in **pu (per unit)** |
| **RMS Voltage Calculation** | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + \sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}$ | Includes harmonic content |
| **Power in Distorted System** | $P = \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_h I_h \cos(\phi_h)$ | Each harmonic contributes separately |

### 2.3 The $I_{sc}/I_L$ Ratio — IEEE 519 Classification

> [!NOTE]
> The **Short Circuit Ratio** $I_{sc}/I_L$ determines permissible current harmonic limits. A **strong system** (high $I_{sc}$) tolerates more current harmonics because the source impedance is lower, minimizing voltage distortion.

| **$I_{sc}/I_L$ at PCC** | **Max Individual Harmonic (%)** | **Max TDD (%)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $< 20$ | 4.0 | 5.0 |
| $20 - 50$ | 7.0 | 8.0 |
| $50 - 100$ | 10.0 | 12.0 |
| $100 - 1000$ | 12.0 | 15.0 |
| $> 1000$ | 15.0 | 20.0 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Power Quality framework underpins every modern electrical engineering domain:
- **Industrial Plants** — Avoiding production losses due to VFD tripping on voltage sags
- **Data Centers** — Maintaining uptime class TIA-942 and uptime tier III/IV standards
- **Renewable Integration** — Managing inverter-induced harmonics in solar/wind farms
- **Electric Vehicle Charging** — Mitigating harmonic injection from high-power chargers
- **Smart Grids** — Real-time PQ monitoring via IoT and advanced metering infrastructure (AMI)

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) from Fourier Coefficients

**Statement:** Prove that the Total Harmonic Distortion of a non-sinusoidal voltage waveform is defined as the ratio of RMS value of all harmonic components to the RMS value of the fundamental component.

**Given:**
- A non-sinusoidal periodic voltage $v(t)$ with Fourier series expansion:
$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{h=1}^{\infty} V_{h,\max} \sin(h\omega_1 t + \phi_h)$$

**Step 1: Express the RMS value of the entire distorted waveform**

By Parseval's theorem, the total RMS value equals the square root of the sum of squares of all individual RMS values:

$$V_{rms,total} = \sqrt{V_0^2 + V_{1,rms}^2 + V_{2,rms}^2 + V_{3,rms}^2 + \cdots + V_{h,rms}^2}$$

Here, $V_{h,rms} = \dfrac{V_{h,\max}}{\sqrt{2}}$ since each harmonic is itself a sinusoid.

**Step 2: Assume no DC component** ($V_0 = 0$)

For AC systems operating at nominal frequency with no DC injection from modern inverters (assumed ideal), we set $V_0 = 0$:

$$V_{rms,total} = \sqrt{V_{1,rms}^2 + \sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Pythagorean decomposition**

We can expand:

$$V_{rms,total}^2 = V_{1,rms}^2 + \sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2$$

Rearranging:

$$\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2 = V_{rms,total}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2$$

**Step 4: Define the RMS value of harmonic distortion**

The RMS value of the "distortion portion" only is:

$$V_{distortion,rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}$$

**Step 5: Form the THD ratio**

Dividing $V_{distortion,rms}$ by the fundamental RMS value gives:

$$THD_V = \frac{V_{distortion,rms}}{V_{1,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}}$$

**Step 6: Convert to percentage**

$$\boxed{THD_V (\%) = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Significance:** A THD of 0% means a pure sine wave. A THD of 5% means the harmonic energy constitutes 5% of the fundamental energy. IEEE 519 mandates THD $\leq 5\%$ at the PCC for voltages below 1 kV.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: True Power Factor in the Presence of Harmonics

**Statement:** Derive the True Power Factor (TPF) of a non-sinusoidal system in terms of Displacement PF and Distortion PF.

**Given:**
- Fundamental voltage $V_1$, fundamental current $I_1$, with phase angle $\phi_1$ between them
- Total current $I_{rms} = \sqrt{I_1^2 + \sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}$
- Harmonic voltages $V_h$ and currents $I_h$ for $h \geq 2$

**Step 1: Total Average (Real) Power**

Real power is delivered only by components of voltage and current at the **same frequency**. Hence, harmonic voltages of order $h$ cannot do real work with currents of a different order. Real power is:

$$P = V_1 I_1 \cos(\phi_1) + \sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h I_h \cos(\phi_h)$$

**Step 2: Simplified Approximation**

In many practical systems, harmonic voltages are small compared to $V_1$. Hence the second term is often neglected:

$$P \approx V_1 I_1 \cos(\phi_1)$$

**Step 3: Apparent Power Calculation**

Apparent power $S$ uses **total RMS** voltage and current:

$$S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$$

**Step 4: Express True Power Factor**

$$TPF = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{V_1 I_1 \cos(\phi_1)}{V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}}$$

**Step 5: Introduce Displacement PF**

Define $DPF = \cos(\phi_1)$ (angle between fundamental voltage and fundamental current).

**Step 6: Define Distortion Factor**

$$\frac{V_1 I_1}{V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}} = \underbrace{\frac{I_1}{I_{rms}}}_{\text{Current distortion factor}} \times \underbrace{\frac{V_1}{V_{rms}}}_{\text{Voltage distortion factor}}$$

For typical systems where voltage THD is small (e.g., $THD_V \leq 5\%$), $V_1/V_{rms} \approx 1$. The current distortion factor becomes:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_{rms}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$$

This is the **Distortion Power Factor**.

**Step 7: Final Equation**

$$\boxed{TPF = DPF \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even if the **Displacement Power Factor is unity (1.0)**, the True Power Factor can be significantly less than 1 due to harmonic distortion. Utilities penalize consumers based on **True PF**, not just DPF.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A 415 V, 50 Hz industrial feeder has the following harmonic current spectrum measured at the PCC:

| **Harmonic Order ($h$)** | **Current $I_h$ (A)** |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1 (Fundamental) | 250.0 |
| 3 | 60.0 |
| 5 | 45.0 |
| 7 | 25.0 |
| 11 | 12.0 |
| 13 | 8.0 |

Maximum demand load current $I_L = 280$ A. Calculate: (i) THD of current, (ii) TDD, (iii) True PF if DPF = 0.92 lagging.

**Solution:**

**Step (i): THD of Current**

First compute the RMS sum of harmonic currents:

$$\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2 = (60)^2 + (45)^2 + (25)^2 + (12)^2 + (8)^2$$

$$= 3600 + 2025 + 625 + 144 + 64 = 6458 \, A^2$$

Therefore:

$$\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2} = \sqrt{6458} \approx 80.36 \, A$$

$$THD_I = \frac{80.36}{250.0} \times 100\% = \boxed{32.14\%}$$

**Step (ii): Total Demand Distortion (TDD)**

$$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_L} \times 100\% = \frac{80.36}{280.0} \times 100\% = \boxed{28.70\%}$$

**Step (iii): True Power Factor**

$$Distortion\,PF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (0.3214)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.1033}} = \frac{1}{1.0504} \approx 0.9520$$

$$TPF = DPF \times Distortion\,PF = 0.92 \times 0.9520 = \boxed{0.8758 \approx 0.876 \text{ lagging}}$$

**Interpretation:** Despite a relatively healthy displacement PF of 0.92, the harmonic pollution drops the True PF to **0.876**, triggering utility penalty tariffs. The current THD of **32.14%** far exceeds the IEEE 519 limit (typically 8%–15% depending on $I_{sc}/I_L$), mandating harmonic mitigation through **Active Harmonic Filters (AHF)** or **passive tuned filters**.

### 4.4 Cause-and-Effect Matrix — Common PQ Issues and Sources

| **PQ Issue** | **Common Causes** | **Affected Equipment** | **Primary Mitigation** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Voltage Sag** | Motor starting, faults, lightning | PLCs, computers, contactors | UPS, DVR, constant voltage transformers |
| **Voltage Swell** | Capacitor switching, load rejection | Electronic drives, capacitors | Surge arrestors, DVR |
| **Harmonics** | VFDs, SMPS, arc furnaces, LED drivers | Transformers, cables, capacitors | Active/Passive filters, K-rated transformers |
| **Transients** | Lightning, switching, capacitor banks | All electronic equipment | Surge protective devices (SPDs), TVS diodes |
| **Flicker** | Arc furnaces, welding machines | Lighting, displays | STATCOM, SVC |
| **Frequency Variation** | Grid instability, islanding | Synchronous machines, clocks | Inertial storage, governor control |

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

---

**Q1. [3 Marks]** Define the term **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**. State the IEEE 519 recommended voltage THD limit at the Point of Common Coupling for low-voltage systems.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is a quantitative measure of the degree of distortion present in a non-sinusoidal periodic waveform. It is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components (from order 2 onwards) to the RMS value of the fundamental component, expressed as a percentage.
>
> Mathematically: $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$
>
> **IEEE 519 Voltage THD Limit:** For systems with nominal voltage $\leq$ 1 kV at the PCC, the recommended maximum **voltage THD is 5%**, with each individual harmonic not exceeding 3% of the fundamental.
>
> *(Examiner expects: Definition formula + 5% limit numerical value)*

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]** Distinguish between **Displacement Power Factor (DPF)** and **True Power Factor (TPF)**. Why is the distinction important in the presence of harmonics?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Displacement Power Factor (DPF)** is the cosine of the phase angle between the **fundamental voltage and fundamental current** only: $DPF = \cos(\phi_1)$. It ignores the contribution of harmonic currents.
>
> **True Power Factor (TPF)** accounts for both the phase shift and the waveform distortion caused by harmonics: $TPF = \dfrac{P}{S} = DPF \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$
>
> **Importance:** Even if a facility maintains unity DPF (e.g., through PF correction capacitors), the True PF can still be poor (e.g., 0.85) due to high current THD. Utility billing and CEA regulations levy penalties on **True PF**, not DPF. Hence, simply installing capacitor banks is insufficient — harmonic filters are also needed.
>
> *(Examiner expects: Both formulas and the penalty/billing implication)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A [14 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the major **categories of power quality disturbances** as classified by IEEE Standard 1159. With neat waveform sketches, distinguish between **voltage sag** and **voltage swell**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CBEMA and ITIC curves**. How are these curves used to assess the ride-through capability of sensitive equipment against voltage disturbances?

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Introduction [1 mark]:**
The IEEE Standard 1159 *"Recommended Practice for Monitoring Electric Power Quality"* (revision 2019) classifies power quality disturbances into seven major categories based on **spectral content, duration, and magnitude**.

**Categories [4 marks]:**

1. **Transients** — Subdivided into *impulsive* (unidirectional, < 50 ns) and *oscillatory* (bidirectional, 50 ns – 5 ms). Caused by lightning, capacitor switching.

2. **Short-duration RMS variations** — Sag (0.1–0.9 pu, 0.5 cycles – 1 min), Swell (1.1–1.8 pu), and Interruption (< 0.1 pu).

3. **Long-duration RMS variations** — Over-voltage, under-voltage, sustained interruption (> 1 min).

4. **Waveform Distortion** — Harmonics, inter-harmonics, DC offset, notching, noise.

5. **Voltage Fluctuations** — Small cyclic variations causing lighting flicker (0.1%–7% amplitude, < 25 Hz).

6. **Power Frequency Variations** — Deviations from 50 Hz nominal, usually < 10 s.

7. **Static Voltage Variations** — Steady-state magnitude errors.

**Waveform Comparison [2 marks]:**

| **Feature** | **Voltage Sag** | **Voltage Swell** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| RMS Magnitude | 0.1 – 0.9 pu | 1.1 – 1.8 pu |
| Typical Duration | 0.5 cycles – 1 min | 0.5 cycles – 1 min |
| Common Causes | Motor starting, short circuits, heavy load switching | Sudden load drop, capacitor energization, single-line-to-ground faults |
| Effect | Contactor drop-out, PLC reset, VFD trip | Insulation stress, electronic equipment damage |

> **ASCII Sketch Hint (Examiner expects labelled diagrams):**
>
> Sag: A momentary dip in RMS amplitude, typically 20%–30% below nominal for 3–10 cycles.
>
> ` _   _   _   _   _   _ ` (normal sine)
>
> ` _   _ ⌒_   _   _   _ ` (sag — middle dip)
>
> Swell: A momentary rise in RMS amplitude.
>
> ` _   _   ⌒_   _   _   _ ` (swell — middle peak)

**Conclusion [0.5 mark]:**
Understanding these classifications enables systematic diagnosis during a Power Quality Audit.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Introduction [1 mark]:**
The **CBEMA (Computer Business Equipment Manufacturers Association) curve**, later revised and rebranded as the **ITIC (Information Technology Industry Council) curve**, is a power acceptability envelope that defines the voltage tolerance of sensitive electronic equipment as a function of disturbance duration.

**CBEMA Curve Description [3 marks]:**

- **X-axis** (logarithmic): Duration of disturbance (cycles to seconds)
- **Y-axis** (linear): RMS voltage magnitude as a percentage of nominal
- **Three regions:**
  - **Acceptable Region** (between the two curves) — Equipment operates normally
  - **Prohibited Region** (above upper curve) — Equipment damage likely due to over-voltage
  - **Prohibited Region** (below lower curve) — Equipment malfunction due to under-voltage/sag

**ITIC Curve Improvements [1 mark]:**
The ITIC curve (2000) refined the CBEMA curve with:
- Sharper boundaries
- Modified categories for *steady-state tolerance*, *transient tolerance*
- Better definition at the **0.5 cycle** boundary (essential for modern electronics)
- A *no-damage region* clearly demarcated

**Application in PQ Audit [2 marks]:**

1. **Equipment Vulnerability Assessment** — Plot measured sags/swells on the curve to determine whether a given event will cause equipment trip/failure.
2. **Specification of Ride-Through Capability** — UPS systems and DVRs are specified to "ride through" events that fall outside the ITIC envelope.
3. **Coordination with Utility** — CBEMA/ITIC serves as a common reference between utility (supply side) and consumer (load side).
4. **Audit Reporting** — PQ audit reports use the CBEMA/ITIC overlay to visually demonstrate the severity of recorded events.

**Conclusion [0.5 mark]:**
The CBEMA/ITIC curve is the de-facto industry benchmark for assessing equipment ride-through and remains a **mandatory inclusion** in KTU PQ audit reports.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **step-by-step methodology** for conducting a **Power Quality Audit** in an industrial facility. List the essential instruments used with their specifications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 415 V, 50 Hz three-phase industrial busbar carries the following harmonic current spectrum:

| $h$ | 1 | 5 | 7 | 11 | 13 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $I_h$ (A) | 320 | 80 | 45 | 20 | 12 |

The short-circuit current at the PCC is 8000 A and maximum demand load current is 350 A. Calculate: (i) Current THD, (ii) TDD, (iii) Verify IEEE 519 compliance, (iv) True Power Factor if DPF = 0.95 lag.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Introduction [1 mark]:**
A Power Quality Audit is a structured engineering exercise following a logical sequence of **planning → measurement → analysis → reporting → remediation**.

**Step-by-Step Methodology [4 marks]:**

1. **Preliminary Site Survey** — Collect single-line diagram, load list, billing history, and equipment criticality ranking.

2. **Define Audit Objectives** — Identify key concerns (e.g., tripping, penalty bills, harmonic heating) and set compliance targets (IEEE 519 / EN 50160).

3. **PQ Meter Installation** — Deploy Class A compliant PQ meters (per IEC 61000-4-30) at:
   - Main incomer (utility side)
   - Point of Common Coupling (PCC)
   - Major sub-panels feeding critical loads

4. **Monitoring Duration** — Continuous recording for **minimum 7 days**, ideally **one complete working cycle** (typically 30 days) to capture weekly load patterns.

5. **Event Capture** — Record both steady-state parameters (RMS, THD, PF) and transient events (sag, swell, transients, flicker).

6. **Data Analysis** — Use software (e.g., Fluke PowerLog, DranView, Qualitrol) to compute:
   - Statistical summaries (95th percentile values)
   - Harmonic spectrum (individual + THD)
   - CBEMA/ITIC plot
   - EN 50160 compliance check

7. **Reporting and Mitigation** — Issue detailed report with PQ indices, violations, root cause analysis, and recommended solutions (passive/active filters, UPS, DVR, K-rated transformers).

**Essential Instruments and Specifications [2 marks]:**

| **Instrument** | **Function** | **Key Specification** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Power Quality Analyzer** | Multi-parameter recording | Class A (IEC 61000-4-30), 0.1% accuracy |
| **Oscilloscope (Digital Storage)** | Transient capture | $\geq$ 100 MHz bandwidth, 1 GSa/s |
| **Clamp Meter (True RMS)** | Current measurement | True RMS, 0.5% accuracy, harmonic capable |
| **Current Transformers (CTs)** | Current sensing | Accuracy class 0.5 or better |
| **Voltage Transformers (PTs)** | Voltage sensing | Class 0.5 metering accuracy |
| **Flicker Meter** | Flicker measurement | IEC 61000-4-15 compliant |
| **Thermal Imager** | Hotspot detection | Resolution $\leq$ 0.1°C |

**Conclusion [0.5 mark]:**
Proper methodology and instrument selection are critical for valid PQ audit conclusions.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Given Data:**
- $V = 415$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, three-phase
- Harmonic spectrum: $I_1 = 320$ A, $I_5 = 80$ A, $I_7 = 45$ A, $I_{11} = 20$ A, $I_{13} = 12$ A
- $I_{sc} = 8000$ A, $I_L = 350$ A
- $DPF = 0.95$ lagging

**Step (i): Current THD [2 marks]**

$$\sum I_h^2 = (80)^2 + (45)^2 + (20)^2 + (12)^2$$

$$= 6400 + 2025 + 400 + 144 = 8969 \, A^2$$

$$\sqrt{\sum I_h^2} = \sqrt{8969} = 94.70 \, A$$

$$THD_I = \frac{94.70}{320} \times 100\% = \boxed{29.59\%}$$

**Step (ii): TDD [1 mark]**

$$TDD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum I_h^2}}{I_L} \times 100\% = \frac{94.70}{350} \times 100\% = \boxed{27.06\%}$$

**Step (iii): IEEE 519 Compliance Check [2 marks]**

First compute the ratio:

$$\frac{I_{sc}}{I_L} = \frac{8000}{350} = 22.86$$

This falls in the bracket **20–50**, so the applicable limits are:
- Maximum individual harmonic = **7%**
- Maximum TDD = **8%**

**Verification:**
- $I_{5}/I_1 = (80/320) \times 100\% = 25\%$ → **EXCEEDS 7% limit** ❌
- $I_{7}/I_1 = (45/320) \times 100\% = 14.06\%$ → **EXCEEDS 7% limit** ❌
- TDD = **27.06%** → **EXCEEDS 8% limit** ❌

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students **forget to compute $I_{sc}/I_L$** and blindly apply limits. Always determine the correct bracket first!
> - Students often **confuse TDD with THD**. THD uses $I_1$ in the denominator; TDD uses $I_L$. They are **different**.
> - Maximum individual harmonic limit must be checked **separately for each harmonic**, not just for the dominant one.
> - Mark loss in KTU valuation: Up to **2 marks** if compliance check is not contextualized to the correct $I_{sc}/I_L$ bracket.

**Conclusion:** The system **FAILS** IEEE 519 compliance on all three counts. **Mitigation required**: Install a **tuned passive filter** for 5th and 7th harmonics or an **Active Harmonic Filter (AHF)**.

**Step (iv): True Power Factor [2 marks]**

$$Distortion\,PF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (0.2959)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.0876}} = \frac{1}{1.0429} = 0.9588$$

$$TPF = DPF \times Distortion\,PF = 0.95 \times 0.9588 = \boxed{0.9109 \approx 0.911 \text{ lagging}}$$

**Conclusion:** Despite a healthy DPF of 0.95, the True PF drops to **0.911** due to harmonic distortion. The utility penalty band (< 0.95 lag in India) is **breached**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power Quality Audit**

- **PQ Definition:** IEEE 1159 — any deviation from nominal voltage, frequency, or waveform.
- **Seven IEEE 1159 Categories:** Transients, short-duration, long-duration, distortion, fluctuation, frequency variation, static.
- **THD Formula (Voltage):** $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$, IEEE 519 limit = **5%** for LV systems.
- **TDD vs THD:** THD uses $I_1$; TDD uses $I_L$ (maximum demand load current).
- **IEEE 519 Voltage Limits:** Individual harmonic ≤ **3%**, THD ≤ **5%** at PCC for $V \leq 1$ kV.
- **IEEE 519 Current Limits:** Depends on $I_{sc}/I_L$ ratio (5 brackets from < 20 to > 1000).
- **DPF vs TPF:** $TPF = DPF \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$. Penalty is on TPF, not DPF.
- **CBEMA / ITIC Curve:** Voltage-tolerance envelope (Y-axis: voltage magnitude, X-axis: duration log scale).
- **CBEMA Regions:** Acceptable (between curves), Prohibited (above = over-voltage damage), Prohibited (below = sag malfunction).
- **PQ Audit Instruments:** Class A PQ analyzer, oscilloscope, true RMS clamp meter, CT/PT, flicker meter, thermal imager.
- **Audit Duration:** Minimum **7 days**, ideal **30 days** (one full weekly production cycle).
- **Common Sources of Harmonics:** VFDs, SMPS, UPS, LED drivers, arc furnaces, saturable reactors.
- **Common Mitigation:** Passive tuned filters (LC), Active Harmonic Filters (AHF), K-rated transformers, 12-pulse rectifiers, UPS with input filters.
- **Compliance Standards:** IEEE 519, IEEE 1159, IEC 61000 series, EN 50160, CBEMA, ITIC, NEC, CEA (India) regulations.
- **PQ Audit Report Must Include:** Single-line diagram, monitoring data, statistical summary (95th percentile), CBEMA/ITIC plot, harmonic spectrum, violations list, root cause analysis, mitigation cost-benefit.
- **Numerical Traps:** Always check $I_{sc}/I_L$ bracket before applying IEEE 519 limits. Always compute both **THD** and **TDD** when asked.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Power Quality Audit** 📘
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation in Buildings: ECBC Code (Basic Aspects)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Conservation in Buildings: ECBC Code (Basic Aspects)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition – The ECBC Code

The **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** is a regulatory document established by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**, under the Ministry of Power, Government of India, that prescribes **minimum energy performance standards** for the **design and construction of new commercial buildings** in India. It was first published in **2007** and subsequently revised in **2017** to align with India's commitment under the **Paris Climate Accord (NDC Targets)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope of ECBC (2024 KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> ECBC applies to any commercial building or building complex having a **connected load of 100 kW or more**, OR a **contract demand of 120 kVA or more**. It covers five major building systems: (1) Building Envelope, (2) Heating, Ventilation and Air Conditioning (HVAC), (3) Service Hot Water, (4) Lighting, and (5) Electrical.

### Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Full Form | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| ECBC | Energy Conservation Building Code | Minimum baseline standard |
| ECBC+ | ECBC Plus | **25% more efficient** than ECBC |
| SuperECBC | Super Energy Conservation Building Code | **50% more efficient** than ECBC |
| EPI | Energy Performance Index | kWh/m²/year – the master metric |
| OTTV | Overall Thermal Transfer Value | W/m² of envelope heat gain |
| LPD | Lighting Power Density | W/m² of installed lighting load |
| SHGC | Solar Heat Gain Coefficient | Fraction of solar radiation admitted |
| VLT | Visible Light Transmittance | Fraction of visible light passing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Residential Counterpart:** The **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)** is the parallel energy code for residential buildings, developed by BEE to ensure comfort and energy efficiency in housing stock.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "House as a Thermos" Idea

Imagine a **glass thermos flask** used to keep tea hot. It works on three principles:
1. **Insulation** (the double glass wall) → reduces unwanted heat flow.
2. **Reflective coating** (silver lining) → bounces back radiant heat.
3. **Tight lid** (no air leaks) → prevents energy escape.

An **ECBC-compliant building** is essentially the **architectural version of a thermos flask**:
- Walls, roof, and windows act as the **insulation layer** with controlled **U-values** and **SHGC**.
- Airtight construction (via dampers and sealing) is the **tight lid**.
- Efficient **HVAC**, **LED lighting**, and **solar integration** are the modern equivalent of a smart heating element.

In contrast, a **non-ECBC building** is like a **sieve bucket** – energy pours out through poor glazing, leaky ducts, oversized AC units, and inefficient lighting, while outside heat pours in. The ECBC essentially "plugs the holes" and ensures every watt of energy does useful work.

## 1.3 Why ECBC Was Needed – The Energy Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Buildings account for **~33% of India's total electricity consumption** (CEA data).
> - HVAC alone consumes **40–60%** of building electricity in tropical India.
> - Without codes, the commercial building stock in India is projected to **grow 5x by 2030** (IGBC estimates).
> - ECBC adoption targets a **25–50% reduction** in building energy use, translating to **~30 GW of avoided generation capacity by 2030**.

## 1.4 Building Typology Covered Under ECBC

ECBC classifies commercial buildings into the following climate-resilient categories based on **Thermal Comfort (TC) and Energy Efficiency (EE)**:

- **Star Label 1★ (TC1–TC4)**: Basic compliance, four climate zones covered.
- **Star Label 2★**: Higher efficiency, mandatory **3R strategy** (Reduce, Recover, Reuse).
- **Star Label 3★**: Mandatory **on-site renewable energy** integration.
- **Star Label 4★ & 5★**: Performance-based, super-efficient class.

> [!NOTE]
> **India's Five Climate Zones (ECBC Reference):**
> 1. **Composite (C)** – Delhi, Lucknow
> 2. **Hot & Dry (HD)** – Jaipur, Ahmedabad
> 3. **Warm & Humid (WH)** – Mumbai, Kochi, Chennai
> 4. **Temperate (T)** – Bengaluru, Pune
> 5. **Cold (CD)** – Leh, Shimla

## 1.5 Fundamental Philosophy of ECBC

The ECBC operates on **three fundamental design pillars**:

1. **Pillar 1 – Envelope Optimization:** Design the building "skin" (walls, roof, windows, floors) to minimize unwanted heat transfer.
2. **Pillar 2 – System Efficiency:** Ensure mechanical and electrical systems (HVAC, lighting, pumps, fans) operate at peak efficiency.
3. **Pillar 3 – Integrated renewables:** Use renewable energy (solar PV, solar thermal) to meet remaining demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** Always remember that ECBC is a **design-time code**, not an operational retrofit standard. The energy savings are "locked in" during the architectural and MEP design phase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ECBC Compliance Framework – Architecture

The ECBC provides **three alternative compliance pathways**. A designer can satisfy ANY one of them to certify the building.

### Path A – Prescriptive Method
Each building component is checked individually against prescribed **minimum or maximum values**. For example:
- Wall U-value ≤ prescribed limit for the climate zone.
- LPD ≤ prescribed limit for the building type.
- Equipment efficiency ≥ prescribed minimum.

This is the **simplest** and most commonly adopted path in KTU numerical problems.

### Path B – Trade-Off (Component Trade-off) Method
Some components may exceed limits if **compensated by superior performance in other components**. Energy "budgets" can be shifted within the envelope, similar to a tax-saving instrument.

> **Analogy:** Imagine a student scoring 80/100 in Maths and 60/100 in Physics. If the school has a "trade-off" rule allowing an average ≥ 70, the student still passes. ECBC's trade-off method works exactly like this – a poor wall U-value can be offset by an excellent window SHGC.

### Path C – Whole Building Performance (WBP) Method
A **dynamic energy simulation** of the entire building (using software like **EnergyPlus, eQUEST, IES-VE, or DesignBuilder**) is performed, and the simulated annual energy use is compared to a **standard baseline building** of identical geometry. If the proposed building consumes ≤ the baseline, it complies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** The WBP method uses **EPI (Energy Performance Index)** as the compliance metric. The standard ECBC threshold for offices is typically **120 kWh/m²/year**, with ECBC+ at **90 kWh/m²/year** and SuperECBC at **60 kWh/m²/year** (indicative values; check BEE updates).

## 2.2 The Five Building Systems – Detailed Breakdown

### System 1: Building Envelope

The envelope separates conditioned interior space from the unconditioned exterior. ECBC controls:

- **Opaque Assembly (Wall, Roof, Floor):** Through maximum permissible **U-value (W/m²K)**.
- **Fenestration (Windows, Skylights):** Through maximum **U-value**, maximum **SHGC**, and minimum **VLT** (to ensure daylight without glare).
- **Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR):** Limits the fraction of façade that can be glazed, because glass is a poor insulator compared to walls.
- **Air Leakage:** Maximum air changes per hour (ACH) at 50 Pa pressure differential.

> **Formula Reference:**
> $$\text{U-value} = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}}$$
> where $R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{inside}} + \sum R_{\text{layers}} + R_{\text{outside}}$ (m²K/W)

### System 2: HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning)

ECBC mandates minimum efficiency for:
- Chillers (governed by **COP** – Coefficient of Performance, or **kW/ton** rating).
- Boilers (governed by **Thermal Efficiency %**).
- Pumps and fans (governed by specific power consumption).
- Ductwork and piping insulation (minimum R-value).

> **Formula Reference:**
> $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Output (Cooling/Heating in kW)}}{\text{Required Input (Electrical in kW)}}$$
> $$\text{kW/ton} = \frac{3.516}{\text{COP}}$$
> where 1 ton of refrigeration = 3.516 kW of cooling.

### System 3: Service Hot Water
Solar water heating systems mandated based on building type and star rating. Minimum **recovery efficiency** and standby losses for storage tanks.

### System 4: Lighting
The most heavily tested area in KTU exams. Key parameter: **Lighting Power Density (LPD)**.

> **Formula Reference:**
> $$\text{LPD} = \frac{\text{Total Installed Lighting Load (W)}}{\text{Illuminated Area (m²)}} \quad \text{(W/m²)}$$

ECBC sets **maximum LPD** values for various space types:
- Offices: 10–12 W/m²
- Classrooms: 12–15 W/m²
- Hospitals: 14–18 W/m²
- Retail: 16–20 W/m²
*(Indicative values, BEE 2017)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Lighting Controls under ECBC:**
> 1. **Daylight-responsive controls** (photo-sensors) for areas near windows.
> 2. **Occupancy sensors** in spaces with intermittent use (conference rooms, restrooms).
> 3. **Time-switch controls** for common areas.
> 4. **Task lighting** as a substitute for ambient lighting in offices.

### System 5: Electrical Systems
- Minimum **transformer efficiency** (98%+) and **PF (Power Factor)** of 0.95+.
- Power factor correction mandatory.
- Use of **energy-efficient motors** (IE3/IE4 class).
- **Voltage drop** in feeders limited to 2% and branch circuits to 3%.
- **Renewable Energy Integration:** On-site solar PV as per star rating.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | U-value (Thermal Transmittance) | U | W/m²K | Wall, roof, window heat flow |
| 2 | R-value (Thermal Resistance) | R | m²K/W | Insulation effectiveness |
| 3 | SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient) | SHGC | Dimensionless (0–1) | Solar heat through glazing |
| 4 | VLT (Visible Light Transmittance) | VLT | Dimensionless (0–1) | Daylight through glazing |
| 5 | LPD (Lighting Power Density) | LPD | W/m² | Lighting energy check |
| 6 | EPI (Energy Performance Index) | EPI | kWh/m²/year | Whole building energy check |
| 7 | COP (Coefficient of Performance) | COP | Dimensionless | Chiller/AC efficiency |
| 8 | kW/ton Rating | – | kW/ton | Chiller efficiency (Indian context) |
| 9 | OTTV (Overall Thermal Transfer Value) | OTTV | W/m² | Envelope heat gain (Asian codes) |
| 10 | WWR (Window-to-Wall Ratio) | WWR | % or fraction | Glazed fraction of façade |
| 11 | Energy Savings % | $\eta_s$ | % | Compliance level check |
| 12 | $\text{ACH}_{50}$ | Air Changes/Hour | 1/h | Envelope airtightness test |
| 13 | Thermal Resistance (multilayer) | $R_{tot}$ | m²K/W | Sum of layer resistances |
| 14 | Cost of Saved Energy | COSE | ₹/kWh | Payback calculations |
| 15 | Simple Payback Period | SPB | years | Investment viability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions (Standard ECBC Test Conditions):**
> - Internal temperature: **24°C DB / 17°C WB** (office comfort).
> - External: Local TMY (Typical Meteorological Year) data.
> - Test pressure for air leakage: **50 Pascal** (referred to as ACH₅₀).

## 2.4 The Concept of OTTV (Overall Thermal Transfer Value)

A critical concept used in **tropical building codes** (Singapore, Malaysia, India) is OTTV, which captures the **combined conductive and radiative heat gain** through the envelope.

$$\text{OTTV} = \frac{(U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T) + (SHGC \cdot A \cdot SF \cdot I_{s})}{A_{total}}$$

where:
- $U$ = thermal transmittance of opaque/glazed assembly
- $A$ = area of the assembly
- $\Delta T$ = indoor-outdoor temperature difference (K)
- $SHGC$ = solar heat gain coefficient
- $SF$ = shading factor of overhang/fins
- $I_s$ = total incident solar radiation (W/m²)
- $A_{total}$ = total envelope area

> **Real-World Utility:** OTTV is used in Singapore's BCA Green Mark and is being adopted in Indian super-tall building audits. It gives a single-number "thermal performance" of the entire envelope, simplifying compliance.

## 2.5 Cost-Effectiveness Criterion in ECBC

For trade-off and WBP methods, ECBC requires that any energy-saving measure must be **cost-effective** over its lifetime. The criterion is:

$$\text{Lifetime Savings} \geq \text{Incremental Investment}$$

Equivalently, the **Cost of Conserved Energy (CCE)** must be less than the **Cost of Supplied Energy (CSE)**:

$$\text{CCE} = \frac{\text{Incremental Cost}}{\text{Annual Energy Saved}} \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{CSE} = \text{Tariff (₹/kWh)}$$

If **CCE < CSE**, the measure is justified.

> **Analogy:** It's like buying a slightly expensive LED bulb (₹500) that saves ₹200/year in electricity. The CCE = 500/200 = ₹2.5/kWh. If your tariff is ₹8/kWh, then CSE = ₹8/kWh, and since ₹2.5 < ₹8, the LED is cost-effective – buy it!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The U-Value for a Multi-Layer Wall

A **typical ECBC-compliant wall** consists of the following layers (from outside to inside):

1. **External plaster** (cement-sand, 12 mm)
2. **Brick masonry** (230 mm)
3. **Air gap / Insulation cavity** (50 mm)
4. **Inner brick layer** (115 mm)
5. **Internal plaster** (12 mm)

**Objective:** Derive the overall U-value of this composite wall and verify ECBC compliance for the **Hot & Dry (HD) climate zone** of India.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** List thermal conductivities (k) of each material.

| Layer | Material | Thickness $L$ (m) | $k$ (W/mK) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | External plaster | 0.012 | 0.721 |
| 2 | Brick masonry | 0.230 | 0.812 |
| 3 | Air cavity (with insulation) | 0.050 | 0.040 (mineral wool) |
| 4 | Inner brick | 0.115 | 0.812 |
| 5 | Internal plaster | 0.012 | 0.721 |

**Step 2:** Recall the standard surface resistances (as per ECBC / ASHRAE):
- Outside film: $R_{so} = 0.04$ m²K/W
- Inside film: $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²K/W

**Step 3:** Calculate the resistance of each individual layer using $R_i = L_i / k_i$.

For Layer 1 (External plaster):
$$R_1 = \frac{0.012}{0.721} = 0.01664 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

For Layer 2 (Brick masonry):
$$R_2 = \frac{0.230}{0.812} = 0.28325 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

For Layer 3 (Insulation):
$$R_3 = \frac{0.050}{0.040} = 1.25000 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

For Layer 4 (Inner brick):
$$R_4 = \frac{0.115}{0.812} = 0.14163 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

For Layer 5 (Internal plaster):
$$R_5 = \frac{0.012}{0.721} = 0.01664 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 4:** Sum all resistances in series (since layers are perpendicular to heat flow).

$$R_{total} = R_{so} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_5 + R_{si}$$

Substituting the values:

$$R_{total} = 0.04 + 0.01664 + 0.28325 + 1.25000 + 0.14163 + 0.01664 + 0.13$$

$$R_{total} = 1.87816 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the overall U-value (thermal transmittance).

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{1.87816} = 0.5324 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 6:** Verify ECBC compliance for Hot & Dry climate (ECBC 2017 limit ≈ 0.40 W/m²K for 24-hr weighted average).

$$U_{calculated} = 0.5324 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K} \;\; \text{vs.} \;\; U_{ECBC} = 0.40 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The calculated U-value is **33% higher** than the ECBC limit. The wall does **NOT comply**. To comply, the insulation thickness must be **increased from 50 mm to ~80 mm** (or use a higher-grade insulation like XPS with $k = 0.025$).

### Verification Calculation for 80 mm Insulation

Recompute $R_3$ for 80 mm of mineral wool:

$$R_3' = \frac{0.080}{0.040} = 2.000 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

New $R_{total}$:
$$R_{total}' = 0.04 + 0.01664 + 0.28325 + 2.000 + 0.14163 + 0.01664 + 0.13$$

$$R_{total}' = 2.62816 \; \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

New $U$-value:
$$U' = \frac{1}{2.62816} = 0.3805 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

> **Compliance Check:** $0.3805 < 0.40$ → **Wall now COMPLIES with ECBC 2017 limits for Hot & Dry climate.** ✅

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: EPI (Energy Performance Index)

**Scenario:** An office building in **Kochi (Warm & Humid climate)** has the following annual energy consumption profile:
- HVAC: 1,80,000 kWh
- Lighting: 60,000 kWh
- Plug loads: 45,000 kWh
- Lifts and pumps: 15,000 kWh

The total **air-conditioned built-up area** is **2,500 m²**.

**Objective:** Compute the EPI and determine the ECBC class.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Sum the total annual energy consumption.
