$$E_{total} = 180{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 15{,}000$$

$$E_{total} = 3{,}00{,}000 \; \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the EPI.

$$\text{EPI} = \frac{E_{total}}{\text{Built-up Area}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{2{,}500}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EPI} = 120 \; \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/year}}$$

**Step 3:** Classify the building (indicative BEE thresholds for office buildings).

| EPI Range (kWh/m²/yr) | Class |
|---|---|
| 180+ | Non-ECBC |
| 120–180 | ECBC Baseline |
| 90–120 | ECBC+ |
| 60–90 | SuperECBC |
| < 60 | Net Zero / SuperECBC+ |

> **Conclusion:** With EPI = 120, the building is at the **ECBC baseline** (just compliant). To upgrade to ECBC+, energy consumption must reduce to 90 × 2,500 = 2,25,000 kWh/year – a **25% reduction** of 75,000 kWh.

### Step 4: Target Energy Reduction

$$E_{target, ECBC+} = 90 \times 2{,}500 = 2{,}25{,}000 \; \text{kWh/year}$$

$$E_{savings} = 3{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}25{,}000 = 75{,}000 \; \text{kWh/year}$$

In monetary terms, at ₹8/kWh:

$$\text{Annual Monetary Savings} = 75{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹} 6{,}00{,}000 \; \text{per year}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: LPD Compliance Check

**Scenario:** A proposed **conference room** of dimensions 12 m × 10 m = 120 m² will be lit with 30 LED downlights, each rated **36 W**.

**Objective:** Check ECBC compliance for an office/conference area (LPD limit = 12 W/m²).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the total connected lighting load.

$$P_{total} = 30 \times 36 = 1{,}080 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the LPD.

$$\text{LPD} = \frac{P_{total}}{A} = \frac{1{,}080}{120}$$

$$\text{LPD} = 9.0 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Check compliance.

$$\text{LPD}_{calculated} = 9.0 \;\; \text{vs.} \;\; \text{LPD}_{ECBC} = 12.0 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

> **Result:** $9.0 < 12.0$ → **The conference room lighting design COMPLIES with ECBC.** ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Headroom available:** $12.0 - 9.0 = 3.0$ W/m² could be added. But the design is already at **25% below the ECBC limit**, qualifying for **ECBC+ lighting** performance.

### Variation Problem: Cost Optimization

If the architect considers upgrading to **40 W downlights** to improve lux levels, what is the maximum number of fixtures that can be installed?

$$\text{Maximum allowable power} = \text{LPD}_{limit} \times A = 12 \times 120 = 1{,}440 \; \text{W}$$

$$\text{Maximum fixtures} = \left\lfloor \frac{1{,}440}{40} \right\rfloor = 36 \; \text{fixtures}$$

So up to **36 fixtures** of 40 W can be installed without violating ECBC.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: ECBC Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ECBC Energy Conservation Building Code] --> B[Applicability Commercial Buildings]
    A --> C[Five Building Systems]

    B --> B1[Connected Load 100 kW]
    B --> B2[Contract Demand 120 kVA]

    C --> D1[System 1 Building Envelope]
    C --> D2[System 2 HVAC]
    C --> D3[System 3 Service Hot Water]
    C --> D4[System 4 Lighting]
    C --> D5[System 5 Electrical]

    D1 --> D1a[Walls Roofs U-value]
    D1 --> D1b[Windows SHGC VLT WWR]
    D1 --> D1c[Air Leakage ACH50]

    D2 --> D2a[Chiller COP kW per ton]
    D2 --> D2b[Boiler Thermal Efficiency]
    D2 --> D2c[Ductwork Insulation R-value]

    D3 --> D3a[Solar Water Heating]
    D3 --> D3b[Storage Tank Recovery]

    D4 --> D4a[Lighting Power Density LPD]
    D4 --> D4b[Occupancy Sensors]
    D4 --> D4c[Daylight Controls]

    D5 --> D5a[Transformer Efficiency]
    D5 --> D5b[Power Factor above 0.95]
    D5 --> D5c[Renewable Integration]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: ECBC Compliance Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[New Commercial Building 100 kW / 120 kVA] --> Q1{Choose Compliance Path}

    Q1 -->|Path A| PA[Prescriptive Method]
    Q1 -->|Path B| PB[Trade Off Method]
    Q1 -->|Path C| PC[Whole Building Performance]

    PA --> PA1[Check each component U-value SHGC LPD COP]
    PA1 --> PA2{All within limits?}
    PA2 -->|Yes| OK1[COMPLIES with ECBC]
    PA2 -->|No| Fail1[Modify Design and Re-check]

    PB --> PB1[Build Energy Budget per component]
    PB1 --> PB2{Overall budget met?}
    PB2 -->|Yes| OK2[COMPLIES via Trade Off]
    PB2 -->|No| Fail2[Improve weak components]

    PC --> PC1[Run Energy Simulation EnergyPlus / eQUEST]
    PC1 --> PC2{Simulated EPI less than baseline?}
    PC2 -->|Yes| OK3[COMPLIES via WBP]
    PC2 -->|No| Fail3[Optimize systems and re-simulate]

    OK1 --> Class{Energy Class?}
    OK2 --> Class
    OK3 --> Class

    Class -->|Baseline| ECBC[ECBC]
    Class -->|25 percent better| ECBCplus[ECBC Plus]
    Class -->|50 percent better| SuperECBC[SuperECBC]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Wall Section Showing Heat Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Hot_Outside [HOT OUTSIDE AIR 38 degree C]
        O[Outside Surface]
    end

    O --> L1[Layer 1 External Plaster R equals 0.0166]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 Brick Masonry R equals 0.2833]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Insulation R equals 1.2500]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Inner Brick R equals 0.1416]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Internal Plaster R equals 0.0166]
    L5 --> I[Inside Surface 24 degree C]

    L3 -.->|Critical layer| Note[Insulation is the dominant thermal resistor]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: ECBC Star Rating Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Base[Standard Building<br/>High Energy Use] --> ECBC1[ECBC Baseline<br/>25 percent Savings]
    ECBC1 --> ECBC2[ECBC Plus<br/>40 percent Savings]
    ECBC2 --> ECBC3[SuperECBC<br/>50 percent Savings]
    ECBC3 --> ECBC4[Net Zero Energy Building<br/>100 percent On-site RE]

    Base -.->|Without code| HighEPI[EPI 180 kWh per m2 per year]
    ECBC1 -.->|Baseline| MidEPI[EPI 120 kWh per m2 per year]
    ECBC2 -.->|Plus| LowEPI[EPI 90 kWh per m2 per year]
    ECBC3 -.->|Super| VLowEPI[EPI 60 kWh per m2 per year]
    ECBC4 -.->|Net Zero| ZeroEPI[EPI approaches 0]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC). State its scope of applicability with respect to connected load and contract demand.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** is a regulatory document developed by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** that prescribes minimum energy performance standards for the design and construction of **new commercial buildings** in India.

**Scope of Applicability:** ECBC applies to commercial buildings having:
- A **connected load of 100 kW or more**, OR
- A **contract demand of 120 kVA or more**.

It covers five building systems: envelope, HVAC, service hot water, lighting, and electrical. (3 marks – 1 mark definition + 2 marks scope details)

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**List any four mandatory lighting controls required under the ECBC code.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four mandatory lighting controls under ECBC are:
1. **Daylight-responsive controls** (automatic dimming using photo-sensors) for areas within 6 m of windows.
2. **Occupancy sensors** (PIR-based) for spaces with intermittent use such as conference rooms, restrooms, and storage areas.
3. **Time-switch / scheduling controls** for common areas like lobbies, corridors, and parking lots.
4. **Task-ambient lighting design** – provision of task lights at work-stations to reduce ambient lighting load.

(3 marks – 0.75 marks per control)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **three compliance pathways** prescribed by ECBC for commercial buildings. Discuss the merits and demerits of each path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 200 m² office floor in **Kochi** is to be lit using 40 W LED panels. Calculate the **maximum number of panels** permissible under ECBC. LPD limit for offices = **12 W/m²**. Also compute the **annual energy consumption** and **annual cost** of lighting at ₹8/kWh assuming 10 hours/day, 6 days/week, 50 weeks/year operation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Three Compliance Pathways (7 Marks):**

**Path 1 – Prescriptive Method:** Each building component (wall, window, light, chiller) is independently checked against prescribed numerical limits from ECBC tables. *Merits:* Simple, no software needed, easy verification. *Demerits:* Inflexible, may over-specify some components.

**Path 2 – Trade-off Method:** Allows flexibility between components using an energy budget. Designers can exceed the U-value of a wall if offset by a better SHGC window. *Merits:* Design freedom, cost optimization. *Demerits:* Complex calculations, requires baseline modeling.

**Path 3 – Whole Building Performance (WBP) Method:** Simulates the entire building using software (e.g., EnergyPlus). The simulated EPI is compared to a baseline building of identical geometry. *Merits:* Most accurate, considers interactions. *Demerits:* Requires specialized software and expertise; cost of simulation is higher.

(7 marks – 2 marks per path + 1 mark for comparison)

---

**(b) Lighting Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute maximum allowable power.**

$$P_{max} = \text{LPD}_{limit} \times A = 12 \; \text{W/m}^2 \times 200 \; \text{m}^2 = 2{,}400 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 2: Compute maximum number of 40 W panels.**

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{2{,}400}{40} \right\rfloor = 60 \; \text{panels}$$

**Step 3: Compute annual operating hours.**

$$H_{annual} = 10 \;\text{hrs/day} \times 6 \;\text{days/week} \times 50 \;\text{weeks/year} = 3{,}000 \; \text{hrs/year}$$

**Step 4: Compute total connected load and annual energy.**

$$P_{total} = 60 \times 40 = 2{,}400 \; \text{W} = 2.4 \; \text{kW}$$

$$E_{annual} = 2.4 \;\text{kW} \times 3{,}000 \;\text{hrs} = 7{,}200 \; \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 5: Compute annual electricity cost.**

$$\text{Cost} = 7{,}200 \times 8 = \text{₹} 57{,}600 \; \text{per year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert the LPD from W/m² to total power** before dividing by fixture wattage. Also, do not forget the **floor function** when computing "maximum number" of fixtures – you cannot install 60.5 fixtures!

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Define **EPI (Energy Performance Index)**. An office building in **Trivandrum** has the following annual consumption: HVAC – 1,20,000 kWh, Lighting – 40,000 kWh, Plug loads – 30,000 kWh, Others – 10,000 kWh. The total air-conditioned built-up area is **1,500 m²**. Compute the EPI and identify the ECBC class achieved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat diagram, explain the **five building systems** covered by ECBC. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) EPI Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Definition of EPI (2 marks).**

The **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** is the annual energy consumption of a building divided by its total built-up (air-conditioned) area, expressed in **kWh/m²/year**. It is the master metric used in the Whole Building Performance method of ECBC.

**Step 2: Total annual energy.**

$$E_{total} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \; \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 3: Compute EPI.**

$$\text{EPI} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}500} = 133.33 \; \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/year}$$

**Step 4: ECBC Classification.**

Since the computed EPI (133.33) lies in the **120–180 kWh/m²/year** range, the building is at the **ECBC Baseline** (just compliant). It does **NOT** qualify for ECBC+ (which would require EPI < 120, i.e., 1,80,000 kWh/year).

(7 marks – 2 marks definition + 3 marks calculation + 2 marks classification)

---

**(b) Five Building Systems (7 Marks):**

| # | System | Key Parameters | Example Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Building Envelope** | U-value, SHGC, VLT, WWR, OTTV | Wall U ≤ 0.40 W/m²K (HD zone) |
| 2 | **HVAC** | COP of chiller, boiler efficiency, duct insulation | Chiller COP ≥ 4.5 (≥ 5.0 for ECBC+) |
| 3 | **Service Hot Water** | Solar fraction, recovery efficiency, standby loss | Solar mandatory for hospitals, hotels |
| 4 | **Lighting** | LPD, controls (occupancy + daylight) | Office LPD ≤ 12 W/m² |
| 5 | **Electrical Systems** | Transformer η, PF, motor efficiency, RE integration | PF ≥ 0.95; IE3 motors mandatory |

Refer to the **Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1** for a visual representation of the five systems.

(7 marks – 1.4 marks per system)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For the EPI question, students frequently make **unit errors** – confusing the area (m²) with ft², or mixing kWh with MWh. Always **show units at every step**. Also, classification thresholds must be **memorized** – examiners will not provide the table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – ECBC Basic Aspects**

- **ECBC** = Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) document, applies to commercial buildings with **≥ 100 kW connected load** or **≥ 120 kVA contract demand**.

- **First published in 2007**, revised in **2017**; further updated as **ECBC 2024** (draft).

- **Three classes:** ECBC (baseline), **ECBC+ (25% better)**, **SuperECBC (50% better)**.

- **Residential counterpart:** **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)**.

- **Five climate zones of India:** Composite, Hot-Dry, Warm-Humid, Temperate, Cold.

- **Five systems covered:** Envelope, HVAC, Service Hot Water, Lighting, Electrical.

- **Three compliance paths:** Prescriptive, Trade-off, Whole Building Performance (WBP).

- **Master metric:** **EPI = E_total / Area (kWh/m²/year)**.

- **Key envelope parameters:** U-value, R-value, SHGC, VLT, WWR, OTTV, ACH₅₀.

- **Key HVAC parameter:** **COP = Useful Output / Electrical Input**; 1 TR = 3.516 kW.

- **Key lighting parameter:** **LPD = Total Lighting Load / Area (W/m²)**.

- **Mandatory lighting controls:** Daylight sensors, occupancy sensors, time switches, task-ambient design.

- **Electrical must-haves:** Power Factor ≥ 0.95, IE3+ motors, voltage drop ≤ 2% in feeders.

- **Renewable integration:** On-site solar PV mandatory for 3★ and above (Star Label program).

- **Cost-effectiveness test:** **CCE < CSE** for any energy-conservation measure to be justified.

- **U-value derivation:** $U = 1 / (R_{so} + \sum R_i + R_{si})$, where $R = L / k$.

- **OTTV formula:** $\text{OTTV} = [U \cdot \Delta T] + [SHGC \cdot SF \cdot I_s / A]$ – critical for tropical envelope analysis.

- **KTU numerical hot topics:** LPD check, EPI computation, U-value calculation, kW/ton to COP conversion, payback analysis.

- **Common pitfall to avoid:** Don't confuse **EPI (kWh/m²/year)** with **LPD (W/m²)** – they differ by both the time unit and the system scope.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When answering any ECBC-related question, always **state the climate zone assumption** explicitly, mention the **applicable ECBC class** (baseline/Plus/Super), and **show units at every step** to earn full marks in the calculation sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Management System (BMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Building Management System (BMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Building Management System (BMS)** — A computer-based **interlocking network of hardware and software** that monitors, controls, and optimizes a building's **Mechanical, Electrical, and Electromechanical (M&E)** services such as **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning)**, lighting, power metering, fire safety, security, and access control from a **centralized workstation**.

A BMS is also widely referred to in industry as a **Building Automation System (BAS)**, **Energy Management and Control System (EMCS)**, or simply a **Smart Building Platform**. In KTU 2024 syllabus parlance, BMS is treated as the *operational backbone* of any modern **energy-conscious commercial infrastructure**.

The standard international benchmark that governs BMS interoperability is **ASHRAE Standard 135 — BACnet Protocol (Building Automation and Control Networks)**. The energy-efficiency performance of a BMS-integrated building is typically evaluated against **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** norms — in India, **ECBC 2017** and its updated **SuperECBC** variant prescribe maximum permissible **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** in **$\text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nervous System" of a Building

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Think of a 50-storey commercial tower without a BMS as a *human body whose nerves have been cut*. Every organ (the lungs = AC, the eyes = lights, the heart = lifts) works independently, wastes energy, and never knows what the others are doing. A **BMS acts as the central nervous system + brain** of the building — sensors are the *nerve endings*, the DDC controllers are the *spinal cord*, and the central workstation is the *brain*. When occupancy drops in a floor at 7 PM, the BMS "feels" it through motion sensors and tells the AC and lights to sleep — exactly as your body conserves energy when you rest.

This analogy is critical for KTU viva voce questions, where examiners frequently ask *"Why is BMS considered essential for energy management?"* — the answer lies in **centralized intelligence enabling real-time, autonomous, optimized decision-making**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in BMS-Driven Buildings

- **EPI (Energy Performance Index):** measured in **$\text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$**
- **COP (Coefficient of Performance):** dimensionless ratio of heating/cooling output to electrical input
- **IPLV (Integrated Part Load Value):** weighted efficiency of chillers
- **SFP (Specific Fan Power):** $\text{kW/(m}^3/\text{s)}$ — used to grade AHU efficiency
- **Occupancy Density:** persons/m²
- **CFM per person:** ventilation supply rate ($0.06 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ per person as per ASHRAE 62.1)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Architecture of a BMS

A modern BMS is structured in **three logical tiers (layers)**. Understanding this layering is *high-yield* for KTU Part A questions.

**Tier 1 — Field Level (Sensors & Actuators)**
- **Sensors:** Temperature (RTD, thermistor), humidity, $\text{CO}_2$, occupancy (PIR), flow meters, current transformers (CTs), lux meters.
- **Actuators:** Damper motors, valve actuators, VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives), contactors, relay coils.
- *Why this layer exists:* Translates physical phenomena (heat, motion, light) into electrical signals and vice-versa.

**Tier 2 — Automation Level (Controllers)**
- **DDC (Direct Digital Controllers):** Microprocessor-based panels (e.g., Honeywell XL800, Schneider MNB, Siemens PX series) executing control loops.
- **PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers):** Used where deterministic ladder-logic is needed (lifts, fire systems).
- *Why this layer exists:* Performs **PID control**, scheduling, and logic decisions locally — even if the central server fails.

**Tier 3 — Management Level (Workstations & Software)**
- **Central Server + Operator Workstations** running SCADA-style graphical interfaces.
- **Historical Data Storage (Trending & Analytics):** Logs performance for audits and **M&V (Measurement & Verification)**.
- **Cloud Gateway / Web Interface:** Modern BMS extend to **IoT dashboards** accessible from any browser.

### 2.2 Functional Subsystems Controlled by a BMS

A BMS integrates and optimizes the following services, and the **energy-saving potential** of each varies:

- **HVAC (largest energy consumer — typically 40–60% of building electricity bill)**
- **Lighting (typically 20–30%)**
- **Power Monitoring & Sub-metering**
- **Fire Detection & Alarm (FDA)**
- **Access Control & CCTV**
- **Lifts / Elevators**
- **Plumbing / Water Management**
- **DG Sets (Diesel Generators) & UPS Monitoring**

### 2.3 Communication Protocols (Network Backbone)

- **BACnet** (ASHRAE 135) — the *de facto* open standard for BMS interoperability.
- **LonWorks** — older, robust, device-level peer-to-peer.
- **Modbus RTU/TCP** — used for power meters and VFDs.
- **KNX** — predominant in European residential/commercial wiring.
- **MQTT / HTTP** — used in modern **IoT-enabled BMS** overlays.
- **DALI** — for digital addressable lighting control.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every high-yield formula, definition, and boundary condition you can be tested on in the Energy Management & Auditing paper under the BMS topic.

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Savings (kWh) | $E_{saved} = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | $\text{kWh}$ | M&V reporting |
| % Energy Savings | $\%\text{Savings} = \dfrac{E_{saved}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Project justification |
| Simple Payback | $SP = \dfrac{C_{capex}}{\text{Annual Cash Saving}}$ | years | Financial screening |
| Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Annual Saving}}{C_{capex}} \times 100$ | % | Investment ranking |
| Cooling Load (sensible) | $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $\text{kW}$ | AHU sizing |
| Cooling Load (latent) | $Q_l = m \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega$ | $\text{kW}$ | Humidity control |
| SFP of a Fan | $SFP = \dfrac{P_{fan}}{Q_{air}}$ | $\text{kW per (m}^3/\text{s)}$ | Fan efficiency audit |
| Chiller COP | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | dimensionless | Chiller benchmarking |
| EPI (ECBC metric) | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{built-up}}$ | $\text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$ | ECBC compliance |
| LCC (Life-Cycle Cost) | $LCC = C_{capex} + \sum \dfrac{C_{op}}{(1+r)^n}$ | ₹ or \$ | LCCA studies |

> **Note on table syntax:** Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rules, all absolute-value or division bars are rendered using `\vert` / `\mid` in LaTeX to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of BMS in Engineering Practice

- Enables **continuous commissioning (Cx)** — never lets a chiller run with a fouled condenser.
- Provides the **data backbone** for **ISO 50001 Energy Management Systems**.
- Required for **Green Building certifications** such as **IGBC (India)**, **LEED (US)**, and **BREEAM (UK)**.
- Critical for **Demand Response (DR)** programs in smart-grid scenarios.
- Reduces **manpower cost** by automating round-the-clock operator tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **most commonly tested numerical derivations** on BMS-linked energy-audit problems.

### 3.1 Worked Numerical — Energy Savings from BMS-Controlled VFD on AHU Fan

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A 15 kW AHU fan runs 12 hours/day, 300 days/year, at a fixed (damper-controlled) speed delivering 100% airflow constantly. A BMS-driven **VFD retrofit** throttles the fan to 70% speed during unoccupied hours (8 of the 12 hours). Calculate the **annual energy savings (kWh)** and the **percentage savings**. Assume fan power follows the **affinity (cube) law**.

**Step 1 — Identify operating regimes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Regime A (Full Speed)} &: 4 \text{ hrs/day} \times 300 \text{ days} = 1200 \text{ hrs/yr} \\
\text{Regime B (Reduced Speed)} &: 8 \text{ hrs/day} \times 300 \text{ days} = 2400 \text{ hrs/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Affinity Law for power reduction at 70% speed.**

The fan affinity law states that power is proportional to the cube of speed ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{reduced} &= P_{rated} \times \left(\dfrac{N_{reduced}}{N_{rated}}\right)^{3} \\
P_{reduced} &= 15 \text{ kW} \times (0.70)^{3} \\
P_{reduced} &= 15 \text{ kW} \times 0.343 \\
P_{reduced} &= 5.145 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic row:** The cube of 0.70 is 0.343, meaning the VFD reduces electrical input demand to 34.3% of the rated value at 70% mechanical speed.

**Step 3 — Compute baseline energy (no VFD, no BMS).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{baseline} &= 15 \text{ kW} \times (1200 + 2400) \text{ hrs} \\
E_{baseline} &= 15 \times 3600 \\
E_{baseline} &= 54{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute post-retrofit energy (with BMS + VFD).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{actual} &= (15 \times 1200) + (5.145 \times 2400) \\
E_{actual} &= 18{,}000 + 12{,}348 \\
E_{actual} &= 30{,}348 \text{ kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate absolute and percentage savings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{saved} &= E_{baseline} - E_{actual} \\
E_{saved} &= 54{,}000 - 30{,}348 \\
E_{saved} &= 23{,}652 \text{ kWh/yr} \\
\\
\% \text{Savings} &= \dfrac{23{,}652}{54{,}000} \times 100 \\
\% \text{Savings} &= 43.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result Box:** A single VFD + BMS scheduling retrofit on one AHU saves **23,652 kWh/year (~43.8%)**. At ₹ 8 / kWh, this equates to **₹ 1,89,216 / year** in direct cost reduction.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Simple Payback of a Full BMS Deployment

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A hospital installs a centralized BMS at a capital cost of **₹ 60,00,000**. The system delivers verified annual savings of **₹ 18,00,000** in electricity bills. Compute (a) Simple Payback period and (b) Return on Investment.

**Step 1 — Apply the Simple Payback formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP &= \dfrac{C_{capex}}{\text{Annual Cash Saving}} \\
SP &= \dfrac{60{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \\
SP &= 3.33 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic row:** The project recovers its capital outlay in 3 years and 4 months — a strong result for any commercial building.

**Step 2 — Apply the ROI formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \dfrac{\text{Annual Saving}}{C_{capex}} \times 100 \\
ROI &= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
ROI &= 30\% \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner's interpretation:** A 30% annual ROI is considered *highly attractive* in real estate, where typical cap-rate yields hover around 8–10%. A BMS therefore not only saves energy but **directly enhances the Net Operating Income (NOI)** of the asset.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of BMS vs Non-BMS Buildings

| Performance Parameter | Conventional (Non-BMS) Building | BMS-Integrated Building | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| HVAC Energy Consumption (kWh/m²/yr) | 180 – 250 | 90 – 130 | 1.8× – 2.0× |
| Lighting Energy (kWh/m²/yr) | 45 – 60 | 22 – 30 | ~2× |
| EPI (kWh/m²/yr) | 250 – 320 | 130 – 180 | 1.7× – 1.9× |
| Equipment Downtime Events/yr | 12 – 18 | 3 – 5 | ~3× – 4× |
| Manpower per 1000 m² | 2.5 FTE | 0.8 FTE | ~3× |
| Indoor Air Quality Compliance | Inconsistent | Continuously monitored | High |
| ECBC Compliance | Often non-compliant | Typically ECBC / SuperECBC | Compliant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **favourite 7-mark sub-question** in KTU ESE papers. Memorize the *directional* improvement (i.e., which side saves more) rather than rote numbers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a BMS

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete functional architecture** of a BMS, mapping the flow of data from field instrumentation all the way to the cloud and end-user dashboards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Field Layer: Sensors and Actuators] --> B[Automation Layer: DDC Controllers and PLCs]
    B --> C[Network Layer: BACnet Modbus LonWorks KNX]
    C --> D[Management Layer: Central Server and SCADA HMI]
    D --> E[Trending Database and Analytics Engine]
    D --> F[Alarm Management and Event Logger]
    D --> G[Cloud Gateway and Web Dashboard]
    G --> H[Facility Manager Mobile App]
    G --> I[ISO 50001 Reporting Module]
    D --> J[Subsystem Integrations: HVAC Lighting Fire Security Power Lifts Plumbing]
    J --> K[Energy Savings in kWh and Cost in INR]
    K --> L[M and V Reports for Auditors]
```

**How to read this diagram (for the examiner's perspective):**
- **Bottom-up data flow** is shown left-to-right: physical phenomena → field devices → controllers → network → management.
- **Right-side branches** represent the *outputs* of the BMS — analytics, alarms, cloud dashboards, and ultimately the **M&V reports** that form the closing of the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** energy-management loop.

### 4.2 Closed-Loop PID Control — The Heart of BMS Automation

The most important control action a BMS executes is the **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) loop** for variables like zone temperature, $\text{CO}_2$ level, and static pressure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Setpoint: e.g. 24 deg C] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    P1[Process Variable from Sensor] --> SUM
    SUM --> E[Error Signal: SP minus PV]
    E --> PID[PID Controller in DDC]
    PID --> O[Output Signal: 0 to 10 V or 4 to 20 mA]
    O --> ACT[Actuator: Valve or Damper]
    ACT --> PROC[Building Zone]
    PROC --> SENSOR[Sensor RTD or Thermistor]
    SENSOR --> P1
```

**Engineering note:** The loop continuously minimizes the **error $e(t) = SP - PV$** using the control law:

$$
u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt + K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Where $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are tuning constants. A *mis-tuned* PID is the **#1 cause of BMS inefficiency** in poorly commissioned systems — it causes **hunting** (oscillation) and wasted actuator life.

### 4.3 BMS in the ECBC Compliance Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Building Design Intent] --> B[Energy Modelling using eQuest or DesignBuilder]
    B --> C[BMS Specification in Tender]
    C --> D[Installation and Commissioning]
    D --> E[Functional Performance Testing]
    E --> F[ECBC Documentation Submission]
    F --> G{Compliant?}
    G -- Yes --> H[ECBC Certificate Issued]
    G -- No --> I[Retrofit BMS Logic and Re-test]
    I --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Building Management System (BMS). List any four sub-systems that are typically integrated under a BMS.*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
A BMS is a centralized, computer-based control system used to monitor and regulate a building's mechanical, electrical, and electromechanical services in real time. It enables optimized operation, fault detection, and energy savings.
**Four integrated sub-systems (1/2 mark each):**
1. HVAC (Heating, Ventilation and Air-Conditioning)
2. Lighting control
3. Fire Detection and Alarm (FDA)
4. Access control / security / CCTV

*(Any 4 accepted. Mention of "centralized workstation" and "monitoring" is essential for full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the role of a DDC controller in a BMS architecture? How does it differ from a PLC?*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
A **Direct Digital Controller (DDC)** is the workhorse controller of the automation layer. It executes **PID control loops**, runs **time schedules (occupancy-based)**, and stores trend data. DDCs are designed for **HVAC-specific analog I/O** (temperature, humidity, pressure).
A **PLC**, in contrast, is optimized for **discrete, high-speed digital logic** (lifts, conveyor lines, fire-alarm matrices) and is programmed in **ladder logic**.
**Key difference:** DDC = continuous analog + HVAC-focused; PLC = discrete digital + industrial-focused.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**Question A:**
*(a)* Explain the **three-tier architecture of a BMS** with a neat block diagram. Detail the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A commercial complex replaces its conventional AHU damper control with a **BMS-driven VFD** on a 22 kW fan. The fan operates 14 hours/day, 330 days/year. It runs at full speed for 6 hours and at **60% speed** for the remaining 8 hours daily. Using the **affinity law**, calculate the annual energy saved in kWh and the **percentage reduction** in consumption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
Draw the three-tier diagram (Field / Automation / Management) and write 2–3 lines per layer as already covered in Section 4.1. Full marks require: **(i)** naming all three layers, **(ii)** giving 2 examples of devices in each, **(iii)** identifying BACnet/Modbus as network glue, **(iv)** mentioning central SCADA & trending.

**Part (b) — 7 marks — Numerical Solution**

Operating hours: $14 \times 330 = 4620$ hours/year.
Regime A (Full speed): $6 \times 330 = 1980$ hrs.
Regime B (60% speed): $8 \times 330 = 2640$ hrs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rated} &= 22 \text{ kW} \\
P_{60\%} &= 22 \times (0.60)^3 = 22 \times 0.216 = 4.752 \text{ kW} \\
E_{baseline} &= 22 \times 4620 = 1{,}01{,}640 \text{ kWh} \\
E_{actual} &= (22 \times 1980) + (4.752 \times 2640) \\
E_{actual} &= 43{,}560 + 12{,}545.28 \\
E_{actual} &= 56{,}105.28 \text{ kWh} \\
E_{saved} &= 1{,}01{,}640 - 56{,}105.28 = 45{,}534.72 \text{ kWh} \\
\% \text{Savings} &= \dfrac{45{,}534.72}{1{,}01{,}640} \times 100 = 44.79\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **Energy saved ≈ 45,535 kWh/year**, equivalent to **44.8% reduction**.

### 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**Question B:**
*(a)* Discuss the **role of BMS in achieving ECBC compliance** for a commercial building. List the **five main functional features** of a modern BMS. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A factory installs a BMS at a capex of **₹ 80,00,000**. The annual energy savings are **1,20,000 kWh** at **₹ 9/kWh**. Compute **(i)** annual monetary savings, **(ii)** simple payback period, and **(iii)** ROI. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- BMS automates HVAC scheduling → reduces EPI → meets ECBC's $\text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$ envelope.
- BMS enables continuous sub-metering → required for ECBC's **M&V clause**.
- BMS trend logs support **annual energy audit submissions**.
- **Five features of modern BMS (1 mark each):** (1) Centralized graphic monitoring (2) Scheduling & calendar functions (3) Alarm management with escalation (4) Trending & historical data logging (5) Open-protocol interoperability (BACnet/Modbus/IoT) — plus optional 6th: Demand limiting / peak shaving.

**Part (b) — 7 marks — Numerical Solution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Monetary Saving} &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 9 = \text{₹ }10{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{Simple Payback} &= \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{10{,}80{,}000} = 7.41 \text{ years} \\
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{10{,}80{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 13.5\% \text{ per annum}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Annual saving = **₹ 10.8 lakh**, SP = **7.41 years**, ROI = **13.5%/yr**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost**
> 1. **Writing "BMS controls the building" without naming sub-systems** → loses 1–2 marks in Part A.
> 2. **Forgetting to cube the speed ratio** in VFD/affinity-law problems → direct 3-mark deduction in Part B numericals.
> 3. **Confusing DDC with PLC** → examiners expect the *analog vs digital* distinction explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring units** in $\text{kWh}$ vs ₹ vs $\text{kW}$ → partial marking is ruthless in numerics.
> 5. **Diagrams drawn without labelled layers** → at least 2 marks reserved for diagram quality; never submit an unlabelled box.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention BACnet** when asked about communication → standard answer expects open-protocol mention for full credit.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BMS = Centralized, computer-based, multi-subsystem control** for M&E services.
- **Three-tier architecture** = Field (sensors/actuators) → Automation (DDC/PLC) → Management (SCADA/Server).
- **BACnet** is the dominant open protocol; remember **Modbus** for power meters, **DALI** for lighting, **KNX** in European contexts.
- **HVAC = 40–60% of building energy** — biggest BMS optimization target.
- **Affinity (cube) law** governs fan/pump energy: $P \propto N^3$.
- **PID control** is the heart of BMS automation; mis-tuning is the #1 cause of inefficiency.
- **EPI (kWh/m²/year)** is the ECBC metric a BMS helps minimize.
- **Simple Payback** = Capex ÷ Annual Saving; **ROI** = (Saving/Capex) × 100.
- **BMS directly enables ISO 50001** energy management systems and **IGBC / LEED** green-building credits.
- For numericals, **always show units at every step** and **cube the speed ratio** in fan-affinity problems.
- Memorize the **analogy**: *BMS = nervous system + brain of the building* — examiners reward it in viva.
- Modern BMS now extends to **IoT, cloud dashboards, and AI-driven predictive maintenance** — relevant for KTU 2024 advanced questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Management Opportunities in Lighting and Motors, Electrolytic Process and Electric Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Management Opportunities in Lighting and Motors, Electrolytic Process and Electric Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Energy Management in Lighting Systems

### Formal Definition
**Lighting Energy Management** refers to the systematic application of engineering principles, technologies, and control strategies to minimize electrical energy consumed for illumination while maintaining or improving the required luminous flux (measured in lumens) and visual comfort (illuminance in lux) for the given task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Lighting alone accounts for approximately **20–30%** of total electrical energy consumption in commercial buildings and **5–10%** in industrial facilities. The Light Output Ratio (LOR) and Efficacy (lumens/Watt) are the two critical metrics for benchmarking lighting efficiency.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of lighting like a **water tap system**: 
- The **lamp** is the tap that releases light (water)
- The **lumens** are the quantity of useful light (water flow rate)
- The **watts** are the electricity consumed (energy used to push the water)
- A **conventional lamp** is like a leaking tap — wastes a lot (heat) for little useful output
- An **LED lamp** is like a precision nozzle — delivers maximum light per unit energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Old incandescent lamps convert only **5–10%** of electrical energy into visible light; the remaining **90–95%** is wasted as heat. Modern LEDs convert **40–50%** into visible light.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)
- **Luminous Efficacy (η)**: lumens per Watt (lm/W) — measures light produced per unit power
- **Illuminance (E)**: lumens per square meter (lux or lx)
- **Luminous Flux (Φ)**: total light emitted by a source (lumens, lm)
- **Luminous Intensity (I)**: lumens per steradian (candela, cd)
- **Color Rendering Index (CRI)**: 0–100 scale measuring how accurately a light source reveals object colors
- **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT)**: measured in Kelvin (K), indicates the "warmth" or "coolness" of light

---

## 1.2 Energy Management in Electric Motors

### Formal Definition
**Motor Energy Management** is the strategic selection, operation, and control of electric motors (predominantly three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors) to minimize energy losses — namely **stator copper losses (I²R)**, **rotor copper losses**, **core (iron) losses**, **friction and windage losses**, and **stray load losses** — across the motor's operating duty cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point**: Electric motors consume **60–70%** of total industrial electricity globally. A motor running at 70% load typically operates at only **88–92%** efficiency, while at 50% load it can drop to **85–88%**. Proper sizing and load matching are crucial.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **gearbox on a bicycle**:
- The **motor** is the engine
- The **mechanical load** is the hill you're climbing
- **Oversizing the motor** is like using a truck to deliver a single letter — wastes enormous fuel
- **Undersizing** causes overheating and burnout
- **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** is like continuously variable gears — matches motor speed to load perfectly

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Motor Efficiency (η)**: Output mechanical power ÷ Input electrical power
- **Power Factor (cos φ)**: ratio of real power (kW) to apparent power (kVA); ideally **0.85–0.95** for motors
- **Load Factor**: Actual load ÷ Rated load
- **International Efficiency (IE) Classes**: IE1 (Standard), IE2 (High), IE3 (Premium), IE4 (Super-Premium), IE5 (Ultra-Premium)

---

## 1.3 Electrolytic Process

### Formal Definition
**Electrolytic Process** is an electrochemical method that uses direct electric current to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction (reduction at the cathode and oxidation at the anode) in an electrolyte solution, separating elements or producing compounds such as chlorine, hydrogen, aluminum, copper, or zinc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note**: Electrolysis is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (1833–1834)**. The energy efficiency of any electrolytic process is determined by the **cell voltage**, **current efficiency**, and **specific energy consumption (kWh/tonne of product)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of electrolysis like a **reverse battery**: 
- A **battery** converts chemical energy INTO electrical energy (spontaneous reaction)
- An **electrolytic cell** uses electrical energy to FORCE a chemical reaction (non-spontaneous)
- The **electrolyte** is the "highway" for ion movement
- The **anode** is the positive terminal (oxidation occurs)
- The **cathode** is the negative terminal (reduction occurs)

### Physical Constants
- **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 26.8 Ah/mol)
- **Electrochemical Equivalent (Z)**: mass deposited per coulomb of charge (kg/C)
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**: kWh per tonne of substance produced

---

## 1.4 Electric Heating

### Formal Definition
**Electric Heating** is the controlled conversion of electrical energy into thermal energy (heat) through one of four fundamental mechanisms — **resistance (Joule) heating**, **induction heating**, **dielectric heating**, or **arc heating** — for industrial processes such as melting, forging, annealing, drying, smelting, and space conditioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight**: Electric heating achieves **100% conversion efficiency at the point of use** (since all electrical energy becomes heat). However, primary energy efficiency depends on the **generation mix** (renewable/nuclear/thermal). For industrial heating above **1000°C**, electric methods often surpass fossil-fuel alternatives in process control and emissions.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of electric heating like **cooking with electricity vs. gas**:
- **Resistance heating** = electric coil on a stove (direct Joule heating of the element)
- **Induction heating** = microwave-style contactless heating using magnetic fields (works only with conductive materials)
- **Dielectric heating** = heating non-conductive materials (plastics, wood, ceramics) using high-frequency electric fields
- **Arc heating** = lightning-like electrical discharge producing extreme temperatures (>3000°C)

### Physical Constants & Metrics
- **Heat Energy (Q)**: Joules (J) or kilojoules (kJ); 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3600 kJ
- **Specific Heat (c)**: J/(kg·K) — energy to raise 1 kg by 1 K
- **Heat Transfer Coefficient (h)**: W/(m²·K)
- **Thermal Efficiency (η_th)**: Useful heat output ÷ Electrical input

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Lighting Energy Management — Detailed Analysis

### Step-by-Step Operating Principles
1. **Lamp Selection**: Choose based on efficacy (lm/W), CRI, CCT, lifetime, and capital cost
2. **Luminaire Design**: Maximize Light Output Ratio (LOR); minimize glare
3. **Illumination Level Calculation**: Use the **Lumen Method**
4. **Control Strategy**: Implement dimming, occupancy sensors, daylight harvesting
5. **Maintenance Scheduling**: Regular cleaning; group lamp replacement

### Comparison of Lamp Technologies

| Lamp Type | Efficacy (lm/W) | Lifetime (hours) | CRI | Starting Time | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Incandescent | 10–17 | 1,000 | 100 | Instant | Domestic (now phased out) |
| Halogen | 15–25 | 2,000–4,000 | 100 | Instant | Showrooms, accent lighting |
| Fluorescent (T8/T5) | 50–100 | 15,000–20,000 | 60–90 | 1–2 sec | Offices, commercial |
| CFL | 50–70 | 8,000–10,000 | 80 | 30 sec–2 min | Domestic replacement |
| Mercury Vapor (HID) | 35–65 | 24,000 | 50 | 5–7 min | Street lighting (legacy) |
| Metal Halide (HID) | 65–115 | 15,000–20,000 | 65–95 | 2–5 min | Stadiums, high-bay industrial |
| High Pressure Sodium (HPS) | 80–140 | 24,000+ | 25 | 3–5 min | Roads, parking, warehouses |
| LED | 100–200+ | 50,000–100,000 | 70–95 | Instant | Universal (future standard) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: When asked to compare lamp efficiencies, always include the **system efficacy** (lamp + ballast), not just lamp efficacy alone. Electronic ballasts add 5–15% efficiency over magnetic ballasts.

---

## 2.2 Motor Energy Management — Detailed Analysis

### Motor Loss Components
1. **Stator Copper Losses (P_scl)**: $P_{scl} = 3 \cdot I_s^2 \cdot R_s$ per phase × 3
2. **Rotor Copper Losses (P_rcl)**: $P_{rcl} = 3 \cdot I_r^2 \cdot R_r = s \cdot P_{gap}$
3. **Core Losses (P_core)**: Hysteresis + Eddy current losses in iron laminations
4. **Friction & Windage Losses (P_fw)**: Bearing friction + rotor air resistance
5. **Stray Load Losses (P_sll)**: ~1% of rated power, due to leakage flux harmonics

### Why Motor Efficiency Matters
- A **10 kW standard motor** running at 90% efficiency wastes **1.11 kW** as heat
- A **premium efficiency (IE3) motor** at 93% wastes only **0.75 kW** → saves **0.36 kW continuously**
- Over **6,000 hours/year** at ₹8/kWh → annual saving ≈ **₹17,280 per motor**
- Simple payback period for IE3 over IE1 is typically **1–3 years**

### Motor Sizing Rules
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Principle**: Motors operate most efficiently between **75–100% of rated load**. Operating consistently below **50% load** causes:
> - Poor power factor (drops to 0.5–0.6)
> - Reduced efficiency
> - Higher temperature rise per kW output
> 
> **Solution**: Replace oversized motors with correctly sized ones OR use VFDs.

---

## 2.3 Electrolytic Process — Theoretical Breakdown

### Faraday's Two Laws of Electrolysis

**Faraday's First Law (1833)**: 
The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = Z \cdot Q$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of substance liberated (kg)
- $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent (kg/C)
- $I$ = current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $Q$ = charge (Coulombs)

**Faraday's Second Law (1834)**:
When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their **chemical equivalent weights**.

$$m_1 : m_2 = E_1 : E_2$$

where $E$ = equivalent weight = atomic weight ÷ valency

### Cell Voltage Components

$$V_{cell} = E_{eq} + \eta_a + \eta_c + IR_{electrolyte} + IR_{contacts}$$

where:
- $E_{eq}$ = reversible decomposition voltage
- $\eta_a$ = anode overpotential
- $\eta_c$ = cathode overpotential
- $IR_{electrolyte}$ = ohmic drop in electrolyte
- $IR_{contacts}$ = ohmic drop in busbars and contacts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Efficiency Insight**: To minimize specific energy consumption:
> 1. **Lower cell voltage** (membrane cells over mercury cells in chlor-alkali saves ~30% energy)
> 2. **Improve current efficiency** (reduce side reactions)
> 3. **Optimize current density** (A/m²)
> 4. **Use better electrode materials** (DSA — Dimensionally Stable Anodes)

---

## 2.4 Electric Heating — Theoretical Foundation

### Modes of Heat Transfer
1. **Conduction**: Through solids; $Q = kA \frac{\Delta T}{L}$
2. **Convection**: Through fluids; $Q = hA \Delta T$
3. **Radiation**: Electromagnetic emission; $Q = \epsilon \sigma A T^4$

### Heating Time Calculation

$$t = \frac{m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T}{\eta \cdot P}$$

where:
- $t$ = time to heat (seconds)
- $m$ = mass of material (kg)
- $c$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K)
- $\eta$ = heating efficiency (0 to 1)
- $P$ = electrical power input (W)

### Heat Losses During Heating
For a furnace operating at steady state, total heat loss = sum of:
1. Conduction through walls: $Q_{cond} = \frac{kA(T_i - T_o)}{L}$
2. Convection from outer surface: $Q_{conv} = hA(T_s - T_a)$
3. Radiation from outer surface: $Q_{rad} = \epsilon \sigma A(T_s^4 - T_a^4)$
4. Heat stored in refractories (during transients)

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Comprehensive Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute value bars in tables to avoid markdown parsing errors.**

| Domain | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lighting | $\Phi = E \cdot A$ | Luminous flux = Illuminance × Area | $\Phi$ in lm, $E$ in lux, $A$ in m² |
| Lighting | $E = \frac{\Phi \cdot N \cdot \eta_{util} \cdot LOR}{A}$ | Lumen Method equation | $N$ = no. of luminaires |
| Lighting | $\eta_{lamp} = \frac{\Phi}{P_{lamp}}$ | Lamp efficacy | lm/W |
| Lighting | $\eta_{system} = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{P_{system}}$ | System efficacy (lamp + ballast) | lm/W |
| Motor | $\eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + \sum losses}$ | Motor efficiency | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Motor | $P_{gap} = \frac{P_{out}}{1-s}$ | Air-gap power | $s$ = slip |
| Motor | $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{gap}$ | Rotor copper loss | W |
| Motor | $P_{out} = (1-s) \cdot P_{gap}$ | Mechanical power developed | W |
| Motor | $N_r = N_s (1-s)$ | Rotor speed | $N_s = \frac{120f}{p}$ |
| Motor | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip | Dimensionless |
| Motor | $PF = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_L} = \cos\phi$ | Power factor (3-phase) | 0 to 1 |
| Electrolysis | $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass deposited (Faraday's 1st Law extended) | $M$ = atomic wt, $n$ = valency |
| Electrolysis | $Z = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | Electrochemical equivalent | kg/C |
| Electrolysis | $SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot F \cdot n}{M \cdot \eta_{current}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/tonne |
| Electrolysis | $\eta_{current} = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Current efficiency | % |
| Heating | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat | J |
| Heating | $Q = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat (phase change) | J |
| Heating | $P = \frac{V^2}{R} = I^2 R$ | Power in resistance heating | W |
| Heating | $\eta_{thermal} = \frac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency | % |
| Heating | $t = \frac{m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T}{\eta \cdot P}$ | Heating time | seconds |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lighting**: Used in BEE Star Rating of buildings (ECBC — Energy Conservation Building Code in India)
- **Motors**: IS 12615 (Indian Standard) aligns with IE-class efficiency; mandatory for industries under PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme
- **Electrolysis**: Critical for chlor-alkali, aluminum smelting, hydrogen production (green H₂ via water electrolysis)
- **Electric Heating**: Electric arc furnaces (EAF) for steel, induction furnaces for foundries, dielectric heating for plastic welding, microwave ovens for food processing
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Lumen Method (Lighting Design Calculation)

### Objective
Calculate the **number of luminaires** required to achieve a specified average illuminance in a room.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Total luminous flux required at the working plane

$$\Phi_{required} = \frac{E \cdot A}{\eta_{util} \cdot LOR \cdot MF}$$

where:
- $E$ = average illuminance required (lux)
- $A$ = area of working plane (m²)
- $\eta_{util}$ = utilization factor (0 to 1)
- $LOR$ = light output ratio of luminaire (0 to 1)
- $MF$ = maintenance factor (typically 0.7 to 0.8)

**Step 2**: Total luminous flux emitted by all luminaires

$$\Phi_{total} = N \cdot \Phi_{lamp}$$

where:
- $N$ = number of luminaires
- $\Phi_{lamp}$ = flux emitted by ONE luminaire (all lamps inside)

**Step 3**: Equating flux supplied and flux required

$$N \cdot \Phi_{lamp} = \frac{E \cdot A}{\eta_{util} \cdot LOR \cdot MF}$$

**Step 4**: Solving for N

$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi_{lamp} \cdot \eta_{util} \cdot LOR \cdot MF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important**: $\Phi_{lamp}$ here is the flux per **luminaire** (if luminaire contains 2 lamps of 40W each, total flux = 2 × lamp flux).

---

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem**: A factory floor measures **30 m × 20 m** = 600 m². Required illuminance = **300 lux**. Use **400W metal halide lamps** with flux = **36,000 lumens per lamp**. Each luminaire contains **1 lamp**. $\eta_{util} = 0.6$, $LOR = 0.7$, $MF = 0.75$.

**Solution**:

$$N = \frac{300 \cdot 600}{36{,}000 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.75}$$

$$N = \frac{180{,}000}{11{,}340}$$

$$N = 15.87 \approx 16 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Power consumption**: $16 \times 400 = 6{,}400$ W = **6.4 kW**

**Energy saved by replacing with 150W LED (18,000 lm each)**:

$$N_{LED} = \frac{180{,}000}{18{,}000 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 0.75} = \frac{180{,}000}{5{,}670} = 31.75 \approx 32 \text{ LED luminaires}$$

$$P_{LED} = 32 \times 150 = 4{,}800 \text{ W} = 4.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Savings** = $6.4 - 4.8 = 1.6$ kW (a 25% reduction in lighting power).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Faraday's First Law from Electrochemical Principles

### Starting Point
The **mole** of electrons contains Avogadro's number of electrons:

$$N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ electrons/mol}$$

Charge of one electron: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**Step 1**: Charge carried by 1 mole of electrons (Faraday's constant)

$$F = N_A \cdot e$$

$$F = (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \cdot (1.602 \times 10^{-19})$$

$$F \approx 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$$

**Step 2**: For a metal of atomic weight $M$ and valency $n$, one mole of atoms requires $n$ moles of electrons for reduction.

Number of moles of electrons required per mole of metal = $n$

**Step 3**: Total charge required for 1 mole of metal deposition

$$Q_{1\ mole} = n \cdot F$$

**Step 4**: Mass deposited by charge $Q$ (in Coulombs)

$$m = \frac{M}{n \cdot F} \cdot Q = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

This is the **extended form of Faraday's First Law**.

**Step 5**: Define electrochemical equivalent

$$Z = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}$$

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = Z \cdot Q$$

---

### Worked Numerical Example (Electrolytic Process)

**Problem**: An electrolytic cell is used to deposit copper. Current = 200 A, time = 2 hours. Calculate the mass of copper deposited. 
- Atomic weight of Cu, $M = 63.5$ kg/kmol
- Valency $n = 2$
- Current efficiency $\eta_c = 95\%$

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Calculate theoretical mass using Faraday's Law

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{63.5 \cdot 200 \cdot (2 \times 3600)}{2 \cdot 96{,}485}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{63.5 \cdot 200 \cdot 7200}{192{,}970}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{91{,}440{,}000}{192{,}970}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = 473.9 \text{ grams}$$

**Step 2**: Apply current efficiency

$$m_{actual} = \eta_c \cdot m_{theoretical} = 0.95 \cdot 473.9 = 450.2 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3**: Calculate specific energy consumption
Cell voltage $V_{cell} = 2.0$ V

$$SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{actual}} = \frac{2.0 \cdot 200 \cdot 7200}{450.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ tonnes}}$$

$$SEC = \frac{2{,}880{,}000}{4.502 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$SEC = 6.397 \times 10^{9} \text{ J/tonne} = 1{,}777 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Motor Efficiency from Fundamental Electrical Quantities

### Starting Point
For a 3-phase induction motor:

**Step 1**: Input electrical power (3-phase)

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

where:
- $V_L$ = line voltage (V)
- $I_L$ = line current (A)
- $\cos\phi$ = power factor

**Step 2**: Air-gap power

$$P_{gap} = P_{in} - P_{scl} - P_{core}$$

**Step 3**: Rotor copper loss (related to slip)

$$P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{gap}$$

**Step 4**: Mechanical power developed

$$P_{developed} = P_{gap} - P_{rcl} = (1-s) \cdot P_{gap}$$

**Step 5**: Shaft output power (after friction, windage, stray losses)

$$P_{out} = P_{developed} - P_{fw} - P_{sll}$$

**Step 6**: Motor efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

---

### Worked Numerical Example (Motor)

**Problem**: A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has rotor speed 1440 rpm. Input power = 50 kW at line voltage 415 V, line current 85 A, power factor 0.85. Stator copper loss = 2 kW, core loss = 1.5 kW, friction & windage = 0.8 kW. Calculate: (a) slip, (b) rotor copper loss, (c) shaft power, (d) efficiency.

**Solution**:

**Step (a)**: Synchronous speed

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{p} = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

$$s = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500} = 0.04 \text{ (i.e., 4\%)}$$

**Step (b)**: Air-gap power

$$P_{gap} = P_{in} - P_{scl} - P_{core} = 50 - 2 - 1.5 = 46.5 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{gap} = 0.04 \cdot 46.5 = 1.86 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (c)**: Shaft power

$$P_{shaft} = P_{gap} - P_{rcl} - P_{fw} = 46.5 - 1.86 - 0.8 = 43.84 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (d)**: Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{43.84}{50} \times 100 = 87.68\%$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Electric Heating Time and Energy

### Scenario
A furnace must heat a steel billet from ambient to forging temperature.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Total heat required

$$Q_{required} = m \cdot c \cdot (T_f - T_i)$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of billet (kg)
- $c$ = specific heat of steel ≈ **460 J/(kg·K)**
- $T_f$ = final temperature (K)
- $T_i$ = initial temperature (K)

**Step 2**: Heat input rate from electrical source

$$P_{electrical} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$$

For pure resistive heating, $\cos\phi = 1$, so $P = V \cdot I = \frac{V^2}{R} = I^2 R$

**Step 3**: Useful heat rate (after losses)

$$P_{useful} = \eta_{thermal} \cdot P_{electrical}$$

**Step 4**: Time to reach required temperature

$$t = \frac{Q_{required}}{P_{useful}} = \frac{m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T}{\eta \cdot P}$$

---

### Worked Numerical Example (Electric Heating)

**Problem**: An electric resistance furnace is used to heat 500 kg of aluminum from 25°C to 700°C for melting. Furnace power = 100 kW, thermal efficiency = 80%, specific heat of aluminum = 900 J/(kg·K). Calculate the heating time (neglecting latent heat for sensible heat part).

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Temperature rise

$$\Delta T = 700 - 25 = 675 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2**: Heat required

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T = 500 \cdot 900 \cdot 675$$

$$Q = 303{,}750{,}000 \text{ J} = 303.75 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3**: Useful power

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P = 0.80 \cdot 100 = 80 \text{ kW} = 80{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4**: Time

$$t = \frac{Q}{P_{useful}} = \frac{303{,}750{,}000}{80{,}000}$$

$$t = 3797 \text{ s} = 63.3 \text{ minutes} \approx 1.05 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5**: Energy consumed (electricity)

$$E_{electric} = P \cdot t = 100 \text{ kW} \cdot 1.05 \text{ h} = 105 \text{ kWh}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

### Energy Saving Opportunities — Comparison

| Domain | Opportunity | Typical Savings (%) | Payback Period |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lighting | Replace incandescent with LED | 75–85% | 6 months – 2 years |
| Lighting | Replace T12 with T5 + electronic ballast | 30–40% | 2–3 years |
| Lighting | Occupancy sensors in offices | 20–40% | 1–2 years |
| Lighting | Daylight harvesting + dimming | 30–50% | 2–4 years |
| Motor | IE1 → IE3 motor replacement | 3–5% (continuous) | 1–3 years |
| Motor | VFD for variable torque loads | 20–50% | 1–3 years |
| Motor | Power factor correction (0.7→0.95) | Reduces kVA billing | 1–2 years |
| Motor | Proper sizing (remove oversizing) | 5–10% | <1 year |
| Electrolysis | Membrane cells replacing mercury cells | 25–30% | 3–5 years |
| Electrolysis | Improved electrode materials | 5–15% | 2–4 years |
| Electrolysis | Heat recovery from cell off-gases | 10–20% | 2–3 years |
| Heating | Replace resistance with induction heating | 20–40% | 1–2 years |
| Heating | Better insulation of furnaces | 10–30% | 1–3 years |
| Heating | Recuperators and regenerators | 20–50% | 1–3 years |
| Heating | Heat recovery from exhaust gases | 15–40% | 1–2 years |
