# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** 
Define **Luminous Efficacy**. Why is it considered the most important parameter for comparing different light sources? Mention typical values for incandescent lamps and LEDs.

**Model Answer**:

**Definition**: Luminous efficacy is the ratio of luminous flux (lumens) emitted by a lamp to the electrical power (Watts) consumed by it. It is expressed in **lumens per Watt (lm/W)**.

$$\eta_{lamp} = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$

**Why it is the most important parameter**:
1. It directly measures how efficiently electrical energy is converted to visible light
2. Higher efficacy means lower electricity bills for the same illumination
3. It is a fair, dimensionless comparison metric independent of lamp size
4. It quantifies energy savings potential during retrofitting

**Typical values**:
- Incandescent lamp: **10–17 lm/W**
- LED lamp: **100–200 lm/W** (typically 120–150 lm/W)

LEDs deliver approximately **10 times more light per Watt** than incandescent lamps.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
State **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. Explain the term **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** with its typical units.

**Model Answer**:

**Faraday's First Law**: The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Faraday's Second Law**: When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes connected in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.

$$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{M_1/n_1}{M_2/n_2}$$

**Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**: SEC is the electrical energy required to produce a unit mass (usually 1 tonne) of the desired product by electrolysis. It is a key indicator of energy efficiency.

$$SEC = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{actual}} = \frac{V_{cell}}{Z \cdot \eta_{current}}$$

**Typical Units**: kWh per tonne (kWh/tonne) or MJ per tonne (MJ/tonne).

**Examples**:
- Aluminum production: ~13,000–15,000 kWh/tonne
- Chlor-alkali (membrane cell): ~2,000–2,500 kWh/tonne of NaOH
- Zinc electro-winning: ~3,000–3,500 kWh/tonne

---

## 5.2 PART B — Detailed Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the different types of electric lamps used for industrial and commercial lighting. Compare their **luminous efficacy**, **color rendering index**, and **lifetime**. Discuss why **LEDs** are considered the future of lighting.

(b) **[7 Marks]** An office floor of dimensions **20 m × 15 m** requires an average illuminance of **500 lux**. Using the Lumen Method, calculate the number of LED luminaires required if each luminaire contains **2 LED tubes of 18W each** with the following specifications: luminous flux per tube = **2,000 lumens**, utilization factor = **0.55**, light output ratio = **0.8**, maintenance factor = **0.8**. Also calculate the total connected load and the annual energy consumption if the office operates **10 hours/day, 300 days/year**. Compare the energy savings if the same floor was originally lit with **30 fluorescent tube luminaires of 4 × 40W each**.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Detailed Solution [7 Marks]**:

**Types of Electric Lamps**:

1. **Incandescent Lamps**: Tungsten filament heated to ~2,500–3,000 K in vacuum/inert gas. 
   - Efficacy: 10–17 lm/W; CRI: 100; Lifetime: 1,000 hours
   
2. **Halogen Lamps**: Improved incandescent with halogen gas cycle (tungsten–halogen regeneration).
   - Efficacy: 15–25 lm/W; CRI: 100; Lifetime: 2,000–4,000 hours
   
3. **Fluorescent Lamps (T8, T5)**: Mercury vapor + phosphor coating, low-pressure discharge.
   - Efficacy: 50–100 lm/W; CRI: 60–90; Lifetime: 15,000–20,000 hours
   
4. **Compact Fluorescent Lamps (CFL)**: Folded fluorescent tubes with built-in ballast.
   - Efficacy: 50–70 lm/W; CRI: 80; Lifetime: 8,000–10,000 hours
   
5. **High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamps**: 
   - **Mercury Vapor**: 35–65 lm/W, CRI: 50, Lifetime: 24,000 h
   - **Metal Halide**: 65–115 lm/W, CRI: 65–95, Lifetime: 15,000–20,000 h
   - **High Pressure Sodium (HPS)**: 80–140 lm/W, CRI: 25, Lifetime: 24,000+ h
   
6. **LED Lamps**: Light Emitting Diodes with semiconductor p–n junction.
   - Efficacy: 100–200+ lm/W; CRI: 70–95; Lifetime: 50,000–100,000 hours

**Comparison Table**:

| Lamp Type | Efficacy (lm/W) | CRI | Lifetime (h) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Incandescent | 10–17 | 100 | 1,000 |
| Halogen | 15–25 | 100 | 2,000–4,000 |
| Fluorescent | 50–100 | 60–90 | 15,000–20,000 |
| CFL | 50–70 | 80 | 8,000–10,000 |
| Metal Halide | 65–115 | 65–95 | 15,000–20,000 |
| HPS | 80–140 | 25 | 24,000+ |
| LED | 100–200+ | 70–95 | 50,000–100,000 |

**Why LEDs are the future of lighting**:
- Highest efficacy → maximum light per Watt consumed
- Longest lifetime → minimum maintenance and replacement cost
- Instant start (no warm-up time)
- Robust (no glass envelope breakage issues)
- Directional emission → less light wasted
- No mercury content (environmentally safe)
- Compatible with smart controls (dimmable, IoT-enabled)
- Continuously improving technology and falling prices

---

**Part (b) — Detailed Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**:

**Given Data**:
- Floor area: $A = 20 \times 15 = 300 \text{ m}^2$
- Required illuminance: $E = 500 \text{ lux}$
- Each luminaire contains 2 LED tubes × 18 W
- Flux per tube: $\Phi_{tube} = 2{,}000$ lumens
- Utilization factor: $\eta_{util} = 0.55$
- Light output ratio: $LOR = 0.8$
- Maintenance factor: $MF = 0.8$
- Operating hours: 10 h/day × 300 days = 3,000 h/year

**Step 1**: Flux per luminaire

$$\Phi_{luminaire} = 2 \times 2{,}000 = 4{,}000 \text{ lumens}$$

**Step 2**: Number of luminaires required

$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{\Phi_{luminaire} \cdot \eta_{util} \cdot LOR \cdot MF}$$

$$N = \frac{500 \cdot 300}{4{,}000 \cdot 0.55 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.8}$$

$$N = \frac{150{,}000}{1{,}408}$$

$$N = 106.5 \approx 107 \text{ LED luminaires}$$

**Step 3**: Total connected LED load

$$P_{LED} = 107 \times (2 \times 18) = 107 \times 36 = 3{,}852 \text{ W} \approx 3.85 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4**: Annual energy consumption

$$E_{LED} = 3.852 \text{ kW} \times 3{,}000 \text{ h} = 11{,}556 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 5**: Original fluorescent installation
- 30 luminaires × 4 tubes × 40 W = 4,800 W = 4.8 kW
- Annual energy = 4.8 × 3,000 = **14,400 kWh/year**

**Step 6**: Energy savings

$$\Delta E = 14{,}400 - 11{,}556 = 2{,}844 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Percentage savings**:

$$\% \text{ saving} = \frac{2{,}844}{14{,}400} \times 100 = 19.75\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note**: Although LED technology is more efficient per Watt, the higher number of luminaires (107 vs 30) is due to lower wattage per luminaire. The savings come primarily from lower wattage per luminaire (36W vs 160W) and matching illumination to requirement.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the various **energy-saving opportunities in electric motors**. Discuss in detail: (i) Use of energy-efficient motors, (ii) Variable Frequency Drives (VFD), (iii) Power factor correction, (iv) Proper motor sizing.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A **3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor** draws **60 A** at **415 V** with a **power factor of 0.88 lag**. The motor has the following losses: stator copper loss = 1.8 kW, core loss = 1.2 kW, friction & windage = 0.9 kW, rotor copper loss = 1.5 kW. Calculate: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Rotor speed if slip is 4%, (iii) Air-gap power, (iv) Shaft power output, (v) Motor efficiency.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

**Part (a) — Detailed Solution [7 Marks]**:

**Energy-Saving Opportunities in Electric Motors**:

**(i) Energy-Efficient Motors (EEMs)**:
- Replace standard (IE1) motors with IE3/IE4 premium efficiency motors
- Use lower-loss electrical steel (e.g., cold-rolled grain-oriented steel)
- Reduce stator copper losses through larger conductor cross-section
- Optimize rotor bar design (copper or aluminum die-cast)
- Use better bearings and precision-balanced rotors to reduce friction
- Typical saving: 3–8% in efficiency → 5–15% in operating cost

**(ii) Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**:
- Vary motor speed by changing supply frequency
- Motor speed: $N_r = \frac{120 f (1-s)}{p}$
- Power saving for fans/pumps (affinity laws): 
  - Flow $\propto N$, Pressure $\propto N^2$, Power $\propto N^3$
- For example, reducing speed by 20% saves nearly **50% power**
- Applications: pumps, fans, conveyors, compressors
- Typical saving: 20–50% for variable torque loads

**(iii) Power Factor Correction (PFC)**:
- Motors at low load have poor power factor (0.5–0.7)
- Install capacitor banks to improve PF from 0.7 to 0.95
- Reduces kVA demand → lower electricity bills (kVA-based tariffs)
- Reduces line losses in distribution system
- Typical saving: 5–15% on electricity cost (where kVA billing applies)

**(iv) Proper Motor Sizing**:
- Motors are most efficient at 75–100% of rated load
- Oversized motors operate inefficiently at low load
- Replace oversized motors with correctly sized units
- For intermittent load, use two-speed motors instead of one oversized motor
- Typical saving: 5–10% in operating cost

**Other opportunities**:
- Soft starters to reduce starting current
- Star-delta starters for lightly loaded motors
- Rewinding vs. replacement analysis (new motor often more efficient)
- Regular maintenance (lubrication, alignment, balancing)

---

**Part (b) — Detailed Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**:

**Given Data**:
- 3-phase induction motor
- Frequency $f = 50$ Hz, Poles $p = 6$
- Line voltage $V_L = 415$ V
- Line current $I_L = 60$ A
- Power factor $\cos\phi = 0.88$ lag
- Stator copper loss $P_{scl} = 1.8$ kW
- Core loss $P_{core} = 1.2$ kW
- Friction & windage loss $P_{fw} = 0.9$ kW
- Rotor copper loss $P_{rcl} = 1.5$ kW

**Step (i) — Synchronous Speed**:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{p} = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (ii) — Rotor Speed**:

Given slip $s = 0.04$

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1000 (1 - 0.04) = 1000 \cdot 0.96 = 960 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (iii) — Air-gap Power**:

From rotor copper loss relation: $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{gap}$

$$P_{gap} = \frac{P_{rcl}}{s} = \frac{1.5}{0.04} = 37.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (iv) — Shaft Power Output**:

Mechanical power developed = $P_{gap} - P_{rcl}$

$$P_{developed} = 37.5 - 1.5 = 36.0 \text{ kW}$$

Shaft output power = $P_{developed} - P_{fw}$

$$P_{shaft} = 36.0 - 0.9 = 35.1 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (v) — Motor Efficiency**:

First, calculate input power:

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.88$$

$$P_{in} = 1.732 \cdot 415 \cdot 60 \cdot 0.88$$

$$P_{in} = 37{,}950 \text{ W} \approx 37.95 \text{ kW}$$

Verification using losses:

$$P_{in} = P_{shaft} + P_{scl} + P_{core} + P_{rcl} + P_{fw}$$

$$P_{in} = 35.1 + 1.8 + 1.2 + 1.5 + 0.9 = 40.5 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy Alert**: The two methods give different results (37.95 kW vs 40.5 kW). In KTU valuation, the **direct $\sqrt{3} V I \cos\phi$** method is generally used as the standard input power. The mismatch indicates the given loss values may be approximate or there are additional stray load losses. Always use the **electrical input method** as primary and treat loss values as confirmation.

Motor efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{35.1}{37.95} \times 100 = 92.49\%$$

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNINGS & PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose Marks Here)**:
> 
> 1. **Lighting**: Confusing **lumen** (flux) with **lux** (illuminance). 1 lux = 1 lm/m². Also, forgetting to multiply by **number of luminaires** when calculating total connected load.
> 
> 2. **Motors**: Using **line values** vs **phase values** inconsistently. For 3-phase star, $V_{phase} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$; for delta, $V_{phase} = V_L$. Always re-check whether the formula requires line or phase values.
> 
> 3. **Electrolysis**: Forgetting **current efficiency** factor. Theoretical mass ≠ actual mass. Also confusing atomic weight with equivalent weight.
> 
> 4. **Electric Heating**: Mixing up units (J vs kJ vs kWh). Recall: **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3,600 kJ = 3.6 × 10⁶ J**.
> 
> 5. **General**: Not showing **units** at every step — KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for missing units in final answers.
> 
> 6. **Penalty**: Missing the **"with neat diagram"** instruction in descriptive questions can cost 1–2 marks. Always include a labeled block diagram.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick Revision Checklist (Module 2)

#### LIGHTING
- [ ] Luminous efficacy = lumens / Watt (lm/W)
- [ ] Illuminance E = Φ × N × η_util × LOR / (A × MF)
- [ ] LED (100–200 lm/W) >> HPS (80–140) >> Metal Halide (65–115) >> Fluorescent (50–100) >> Incandescent (10–17)
- [ ] Energy savings: 30–80% by replacing conventional with LED
- [ ] Controls: occupancy sensors, daylight harvesting, timers

#### MOTORS
- [ ] Synchronous speed: $N_s = \frac{120 f}{p}$
- [ ] Slip: $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$
- [ ] Rotor copper loss: $P_{rcl} = s \cdot P_{gap}$
- [ ] Air-gap power: $P_{gap} = \frac{P_{out}}{1-s}$
- [ ] Input power: $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$
- [ ] Motors are most efficient at 75–100% load
- [ ] IE3 (Premium) > IE2 (High) > IE1 (Standard) efficiency classes
- [ ] VFD saves up to 50% power in variable torque loads (fans/pumps)
- [ ] Power factor correction: target cosφ > 0.95

#### ELECTROLYTIC PROCESS
- [ ] Faraday's 1st Law: $m = Z I t = \frac{M I t}{n F}$
- [ ] Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol
- [ ] Faraday's 2nd Law: mass ratio = equivalent weight ratio
- [ ] Cell voltage = equilibrium + overpotentials + IR drops
- [ ] SEC (kWh/tonne) = $V_{cell} / (Z \cdot \eta_{current})$
- [ ] Current efficiency = (actual mass / theoretical mass) × 100
- [ ] Energy efficiency improvement: membrane cells, DSA anodes, heat recovery

#### ELECTRIC HEATING
- [ ] 4 modes: Resistance, Induction, Dielectric, Arc
- [ ] Resistance heating: $P = I^2 R = V^2 / R$
- [ ] Induction heating: contactless, conductive materials only, 100–500 kHz
- [ ] Dielectric heating: non-conductors (plastics, wood), MHz range
- [ ] Arc heating: highest temperature (>3000°C), steel making (EAF)
- [ ] Heating time: $t = \frac{m c \Delta T}{\eta P}$
- [ ] 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3,600 kJ
- [ ] Energy savings: insulation, recuperators, induction over resistance

### Final High-Yield Tips for KTU Exam
1. **Always state units** in every step of numerical problems
2. **Draw a labeled diagram** wherever the question asks for explanation
3. **Use Faraday's constant** = 96,485 C/mol (memorize it)
4. **Memorize the 5 motor losses**: Stator Cu, Rotor Cu, Core, Friction & Windage, Stray
5. **Affinity laws for pumps/fans** with VFDs: P ∝ N³
6. **Heat equation** $Q = mc\Delta T$ applies to all sensible heating
7. **Color codes**: Incandescent = warm white, Fluorescent = cool white, Daylight = 6500 K
8. **Indian context**: BEE Star Rating, ECBC, IS 12615 for motors, PAT scheme for industries
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HVAC System: Coefficient of Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** HVAC System: Coefficient of Performance ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is the fundamental dimensionless performance metric used to evaluate the energy effectiveness of refrigeration, air-conditioning, and heat-pump systems. It is defined as the ratio of the **desired energy output** (either the cooling effect or the heating effect delivered) to the **energy input** required to produce that effect (typically shaft work or electrical energy supplied to the compressor). $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Energy Output (Useful Effect)}}{\text{Energy Input (Work or Heat Supplied)}}$$ For an HVAC system, COP is expressed in two distinct forms depending on the desired useful effect: $$\text{COP}_{\text{cooling}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net,in}}} \quad \text{(Refrigeration / A/C mode)}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{heating}} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net,in}}} \quad \text{(Heat Pump mode)}$$ where: - $Q_L$ = refrigeration effect / cooling load extracted from the conditioned space (kW) - $Q_H$ = heat rejected / heating delivered to the conditioned space (kW) - $W_{\text{net,in}}$ = net work input to the compressor (kW) > [!IMPORTANT] > **COP is NOT efficiency in the thermodynamic sense.** It can be greater than unity (often 2 to 5 for modern systems) because the system *moves* heat rather than *converts* energy. COP exceeding 1 does **not** violate the First Law of Thermodynamics, but is bounded above by the Carnot (reversible) limit, set by the Second Law. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Under NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE), this topic is mapped to **CO3 – Analyse the energy performance of thermal systems**. It is a high-weightage topic in Module 3 of PEMRT746 and forms the conceptual foundation for Part-B (14-mark) analytical problems in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Bucket Relay" Imagine a team of workers transferring water from a *low well* (cold reservoir) to a *high well* (hot reservoir). A **water pump (the compressor)** does useful work, lifting water *up*. But the *real prize* for the farmer is the water that ends up in the **upper well** ($Q_H$) — or, alternatively, the water removed from the **lower well** ($Q_L$). - **COP of a Heat Pump** = Total water in upper well ÷ Energy spent pumping. If the pump is efficient, it moves *more* water than the work equivalent of heat, so COP $> 1$. - **COP of a Refrigerator** = Water extracted from the cold well (preserved milk, ice, AC-cooled room) ÷ Energy spent pumping. - **Electric heater analogy:** If instead of pumping water, you simply *boiled* it with the same amount of electricity, you would only get 1 unit of heat per 1 unit of electricity — that is the baseline (COP = 1). A heat pump at COP = 3.5 delivers **3.5 units of heat** per 1 unit of electricity. This analogy makes it intuitively clear why COP $> 1$ is normal — the pump is *moving existing heat uphill*, not creating it. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Conversion factor (COP $\to$ EER) | **3.412** | Btu/h per W | | Conversion factor (EER $\to$ COP) | **0.293** | W per Btu/h | | Typical Window AC COP | **2.5 – 3.0** | dimensionless | | Typical Inverter Split AC COP | **4.0 – 5.5** | dimensionless | | Typical Chiller (VFD) COP | **5.0 – 6.5** | dimensionless | | Theoretical Carnot COP (cooling, 5°C $\to$ 35°C) | **8.85** | dimensionless | | Reference ambient (ASHRAE) | **35°C / 308 K** | for outdoor | | Reference indoor (cooling) | **26.7°C / 299.8 K** | conditioned space | > [!NOTE] > **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)** = $\frac{\text{Cooling Capacity in Btu/hr}}{\text{Power Input in Watts}}$ = 3.412 $\times$ COP. **SEER** (Seasonal EER) and **IPLV** (Integrated Part Load Value) are industry standards used to rate real-world ACs across varying load conditions. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Three Pillars of COP The performance of any HVAC system is governed by three interconnected analytical pillars: **Pillar 1 — Energy Balance (First Law of Thermodynamics)** For a cyclic refrigeration/heat-pump device operating in steady state, energy conservation dictates that all heat rejected at the hot reservoir equals the heat absorbed at the cold reservoir plus the net work input: $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{net,in}}$$ This single equation is the origin of the elegant identity linking heating and cooling COPs: $$\text{COP}_{\text{heating}} = \text{COP}_{\text{cooling}} + 1$$ **Pillar 2 — Reversible (Carnot) Bound (Second Law of Thermodynamics)** A reversible cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at absolute temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$ (in Kelvin) achieves the *maximum possible* COP. Real, irreversible cycles always fall short of this limit. $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, cooling}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, heating}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Pillar 3 — Second-Law Efficiency (Exergy Utilisation)** To assess how close an *actual* machine approaches its theoretical ideal, we define: $$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} \times 100\%$$ This quantifies the *true thermodynamic quality* of the device — useful in energy audits to identify equipment needing retrofit. --- ### 2.2 Why and How — Operational Logic Walk-Through Each logical step below explains the engineering rationale behind HVAC-COP relationships: - **Step 1 — Why does COP have two forms?** Because the *useful output* depends on the application. In cooling mode (refrigerator/AC), the useful effect is the heat *removed* from the cold space ($Q_L$). In heating mode (heat pump), the useful effect is the heat *delivered* to the warm space ($Q_H$). Both $Q_L$ and $Q_H$ include the work input, hence $\text{COP}_{\text{heat}} > \text{COP}_{\text{cool}}$. - **Step 2 — Why does COP drop with rising outdoor temperature?** As $T_H$ rises (hotter ambient), the denominator $(T_H - T_L)$ grows, but the numerator $T_L$ is fixed. Thus $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ decreases. This is why ACs perform poorly on extreme summer afternoons — the *theoretical* limit itself shrinks. - **Step 3 — Why is sub-cooling and super-heating important?** Sub-cooling the liquid leaving the condenser and super-heating the vapour leaving the evaporator increases the refrigerant's *effective* enthalpy difference, raising the refrigeration effect $Q_L$ per unit mass flow, thereby increasing COP. - **Step 4 — Why does compressor isentropic efficiency matter?** For real (irreversible) compressors, actual work input exceeds the ideal isentropic work. The ratio $\eta_{\text{isentropic}}$ directly scales COP down — for example, a 70% isentropic compressor at the same evaporator/condenser temperatures will deliver roughly 70% of the ideal COP. - **Step 5 — Why is Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) so effective?** At part loads, traditional ON-OFF compressors cycle inefficiently. VFD modulates compressor speed to match load, keeping the system near its *peak* COP across a wide operating envelope. The metric **IPLV** specifically captures this part-load efficiency. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are expected to be reproduced from memory in the KTU ESE. Memorise the **boundary conditions** (temperatures in Kelvin!) and the **identity** $\text{COP}_{\text{heat}} = \text{COP}_{\text{cool}} + 1$. | \# | Formula / Identity | Application / Boundary Condition | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{COP}_{\text{cool}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}$ | Standard refrigeration COP (A/C, chiller) | dimensionless | | 2 | $\text{COP}_{\text{heat}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{in}}}$ | Heat pump COP (winter heating) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\text{COP}_{\text{heat}} = \text{COP}_{\text{cool}} + 1$ | Derived from $Q_H = Q_L + W$ | dimensionless | | 4 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, cool}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible limit; temps in **Kelvin** | dimensionless | | 5 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, heat}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible heat-pump limit | dimensionless | | 6 | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} \times 100$ | Second-law efficiency / energy audit metric | \% | | 7 | $\text{EER} = 3.412 \times \text{COP}$ | Btu/h per W; used in AC nameplates | Btu/h$\cdot$W | | 8 | $W_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{\text{cool}}}$ | Power consumption from cooling load | kW | | 9 | $W_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{\text{COP}_{\text{heat}}}$ | Power consumption from heating load | kW | | 10 | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{in}}$ | First-law energy balance | kW | | 11 | $\text{SCOP} = \dfrac{\sum Q_{\text{annual}}}{\sum W_{\text{annual}}}$ | Seasonal COP — full-year basis | dimensionless | | 12 | $\text{IPLV} = \sum_{i} (\text{PLV}_i \cdot w_i)$ | Integrated Part Load Value (AHRI Std 550/590) | dimensionless | | 13 | $\text{Energy Savings Fraction} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\text{COP}_{\text{heat}}}$ | Heat pump vs electric resistance heater | fraction | | 14 | $T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15 = T(\text{K})$ | **Mandatory conversion** — never use °C in Carnot eqns | K | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary-condition discipline (KTU pitfall):** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by plugging °C directly into the Carnot-COP formula. Convert to Kelvin first — always. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice In **energy auditing** (the central theme of PEMRT746), COP analysis drives several practical decisions: 1. **Equipment Replacement Decision:** If an existing chiller has $\eta_{II} < 35\%$, retrofitting with an inverter-driven scroll/chiller typically reduces annual electricity by 25–40%, paying back capital in 2–4 years. 2. **Heat Pump vs Electric Heater:** At COP = 3.5, a heat pump delivers 3.5 kW of heat per kW of electricity, displacing 71.4% of the energy an electric resistance heater would consume. Kerala households adopting heat-pump water heaters typically save ₹4,000–₹8,000 per year. 3. **Refrigerant Selection:** Low-GWP refrigerants (R-32, R-290) with higher saturation pressures yield better thermodynamic performance and thus higher COP than legacy R-22 systems. 4. **Tariff-Based Sizing:** The IPLV/SEER metrics align capital cost decisions with operational electricity bills — critical in large commercial HVAC systems. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Identity $\text{COP}_{\text{heat}} = \text{COP}_{\text{cool}} + 1$ This identity is the most frequently tested identity in KTU exams. We derive it rigorously from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a cyclic refrigeration device. **Starting Point — Steady-State Energy Balance** For a refrigeration cycle operating as a control volume in steady state, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes: $$\sum \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} + \sum \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{Q}_{\text{out}} + \sum \dot{W}_{\text{out}}$$ The cycle absorbs heat $\dot{Q}_L$ at the evaporator (cold reservoir) and rejects heat $\dot{Q}_H$ at the condenser (hot reservoir). The only work interaction is the compressor input $\dot{W}_{\text{in}}$. Therefore: $$\dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \dot{Q}_H$$ Rearranging to express each COP in terms of $\dot{W}_{\text{in}}$: **Divide the energy balance by $\dot{W}_{\text{in}}$:** $$\frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}} + \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}}$$ **Recognise each ratio as a COP definition:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{cool}} + 1 = \text{COP}_{\text{heat}}$$ **Q.E.D.** > **Engineering significance:** If a refrigerator has COP$_{\text{cool}}$ = 2.5, then the *same hardware* operating as a heat pump will have COP$_{\text{heat}}$ = 3.5 — a 40% higher figure. This explains why reversible heat pumps are favoured for both cooling and heating in modern HVAC integrated designs. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carnot COP for a Refrigerator The Carnot (reversible) refrigerator sets the **theoretical upper limit** for any cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs. **Reversible Cycle Property** For a *reversible* cycle, the ratio of heat transferred to absolute temperature is conserved (Clausius equality): $$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{\text{rev}}}{T} = 0$$ Applying this to a cycle exchanging $\dot{Q}_L$ at $T_L$ and $\dot{Q}_H$ at $T_H$: $$\frac{\dot{Q}_L}{T_L} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{T_H}$$ **Express the ratio in terms of temperature only:** $$\frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{Q}_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ **Substitute the First-Law relation** $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}}$ into the COP definition: $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, cool}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{Q}_H - \dot{Q}_L}$$ **Divide numerator and denominator by $\dot{Q}_H$:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, cool}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L/\dot{Q}_H}{1 - \dot{Q}_L/\dot{Q}_H}$$ **Substitute** $\dot{Q}_L/\dot{Q}_H = T_L/T_H$: $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, cool}} = \frac{T_L/T_H}{1 - T_L/T_H} = \frac{T_L/T_H}{(T_H - T_L)/T_H}$$ **Cancel $T_H$ in numerator and denominator:** $$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, cool}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\;}$$ **Similarly, the heat-pump Carnot COP is:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, heat}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{\dot{Q}_H - \dot{Q}_L}$$ Following analogous algebra: $$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, heat}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}\;}$$ > **Engineering insight:** The temperature difference $(T_H - T_L)$ acts as the "penalty" — the smaller this gap, the higher the COP. Heat pumps operating in Kerala's mild climate (where winter lows rarely fall below 20°C) achieve much higher COPs than those operating in Delhi winters (5°C). For each 10°C drop in outdoor temperature, COP drops roughly 30–40%. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Work Input, Heat Rejection, and Energy Savings **Given data (Kerala-monsoon scenario):** - Cooling load $Q_L = 50 \text{ kW}$ - $\text{COP}_{\text{cool}} = 3.0$ - Outdoor temperature = 35°C = 308 K - Indoor temperature = 24°C = 297 K **Step 1 — Calculate Work Input** $$W_{\text{in}} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{\text{cool}}} = \frac{50}{3.0} = 16.67 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Heat Rejected to Outdoor** Using the energy balance $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}}$: $$\dot{Q}_H = 50 + 16.67 = 66.67 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Carnot COP** $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{297}{308 - 297} = \frac{297}{11} = 27.0$$ **Step 4 — Calculate Second-Law Efficiency** $$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} = \frac{3.0}{27.0} \times 100 = 11.11\%$$ **Step 5 — Compare with Electric Resistance Heating Baseline** If the *same* 50 kW of heating were provided by an electric resistance heater (COP = 1.0), the required power would be 50 kW. The heat pump instead consumes only 16.67 kW. **Energy Saved:** $$\text{Savings Fraction} = 1 - \frac{1}{\text{COP}_{\text{heat}}} = 1 - \frac{1}{4.0} = 0.75 = 75\%$$ (The heat-pump COP = 3.0 + 1 = 4.0.) **Annual Energy Saved** (assuming 1,500 heating-equivalent hours/year): $$\Delta E = 50 \times 1500 \times 0.75 = 56{,}250 \text{ kWh/year}$$ **Annual Cost Saved** at ₹7/kWh (Kerala domestic tariff): $$\Delta C = 56{,}250 \times 7 = \text{₹}3{,}93{,}750 \text{ per year}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Second-law efficiency of just 11.11%** might appear low, but this is *normal* for real HVAC systems — the gap between actual and reversible performance is dominated by unavoidable heat-transfer irreversibilities across finite temperature differences in the evaporator and condenser. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Variable Analysis of COP This matrix helps students intuitively reason about how each design parameter shifts COP. Memorise the **directional trend** for each row. | Parameter | Increase Direction | Effect on COP$_{\text{cool}}$ | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | Outdoor temperature $T_H$ | $\uparrow$ | **Decreases** | Larger $(T_H - T_L)$ denominator in Carnot | | Indoor (evaporator) temperature $T_L$ | $\uparrow$ | **Increases** | Larger numerator $T_L$ in Carnot | | Sub-cooling at condenser exit | $\uparrow$ | **Increases** | Higher refrigerant enthalpy lift | | Super-heat at evaporator exit | $\uparrow$ | **Decreases** (initially); **Increases** if no liquid carryover | Trade-off curve | | Compressor isentropic efficiency | $\uparrow$ | **Increases** | Less work for same pressure rise | | Condenser heat-transfer coefficient | $\uparrow$ | **Increases** | Smaller temperature glide, lower $T_H$ | | Evaporator heat-transfer coefficient | $\uparrow$ | **Increases** | Larger temperature glide, higher $T_L$ | | Pressure drop in suction line | $\uparrow$ | **Decreases** | Higher compressor work | | Refrigerant mass flow | $\uparrow$ | Neutral per-unit, but scales total $Q_L$ | Direct proportionality | | Part-load ratio (PLR) | Variable | Peaks at 60–80% PLR (VFD) | Efficiency curve | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle with COP Tagging The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the four key components of a standard HVAC refrigeration cycle, with annotated COP calculation flow. This functional schematic replaces a physical P&H drawing and is sufficient for KTU answer scripts. ```mermaid flowchart LR comp1[COMPRESSOR
Low Pressure Vapour
to High Pressure Vapour] -->|Work W_in| cond2[CONDENSER
High Pressure Vapour
to High Pressure Liquid] cond2 -->|Q_H Rejected
to Outdoor Air| cond2 cond2 --> expv3[EXPANSION VALVE
Throttling Process
H Constant] expv3 --> evap4[EVAPORATOR
Low Pressure Liquid
to Low Pressure Vapour] evap4 -->|Q_L Absorbed
from Indoor Air| evap4 evap4 --> comp1 copcalccool[COP_cool equals
Q_L divided by W_in] copcalcheat[COP_heat equals
Q_H divided by W_in] copleft[COP Carnot Cool equals
T_L divided by T_H minus T_L] evap4 -. Q_L .-> copcalccool cond2 -. Q_H .-> copcalcheat comp1 -. W_in .-> copcalccool comp1 -. W_in .-> copcalcheat copleft -. Theoretical Bound .-> copcalccool classDef hw fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef sw fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#993300,color:#000 class comp1,cond2,evap4,expv3 hw class copcalccool,copcalcheat,copleft sw ``` **Component-wise energy roles:** - **Compressor:** Sole work input $W_{\text{in}}$; raises refrigerant pressure and temperature. - **Condenser:** Rejects $\dot{Q}_H$ to outdoor ambient — this is the *unwanted by-product* in cooling mode, but the *desired output* in heating mode. - **Expansion Valve:** Isenthalpic throttling — no work, no heat transfer, but creates the low-temperature low-pressure condition needed for evaporation. - **Evaporator:** Absorbs $\dot{Q}_L$ from the conditioned space — the *useful output* in cooling mode. --- ### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Energy Audit Flow for COP Improvement This block diagram maps the energy auditor's decision tree when analysing an HVAC system for COP-based retrofits, suitable for KTU 14-mark answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[START
Collect Operating Data
of HVAC System] --> stepB[Measure Q_L Tons of
Refrigeration and
Power Input kW] stepB --> stepC[Compute Actual COP
COP equals Q_L divided by W_in] stepC --> stepD[Compute Carnot COP
using T_H and T_L in Kelvin] stepD --> stepE[Compute Second Law
Efficiency eta II equals
COP actual divided by COP Carnot] stepE --> stepF{Decision
eta II less than 35 percent} stepF -->|YES| stepG[Recommend Retrofit
Options A B C] stepF -->|NO| stepH[System Acceptable
Schedule Routine Maintenance] stepG --> optA[Option A
Install VFD on Compressor
Expected COP Gain 20 to 30 percent] stepG --> optB[Option B
Replace with Inverter
Chiller COP Gain 30 to 40 percent] stepG --> optC[Option C
Improve Heat Exchanger
Cleaning and Insulation
COP Gain 5 to 10 percent] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) for a refrigeration system. Why is COP always greater than unity for a practical refrigerator, while ordinary thermal efficiency is always less than unity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** COP of a refrigeration system is defined as the ratio of the desired cooling effect $Q_L$ to the work input $W_{\text{in}}$: $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{in}}}$. *(1 mark)* A refrigerator transfers heat from a cold body to a hot body using external work. By the First Law, $Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{in}}$, so $Q_L < Q_H$, but the *work* $W_{\text{in}}$ is smaller than $Q_L$ for any reasonably efficient cycle. *(1 mark)* Since $Q_L > W_{\text{in}}$, COP exceeds unity. Thermal efficiency of a heat engine is $\eta = W/Q_{\text{in}} \leq 1$ because work output cannot exceed heat input (First-Law bound). Refrigerator COP is bounded differently — by the Second Law (Carnot limit), not by unity. *(1 mark)* #### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **An air-conditioner operates between indoor temperature 25°C and outdoor temperature 40°C. Compute the maximum possible COP and comment on the practical COP.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Convert to Kelvin: $T_L = 25 + 273 = 298$ K, $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313$ K. *(1 mark)* $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{298}{313 - 298} = \frac{298}{15} = 19.87$$ *(1 mark)* Comment: This is the *theoretical* upper bound assuming reversible operation. In practice, real air-conditioners achieve COP = 2.5–4.0 due to irreversibilities (finite temperature differences in heat exchangers, compressor inefficiency, pressure drops). The second-law efficiency $\eta_{II}$ typically ranges from 12–25%. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always convert °C to Kelvin** before applying Carnot formulas — this single mistake costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Do not confuse cooling and heating COPs** — the +1 identity is tested every year. Read the question carefully. > 3. **State units explicitly** in every numerical answer. Marks are deducted for "bare numbers" without kW, kJ, or %. > 4. **Show energy-balance steps** when computing $Q_H$ or $W$ — students often write the final answer without intermediate work, losing process marks. > 5. **Involve all sub-parts** in your final answer — incomplete sub-parts attract proportional cut in marks. --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **A commercial refrigeration plant maintains a cold storage at $-10°C$ while rejecting heat to surroundings at $35°C$. The system delivers 20 kW of cooling with an actual COP of 2.8.** **(a)** Determine: (i) The COP of a Carnot refrigerator operating between the same temperature limits. *(2 marks)* (ii) The Second-Law (exergy) efficiency of the actual system. *(2 marks)* (iii) The compressor work input and the heat rejected per hour. *(3 marks)* **(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram any **four** methods to improve COP of a vapour compression refrigeration system, and indicate the typical percentage gain expected from each. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — (i) Carnot COP** *(2 marks)* Convert temperatures to Kelvin: $$T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}, \quad T_H = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, cool}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{308 - 263} = \frac{263}{45} = 5.84$$ *(1 mark for Kelvin conversion; 1 mark for final answer.)* **Part (a) — (ii) Second-Law Efficiency** *(2 marks)* $$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} \times 100 = \frac{2.8}{5.84} \times 100 = 47.95\%$$ *(1 mark for formula; 1 mark for calculation.)* **Part (a) — (iii) Compressor Work and Heat Rejected** *(3 marks)* **Step 1 — Compressor work input:** $$W_{\text{in}} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{\text{cool}}} = \frac{20}{2.8} = 7.14 \text{ kW}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 2 — Heat rejected per second:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{in}} = 20 + 7.14 = 27.14 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Heat rejected per hour:** $$Q_{H,\text{hourly}} = 27.14 \times 3600 = 97{,}704 \text{ kJ/h} = 27.14 \text{ kWh}$$ *(2 marks for energy balance + time conversion)* **Part (b) — Four Methods to Improve COP** *(7 marks)* | \# | Method | Mechanism | Expected COP Gain | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sub-cooling the liquid refrigerant** | Increases refrigeration effect $h_1 - h_4$ per kg of refrigerant | **8–15%** | | 2 | **Super-heating the vapour (within safe limits)** | Ensures no liquid carryover to compressor; minimal COP change | **2–5%** | | 3 | **Reducing condensing temperature** (clean condenser coils, lower ambient) | Decreases $T_H$, shrinking $(T_H - T_L)$ gap | **10–20%** | | 4 | **Increasing evaporating temperature** (clean evaporator, proper air flow) | Raises $T_L$, increasing COP numerator | **8–12%** | | 5 | **Using VFD / inverter compressor** | Maintains high COP at part-load conditions | **20–35% (seasonal)** | | 6 | **Multi-staging with inter-cooling** | Reduces compressor work for same pressure ratio | **10–15%** | *(Award 1.5 marks per method × 4 methods = 6 marks; 1 mark for conclusion statement that COP improvements must be balanced against capital cost.)* **Block Diagram:** Draw the standard vapour compression cycle with annotated sub-cooler, expansion valve, evaporator, and compressor. Label the increased enthalpy differences. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **A heat-pump water heater is used in a Kerala household to provide domestic hot water at $55°C$. The heat pump extracts heat from ambient air at $25°C$ and has an actual COP of 3.2 in heating mode.** **(a)** Calculate: (i) The Carnot COP (heating) for the given temperature limits. *(2 marks)* (ii) The power consumed if the heating capacity required is 8 kW. *(2 marks)* (iii) The heat extracted from the ambient air per hour. *(3 marks)* (iv) The annual electricity savings (in ₹) if the heat pump replaces a conventional electric geyser, assuming 5 hours daily operation and electricity tariff ₹6.50/kWh. *(2 marks — bonus extension)* **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of **IPLV (Integrated Part Load Value)** and **SCOP (Seasonal Coefficient of Performance)**. Why are these preferred over single-point COP in real-world HVAC selection? *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — (i) Carnot COP (Heating)** *(2 marks)* Convert temperatures: $$T_{\text{hot}} = 55 + 273 = 328 \text{ K}, \quad T_{\text{cold}} = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, heat}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{328}{328 - 298} = \frac{328}{30} = 10.93$$ *(1 mark for Kelvin conversion; 1 mark for the value 10.93.)* **Part (a) — (ii) Power Consumed** *(2 marks)* Given COP$_{\text{heat}}$ = 3.2 and heating capacity $Q_H = 8$ kW: $$W_{\text{in}} = \frac{Q_H}{\text{COP}_{\text{heat}}} = \frac{8}{3.2} = 2.5 \text{ kW}$$ *(1 mark for formula; 1 mark for answer.)* **Part (a) — (iii) Heat Extracted from Ambient** *(3 marks)* By First-Law energy balance: $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{\text{in}} = 8 - 2.5 = 5.5 \text{ kW}$$ Heat extracted per hour: $$Q_{L,\text{hourly}} = 5.5 \times 3600 = 19{,}800 \text{ kJ/h} = 5.5 \text{ kWh}$$ *(1 mark for energy balance; 1 mark for per-hour calculation; 1 mark for unit consistency.)* **Part (a) — (iv) Annual Electricity Savings** *(2 marks — extension)* Electric geyser would consume = 8 kW × 5 h/day × 365 days = **14,600 kWh/year** Heat pump consumes = 2.5 kW × 5 h/day × 365 days = **4,562.5 kWh/year** Energy saved = 14,600 − 4,562.5 = **10,037.5 kWh/year** Cost saved = 10,037.5 × ₹6.50 = **₹65,244 per year** *(1 mark for each correct value — energy saved and ₹ saved.)* **Part (b) — IPLV and SCOP Explanation** *(7 marks)* **IPLV (Integrated Part Load Value)** is defined by AHRI Standard 550/590 as a single-number efficiency metric weighted across four operating part-load conditions (100%, 75%, 50%, 25%) corresponding to typical chiller operating hours. $$\text{IPLV} = 0.01 \cdot \text{EER}_{100\%} + 0.42 \cdot \text{EER}_{75\%} + 0.45 \cdot \text{EER}_{50\%} + 0.12 \cdot \text{EER}_{25\%}$$ *(2 marks for definition + formula)* **SCOP (Seasonal COP)** is the ratio of the *total* annual heating or cooling energy delivered by an HVAC system to the *total* annual electricity consumed, measured over a realistic operating year: $$\text{SCOP} = \frac{\sum_{j} Q_{\text{delivered},j}}{\sum_{j} W_{\text{in},j}}$$ *(2 marks for definition + formula)* **Why preferred over single-point COP:** *(3 marks)* 1. Real HVAC systems operate mostly at *part-load* (40–70% of rated capacity) due to diurnal and seasonal variations. Single-point full-load COP can overstate performance by 30–50%. 2. IPLV captures chiller efficiency across standard part-load points typical of commercial buildings. 3. SCOP further accounts for ambient temperature variations, standby losses, defrost cycles (heat pumps), and auxiliary power (pumps, fans), giving a true field-performance figure. 4. Both metrics enable *apples-to-apples* comparison of competing HVAC equipment during procurement decisions — critical for energy auditors and consultants. **Block Diagram:** Draw a flowchart showing outdoor ambient variation → chiller part-load response → EER at each part-load → weighted aggregation into IPLV/SCOP. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3, COP Topic** - **Definition trio:** Know the exact equations for $\text{COP}_{\text{cool}}$, $\text{COP}_{\text{heat}}$, and $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$ — both cooling and heating variants. - **The Master Identity:** $\text{COP}_{\text{heat}} = \text{COP}_{\text{cool}} + 1$ — derived from First Law, frequently tested. - **Kelvin conversion is non-negotiable:** Always convert °C → K before plugging into any Carnot formula. - **EER vs COP:** $\text{EER} = 3.412 \times \text{COP}$; often asked in conversion-type MCQs. - **Second-Law Efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}} \times 100\%$ — central metric in energy audits. - **Work-from-load:** $W_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{\text{cool}}}$ — used in 80% of numerical problems. - **Heat rejection:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{in}}$ — never skip this when the question asks for condenser duty. - **Heat-pump economics:** Energy savings fraction $= 1 - \dfrac{1}{\text{COP}_{\text{heat}}}$ — important for retrofit justifications. - **IPLV vs SCOP:** IPLV is a 4-point weighted rating; SCOP is annual real-world aggregate — know when to use which. - **Improvement methods:** Sub-cooling, super-heating, multi-staging, VFD, condenser cooling, evaporator air-flow tuning — each with realistic percentage gains. - **Performance indicators to remember:** Modern chiller COP = 5–6.5; inverter AC COP = 4–5.5; typical window AC COP = 2.5–3. - **Pitfall — Kelvin vs Celsius:** Apply the rule "add 273, not 273.15" for engineering approximation unless problem specifies otherwise. - **Pitfall — Direction of COP improvement:** A *lower* condensing temperature and a *higher* evaporating temperature both improve COP. Memorise the trend. ---

topics.title: Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Capacity # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Energy Management & Auditing (EMA)**, the term **"Capacity"** refers to the maximum rated output that an energy-conversion system, plant, equipment, or process is designed to deliver under specified operating conditions, expressed in standard units of energy or power (e.g., kW, MW, kWh/day, tonnes/day, kg/h). The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) and the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** classify capacity into several hierarchical tiers, each carrying distinct audit significance: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMRT746, Module 3):** > *"Capacity is the maximum continuous output or throughput that a system is rated to produce under nameplate/design conditions, against which actual performance is benchmarked during an energy audit."* ### Hierarchy of Capacity Definitions | # | Capacity Type | Definition | Audit Significance | |---|---------------|------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Installed Capacity** | The maximum theoretical output of all equipment as erected on site, before derating. | Baseline benchmark | | 2 | **Rated / Nameplate Capacity** | Manufacturer's guaranteed output under standard test conditions (ISO/ASME). | Design reference | | 3 | **Maximum Continuous Rating (MCR)** | The highest continuous load the equipment can sustain indefinitely without damage. | Operational ceiling | | 4 | **Operating / Effective Capacity** | Actual sustainable output after derating for ambient, aging, auxiliary loads. | Realistic baseline | | 5 | **Demonstrated Capacity** | The highest output actually achieved in a defined test run. | Performance proof | > [!NOTE] > **Key Mnemonic — "I-R-M-O-D"** (Installed → Rated → MCR → Operating → Demonstrated) — these capacities always form a descending sequence once ambient derating and auxiliary loads are accounted for. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water tank on the roof of a building**: - **Installed Capacity** = The tank's total geometric volume (e.g., 5000 L). - **Rated Capacity** = The manufacturer's claim of "5000 L" stamped on the nameplate, tested under standard pressure. - **MCR** = The maximum water the pump can deliver every minute, 24/7, without burning out. - **Operating Capacity** = Real amount the tank supplies daily after accounting for sediment, scaling, and one pipe partially clogged. - **Demonstrated Capacity** = What the plumber actually measured coming out of the tap on his last visit. In an **energy audit**, we compare *Actual Performance* against *Operating Capacity* (not the inflated Installed Capacity) — otherwise every plant would look like it is operating at 30 % efficiency forever. > [!TIP] > **Physical Constant to Memorize:** In SI, **1 kW = 3.6 MJ/h = 860 kcal/h**. Energy audits in India (BEE) primarily use **kWh** as the unit of capacity energy and **kW** as the unit of power capacity. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Do We Need Different Definitions of Capacity? A raw "Installed Capacity" is misleading in energy audits because: 1. Equipment derates with **ambient temperature** (boilers lose ~1 % output per 5 °C rise in feed-water temperature). 2. **Auxiliary loads** (pumps, fans, lighting) consume 5–12 % of gross output internally. 3. **Aging, fouling, scaling** reduces the heat-transfer coefficient over time. 4. **Process constraints** (raw material quality, shift schedules) prevent continuous full-load operation. Therefore, the auditor uses capacity as a **moving benchmark** to detect wastage. ## 2.2 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Derived from Capacity ### (a) Capacity Utilization (CU) Measures how much of the **Maximum Continuous Rating** is actually being used. $$\text{Capacity Utilization (CU)} = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Continuous Rating (MCR)}} \times 100\%$$ - **Acceptable Benchmark:** 80–90 % for continuous process plants. - **Below 60 %:** Indicates inefficient capital deployment. - **Above 95 %:** Indicates capacity bottleneck; plant may need expansion. ### (b) Plant Load Factor (PLF) Time-weighted average load over a period versus MCR. $$\text{Plant Load Factor} = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Maximum Continuous Rating}} \times 100\%$$ ### (c) Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Energy used per unit of product output — directly tied to operating capacity. $$\text{SEC} = \frac{\text{Total Energy Input}}{\text{Actual Useful Output}}$$ ### (d) Capacity Factor (CF) — used for captive power plants / DG sets $$\text{Capacity Factor} = \frac{\text{Energy Actually Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Plant Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Hours in Period}}$$ ### (e) Utilization Factor (UF) — accounts for actual running hours $$\text{Utilization Factor} = \frac{\text{Energy Actually Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Plant Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Actual Running Hours}}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------| | 1 | Capacity Utilization (CU) | $\dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{MCR}} \times 100$ | % | 70 – 90 | | 2 | Plant Load Factor (PLF) | $\dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{MCR}} \times 100$ | % | 60 – 85 | | 3 | Capacity Factor (CF) | $\dfrac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{period}}}$ | decimal / % | 0.4 – 0.8 | | 4 | Utilization Factor (UF) | $\dfrac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{running}}}$ | decimal / % | 0.7 – 0.95 | | 5 | Specific Energy Consumption | $\dfrac{E_{\text{input}}}{m_{\text{product}}}$ | kWh/tonne | Industry specific | | 6 | Spare Capacity | $\text{MCR} - \text{Operating Capacity}$ | kW, tonne/h | — | | 7 | Annual Operating Hours | $8760 \times \text{UF}$ | h/year | 6000 – 8000 | | 8 | Heat Rate (Thermal) | $\dfrac{\text{Fuel Energy Input}}{\text{Electrical Energy Output}}$ | kcal/kWh | 2000 – 3500 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** PLF uses *Average Load* (averaged over calendar hours), while UF uses *Energy Output / (Capacity × Running Hours)*. Do not interchange them in viva or numerical problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility Capacity-based KPIs are used by: - **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India)** for PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme benchmarking. - **ISO 50001** Energy Management Systems for setting EnPIs (Energy Performance Indicators). - **Captive Power Plants** to decide whether to buy power from the grid vs. generate in-house. - **Boiler / Steam System Audits** to find opportunities for de-bottlenecking without capital expansion. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Capacity Factor from First Principles Let: - $C_{\text{rated}}$ = Rated plant capacity in **kW**. - $t_{\text{period}}$ = Total hours in the audit period (typically 8760 h for 1 year, or 720 h for 1 month). - $E_{\text{actual}}$ = Energy actually generated during the period in **kWh**. **Step 1 — Definition of Energy as Power × Time** $$E_{\text{ideal}} = C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{period}}$$ This is the maximum energy the plant *could* have produced had it run continuously at full rated output. **Step 2 — Definition of Capacity Factor** By convention, Capacity Factor is the ratio of actual energy produced to this ideal maximum: $$\text{CF} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{ideal}}}$$ **Step 3 — Substituting** $$\text{CF} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{period}}}$$ **Step 4 — Expressing in Percentage** $$\text{CF (\%)} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{period}}} \times 100$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Utilization Factor Let $t_{\text{running}}$ be the actual running hours of the plant (excluding idle / maintenance time). **Step 1 — Energy that would have been produced if plant ran at rated output for its running hours only** $$E_{\text{available}} = C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{running}}$$ **Step 2 — Ratio** $$\text{UF} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{running}}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Exam Style **Problem:** A captive power plant has a rated capacity of **10 MW**. During the audit month of 30 days, the plant generated **4,32,000 kWh** of electricity. The plant actually ran for **600 hours**. Calculate (a) Capacity Factor, (b) Utilization Factor, (c) Plant Load Factor if average load was 7.2 MW. ### Solution **Given Data** - $C_{\text{rated}} = 10 \text{ MW} = 10{,}000 \text{ kW}$ - Audit period = 30 days = $30 \times 24 = 720$ h - $E_{\text{actual}} = 4{,}32{,}000$ kWh - $t_{\text{running}} = 600$ h - Average load = 7.2 MW ### (a) Capacity Factor $$\begin{aligned} \text{CF} &= \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{period}}} \\ &= \frac{4{,}32{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{10{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 720 \text{ h}} \\ &= \frac{4{,}32{,}000}{72{,}00{,}000} \\ &= 0.06 = 6\% \end{aligned}$$ ### (b) Utilization Factor $$\begin{aligned} \text{UF} &= \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{running}}} \\ &= \frac{4{,}32{,}000}{10{,}000 \times 600} \\ &= \frac{4{,}32{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \\ &= 0.072 = 7.2\% \end{aligned}$$ ### (c) Plant Load Factor $$\begin{aligned} \text{PLF} &= \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{MCR}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{7.2 \text{ MW}}{10 \text{ MW}} \times 100 \\ &= 72\% \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The PLF is 72 %, which appears healthy, but CF is only 6 % because the plant was idle for most of the month. The auditor should recommend either higher utilization hours or **right-sizing** of the plant. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical on Boiler Capacity **Problem:** A boiler has MCR of **10 tonne/h** of steam. During an 8-hour shift, it produced **64 tonnes** of steam. Calculate Capacity Utilization and Spare Capacity. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Actual Output (per h)} &= \frac{64 \text{ t}}{8 \text{ h}} = 8 \text{ t/h} \\ \text{CU} &= \frac{8}{10} \times 100 = 80\% \\ \text{Spare Capacity} &= 10 - 8 = 2 \text{ t/h} \end{aligned}$$ --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Capacity Hierarchy Flow Diagram The following Mermaid block renders the hierarchical relationship between the five types of capacity. Alphanumeric node IDs (capA–capE) are used; no reserved keywords appear as standalone labels. ```mermaid graph TD capA["INSTALLED CAPACITY
Maximum theoretical output of all erected equipment"] capB["RATED / NAMEPLATE CAPACITY
Manufacturer guarantee under standard test conditions"] capC["MAXIMUM CONTINUOUS RATING MCR
Highest load sustainable indefinitely"] capD["OPERATING / EFFECTIVE CAPACITY
After derating for ambient aging auxiliary loads"] capE["DEMONSTRATED CAPACITY
Highest output measured in actual test run"] capA -->|Derating for design margin| capB capB -->|Operational safety margin| capC capC -->|Site ambient and aging factor| capD capD -->|Real process constraints| capE capA -.->|Comparison during audit| audit["ENERGY AUDIT BENCHMARK"] capD -.->|Comparison during audit| audit style capA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style capB fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style capC fill:#FFC988,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style capD fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style capE fill:#FF8C42,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style audit fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Capacity-Based Performance Assessment This diagram maps the data flow of a typical energy audit, showing how capacity inputs are processed into KPI outputs. ```mermaid graph LR inpA["Plant Rated Capacity kW"] inpB["Actual Energy Output kWh"] inpC["Operating Hours h"] inpD["Period Hours h"] inpE["Average Load kW"] proc1["Compute Capacity Factor"] proc2["Compute Utilization Factor"] proc3["Compute Plant Load Factor"] proc4["Compute Spare Capacity"] outA["CF percent"] outB["UF percent"] outC["PLF percent"] outD["Spare Capacity kW"] outE["Audit Recommendation Report"] inpA --> proc1 inpB --> proc1 inpD --> proc1 proc1 --> outA inpA --> proc2 inpB --> proc2 inpC --> proc2 proc2 --> outB inpE --> proc3 inpA --> proc3 proc3 --> outC inpA --> proc4 inpE --> proc4 proc4 --> outD outA --> outE outB --> outE outC --> outE outD --> outE style inpA fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style inpB fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style inpC fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style inpD fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style inpE fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style proc1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style proc2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style proc3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style proc4 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style outA fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style outB fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style outC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style outD fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style outE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Capacity Benchmark Decision Matrix The following Mermaid graph provides a decision-support schematic that an energy auditor follows once the capacity utilization values are known. ```mermaid graph TD start["Compute Capacity Utilization CU percent"] q1{"CU greater than 95 percent"} q2{"CU between 80 and 95"} q3{"CU between 60 and 80"} q4{"CU less than 60"} r1["Plant is at capacity ceiling. Plan de-bottlenecking or capacity expansion."] r2["Healthy operation. Focus on Specific Energy Consumption optimization."] r3["Underutilized. Investigate downtime causes and shift scheduling."] r4["Severely underutilized. Recommend right sizing shutdown of idle assets."] start --> q1 start --> q2 start --> q3 start --> q4 q1 --> r1 q2 --> r2 q3 --> r3 q4 --> r4 style start fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style r1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 style r2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style r3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style r4 fill:#FF4500,stroke:#333 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Capacity Utilization. State its significance in energy auditing. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Capacity Utilization (CU)** is the ratio of actual output of a plant or equipment to its Maximum Continuous Rating (MCR), expressed as a percentage. $$\text{CU} = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{MCR}} \times 100\%$$ **Significance:** 1. It reveals whether the installed capital is being effectively used or lying idle. 2. A low CU (< 60 %) indicates scope for **right-sizing** the plant or merging loads. 3. A very high CU (> 95 %) signals a **bottleneck** and need for capacity augmentation. 4. It is the first KPI computed in any energy audit to decide further detailed investigations. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** and **Utilization Factor (UF)**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Basis | PLF | UF | |-------|-----|-----| | Denominator Time Base | Calendar hours of the period | Actual running hours only | | Formula | $\dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{MCR}} \times 100$ | $\dfrac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{running}}}$ | | Includes Idle Hours | Yes | No | | Indicates | How heavily loaded the plant is on average | How efficiently the plant operates when running | --- ## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has internal choice. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Define the terms *Installed Capacity*, *Rated Capacity*, and *Maximum Continuous Rating* with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A diesel generator set has a rated capacity of **500 kVA** at 0.8 power factor. During an audit month of 30 days, it ran for **250 hours** and generated **80,000 kWh**. Compute **(i)** Capacity Factor, **(ii)** Utilization Factor, **(iii)** Average Load in kW, and **(iv)** recommend a suitable action if the rating appears excessive. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) **1. Installed Capacity (3 marks):** It is the maximum rated output of all equipment as erected and commissioned at the site, before any derating. Example: A boiler with three burners each rated 5 tonne/h has an installed capacity of **15 tonne/h**. **2. Rated Capacity (2 marks):** It is the manufacturer's nameplate output under standard test conditions (e.g., 25 °C ambient, sea-level, ISO conditions). Example: A chiller nameplate of **100 TR** at ARI conditions. **3. Maximum Continuous Rating — MCR (2 marks):** The highest output that the equipment can sustain continuously **24 × 7 × 365** without compromising safety or service life. Example: A steam turbine with MCR of **20 MW** under ISO conditions. #### Model Solution — (b) **Given:** - $C_{\text{rated}} = 500 \text{ kVA} \times 0.8 \text{ pf} = 400 \text{ kW}$ - $t_{\text{period}} = 720 \text{ h}$ (30 days) - $t_{\text{running}} = 250 \text{ h}$ - $E_{\text{actual}} = 80{,}000 \text{ kWh}$ **(i) Capacity Factor** $$\begin{aligned} \text{CF} &= \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{period}}} \\ &= \frac{80{,}000}{400 \times 720} \\ &= \frac{80{,}000}{2{,}88{,}000} \\ &= 0.278 = 27.8\% \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Utilization Factor** $$\begin{aligned} \text{UF} &= \frac{80{,}000}{400 \times 250} \\ &= \frac{80{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \\ &= 0.80 = 80\% \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Average Load** $$\text{Average Load} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{running}}} = \frac{80{,}000}{250} = 320 \text{ kW}$$ **(iv) Recommendation (1 mark):** The DG set is rated at 400 kW but averages only 320 kW (80 % UF), running only 250 hours/month. The plant is **severely oversized**; right-sizing to a **250–300 kVA** DG set is recommended for higher efficiency at partial load. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the importance of determining **Spare Capacity** and **Capacity Factor** in an energy audit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A boiler has MCR of **8 tonne/h** of steam at 10 bar. During a 24-hour test it produced **168 tonnes**. The plant operates **2 shifts × 300 days/year** and consumes **12,000 kWh/day**. Specific steam consumption of the process is **2.5 tonne/tonne of product**. Determine **(i)** Capacity Utilization, **(ii)** Annual Steam Output, **(iii)** Annual Production in tonnes, and **(iv)** Annual Specific Energy Consumption in kWh/tonne. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) **Spare Capacity (3.5 marks):** - Defined as: $\text{Spare Capacity} = \text{MCR} - \text{Operating Capacity}$. - Indicates **how much additional load** the equipment can absorb before becoming a bottleneck. - Helps decide whether capital expansion is needed or merely better load scheduling will suffice. - A high spare capacity (> 30 %) suggests **right-sizing opportunity**. **Capacity Factor (3.5 marks):** - Defined as: $\text{CF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{period}}}$. - Quantifies how much of the time the equipment operated at full rated output. - A low CF (< 30 %) despite good UF shows **insufficient running hours** — typical of standby DG sets. - A high CF (> 70 %) with low UF shows **partial loading** — poor efficiency in boilers, motors, DG sets. #### Model Solution — (b) **Given:** - MCR = 8 t/h - 24-h production = 168 t → hourly output = 7 t/h - Operating days = 300, shifts = 2 (assumed 8 h each → 16 h/day running) **(i) Capacity Utilization** $$\text{CU} = \frac{7}{8} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$ **(ii) Annual Steam Output** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Daily Output} &= 168 \text{ t} \\ \text{Annual Output} &= 168 \times 300 = 50{,}400 \text{ t/year} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Annual Production** $$\text{Annual Production} = \frac{50{,}400}{2.5} = 20{,}160 \text{ t/year}$$ **(iv) Annual SEC** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Annual Energy} &= 12{,}000 \times 300 = 36{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh} \\ \text{SEC} &= \frac{36{,}00{,}000}{20{,}160} = 178.57 \text{ kWh/tonne} \end{aligned}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing PLF with UF** — they use *different denominators* (calendar hours vs. running hours). One wrong substitution = full 7 marks lost in Part B. > 2. **Forgetting power factor** when kVA is given. Always convert: $\text{kW} = \text{kVA} \times \text{pf}$. > 3. **Unit mismatch** — mixing kW with kWh without multiplying/dividing by hours. Always write units at every step. > 4. **Annual Operating Hours = 8760** is wrong for plants running only 2 shifts × 300 days. Correctly compute: $16 \text{ h/day} \times 300 = 4800 \text{ h/year}$. > 5. **Spare Capacity ≠ Utilisation Factor** — examiners specifically test this distinction. Do not write UF in answer to a Spare Capacity question. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Capacity** is a *benchmark*, not an *output*. The output is what is measured; capacity is what is compared against. - The hierarchy is **Installed → Rated → MCR → Operating → Demonstrated** (each step is lower). - **Capacity Utilization (CU)** uses MCR in the denominator and tells you how close the plant is running to its safe limit. - **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** uses Average Load over *calendar* hours — best for grid-connected plants. - **Utilization Factor (UF)** uses *running* hours only — best for captive DG sets and standby equipment. - **Capacity Factor (CF)** expresses energy produced as a fraction of maximum possible energy in the period — dimensionless. - **Spare Capacity = MCR − Operating Capacity**; reported in **kW** or **tonne/h**. - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = Energy Input ÷ Product Output; the unit is **kWh/tonne** (or **MJ/kg**). - **Right-sizing** is recommended when CU < 60 %; **de-bottlenecking** when CU > 95 %. - Always **show units in every step** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. - BEE benchmarks: **Boiler efficiency ≥ 85 %**, **Compressor SEC ≤ 0.06 kWh/Nm³**, **Lighting ≤ 1 W/ft²**. - For SI conversion, remember: **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal**. - **CF ≤ UF** always holds true, because $t_{\text{period}} \geq t_{\text{running}}$. - When average load equals MCR, **PLF = 100 %**; this rarely happens in real plants due to inherent operational variability.

topics.title: Factors Affecting Refrigeration and Air Conditioning System Performance and Savings Opportunities
Classification and Advantages of Waste Heat Recovery System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification and Advantages of Waste Heat Recovery System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR) System** is an engineered arrangement of equipment and processes designed to capture, transfer, and reuse the thermal energy that is generated as an unavoidable by-product of industrial operations, combustion processes, or chemical reactions, which would otherwise be discharged into the environment without performing any useful work.

In the context of the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** and the **Patron National Energy Productivity Framework**, waste heat is formally defined as:

> **Waste Heat** = The heat rejected from any process at a temperature higher than the ambient (surrounding) temperature, possessing no immediate useful purpose in its original location, but thermodynamically capable of being recovered and utilized elsewhere.

### 1.2 Quantitative Foundation (KTU High-Yield Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Constants (Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Ambient (Reference) Temperature:** **$T_0 = 25^\circ C$ (298 K)**
> - **High-Temperature Threshold:** $> \mathbf{650^\circ C}$
> - **Medium-Temperature Range:** $\mathbf{230^\circ C - 650^\circ C}$
> - **Low-Temperature Threshold:** $< \mathbf{230^\circ C}$
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = \mathbf{8.314\ J/(mol\cdot K)}$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cup of Tea Principle"

Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle on a gas stove. Once the water has boiled, you turn off the burner, but the **stove body, the kettle base, and the steam escaping the spout** are all extremely hot. A KTU engineering student who understands WHR would say: *"Why waste all that thermal energy? I could place a small pot of rice on the still-hot stove (a *recuperator* concept), or I could channel the steam through a small radiator to warm the kitchen (a *heat pipe* concept)."*

The industrial equivalent is exactly this: instead of letting **flue gases at 300°C** escape up a chimney, we route them through a **heat exchanger** to preheat the incoming combustion air, feed water, or process fluid. **Energy that was being thrown away is now being put back to work.**

### 1.4 The Strategic Importance in KTU 2024 Module 3

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In typical Indian industries, **20%–50% of the total energy input** is lost as waste heat. KTU's 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes WHR because it represents the **single largest "low-hanging fruit"** in any energy audit — meaning the **highest ROI (Return on Investment)** opportunity without major process redesign.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Logic of Waste Heat Recovery

The entire philosophy of WHR rests on a single, elegant principle: **The Second Law of Thermodynamics does not forbid heat transfer from a hot source — it only forbids spontaneous flow from cold to hot.** Waste heat, by definition, is at a temperature *higher than the surroundings*, so it can be tapped without violating any natural law.

### 2.2 Classification of Waste Heat Recovery Systems (KTU Board Favourite)

WHR systems are classified along **four independent axes**. KTU questions frequently test the ability to identify *which* classification is being discussed.

#### **Axis 1: Classification Based on Temperature of Waste Heat**

| Category | Temperature Range | Typical Industrial Source | Recovery Feasibility |
|----------|------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **High-Temperature WHR** | $> 650^\circ C$ | Blast furnace gas, steel re-heat furnaces, cement kilns | Highest (can generate steam/power) |
| **Medium-Temperature WHR** | $230^\circ C - 650^\circ C$ | Boiler flue gas, diesel exhaust, process steam vents | High (steam generation, air preheating) |
| **Low-Temperature WHR** | $< 230^\circ C$ | Hot process water, HVAC exhaust, condenser outlets | Moderate (preheating, space heating) |

#### **Axis 2: Classification Based on the Recovery Equipment (Technology)**

This is the **most frequently asked classification in KTU examinations**. There are **six primary device categories**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Pillars of WHR Equipment (Mnemonic: "HR-PEWA-R")**
> **H**eat Exchangers (Recuperators) → **R**egenerators (Heat Wheels) → **P**eat Pipes → **E**conomizers → **W**aste Heat Boilers → **A**ir Preheaters → **R**un-Around Coils

1. **Recuperators (Direct-Contact Heat Exchangers):** Waste gas flows on one side, fresh process fluid on the other, separated by a metallic wall. Heat transfers through conduction. *Example: Boiler economizer section.*

2. **Regenerators (Heat Wheels / Thermal Storage Wheels):** A rotating matrix absorbs heat from the hot waste stream, then releases it to the cold incoming stream as the wheel rotates. *Example: Rotary heat exchanger in HVAC systems.*

3. **Heat Pipes:** Sealed tubes containing a working fluid (e.g., water, ammonia) that evaporates at the hot end and condenses at the cold end, transferring heat with near-zero power input. *Example: Flue gas heat recovery in chemical plants.*

4. **Waste Heat Boilers (WHB):** A specialized boiler that uses hot flue gas to generate steam, which can drive a turbine or supply process heating. *Example: Coke oven gas WHB in steel plants.*

5. **Economizers:** Specifically recover heat from boiler flue gas to preheat the boiler feedwater, raising its temperature before it enters the steam drum. *Example: Lancashire boiler economizer.*

6. **Air Preheaters (APH):** Recover heat from flue gas to preheat combustion air supplied to the burner, improving furnace efficiency dramatically. *Example: Ljungström air preheater.*

#### **Axis 3: Classification Based on Phase of Working Fluid**

| Transfer Type | Mechanism | Common Application |
|---------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Gas-to-Gas** | Sensible heat exchange between two gas streams | Air preheaters, flue gas-to-combustion air |
| **Gas-to-Liquid** | Waste gas heats a liquid stream | Economizers, waste heat boilers |
| **Liquid-to-Liquid** | Two liquid streams exchange heat | Process-to-process heat recovery |
| **Condensing (Latent)** | Recovery of latent heat from vapour condensation | Steam trap recovery, vapour recompression |

#### **Axis 4: Classification Based on Industrial Source**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *"List any five sources of waste heat in an industrial plant."*

- **Combustion Systems:** Furnaces, kilns, boilers, gas turbines, IC engines
- **Process Industries:** Cement (clinker cooler), steel (blast furnace, coke oven), glass, ceramics
- **Chemical Plants:** Exothermic reactor cooling, distillation column overheads
- **Incinerators & Refineries:** FCC regenerators, ethylene crackers
- **HVAC & Building Systems:** Ventilation exhaust, refrigeration condensers

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Energy Management Module 3**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat recovery from a fluid stream | kW |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat recovery from a condensing vapour | kW |
| 3 | $\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{Q_{recovered}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | WHR system efficiency | % |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \dot{V}}{3600}$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 5 | $P_{saved} = Q_{recovered} \times t \times C_f$ | Annual monetary savings from WHR | ₹/year |
| 6 | $CO_2_{reduced} = E_{saved} \times 0.82$ | CO₂ emission reduction factor (India grid average) | tCO₂/yr |
| 7 | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{T_{in} + T_{out}}{2}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD base) | °C or K |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference for heat exchangers | °C or K |
| 9 | $U \cdot A = \dfrac{Q}{\Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient × area | W/K |
| 10 | $PBP = \dfrac{C_{install}}{A_{savings}}$ | Simple Pay Back Period for WHR investment | Years |

> **Where:** $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s), $c_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K), $\Delta T$ = temperature difference, $h_{fg}$ = latent heat (kJ/kg), $Q$ = heat transfer rate (kW), $C_f$ = fuel cost (₹/kWh), $t$ = operating hours/yr, $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), $A$ = heat transfer area (m²).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel Industry (SAIL, Tata Steel):** Top recovery turbine (TRT) recovers energy from blast furnace top gas — a classic **high-temperature WHR** application.
- **Cement Industry (UltraTech, ACC):** Clinker cooler waste heat boilers generate up to **30 kWh/ton of clinker** — among the highest WHR potentials globally.
- **Glass Industry (Saint-Gobain):** Regenerative heat exchangers (Cowper stoves) preheat combustion air up to **1200°C**, cutting fuel use by 25-30%.
- **Automotive (Maruti, Tata Motors):** Thermoelectric generators on exhaust manifolds recover **2-3 kW** per vehicle from waste exhaust heat.
