# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Available Energy (Exergy) from a Waste Heat Stream

This derivation is **extremely high-yield** for KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) questions. It establishes the *theoretical maximum* recoverable energy from a waste stream — the benchmark every real WHR system is judged against.

### **Given:**
A flue gas stream at temperature $T_h$ (K) is discharged into the environment at $T_0 = 298$ K. We want to determine the **maximum possible work** (exergy) recoverable from this stream.

### **Step 1: Define the system and write the First Law energy balance.**

The total energy carried by the flue gas per unit mass is its enthalpy:

$$h = c_p \cdot T$$

The energy balance tells us the *total* heat content, but it does **not** tell us how much is *useful* (convertible to work). This is where the Second Law (Exergy) is required.

### **Step 2: Apply the Second Law — Define Exergy.**

Exergy ($B$) is defined as the **maximum useful work** obtainable as a stream comes to equilibrium with the reference environment.

$$B = (h - h_0) - T_0 \cdot (s - s_0)$$

Here, $h_0$ and $s_0$ are the enthalpy and entropy at the dead state ($T_0$, $P_0$).

### **Step 3: Substitute the ideal gas relations for enthalpy and entropy change.**

For an ideal gas with constant specific heat:

$$h - h_0 = c_p \cdot (T_h - T_0)$$

$$s - s_0 = c_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_h}{T_0}\right) - R \cdot \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$

For an open discharge to atmosphere, $P = P_0$, so the pressure term vanishes:

$$s - s_0 = c_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_h}{T_0}\right)$$

### **Step 4: Substitute into the exergy equation.**

$$B = c_p \cdot (T_h - T_0) - T_0 \cdot c_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_h}{T_0}\right)$$

Factor out $c_p$:

$$B = c_p \cdot \left[(T_h - T_0) - T_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_h}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

### **Step 5: Convert to per unit mass of flue gas (specific exergy).**

$$b = c_p \cdot \left[(T_h - T_0) - T_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_h}{T_0}\right)\right] \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$

This is the **specific exergy** of the waste heat — the absolute maximum work theoretically recoverable per kg of flue gas.

### **Step 6: Final analytical matrix — Worked Numerical Example.**

**Given:** A cement kiln discharges **$1.5\ kg/s$** of flue gas at **$T_h = 873$ K** ($600^\circ C$). Specific heat $c_p = 1.05\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$. Ambient $T_0 = 298$ K. Universal gas constant $R = 8.314\ J/(mol\cdot K)$.

**Step (a) — Compute the temperature ratio:**

$$\frac{T_h}{T_0} = \frac{873}{298} = 2.930$$

**Step (b) — Compute the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(2.930) = 1.0750$$

**Step (c) — Compute the bracket term in the exergy equation:**

$$(T_h - T_0) - T_0 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_h}{T_0}\right)$$

$$(873 - 298) - 298 \times 1.0750$$

$$575 - 320.35 = 254.65\ K$$

**Step (d) — Compute specific exergy:**

$$b = 1.05 \times 254.65 = 267.38\ kJ/kg$$

**Step (e) — Compute total exergy (power) available:**

$$B_{total} = \dot{m} \times b = 1.5 \times 267.38 = 401.07\ kW$$

**Step (f) — Compute the second-law efficiency of a real WHR system.**

If a real waste heat boiler recovers **$Q_{recovered} = 250\ kW$** as useful steam, the second-law efficiency is:

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{Q_{recovered}}{B_{total}} = \frac{250}{401.07} = 62.33\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This derivation transforms a vague "waste heat" concept into a *numerical* engineering decision. The 401 kW exergy figure is the **design ceiling** for any WHR system on this stream. A real plant achieving even **50% of the exergy** is considered to have an excellent recovery system.

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | KTU Interpretation |
|-----------|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Mass flow rate of flue gas | $\dot{m}$ | $1.5\ kg/s$ | Industrial scale indicator |
| Hot stream temperature | $T_h$ | $873\ K\ (600^\circ C)$ | **High-Temperature WHR** |
| Ambient (dead state) | $T_0$ | $298\ K\ (25^\circ C)$ | KTU standard reference |
| Specific heat at constant pressure | $c_p$ | $1.05\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$ | Typical for combustion flue gas |
| Specific exergy | $b$ | $267.38\ kJ/kg$ | Theoretical work potential |
| Total exergy (power) | $B_{total}$ | $401.07\ kW$ | Design ceiling for WHR |
| Actual recovered heat | $Q_{rec}$ | $250\ kW$ | Real plant performance |
| Second-law efficiency | $\eta_{II}$ | $62.33\%$ | Excellent (>50% is top-tier) |


## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Flow of a Typical WHR System (Boiler Flue Gas to Feedwater)

This flowchart shows the **operational flow** of a waste heat boiler economizer arrangement — a classic KTU problem scenario.

```mermaid
graph LR
    furnaceNode["FURNACE 1200 C"] -->|"Hot Flue Gas 600 C"| ecoNode["ECONOMIZER HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    ecoNode -->|"Cool Flue Gas 180 C"| stackNode["STACK TO ATMOSPHERE"]
    
    feedInNode["COLD FEEDWATER 30 C"] -->|"Pump"| ecoNode
    ecoNode -->|"Preheated Feedwater 150 C"| boilerDrumNode["BOILER DRUM"]
    boilerDrumNode -->|"Steam"| turbineNode["TURBINE OR PROCESS USE"]
    
    ecoNode -.->|"Recovered Heat Q equals m dot Cp Delta T"| savingsNode["FUEL SAVINGS AND CO2 REDUCTION"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Matrix for Selecting a WHR Technology

This diagram acts as a **selection guide** — a typical KTU Module 3 question asks *"Which WHR system is suitable for a given temperature range?"*

```mermaid
graph TD
    decStart["START WASTE HEAT AVAILABLE"]
    
    decStart --> checkTemp{"WHAT IS THE WASTE HEAT TEMPERATURE"}
    
    checkTemp -->|"Above 650 C"| branchH["USE WASTE HEAT BOILER OR STEAM TURBINE SYSTEM"]
    checkTemp -->|"230 C to 650 C"| branchM["USE ECONOMIZER OR AIR PREHEATER"]
    checkTemp -->|"Below 230 C"| branchL["USE HEAT PIPE OR HEAT WHEEL OR RUN AROUND COIL"]
    
    branchH --> dev1["RECOVERS HIGH GRADE HEAT FOR POWER GENERATION"]
    branchM --> dev2["RECOVERS MEDIUM GRADE HEAT FOR FEEDWATER OR COMBUSTION AIR"]
    branchL --> dev3["RECOVERS LOW GRADE HEAT FOR PREHEATING OR SPACE HEATING"]
    
    dev1 --> outNode["FINAL OUTPUT STEAM ELECTRICITY OR PROCESS HEAT"]
    dev2 --> outNode
    dev3 --> outNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Learning Tip:** The **higher the temperature**, the **higher the grade of recovery** (e.g., electricity generation is only feasible above ~400°C with current technology). Low-temperature WHR usually justifies itself only when the heat is *very close* to its point of use, to avoid transmission losses.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Waste Heat Recovery (WHR) system. List any two industrial sources of waste heat.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Waste Heat Recovery (WHR) system is an arrangement of equipment and processes that captures the thermal energy discharged as a by-product of industrial operations at a temperature higher than the ambient surroundings and reuses it for productive purposes such as preheating, steam generation, or power generation.
- **Any Two Industrial Sources (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
  1. Flue gases from boilers and furnaces (combustion processes).
  2. Hot exhaust gases from internal combustion engines and gas turbines.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Recuperator and a Regenerator with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

| Feature | Recuperator | Regenerator |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| **Heat Transfer Mode** | Continuous, simultaneous flow of hot and cold streams separated by a wall | Cyclic — matrix absorbs heat, then releases it in a separate phase |
| **Construction** | Stationary tubes/plates | Rotating matrix or fixed beds with valves |
| **Example** | Economizer, air preheater (gas-gas type) | Cowper stove, rotary heat wheel |
| **Mark Awarded** | 1.5 | 1.5 |

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Definition marks** (usually 2 of 14) are lost if students write vague answers like *"WHR is saving heat"* instead of formally defining it.
> 2. **Numerical problems** demand *units at every step* — missing units = 0.5–1 mark lost per sub-part.
> 3. **Diagrams** for classification questions must include *all four axes* or examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Exergy problems** (like the one below) require the *LMTD equation* and the *natural log* step shown explicitly — skipping steps costs 2 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**A (a) [7 Marks]:** Classify Waste Heat Recovery systems based on **(i)** temperature of waste heat, and **(ii)** the type of recovery equipment. For each equipment type, mention one typical industrial application.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**(i) Classification based on temperature (3.5 marks):**

| Category | Range | Industrial Source |
|----------|-------|-------------------|
| High-Temperature WHR | $> 650^\circ C$ | Blast furnace, cement kiln, steel re-heat furnace |
| Medium-Temperature WHR | $230^\circ C - 650^\circ C$ | Boiler flue gas, IC engine exhaust, process steam vent |
| Low-Temperature WHR | $< 230^\circ C$ | Hot water discharge, HVAC exhaust, condenser cooling water |

**(ii) Classification based on equipment (3.5 marks):**

| Equipment Type | Industrial Application |
|----------------|------------------------|
| Recuperator (Heat Exchanger) | Economizer in a fire-tube boiler |
| Regenerator (Heat Wheel) | Air preheater in glass furnace |
| Heat Pipe | Flue gas heat recovery in a chemical reactor |
| Waste Heat Boiler (WHB) | Clinker cooler in cement industry |
| Air Preheater (APH) | Ljungström APH in thermal power station |
| Run-Around Coil | Multiple-zone HVAC heat recovery |

---

**A (b) [7 Marks]:** A boiler discharges **$2.0\ kg/s$** of flue gas at **$T_h = 723\ K$** ($450^\circ C$) to the atmosphere at **$T_0 = 298\ K$**. The specific heat of the flue gas is $c_p = 1.05\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$. The proposed WHR system is an economizer that recovers heat to preheat feedwater, raising the feedwater temperature by **$80^\circ C$**. Calculate:

**(i)** The total heat recovered by the economizer (in kW).

**(ii)** The annual monetary savings (in ₹) if the plant operates 6000 hours/year and the fuel cost is ₹7.00 per kWh of thermal energy.

**(iii)** The annual CO₂ reduction in tonnes, given India's grid emission factor of $0.82\ tCO_2/MWh_{thermal}$.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**(i) Heat recovered (2 marks):**

The heat recovered by the economizer is given by:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{fw} \cdot c_{p,fw} \cdot \Delta T_{fw}$$

Assuming a feedwater mass flow rate of $\dot{m}_{fw} = 1.5\ kg/s$ and $c_{p,fw} = 4.18\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$:

$$Q = 1.5 \times 4.18 \times 80 = 501.6\ kW$$

*(If student uses flue gas side: $Q = 2.0 \times 1.05 \times (450-180) = 567\ kW$. Examiner accepts both as long as the working fluid side is stated.)*

**(ii) Annual monetary savings (3 marks):**

$$P_{saved} = Q \times t \times C_f$$

$$P_{saved} = 501.6 \times 6000 \times 7.00$$

$$P_{saved} = 3,009,600 \times 7.00 = ₹\ 21,067,200\ \text{per year}$$

**≈ ₹ 2.11 Crore per year**

**(iii) Annual CO₂ reduction (2 marks):**

Convert kW to MWh:

$$E_{saved} = 501.6\ kW \times 6000\ h = 3,009,600\ kWh = 3009.6\ MWh$$

Apply emission factor:

$$CO_2_{reduced} = 3009.6 \times 0.82 = 2467.87\ tCO_2/\text{year}$$

**≈ 2468 tonnes of CO₂ avoided annually**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**B (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **six major equipment types** used in waste heat recovery systems. State the working principle and one application of each.

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 1.0–1.2 marks per equipment):**

1. **Recuperator (Heat Exchanger):** Hot and cold fluids separated by a metallic wall; heat transfers by conduction. *Application: Economizer in a Lancashire boiler.*

2. **Regenerator (Heat Wheel):** A rotating porous matrix absorbs heat from the hot stream in one half-cycle and releases it to the cold stream in the other half-cycle. *Application: Cowper stove in a steel plant for blast furnace air preheating.*

3. **Heat Pipe:** A sealed evacuated tube with a wick and working fluid; fluid evaporates at the hot end, vapour migrates to the cold end, condenses, and returns via capillary action. *Application: Flue gas-to-air heat recovery in petrochemical heaters.*

4. **Waste Heat Boiler (WHB):** A boiler in which the energy source is hot flue gas or process gas rather than a fired burner; generates steam for process or power use. *Application: Cement plant clinker cooler WHB producing ~30 kWh/ton of clinker.*

5. **Air Preheater (APH):** Recovers heat from flue gas to preheat combustion air supplied to the furnace, improving combustion efficiency. *Application: Ljungström rotary APH in a 500 MW thermal power station.*

6. **Run-Around Coil:** A closed-loop heat recovery system with two coils (exhaust-side and supply-side) connected by a pumped fluid loop; useful when the two streams are physically distant. *Application: Multi-zone commercial building ventilation heat recovery.*

---

**B (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss any **five significant advantages** of implementing a Waste Heat Recovery system in an industrial plant. Support your answer with **one quantitative benefit** for each advantage.

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 1.4 marks per advantage):**

| # | Advantage | Quantitative Support |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Reduction in Fuel Consumption** | A 20% reduction in fuel use is typical in a well-designed WHR system, saving 2–6% of total plant energy cost. |
| 2 | **Lower Operating Cost** | As computed in Question A(b) above, a 500 kW economizer saves ≈ ₹2.1 crore/year in fuel cost for a 6000 hr/yr operation. |
| 3 | **Reduction in Greenhouse Gas Emissions** | The same economizer avoids ≈ 2468 tonnes of CO₂ per year, supporting India's net-zero 2070 commitment. |
| 4 | **Improved Overall Plant Efficiency** | A boiler's thermal efficiency can rise from 75% to 88% by adding an economizer and air preheater — a 13 percentage point gain. |
| 5 | **Conservation of Natural Resources** | For every 1 kWh of waste heat recovered, 0.25–0.30 kg of coal is conserved, extending the life of finite fossil reserves. |

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — WHR Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Memorize)**

- [x] **Definition:** WHR = capturing heat discharged above ambient and reusing it productively.
- [x] **Temperature Bands:** High ($>650^\circ C$) / Medium ($230-650^\circ C$) / Low ($<230^\circ C$).
- [x] **Six Equipment Types** (Mnemonic: **HR-PEWA-R**): Heat Exchangers, Regenerators, Heat Pipes, Economizers, Waste Heat Boilers, Air Preheaters, Run-Around Coils.
- [x] **Phase Classification:** Gas-Gas, Gas-Liquid, Liquid-Liquid, Latent (Condensing).
- [x] **Master Equation (Sensible Heat):** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.
- [x] **Master Equation (Latent Heat):** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$.
- [x] **Exergy Equation (must memorize):** $b = c_p \cdot [(T_h - T_0) - T_0 \cdot \ln(T_h/T_0)]$.
- [x] **LMTD Equation (for heat exchanger design):** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$.
- [x] **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 298$ K (always used in KTU problems).
- [x] **India CO₂ Emission Factor:** $0.82\ tCO_2/MWh_{thermal}$ (memorize for numericals).
- [x] **Payback Formula:** $PBP = C_{install} / A_{savings}$ (typical WHR payback in India: 6–18 months).
- [x] **Top Industrial Sources of WHR:** Cement kilns, steel furnaces, glass works, IC engines, chemical reactors.
- [x] **Golden Rule:** *Higher waste-heat temperature → Higher grade of recovery → Higher exergy → More cost-effective WHR system.*
- [x] **Examiner's Note:** Always specify the **working fluid**, **mass flow rate**, and **units** in numerical answers to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Waste Heat Recovery for Energy Saving Opportunities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Waste Heat Recovery for Energy Saving Opportunities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)** is the process of capturing and reusing the thermal energy that is otherwise discharged as a by-product of industrial, commercial, or domestic processes to the environment, instead of allowing it to escape uselessly. The recovered heat can be used either directly (preheating combustion air, feed water, or process fluids) or indirectly (converted into mechanical/electrical work via thermodynamic cycles), thereby improving the overall **energy efficiency** of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Waste Heat Recovery is the recovery of heat contained in the discharged hot gases, liquids, or solids from industrial processes, classified according to temperature range and quantified via specific heat calculations.*

### 1.2 Sources of Waste Heat

Waste heat is generated in almost every energy conversion process:

- **Hot gaseous streams:** Flue gases from boilers, furnaces, kilns, IC engines, gas turbines
- **Hot liquid streams:** Cooling tower blowdown, condenser water, spent process liquids
- **Vapors and steam:** Blow-down steam, exhaust steam, leakage steam
- **Convective and radiant heat:** From hot equipment surfaces, ducts, and pipework
- **Solid waste heat:** Slag, clinker, hot products from metallurgical processes

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Morning Tea Kettle**
> 
> Imagine you are boiling water for morning tea. Once the water has boiled, the kettle continues to radiate heat for many minutes, but you just let it cool on the counter — that heat is "wasted." In a smart kitchen, you would place the hot kettle inside an insulated box (a **heat exchanger**), and the residual heat would warm a dish sitting on top. That is exactly what industry does with **Waste Heat Recovery** — instead of letting exhaust heat vanish into the sky, it is captured and redirected to perform useful work, such as preheating cold water entering a boiler.

### 1.4 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The fundamental quantity governing WHR analysis is **Heat Content (Q)**:

$$Q = m \times C_p \times \Delta T$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat content / heat recoverable in **kW** or **kJ/s**
- $m$ = Mass flow rate of waste stream in **kg/s**
- $C_p$ = Specific heat capacity in **kJ/(kg·K)**
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference between waste stream and target process temperature in **°C** or **K**

Standard Metrics used in WHR Analysis:
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**
- **Net Present Value (NPV)**
- **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**
- **CO₂ Reduction (tons/year)**
- **Waste Heat Recovery Efficiency (η_WHR)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Waste Heat by Temperature Range

Waste heat is broadly classified by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** and the **UNIDO WHR Manual** into three temperature bands, because available recovery technology depends strongly on the temperature level:

| Class | Temperature Range | Typical Sources | Recoverable? |
|:------|:------------------|:----------------|:-------------|
| **High-Temperature** | > 650 °C | Furnaces, cement kilns, steel arc furnaces, glass tanks | Yes — strong potential |
| **Medium-Temperature** | 230 °C – 650 °C | Boiler flue gases, steam exhausts, IC engine exhausts | Yes — moderate potential |
| **Low-Temperature** | < 230 °C | Drying, cleaning, steam condensate, cooling tower water | Marginal / need heat pump |

### 2.2 Categories of Waste Heat Recovery Systems

Recovery systems are categorized by the **principle of operation**:

- **Direct Recovery (Open-loop):** Hot stream and cold stream contact physically — e.g., direct contact preheaters, exhaust gas economizers.
- **Indirect Recovery (Closed-loop via surface):** Heat transferred through a separating wall — e.g., shell-and-tube heat exchangers, plate heat exchangers.
- **Regenerative Recovery:** Heat stored in a matrix (e.g., ceramic balls) in one phase and released in the next — e.g., regenerators, Ljungström air preheaters.
- **Conversion-based Recovery:** Heat converted to other energy forms — e.g., Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC), Kalina cycle, thermoelectric generators.

### 2.3 The "Why" — Energy Recovery Logic

1. **Why recover waste heat?** Fuel cost is the largest operating expense in energy-intensive industries. A 1% rise in boiler efficiency can cut fuel bills by ~1.5%.
2. **How is it quantified?** By computing the recoverable heat content using $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$, then translating this into **fuel savings**, **money saved**, and **CO₂ avoided**.
3. **When is recovery feasible?** When the recovered heat can be used at a temperature greater than the **Pinch Temperature** of the receiving process.

### 2.4 Pinch Analysis — The Heart of WHR Targeting

**Pinch Analysis** is a thermodynamic methodology that identifies the maximum achievable heat recovery in a process. The key concept is the **Pinch Point** — the location in the heat-exchange network where the temperature difference between hot and cold streams is at a minimum (typically **ΔT_min = 10 °C** for a process, **20 °C** for a refinery).

| Pinch Concept | Engineering Meaning |
|:--------------|:--------------------|
| Above Pinch | Heat sources — should reject no heat |
| Below Pinch | Heat sinks — should receive no heat |
| At the Pinch | Avoid cross-pinch heat transfer |
| ΔT_min | Trade-off between energy vs. capital cost |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table — it is the most-tested segment in KTU ESE for WHR questions.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------|:------|:------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat recovery | kW | Recoverable heat from stream |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{in} - h_{out})$ | Enthalpic heat (steam/gas) | kW | Flue gas / steam recovery |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{Q \times H}{GCV \times \eta_{boiler}}$ | Annual fuel saving | kg/yr | Boiler efficiency gain |
| 4 | $\eta_{WHR} = \dfrac{Q_{recovered}}{Q_{input}}$ | WHR efficiency | % | System performance |
| 5 | $SPP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{A_{saving}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years | Project feasibility |
| 6 | $CO_2 = S \times F_{CO_2}$ | Annual CO₂ avoided | ton/yr | Environmental impact |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temp. Difference | °C | Heat exchanger sizing |
| 8 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$ | Heat exchanger area | m² | Capital estimation |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{V \times \rho}{3600}$ | Volumetric to mass flow | kg/s | From flue gas meter data |
| 10 | $C_p^{mix} = \sum y_i C_{p,i}$ | Mixture specific heat | kJ/kg·K | Combustion gas properties |

Where $F_{CO_2}$ is the **carbon emission factor** (≈ 3.16 ton CO₂/ton of oil, 2.66 ton CO₂/ton of coal, 1.83 ton CO₂/ton of NG).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** ORC plants on cement kiln exhausts, biomass exhausts.
- **Process Industry:** Preheating combustion air in furnaces, regenerators in glass industry.
- **Marine & Automobiles:** Turbochargers, EGR systems, bottoming cycles.
- **Building Services:** Heat recovery ventilators, heat recovery wheels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Recoverable Heat from a Combustion Flue Gas

A boiler burns **fuel** producing flue gas at a certain temperature $T_g$, leaving via a stack at temperature $T_s$. The ambient (inlet combustion air) is at $T_a$. The recoverable heat is the portion of enthalpy between the stack temperature and the useful lower limit.

**Step 1:** Identify the mass flow rate of flue gas.

$$\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_f \times AF$$

where $\dot{m}_f$ is the fuel mass flow rate and $AF$ is the air–fuel ratio (by mass).

**Step 2:** Compute the sensible heat of the flue gas above the reference (ambient) temperature.

$$Q_{sens} = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_{p,g} \cdot (T_s - T_a)$$

This is the **gross heat content** in the waste gas.

**Step 3:** Compute the actual recoverable heat by subtracting the **minimum approach temperature** $\Delta T_{min}$ needed for the heat exchanger to work.

$$Q_{recoverable} = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_{p,g} \cdot (T_s - T_a - \Delta T_{min})$$

**Step 4:** Translate this into fuel savings per hour.

$$\dot{m}_{fuel,saved} = \dfrac{Q_{recoverable}}{GCV \times \eta_{boiler}}$$

**Step 5:** Annual saving by multiplying with operating hours.

$$S_{annual} = \dot{m}_{fuel,saved} \times H_{annual}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A furnace exhausts 2.5 kg/s of flue gas at 480 °C. The combustion air enters at 30 °C. The average specific heat of flue gas is **1.05 kJ/(kg·K)**, minimum approach ΔT = 50 °C. The boiler efficiency is 80%, and the fuel has a GCV of 42,000 kJ/kg. The plant operates 7200 hours/year. Determine:

(i) Hourly heat recovery
(ii) Annual fuel saving
(iii) Annual monetary saving if fuel cost is ₹ 8/kg
(iv) Annual CO₂ reduction (use $F_{CO_2}$ = 3.0 ton CO₂/ton of fuel)

#### Step 1 — Heat Recovery Rate

$$Q_{rec} = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_p \cdot (T_s - T_a - \Delta T_{min})$$

Substituting all known values:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{rec} &= 2.5 \, \tfrac{kg}{s} \times 1.05 \, \tfrac{kJ}{kg \cdot K} \times (480 - 30 - 50) \, K \\
&= 2.5 \times 1.05 \times 400 \\
&= 1050 \, \tfrac{kJ}{s} \\
&= 1050 \, kW = 1.05 \, MW
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2 — Hourly Fuel Saving

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel} &= \dfrac{Q_{rec}}{GCV \times \eta_{boiler}} \\
&= \dfrac{1050 \, kW}{42000 \, \tfrac{kJ}{kg} \times 0.80} \\
&= \dfrac{1050}{33600} \\
&= 0.03125 \, \tfrac{kg}{s}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to per hour:

$$0.03125 \times 3600 = 112.5 \, \tfrac{kg}{hr}$$

#### Step 3 — Annual Fuel Saving

$$S_{annual} = 112.5 \times 7200 = 810{,}000 \, \tfrac{kg}{yr} = 810 \, \tfrac{tonnes}{yr}$$

#### Step 4 — Annual Monetary Saving

$$Money_{saved} = 810{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 64{,}80{,}000 \text{ per year} = \text{₹ } 64.8 \, \text{lakh/yr}$$

#### Step 5 — Annual CO₂ Reduction

$$CO_2 = 810 \times 3.0 = 2430 \, \tfrac{tonnes}{yr}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for WHR Technology Selection

| Parameter | Recuperator (Gas–Gas) | Regenerator (Cyclical) | Economizer (Flue gas–Water) | ORC (Power Gen.) | Heat Pump (Low-grade) |
|:----------|:----------------------|:-----------------------|:----------------------------|:-----------------|:----------------------|
| **Temp Range** | 200 – 1000 °C | 400 – 1500 °C | 100 – 500 °C | 150 – 600 °C | < 200 °C |
| **Heat Duty** | High | Very high | High | High | Low |
| **Recovery %** | 60 – 80 | 70 – 90 | 50 – 75 | 8 – 25 (electrical) | 300 – 500 (COP) |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate | High | Low | Very high | High |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Moderate | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| **KTU Typical Application** | Air-preheater in furnaces | Ljungström in glass & cement | Boiler feed-water heating | Cement/biomass plants | Building HVAC, drying |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For low-grade heat (< 200 °C), direct heat exchange is often uneconomical. The standard KTU approach is to deploy a **Vapor Absorption Refrigeration** or **Heat Pump** to "lift" the temperature, raising it to a usable level — the higher Coefficient of Performance (COP 3–5) often justifies the higher capital cost.

### 3.4 Pinch Analysis — Worked Sketch

Consider two streams:
- **Hot stream (flue gas):** 10 kg/s, cooling from **500 °C → 200 °C**, $C_p$ = 1.0 kJ/kg·K
- **Cold stream (combustion air):** 5 kg/s, heating from **30 °C → 250 °C**, $C_p$ = 1.0 kJ/kg·K

**Heat available from hot stream:**

$$Q_H = 10 \times 1.0 \times (500 - 200) = 3000 \, kW$$

**Heat required by cold stream:**

$$Q_C = 5 \times 1.0 \times (250 - 30) = 1100 \, kW$$

Since $Q_H \gg Q_C$, the **Pinch Pin** lies on the hot-stream side, and the maximum recoverable heat is **1100 kW**, limited by the cold-stream demand. The cold stream leaves at the pinch while the hot stream still retains $3000 - 1100 = 1900$ kW of waste, which must be dumped.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Industrial WHR System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the entire waste heat recovery chain from a high-temperature industrial source to the multiple end-use applications. The architecture mirrors real cement, steel, and thermal power plant layouts used in **KTU industrial case studies**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Process Source] --> B[High Temperature Waste Stream]
    A1[Furnace Flue Gas 500 to 900 C] --> B
    A2[IC Engine Exhaust 300 to 600 C] --> B
    A3[Kiln Exit Gas 200 to 1000 C] --> B

    B --> C{Waste Heat Class?}
    C -->|Class I High 650 to 1000 C| D[Heat Recovery Boiler]
    C -->|Class II Medium 230 to 650 C| E[Economizer or Air Preheater]
    C -->|Class III Low under 230 C| F[Heat Pump or ORC Module]

    D --> G[Superheated Steam]
    D --> H[Process Steam Generation]
    D --> I[Organic Rankine Cycle ORC]

    E --> J[Hot Combustion Air to Furnace]
    E --> K[Preheated Boiler Feedwater]
    E --> L[Space Heating or Drying Load]

    F --> M[Upgraded Hot Water via Heat Pump]
    F --> N[Electrical Power via ORC Turbine]
    F --> O[Vapour Absorption Refrigeration]

    G --> P[End Use Applications]
    H --> P
    I --> P
    J --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P

    P --> Q[Process Heating]
    P --> R[Power Generation]
    P --> S[Building HVAC]
    P --> T[Refrigeration]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow for WHR Technology Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Identify Waste Heat Stream] --> B0{Measured Temperature T}
    B0 -->|T greater than 650 C| C0[High Temp Zone]
    B0 -->|230 to 650 C| D0[Medium Temp Zone]
    B0 -->|T less than 230 C| E0[Low Temp Zone]

    C0 --> F0[Recuperator or Waste Heat Boiler]
    C0 --> G0[ORC Bottoming Cycle for Power]

    D0 --> H0[Economizer for Feedwater Heating]
    D0 --> I0[Air Preheater for Combustion Air]
    D0 --> J0[Plate or Shell Tube Heat Exchanger]

    E0 --> K0{Quantity of Heat Available}
    K0 -->|Large continuous| L0[Heat Pump with High COP]
    K0 -->|Small intermittent| M0[Reject or Low Grade Reuse]
```

### 4.3 Pinch Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1Node[Hot Stream Inlet 500 C] --> A2Node[Hot Side Heat Exchange]
    A2Node --> A3Node[Hot Stream Outlet 200 C]
    A4Node[Cold Stream Inlet 30 C] --> A5Node[Cold Side Heat Exchange]
    A5Node --> A6Node[Cold Stream Outlet 250 C]
    A2Node <--> A5Node
    PNode[Pinch Point at 230 C] -.-> A2Node
    PNode -.-> A5Node
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Reading Note:** The arrow connecting **Hot Side** to **Cold Side** represents the heat-transfer surface (the wall of the heat exchanger). The **Pinch Point** marker is the bottleneck — the temperature gap between hot and cold streams is smallest there, and that gap equals the **ΔT_min** assumed for the network.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
Define Waste Heat Recovery and state its classification based on temperature range.

**Model Answer:**
*Waste Heat Recovery (WHR) is the process of capturing thermal energy from hot streams (gases, liquids, vapours) that would otherwise be discharged to the environment, and reusing it for a useful purpose such as preheating, steam generation, or power production.*
*Classification by Temperature:*
*(a) High-Temperature WHR: Source temperature above 650 °C (furnaces, kilns).*
*(b) Medium-Temperature WHR: Source temperature between 230 °C and 650 °C (boiler flue gas, IC engine exhaust).*
*(c) Low-Temperature WHR: Source temperature below 230 °C (cooling water, condensate, drying exhausts).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]**
What is the role of Pinch Analysis in Waste Heat Recovery?

**Model Answer:**
*Pinch Analysis is a systematic thermodynamic method used to identify the maximum possible heat recovery between hot and cold process streams, while respecting a minimum approach temperature (ΔT_min). It determines the Minimum Heating and Cooling Utility requirements of a process, locates the **Pinch Point** (the location of minimum temperature difference), and provides a quantitative target — the **Energy Target** — for designing the most efficient heat exchanger network (HEN).*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Long-Answer Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]

A boiler exhausts **3.0 kg/s** of flue gas at a temperature of **550 °C**, while the combustion air is available at **35 °C**. The average $C_p$ of the flue gas is **1.05 kJ/(kg·K)**. A waste heat recovery unit is proposed with a minimum approach temperature of **40 °C**.

Given: GCV of fuel = **44,000 kJ/kg**, boiler efficiency = **0.78**, plant operating hours = **7000 hours/year**, fuel cost = **₹ 9.5/kg**, $F_{CO_2}$ = **2.8 ton CO₂/ton fuel**.

**(a) Calculate the hourly and annual heat recovery possible.** (7 Marks)
**(b) Determine the annual fuel saving, monetary saving, and reduction in CO₂ emissions.** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

##### Part (a) — Hourly and Annual Heat Recovery

**Step 1 — Apply the WHR heat equation:**

$$Q_{rec} = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_p \cdot (T_s - T_a - \Delta T_{min})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{rec} &= 3.0 \times 1.05 \times (550 - 35 - 40) \\
&= 3.0 \times 1.05 \times 475 \\
&= 1496.25 \, kW
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hourly energy:**

$$Q_{hourly} = 1496.25 \times 3600 = 5{,}386{,}500 \, \tfrac{kJ}{hr}$$

**Step 4 — Annual heat recovery (over 7000 hours):**

$$Q_{annual} = 5{,}386{,}500 \times 7000 = 3.7706 \times 10^{10} \, \tfrac{kJ}{yr} = 37{,}706 \, GJ/yr$$

---

##### Part (b) — Fuel, Money, and CO₂ Savings

**Step 1 — Hourly fuel saving:**

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{Q_{rec}}{GCV \times \eta_{boiler}} = \dfrac{1496.25}{44000 \times 0.78}$$

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{1496.25}{34320} = 0.0436 \, \tfrac{kg}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to per hour:**

$$0.0436 \times 3600 = 156.96 \, \tfrac{kg}{hr}$$

**Step 3 — Annual fuel saving:**

$$S_{annual} = 156.96 \times 7000 = 1{,}098{,}720 \, \tfrac{kg}{yr} \approx 1098.7 \, \tfrac{tonnes}{yr}$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary saving:**

$$Money_{saved} = 1{,}098{,}720 \times 9.5 = \text{₹ } 1{,}04{,}37{,}840 \approx \text{₹ } 1.04 \, \text{crore/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Annual CO₂ reduction:**

$$CO_2 = 1098.7 \times 2.8 = 3076.4 \, \tfrac{tonnes}{yr}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Alternative Choice]

A furnace in a steel plant discharges **5 kg/s** of flue gas at **750 °C**. Ambient combustion air is at **40 °C**. The $C_p$ of flue gas = **1.10 kJ/(kg·K)**. An air-preheater is proposed with **ΔT_min = 60 °C** for combustion air preheating.

Given: GCV = **38,000 kJ/kg**, η_boiler = **0.75**, hours = **8000 hr/yr**, fuel cost = **₹ 7.5/kg**, $F_{CO_2}$ = **2.5 ton CO₂/ton fuel**.

**(a) Compute the hourly and annual heat recovery from the flue gas.** (7 Marks)
**(b) Compute the annual fuel, monetary, and CO₂ savings.** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

##### Part (a) — Hourly and Annual Heat Recovery

**Step 1 — Apply the WHR heat equation:**

$$Q_{rec} = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_p \cdot (T_s - T_a - \Delta T_{min})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{rec} &= 5 \times 1.10 \times (750 - 40 - 60) \\
&= 5 \times 1.10 \times 650 \\
&= 3575 \, kW
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to per hour:**

$$Q_{hourly} = 3575 \times 3600 = 1.287 \times 10^7 \, \tfrac{kJ}{hr}$$

**Step 4 — Annual heat recovery:**

$$Q_{annual} = 1.287 \times 10^7 \times 8000 = 1.0296 \times 10^{11} \, \tfrac{kJ}{yr} \approx 102{,}960 \, GJ/yr$$

---

##### Part (b) — Fuel, Money, and CO₂ Savings

**Step 1 — Hourly fuel saving:**

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{3575}{38000 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{3575}{28500} = 0.12544 \, \tfrac{kg}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to per hour:**

$$0.12544 \times 3600 = 451.6 \, \tfrac{kg}{hr}$$

**Step 3 — Annual fuel saving:**

$$S_{annual} = 451.6 \times 8000 = 3{,}612{,}800 \, \tfrac{kg}{yr} \approx 3613 \, \tfrac{tonnes}{yr}$$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary saving:**

$$Money_{saved} = 3{,}612{,}800 \times 7.5 = \text{₹ } 2{,}70{,}96{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2.71 \, \text{crore/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Annual CO₂ reduction:**

$$CO_2 = 3613 \times 2.5 = 9032.5 \, \tfrac{tonnes}{yr}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Read This Before You Write the Exam!**
> 
> 1. **Units Pitfall (3 marks lost frequently):** The most common error is using $Q$ in **kJ/hr** but the fuel formula in **kJ/kg** and forgetting to convert. Always state units clearly at every step.
> 2. **ΔT_min Subtract Pitfall (2 marks lost):** Students often compute $Q = \dot{m} C_p (T_s - T_a)$ without subtracting $\Delta T_{min}$. The question specifically says "minimum approach temperature = X °C." Subtract it from the available temperature span.
> 3. **Boiler Efficiency Placement (2 marks lost):** The denominator must be $GCV \times \eta_{boiler}$, **not** $GCV / \eta_{boiler}$. The efficiency multiplies with the heating value because the boiler converts fuel energy to useful heat.
> 4. **Final Rounding:** Always present the answer with **3–4 significant figures** and clearly state the unit (kg/hr, ton/yr, ₹/yr). Don't write "huge number" — examiners want numbers.
> 5. **Pinch Definition Trap:** If a question asks "what is the pinch," don't just say "minimum temperature difference." Mention it in the context of the **HEN design** and **minimum utility target**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This on Your Wall Before the Exam**

- ✅ **WHR = Recovered heat = $\dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_s - T_a - \Delta T_{min})$** in **kW** (or multiply by 3600 for kJ/hr).
- ✅ **Temperature Classification:** High > 650 °C, Medium 230–650 °C, Low < 230 °C.
- ✅ **Fuel Saving Formula:** $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{Q_{rec}}{GCV \times \eta_{boiler}}$ — the efficiency is in the **denominator**.
- ✅ **Pinch Point:** Location of minimum temperature difference in a heat exchange network; sets the ΔT_min for the whole process.
- ✅ **ΔT_min values:** Process industry = 10 °C, Refinery = 20 °C, Cement/Sponge iron = 30–50 °C.
- ✅ **Monetary saving** = Annual fuel saved (kg) × Fuel price (₹/kg).
- ✅ **CO₂ avoided** = Annual fuel saved (ton) × Emission factor (ton CO₂/ton fuel). Values to memorize: Coal ≈ 2.66, Oil ≈ 3.16, Gas ≈ 1.83.
- ✅ **Common WHR Devices (draw these in the exam if asked):** Economizer, Air-preheater, Recuperator, Regenerator, Waste Heat Boiler, ORC, Heat Pump, Plate HX, Shell-and-Tube HX.
- ✅ **Always** convert mass flow from volumetric (m³/hr) to mass flow (kg/s) using $\dot{m} = \dfrac{V \times \rho}{3600}$.
- ✅ **Mix gases:** $C_p^{mix} = \sum y_i C_{p,i}$ — must use this for combustion flue gas in exams.
- ✅ **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is needed only if asked for heat exchanger area — not for basic WHR savings problems.
- ✅ **Time conversions:** 1 year = 8000 hr (2-shift), 7200 hr (shift with maintenance), 8760 hr (continuous). Read the question carefully.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimal Operation of Cogeneration Plants - Case Study
Computer Aided Energy Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Computer Aided Energy Management # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Computer Aided Energy Management (CAEM)** refers to the systematic application of computer-based hardware, software, communication networks, and information technology tools to monitor, control, measure, analyze, and optimize energy consumption across industrial, commercial, and institutional facilities. It is an integrated digital framework that transforms raw energy data into actionable engineering intelligence, enabling continuous energy performance improvement beyond the limits of manual audits. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** CAEM is the application of information technology, control systems, and analytical software for the purpose of continuous energy monitoring, performance benchmarking, load management, and decision support in energy management programs. The core sub-systems that fall under the CAEM umbrella include: - **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** – for real-time remote monitoring and control. - **EMS / EMIS (Energy Management System / Energy Management Information System)** – for centralized data logging, analysis, and reporting. - **BMS (Building Management System)** – specifically for HVAC, lighting, and facility automation. - **M&V Software** – for Measurement & Verification of energy savings as per **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)**. - **Smart Metering & IoT Sensor Networks** – for granular, sub-circuit level data acquisition. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of CAEM as a **"fitness tracker for a building or factory"**. - Just as a smartwatch continuously monitors your heart rate, calories burnt, and sleep cycles, a CAEM system continuously tracks the electrical, thermal, and fuel energy of a facility. - Just as the watch's mobile app converts raw sensor data into a fitness score and recommends workouts, the CAEM software converts raw kWh data into **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**, **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**, and savings recommendations. - Just as you need a doctor to interpret an ECG report, you still need a Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) to interpret CAEM outputs — the system **augments** human expertise, it does not replace it. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world example:** A cement plant installs clamp-on CT sensors on its 200 kW ball mill motor. These sensors feed data every 15 minutes to a cloud-based EMS dashboard. The dashboard reveals that the motor runs idle (no material feed) for 28% of operating hours. Without CAEM, this 28% wastage would never be visible in monthly electricity bills. ## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics & Constants - **Sampling Interval:** typically **1 min / 5 min / 15 min** (IPMVP recommends 15-min intervals for monthly M&V). - **Standard Reference Voltage (India):** **230 V (single phase)**, **415 V (three phase)** at **50 Hz**. - **Data Logger Resolution:** **0.1% of full scale** for Class 0.2S / 0.5S accuracy meters. - **Power Factor Benchmark:** **0.95 lagging** (BEE 5-star industrial rating threshold). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of a Computer Aided Energy Management System A CAEM system is built on a **layered architecture** (often called the **4-layer pyramid**): ### Layer 1 — Field Level (Sensors & Actuators) - **CTs (Current Transformers)**, **PTs (Potential Transformers)**, **Temperature sensors (RTD/Thermocouple)**, **Flow meters**, **Gas analyzers**, **Lux meters**. - Function: Convert physical parameters (kW, °C, m³/h, ppm) into standardized electrical signals (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, Modbus RTU). ### Layer 2 — Data Acquisition Level (Controllers & Data Loggers) - **PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers)**, **RTUs (Remote Terminal Units)**, **Smart meters**, **Dedicated Energy Data Loggers (e.g., Fluke 1730, Hioki PW3198, Schneider PM5000 series)**. - Function: Time-stamp, log, and transmit data to the supervisory level. ### Layer 3 — Communication Level (Network Backbone) - **Wired protocols:** Modbus RTU/TCP, BACnet, PROFIBUS, RS-485. - **Wireless / IoT protocols:** LoRaWAN, Zigbee, Wi-Fi, MQTT, NB-IoT. - Function: Secure, reliable transport of data from field to control room / cloud. ### Layer 4 — Supervisory / Application Level (Software) - **SCADA / HMI software** (real-time mimic diagrams, alarms). - **EMS / EMIS software** (historical trending, KPI dashboards, reports). - **Energy Modeling software** (e.g., **eQUEST, EnergyPRO, RETScreen, HOMER, SimaPro, TRACE 700**). - Function: Data analytics, M&V, predictive load forecasting, automated reporting. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **"Why"** behind layering is **separation of concerns** — the sensor layer is replaceable, the network is scalable, and the analytics layer can be upgraded without touching hardware. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Sensing:** Sensors measure voltage, current, temperature, flow, pressure, and gas composition at strategic points (utility incomer, feeders, sub-panels, individual machines). 2. **Acquisition:** Data loggers or smart meters sample the signals at a fixed interval (e.g., 1-min) and compute derived quantities (kW, kVA, kVAr, kWh, kVAh, PF, Hz, THD). 3. **Transmission:** Data is pushed to a central server or cloud platform using the chosen protocol. 4. **Storage:** Time-series database (e.g., InfluxDB, SQL) stores the data with timestamps for at least **12–24 months** (BEE EA&EM regulation). 5. **Analysis:** Software computes **SEC, EnPI, CUSUM, load factor, demand factor, diversity factor**, and benchmarks against baseline. 6. **Reporting & Decision Support:** Dashboards (KPI cards), automated PDF reports, and alarm notifications (SMS/email) trigger corrective action. 7. **Verification:** Savings are quantified using **M&V Option A / B / C / D** of IPMVP. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Description | Standard Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Electrical energy = Power × Time | kWh | | 2 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{P_{production}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/ton, kWh/unit | | 3 | $CUSUM = \sum (E_{actual,i} - E_{baseline,i})$ | Cumulative Sum for savings tracking | kWh | | 4 | $PF = \dfrac{P}{S} = \cos\phi$ | Power Factor = Real Power / Apparent Power | dimensionless (0–1) | | 5 | $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | Apparent Power (vector sum) | kVA | | 6 | $Q_c = P \times (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | Required capacitor rating for PF correction | kVAr | | 7 | $PF_{new} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Q - Q_c}{P}\right)^2}}$ | Improved power factor after correction | dimensionless | | 8 | $LF = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{P_{max}}$ | Load Factor = Average Load / Peak Load | dimensionless | | 9 | $DF = \dfrac{\sum P_{individual}}{P_{max,simultaneous}}$ | Diversity Factor | dimensionless | | 10 | $\eta_{transformer} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Transformer Efficiency | % | | 11 | $C_{pump} = \dfrac{P_{pump}}{Q \times H \times \rho \times g}$ | Specific energy of pumping | kWh/m³·m | | 12 | $TCO = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_{opex,t}}{(1+r)^t}$ | Total Cost of Ownership (Net Present Value) | ₹ (INR) | | 13 | $SPP = \dfrac{C_{capex}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years | | 14 | $EnPI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{production}}$ | Energy Performance Indicator | kWh/tonne | | 15 | $I_{sd} = I_{full\,load} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{\% \, Load}{100}}$ | Reduced current at part load (motor) | Amps | | 16 | $C_{demand} = MD \times T_{rate}$ | Maximum Demand charge | ₹ | | 17 | $P_{loss} = 3 \times I^2 \times R \times L$ | Three-phase line losses | W | | 18 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling/heating}}{W_{input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (HVAC) | dimensionless | | 19 | $kVAh_{billable} = \dfrac{kWh_{consumed}}{PF_{avg}}$ | Billing demand in kVAh | kVAh | | 20 | $\% \, Savings = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage energy savings | % | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions for PF Correction:** PF must not be corrected to **unity (1.0)** for industrial loads, as it leads to **leading PF** and resonance with transformers. Target is **0.98 to 0.99 lagging**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) from M&V Data The **SEC** is the most commonly used EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator) in KTU board problems. **Statement:** A manufacturing plant produces 8,500 tonnes/month and consumes 2,55,000 kWh/month. Compute (i) SEC, (ii) total annual energy cost at ₹7.50/kWh, (iii) kVAh billed if average PF is 0.82. ### Step 1: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) $$SEC = \frac{E_{consumed}}{P_{production}}$$ $$SEC = \frac{2{,}55{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{8{,}500 \text{ tonnes}} = 30 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$ ### Step 2: Total Annual Energy Cost $$C_{annual} = E_{consumed/month} \times 12 \times \text{Rate}$$ $$C_{annual} = 2{,}55{,}000 \times 12 \times 7.50 = ₹ 2{,}29{,}50{,}000$$ ### Step 3: Billing kVAh from PF $$kVAh_{billed} = \frac{kWh_{consumed}}{PF_{avg}} = \frac{2{,}55{,}000}{0.82} = 3{,}10{,}976 \text{ kVAh}$$ ### Step 4: Recommended kVAr for PF Correction to 0.95 The current reactive energy kVArh: $$Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2} = kWh \times \tan\phi_1 = 2{,}55{,}000 \times \tan(\cos^{-1} 0.82)$$ $$\cos^{-1}(0.82) = 34.91° \Rightarrow \tan(34.91°) = 0.698$$ $$Q_{current} = 2{,}55{,}000 \times 0.698 = 1{,}77{,}990 \text{ kVArh}$$ $$\text{For } PF = 0.95 \Rightarrow \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19° \Rightarrow \tan(18.19°) = 0.328$$ $$Q_{target} = 2{,}55{,}000 \times 0.328 = 83{,}640 \text{ kVArh}$$ $$Q_c \text{ (capacitor required)} = 1{,}77{,}990 - 83{,}640 = 94{,}350 \text{ kVArh/month}$$ $$Q_c \text{ per day} = \frac{94{,}350}{30} = 3145 \text{ kVAr/day (average)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Logic Note:** Capacitor rating is decided by **kVAr demand** (instantaneous), not kVArh (energy). For a 24-hour operation cycle, **kVAr demand ≈ kVArh per hour** = 3,145 kVAr. Standard capacitor banks come in 25/50/100/200 kVAr steps — you would select **16 × 200 kVAr = 3,200 kVAr**. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Energy Saving Quantification (CUSUM Method) | Month | Baseline kWh (predicted) | Actual kWh (measured) | Monthly Savings kWh | Cumulative Savings kWh | |---|---|---|---|---| | Jan | 1,00,000 | 95,000 | 5,000 | 5,000 | | Feb | 1,02,000 | 96,500 | 5,500 | 10,500 | | Mar | 1,05,000 | 98,000 | 7,000 | 17,500 | | Apr | 1,08,000 | 1,00,500 | 7,500 | 25,000 | | May | 1,10,000 | 1,03,000 | 7,000 | 32,000 | | Jun | 1,12,000 | 1,05,000 | 7,000 | 39,000 | **Interpretation:** CUSUM rises **monotonically** if savings are sustained, and **flattens** if savings dissipate (often a sign of regression to baseline). ## 3.3 Derivation: Payback Period using Software-Based LCCA A CAEM audit recommends a **VFD retrofit** for a 75 kW motor. - Capital cost (CapEx) = **₹ 6,50,000** (VFD + installation + commissioning) - Annual operating hours = **5,000 hrs** - Energy saved per hour = **9 kW** (from CAEM logged data) - Tariff = **₹ 8.00/kWh** ### Step 1: Annual Energy Savings $$E_{saved} = 9 \text{ kW} \times 5{,}000 \text{ hrs} = 45{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$ ### Step 2: Annual Monetary Savings $$S_{annual} = 45{,}000 \times 8.00 = ₹ 3{,}60{,}000$$ ### Step 3: Simple Payback Period $$SPP = \frac{C_{capex}}{S_{annual}} = \frac{6{,}50{,}000}{3{,}60{,}000} = 1.81 \text{ years} \approx 1 \text{ year } 10 \text{ months}$$ ### Step 4: Return on Investment over 5 years $$ROI = \frac{(S_{annual} \times 5) - C_{capex}}{C_{capex}} \times 100$$ $$ROI = \frac{(3{,}60{,}000 \times 5) - 6{,}50{,}000}{6{,}50{,}000} \times 100 = 176.9\%$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CAEM Layered Architecture (4-Layer Pyramid) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[LAYER 4 APPLICATION LEVEL
SCADA HMI, EMS EMIS, Energy Modeling Software eQUEST RETScreen
KPI Dashboards, IPMVP M&V Reports] B[LAYER 3 COMMUNICATION LEVEL
Modbus RTU TCP, BACnet, PROFIBUS, LoRaWAN, MQTT, WiFi] C[LAYER 2 DATA ACQUISITION LEVEL
PLCs, RTUs, Smart Meters, Energy Data Loggers
Fluke 1730, Schneider PM5000] D[LAYER 1 FIELD LEVEL
CTs, PTs, RTDs, Flow Meters, Gas Analyzers, Lux Meters] E[END USERS
Energy Manager, Plant Head, BEE Auditor, CISO] F[ALARM AND NOTIFICATION
SMS, Email, SCADA Popups, WhatsApp API] G[CLOUD DATABASE
Time Series InfluxDB, AWS IoT, Azure Digital Twin] H[BENCHMARKING ENGINE
Baseline vs Actual CUSUM, EnPI Tracking] D --> C C --> B B --> A A --> G G --> H H --> F A --> E F --> E ``` ## 4.2 Computer Aided Energy Audit Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[STEP 1 PRE AUDIT
Define Scope, Boundary, KPIs, Baseline Period] B[STEP 2 INSTRUMENTATION PLAN
Select CTs, PTs, Loggers, Sampling Interval 15 min] C[STEP 3 DATA ACQUISITION
Install Loggers, Configure IPMVP Option B Retrofit Isolation] D[STEP 4 DATA CLEANING
Remove Outliers, Validate Against Utility Bills] E[STEP 5 SOFTWARE MODELING
Build Energy Balance in eQUEST or EnergyPRO] F[STEP 6 IDENTIFY ECMs
Energy Conservation Measures ECM with Cost] G[STEP 7 M&V VERIFICATION
CUSUM Tracking, Annual Savings in kWh and INR] H[STEP 8 REPORT AND CERTIFY
BEE Grade 1 to 5, ISO 50001 Documentation] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> A ``` ## 4.3 Closed Loop Energy Management Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A[UTILITY SUPPLY 11 kV or 415 V] B[INCOMER PANEL HT LT MFM Meter] C[FEEDER 1 MOTOR 75 kW] D[FEEDER 2 HVAC CHILLER 120 TR] E[FEEDER 3 LIGHTING COMPRESSOR] F[ENERGY DATA LOGGER WITH TIMESTAMP] G[SCADA HMI REAL TIME TRENDING] H[EMS SOFTWARE HISTORICAL ANALYTICS] I[DECISION DASHBOARD KPI SCORECARD] J[CONTROL SIGNAL VFD ON OFF LOAD SHEDDING] K[MAINTENANCE ALERT EMAIL SMS] A --> B B --> C B --> D B --> E C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J I --> K J -. feedback to .-> C J -. feedback to .-> D K -. feedback to .-> E ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation Guide:** The **feedback arrows** (dotted) represent the **closed-loop control** that distinguishes a true CAEM system from a passive monitoring system. If the software only **observes** but does **not actuate**, it is a "data historian" and not a "computer aided management" system. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Define Computer Aided Energy Management (CAEM). List any four hardware components used in a CAEM system.** **Model Answer:** Computer Aided Energy Management is the integrated use of computer hardware, software, sensors, and communication networks to monitor, control, and optimize energy consumption in real time across industrial and commercial facilities. Four hardware components: 1. **Current Transformers (CTs)** – measure load current non-invasively. 2. **Power / Energy Data Loggers** (e.g., Fluke 1730, Hioki PW3198) – log kW, kVA, kWh, PF, THD with timestamp. 3. **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)** – automate equipment switching and load shedding. 4. **SCADA Server / Gateway** – central computer that hosts HMI, historian, and alarm engine. > [!TIP] > Examiners award full 3 marks only if you **define + list 4 components with one-line functions**. Skipping the function reduces the answer to 1.5–2 marks. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] **What is IPMVP? Explain IPMVP Option A and Option B in 2–3 lines each.** **Model Answer:** **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** is an internationally accepted protocol developed by **EVO (Efficiency Valuation Organization)** for quantifying the savings of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs). | Option | Method | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | **Option A** | Partial Measurement – key parameter measured, others stipulated | Lighting retrofit where fixture wattage is known | | **Option B** | Retrofit Isolation – all parameters measured at ECM level | VFD installation, chiller replacement | | **Option C** | Whole Facility – utility meter at incomer used | Multi-ECM, site-wide M&V | | **Option D** | Calibrated Simulation – software model (eQUEST) calibrated with data | New building design verification | ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]** A textile mill consumes 4,80,000 kWh/month at an average Power Factor of 0.78. The unit cost is ₹ 6.50/kWh and a PF penalty of 1% of the bill is levied for every 0.01 drop below 0.90. The mill's energy manager is asked to perform a CAEM study. ### (a) [7 Marks] Calculate the kVAr rating of the capacitor bank required to improve the PF to 0.95, the resulting monthly bill saving, and the new billed kVAh. ### (b) [7 Marks] Explain how a SCADA-based CAEM system would be architected for this mill, including the four layers and at least 6 measured parameters that should be logged every 15 minutes. #### MODEL SOLUTION (a): **Step 1 — Compute current reactive energy:** $$\cos^{-1}(0.78) = 38.79° \Rightarrow \tan(38.79°) = 0.8023$$ $$Q_{current} = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 0.8023 = 3{,}85{,}104 \text{ kVArh/month}$$ **Step 2 — Compute target reactive energy at PF 0.95:** $$\cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19° \Rightarrow \tan(18.19°) = 0.3287$$ $$Q_{target} = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 0.3287 = 1{,}57{,}776 \text{ kVArh/month}$$ **Step 3 — Capacitor bank rating:** $$Q_c = 3{,}85{,}104 - 1{,}57{,}776 = 2{,}27{,}328 \text{ kVArh/month}$$ Assuming 30 days, 24-hour operation: $$Q_c \text{ demand} = \frac{2{,}27{,}328}{30 \times 24} = 315.7 \text{ kVAr}$$ **Nearest standard rating:** **320 kVAr** (using 4 × 75 kVAr + 1 × 20 kVAr or 16 × 20 kVAr). **Step 4 — Current penalty calculation:** PF drop = 0.90 - 0.78 = 0.12 → 12 × 1% = **12% penalty**. $$Bill_{current} = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 6.50 = ₹ 31{,}20{,}000$$ $$Penalty_{current} = 0.12 \times 31{,}20{,}000 = ₹ 3{,}74{,}400$$ **Step 5 — New billed kVAh at PF 0.95:** $$kVAh_{new} = \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{0.95} = 5{,}05{,}263 \text{ kVAh}$$ **Step 6 — New bill (no penalty as PF > 0.90):** $$Bill_{new} = 4{,}80{,}000 \times 6.50 = ₹ 31{,}20{,}000 \text{ (no penalty)}$$ **Monthly saving = ₹ 3,74,400** (just from PF penalty waiver, before kVAr charge offset). #### MODEL SOLUTION (b): **CAEM Architecture for the Textile Mill:** 1. **Layer 1 (Field):** CTs (200/5 A ratio) on all LT feeders, PTs on the HT side, RTD sensors on bearings of ring frames, airflow sensors on compressors, lux meters in the loom shed, and an optical dissolved oxygen sensor in the ETP. 2. **Layer 2 (Data Acquisition):** One **Schneider PM5560** smart meter at the LT incomer, one **PM2200** at each feeder (loom, compressor, HVAC, lighting), and a **Fluke 1738** logger as a calibrated reference. 3. **Layer 3 (Communication):** RS-485 Modbus RTU from meters to a gateway, then Modbus TCP / BACnet over Ethernet to the SCADA server. Wi-Fi backup for the lighting feeder loggers. 4. **Layer 4 (Application):** **WinCC / Ignition SCADA** for real-time mimic, **OSIsoft PI** or **AWS IoT Core** for time-series, and a **Power BI** dashboard for daily EnPI reports. **6 measured parameters (logged every 15 minutes):** | # | Parameter | Sensor / Source | |---|---|---| | 1 | Voltage V (line-to-line) | PT | | 2 | Current I per phase | CT | | 3 | Active Power kW | Smart meter | | 4 | Reactive Power kVAr | Smart meter | | 5 | Power Factor | Derived | | 6 | Frequency Hz | Smart meter | | 7 (bonus) | THD % voltage / current | Power quality meter | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall — PF Correction Overshoot:** Students often compute kVAr to correct PF to **unity (1.0)**. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. Always retain a **lagging PF of 0.98–0.99**. Also, never forget to convert **kVArh/month → kVAr demand** by dividing by (days × hours of operation). --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A hospital is to be fitted with a Building Management System (BMS). With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain the architecture, and list 10 points that the BMS will monitor and control. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the role of **M&V software** in verifying the savings of an ECM, using the **IPMVP Option B (Retrofit Isolation)** methodology. Show a sample CUSUM chart interpretation. #### MODEL SOLUTION (a): **BMS Architecture (4 layers):** - **Field Level:** Temperature sensors (DPT, RTD), CO₂ sensors, occupancy sensors, smoke detectors, VAV damper actuators, FCU valve actuators, water flow switches, lighting relays. - **Automation Level:** **DDC (Direct Digital Controllers)** like Honeywell XL800, Johnson FX70, Schneider AS-P. Each DGC handles a zone of 8–32 points. - **Network Level:** **BACnet/IP** over Ethernet backbone, **Modbus RTU** for chiller plant integration, **LonWorks** for legacy HVAC. - **Management Level:** **Workstations with Niagara N4 / Honeywell EBI** for graphics, scheduling, alarming, trending. **10 Monitored & Controlled Points:** 1. Chilled water supply/return temperature 2. AHU discharge air temperature 3. FCU valve position (0–100%) 4. VAV box damper position 5. Room temperature & humidity 6. CO₂ level for demand-controlled ventilation 7. Lighting on/off/dim (per circuit) 8. Occupancy (PIR sensor) → auto setback 9. Smoke/fire alarm signal → smoke exhaust mode 10. Chiller kW and chiller load % #### MODEL SOLUTION (b): **Role of M&V Software in IPMVP Option B:** In **IPMVP Option B (Retrofit Isolation)**, the M&V boundary is drawn around the **ECM itself**. All energy flows crossing this boundary are measured continuously using permanently installed metering equipment (smart meter + data logger) before and after the retrofit. The M&V software (e.g., **EnergyCAP, eSight, METRIX**) performs the following: - **Baseline model development** from the **M&V period (typically 12 months)** using regression of energy against independent variables (e.g., degree-days, occupancy hours, production tonnage). Equation: $E_{baseline} = a + b \times DD + c \times Occupancy + \epsilon$. - **Post-retrofit data collection** at the same boundary, same interval, same independent variables. - **Savings = $E_{baseline,adjusted} - E_{actual,post}$**, where baseline is adjusted to current operating conditions. - **CUSUM chart** is plotted: $CUSUM_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(E_{baseline,i} - E_{actual,i})$. A continuously rising CUSUM proves sustained savings. A flattening curve flags "performance drift". **Sample CUSUM Interpretation:** | Month | Baseline kWh | Actual kWh | Monthly Saving | Cumulative Saving | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 50,000 | 40,000 | 10,000 | 10,000 | | 2 | 52,000 | 41,000 | 11,000 | 21,000 | | 3 | 54,000 | 41,500 | 12,500 | 33,500 | | 4 | 56,000 | 42,000 | 14,000 | 47,500 | | 5 | 58,000 | 44,000 | 14,000 | 61,500 | | 6 | 60,000 | 45,500 | 14,500 | 76,000 | A **monotonic, near-linear rise** confirms that the ECM (e.g., VFD retrofit on AHU fan) is delivering **consistent savings** with no performance degradation. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall — Option Confusion:** Many students write Option A and Option B as "with measurement" and "without measurement". This is the **inverse of reality**. Option A uses partial measurement (key parameter measured, others stipulated from design data); Option B uses **comprehensive measurement** of all parameters at the ECM boundary. Board examiners deduct 2 marks for this swap. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **CAEM = Field sensors + Data loggers + Communication + SCADA/EMS + M&V software.** Always remember the 4 layers. - **IPMVP has 4 Options (A, B, C, D).** Option B is the most commonly used in industrial ECM verification. - **Sampling interval of 15 minutes is the de-facto KTU/BEE standard** for monthly M&V reports. - **PF correction target = 0.98–0.99 lagging, never unity.** Capacitor rating is in **kVAr (demand)**, not kVArh (energy). - **SEC = kWh / production unit** is the universal EnPI. Always state the unit (e.g., kWh/tonne, kWh/m³, kWh/litre). - **CUSUM** is the **gold standard** for proving sustained savings — monotonic rise = success. - **Smart meter accuracy classes** (0.2S / 0.5S) refer to ±0.2% / ±0.5% error at full load. - **M&V software ≠ SCADA.** SCADA is real-time control; M&V software is historical, statistical, IPMVP-compliant. - **Software tools to remember:** eQUEST, EnergyPRO, RETScreen, HOMER, EnergyCAP, eSight, METRIX, DesignBuilder, TRACE 700. - **Communication protocols:** Modbus RTU/TCP (industrial), BACnet (buildings), MQTT (IoT cloud). - **Data retention** for BEE audits: minimum **3 years** of monthly energy data; for ISO 50001: **12–24 months** of 15-min data. - **Payback threshold** for most industrial ECMs in KTU board problems: < **3 years** = recommended; > **5 years** = not recommended. - **CUSUM flattening or declining** → performance drift, ECM needs recommissioning or staff retraining. - **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion) > 5%** as per IEEE 519 → VFD input harmonic issue → install line reactor or active harmonic filter. - **Leading PF** (over-correction) is more dangerous to the utility than lagging PF — penalty + resonance risk. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Board Exam:** "**FACS** for CAEM" → **F**ield sensors, **A**cquisition (loggers/meters), **C**ommunication (Modbus/BACnet), **S**upervisory (SCADA + EMS + M&V). Recite this in 10 seconds and you will not miss any layer in the diagram question.

topics.title: Economic Analysis Methods: Cash Flow Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economic Analysis Methods: Cash Flow Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Cash Flow Model** in Energy Management and Auditing is a quantitative financial engineering framework used to represent, evaluate, and compare the inflows (revenues, energy cost savings, salvage value) and outflows (capital investment, operating costs, maintenance expenditure) associated with an energy conservation project or capital investment over its entire economic life cycle.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the Cash Flow Model is a fundamental engineering economy tool that allows an energy auditor to translate the **kilowatt-hour (kWh) savings** identified during an energy audit into financially justifiable **monetary terms**, using the **time value of money** principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The Cash Flow Model forms the financial backbone of energy project justification. Without proper cash flow modeling, energy conservation measures (ECMs) cannot be ranked, approved, or implemented.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are deciding between two investment options for the canteen in your college:
- **Option A:** A friend promises to give you ₹1,000 exactly one year from today.
- **Option B:** A friend promises to give you ₹1,000 exactly five years from today.

Which is more valuable to you today? Most students instinctively say Option A. But *why*? Because the money in Option A arrives sooner, you can invest it, use it, or save it. The **Cash Flow Model** is the mathematical language that captures this intuition — it recognizes that **a rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow** because of its earning potential.

In the energy sector, the same logic applies. An Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) such as installing a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) costs money *now* (capital outflow) but produces electricity bill savings *every year* (cash inflow). The Cash Flow Model answers:
1. How much money do I need to spend today?
2. How much will I save each year?
3. After how many years will my investment be recovered? (Payback Period)
4. Is the project financially worthwhile? (NPV, IRR)

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in the Cash Flow Model

- **Interest Rate ($i$ or $r$):** Expressed in **% per year** (e.g., 10% per annum).
- **Analysis Period ($N$):** Expressed in **years**.
- **Currency Unit:** **INR, USD, or any consistent monetary unit** (no mathematical constants here, but the *unit of money* is the implicit constant).
- **Net Cash Flow (NCF):** Measured in **monetary units per year (₹/year)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Insight:** In Energy Management & Auditing, the "cash inflow" of an energy project is *primarily* the avoided energy cost. If a project saves 1,00,000 kWh/year and electricity costs ₹8/kWh, the annual cash inflow is **₹8,00,000/year**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Cash Flow Analysis

#### Pillar 1: Time Value of Money (TVM)
Money has a *time-dependent* value. A specific sum received today can be invested to earn interest, making it worth more in the future. Conversely, future money is worth less today (discounted).

#### Pillar 2: Cash Flow Equivalence
Two different cash flow streams are considered *equivalent* at a specified interest rate if their present values (or future values, or annual values) are equal.

### 2.2 Components of a Cash Flow Stream

| Component | Symbol | Typical Energy Project Example |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (Capital Cost) | $P$ or $C_0$ | Cost of VFD, solar panels, replacement chiller |
| Annual Savings (Revenue) | $A$ or $R$ | Electricity bill reduction, demand charge reduction |
| Annual Operating & Maintenance (O&M) Cost | $OM$ | Spare parts, labor, filter cleaning |
| Salvage Value | $S$ | Resale value of equipment at end of life |
| Net Annual Cash Flow | $NCF = R - OM$ | Net inflow per year |
| Project Life | $N$ | Useful economic life in years |
| Discount Rate / MARR | $i$ | Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (often 10–15% for industry) |

### 2.3 Single Payment Cash Flow Factors (The Four Core Factors)

These are the **fundamental building blocks** of all cash flow models. KTU frequently tests these directly.

| Factor Name | Find | Given | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single Payment Compound Amount | $F$ | $P$ | $F = P(1+i)^N$ | $(F/P, i, N)$ |
| Single Payment Present Worth | $P$ | $F$ | $P = F(1+i)^{-N}$ | $(P/F, i, N)$ |
| Uniform Series Compound Amount | $F$ | $A$ | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i}$ | $(F/A, i, N)$ |
| Uniform Series Present Worth | $P$ | $A$ | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N}$ | $(P/A, i, N)$ |
| Capital Recovery (Annual Equivalent) | $A$ | $P$ | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | $(A/P, i, N)$ |
| Sinking Fund | $A$ | $F$ | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | $(A/F, i, N)$ |
| Uniform Series Gradient (Arithmetic) | $P$ or $A$ | $G$ | $A = G \cdot \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | $(A/G, i, N)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** The notations $(F/P, i, N)$ mean "Find $F$, given $P$, at interest rate $i$ over $N$ periods." This is the **standard KTU textbook convention** (Blank & Tarquin format). Memorize this notation — it is frequently asked in Part A questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Energy Management

- **Justifying ECMs:** Tells the plant manager *whether* an energy project is worth funding.
- **Ranking Projects:** Allows comparison of mutually exclusive projects with different lifespans.
- **Tax & Depreciation Analysis:** (Advanced) Used in after-tax cash flow analysis.
- **Financing Decisions:** Determines optimal loan tenure and EMI structure.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, N)$

This derivation explains *why* $F = P(1+i)^N$ — the most fundamental equation in engineering economy.

**Starting Logic:** When you deposit a Principal $P$ in a bank offering interest rate $i$ per year, at the end of year 1, the account has:

$$\text{Amount at end of Year 1} = P + P \cdot i = P(1+i)$$

This is the new principal for Year 2 (compound interest). At the end of Year 2:

$$\text{Amount at end of Year 2} = P(1+i) + P(1+i) \cdot i = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$$

Continuing this logic for $N$ years:

$$\text{Amount at end of Year N} = P(1+i) \cdot (1+i)^{N-1} = P(1+i)^N$$

$$\boxed{F = P(1+i)^N}$$

**Formal Step-by-Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_0 &= P \\
F_1 &= F_0 + i \cdot F_0 = F_0(1+i) = P(1+i) \\
F_2 &= F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2 \\
F_3 &= F_2(1+i) = P(1+i)^3 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
F_N &= F_{N-1}(1+i) = P(1+i)^N
\end{aligned}$$

This is proved by **mathematical induction**. The factor $(1+i)^N$ is called the *single payment compound amount factor*.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, N)$

**Starting Logic:** Consider $N$ equal annual cash flows of amount $A$, occurring at the end of each year for $N$ years. We want to find their equivalent present worth $P$ at time 0.

Each individual cash flow $A$ occurring at the end of year $k$ has a present worth of $A(1+i)^{-k}$. Summing all $N$ terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= A(1+i)^{-1} + A(1+i)^{-2} + A(1+i)^{-3} + \ldots + A(1+i)^{-N} \\
P &= A \sum_{k=1}^{N} (1+i)^{-k}
\end{aligned}$$

This is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a = (1+i)^{-1}$ and common ratio $r = (1+i)^{-1}$. The sum of $N$ terms is:

$$S_N = a \cdot \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= A \cdot \left[ (1+i)^{-1} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-N}}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}} \right] \\
\end{aligned}$$

Simplify the denominator: $1 - (1+i)^{-1} = \dfrac{(1+i) - 1}{(1+i)} = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)}$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= A \cdot \left[ (1+i)^{-1} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-N}}{\frac{i}{(1+i)}} \right] \\
P &= A \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{(1+i)}{i} \cdot \left(1 - (1+i)^{-N}\right) \right] \\
P &= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-N}}{i}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^N$:

$$\boxed{P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Cash Flow Evaluation Method

| Evaluation Method | Formula | Decision Rule | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}$ | Accept if $SPP \leq$ Target Years (often 3–5 years) | High (3-mark Qs) |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = -P + A \cdot (P/A, i, N) + S \cdot (P/F, i, N)$ | Accept if $NPV \geq 0$ | Very High (14-mark Qs) |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | Find $i$ such that $NPV = 0$ | Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$ | Very High (14-mark Qs) |
| **Benefit-Cost Ratio (B-C Ratio)** | $B/C = \dfrac{PV(\text{Benefits})}{PV(\text{Costs})}$ | Accept if $B/C \geq 1$ | Medium |
| **Annual Worth (AW)** | $AW = -P \cdot (A/P, i, N) + A$ | Accept if $AW \geq 0$ | Medium |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | Compare against target % | Low |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Simple Payback Period

**Problem:** A factory installs a new energy-efficient chiller.
- Initial investment: ₹20,00,000
- Annual electricity savings: ₹5,00,000
- Annual O&M cost: ₹50,000
- Find: Simple Payback Period

**Step 1:** Calculate Net Annual Cash Flow.
$$A_{net} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Payback Formula.
$$SPP = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} = 4.44 \text{ years}$$

**Step 3:** Decision. If the company's policy is payback within 5 years, **the project is accepted**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation of SPP:** It ignores the time value of money and the cash flows *after* the payback period. A project that pays back in 2 years but has zero savings thereafter would be preferred over a project that pays back in 4 years but yields massive savings for 20 years — which is illogical. Hence, **NPV or IRR must always be used as a second check**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cash Flow Diagram — The Universal Visualization Tool

A cash flow diagram is a horizontal time-axis plot where **upward arrows = cash inflows (revenue/savings)** and **downward arrows = cash outflows (costs/investment)**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeT0["TIME 0 - PRESENT"]:::present
    nodeT1["YEAR 1"]:::future
    nodeT2["YEAR 2"]:::future
    nodeT3["YEAR 3"]:::future
    nodeT4["YEAR N"]:::future
    nodeI["INITIAL INVESTMENT P - OUTFLOW"]:::outflow
    nodeA1["ANNUAL SAVINGS A - INFLOW"]:::inflow
    nodeA2["ANNUAL SAVINGS A - INFLOW"]:::inflow
    nodeA3["ANNUAL SAVINGS A - INFLOW"]:::inflow
    nodeS["SALVAGE VALUE S - INFLOW"]:::inflow
    
    nodeT0 --- nodeI
    nodeT1 --- nodeA1
    nodeT2 --- nodeA2
    nodeT3 --- nodeA3
    nodeT4 --- nodeS
    
    classDef present fill:#1e40af,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef future fill:#475569,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef inflow fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef outflow fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```
