# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Simple Payback Period. State its two major limitations as an evaluation criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

Simple Payback Period (SPP) is the time required for the cumulative net cash inflows from an energy conservation project to equal the initial capital investment.

$$\text{SPP (years)} = \frac{\text{Initial Capital Investment } (I_0)}{\text{Net Annual Cash Inflow } (A)}$$

**Two major limitations:**

1. **Ignores the Time Value of Money** — A rupee saved in year 5 is treated equal to a rupee saved in year 1, which is economically incorrect.
2. **Ignores Post-Payback Cash Flows** — A project with long life and large savings after payback is undervalued, while a short-life, low-savings project may falsely appear equally attractive.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + formula, 2 for two limitations)*

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between NPV and IRR. Which is considered superior for ranking mutually exclusive proposals?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | NPV | IRR |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Present value of all cash flows minus initial investment | Discount rate at which NPV = 0 |
| Output | Absolute currency (₹) | Percentage (%) |
| Decision Rule | NPV ≥ 0 → Accept | IRR ≥ MARR → Accept |
| Scale-Dependent? | Yes | No (relative measure) |
| Multiple Solutions? | No (single NPV) | Yes (if cash flows change sign) |

**Superior Method for Ranking Mutually Exclusive Proposals:**

**NPV is superior.** IRR can be misleading when comparing projects of different scales because it ignores the absolute wealth created. NPV directly measures the rupee value added to the firm and is therefore the consistent, theoretically correct metric per *Fisher's Rate of Return Theory* and standard engineering economics texts.

*(3 marks: 2 for table, 1 for conclusion with reason)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model]`

**Q: A manufacturing plant is considering two mutually exclusive energy conservation proposals:**

| Parameter | Proposal X (LED Retrofit) | Proposal Y (VFD on Pumps) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | ₹5,00,000 | ₹9,00,000 |
| Annual Savings | ₹1,80,000 | ₹2{,}80{,}000 |
| Project Life | 5 years | 5 years |
| Salvage Value | ₹50,000 | ₹1,00,000 |
| Annual O&M | ₹10,000 | ₹20,000 |

**MARR = 12%. Using (a) NPV and (b) BCR, recommend the better proposal. Use incremental analysis if needed.**

---

#### **Part (a) — NPV Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Net Annual Cash Inflow for each:**

$$A_X = 1{,}80{,}000 - 10{,}000 = ₹1{,}70{,}000$$

$$A_Y = 2{,}80{,}000 - 20{,}000 = ₹2{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(P/A, 12\%, 5)$:**

$$(1.12)^5 = 1.7623,\quad (1.12)^{-5} = 0.5674$$

$$(P/A, 12\%, 5) = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \frac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.6048$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(P/F, 12\%, 5) = 0.5674$ for salvage value.**

**Step 4 — NPV of Proposal X:**

$$\text{NPV}_X = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}70{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 50{,}000 \times 0.5674$$

$$= -5{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}12{,}816 + 28{,}370 = ₹1{,}41{,}186$$

**Step 5 — NPV of Proposal Y:**

$$\text{NPV}_Y = -9{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}60{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674$$

$$= -9{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}37{,}248 + 56{,}740 = ₹93{,}988$$

**Conclusion (NPV alone):** Proposal **X** has higher NPV (₹1,41,186 vs ₹93,988). But because they are mutually exclusive and have different initial costs, we must run **Incremental Analysis**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Incremental Analysis on Y over X [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Incremental Investment:**

$$\Delta I_0 = 9{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Incremental Annual Savings:**

$$\Delta A = 2{,}60{,}000 - 1{,}70{,}000 = ₹90{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Incremental Salvage:**

$$\Delta \text{SV} = 1{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 = ₹50{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Incremental NPV:**

$$\Delta \text{NPV} = -4{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000 \times 3.6048 + 50{,}000 \times 0.5674$$

$$= -4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}24{,}432 + 28{,}370 = -₹47{,}198$$

**Step 5 — BCR on Incremental:**

$$\text{BCR}_{\text{inc}} = \frac{3{,}24{,}432 + 28{,}370}{4{,}00{,}000} = \frac{3{,}52{,}802}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.882$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $\Delta\text{NPV} < 0$ and $\text{BCR}_{\text{inc}} < 1$, the additional investment in **Y is NOT justified**. Hence, **Proposal X (LED Retrofit) is recommended**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** A common student error is to recommend Y based on higher gross savings. **Incremental analysis** reveals that the extra ₹4 lakh invested in Y earns less than the 12% MARR — a classical example of *why absolute savings can mislead.*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model]`

**Q: A factory installs a heat recovery system from a furnace exhaust. The investment is ₹15,00,000. The system generates the following net annual savings (after O&M):**

| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Savings (₹) | 4,00,000 | 4,50,000 | 5,00,000 | 5,50,000 | 6,00,000 |

**At the end of Year 5, the system has a salvage value of ₹2,00,000. MARR = 10%.**

**(a) Compute the NPV of the proposal and decide whether to accept.**
**(b) Compute the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) by interpolation.**

---

#### **Part (a) — NPV [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute Discount Factors at $i = 10\%$:**

| Year $t$ | CF (₹) | $(1.10)^{-t}$ | PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4,00,000 | 0.9091 | 3,63,640 |
| 2 | 4,50,000 | 0.8264 | 3,71,880 |
| 3 | 5,00,000 | 0.7513 | 3,75,650 |
| 4 | 5,50,000 | 0.6830 | 3,75,650 |
| 5 | 6,00,000 + 2,00,000 = 8,00,000 | 0.6209 | 4,96,720 |

**Step 2 — Sum of Present Values:**

$$\sum \text{PV} = 3{,}63{,}640 + 3{,}71{,}880 + 3{,}75{,}650 + 3{,}75{,}650 + 4{,}96{,}720 = ₹19{,}83{,}540$$

**Step 3 — Compute NPV:**

$$\text{NPV} = -15{,}00{,}000 + 19{,}83{,}540 = ₹4{,}83{,}540$$

**Decision:** Since NPV > 0, **ACCEPT the proposal**.

---

#### **Part (b) — IRR by Interpolation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Try $i = 20\%$:**

| Year $t$ | CF (₹) | $(1.20)^{-t}$ | PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4,00,000 | 0.8333 | 3,33,333 |
| 2 | 4,50,000 | 0.6944 | 3,12,500 |
| 3 | 5,00,000 | 0.5787 | 2,89,352 |
| 4 | 5,50,000 | 0.4823 | 2,65,250 |
| 5 | 8,00,000 | 0.4019 | 3,21,510 |

Sum = ₹15,21,945.

$$\text{NPV}_{20\%} = -15{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}21{,}945 = ₹21{,}945 > 0$$

**Step 2 — Try $i = 22\%$:**

$(1.22)^5 \approx 2.7027$, $(1.22)^{-5} = 0.3700$

| Year $t$ | CF (₹) | $(1.22)^{-t}$ | PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4,00,000 | 0.8197 | 3,27,869 |
| 2 | 4,50,000 | 0.6719 | 3,02,341 |
| 3 | 5,00,000 | 0.5507 | 2,75,371 |
| 4 | 5,50,000 | 0.4514 | 2,48,279 |
| 5 | 8,00,000 | 0.3700 | 2,96,030 |

Sum = ₹14,49,890.

$$\text{NPV}_{22\%} = -15{,}00{,}000 + 14{,}49{,}890 = -₹50{,}110 < 0$$

**Step 3 — Interpolate:**

$$\text{IRR} \approx 20 + \frac{21{,}945}{21{,}945 + 50{,}110} \times (22 - 20)$$

$$= 20 + \frac{21{,}945}{72{,}055} \times 2 = 20 + 0.609 = 20.61\%$$

**Decision:** IRR ≈ 20.61% > MARR = 10% ⇒ **ACCEPT**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARK-LOSING MISTAKES on Evaluation of Proposals (per KTU 2024 valuation key):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percent to decimal** ($i = 12$ instead of $i = 0.12$) — yields NPV ~₹8,00,00,000 instead of correct value. Full 7 marks lost.
> 2. **Confusing "Savings" with "Net Cash Inflow"** — students forget to subtract incremental O&M, labor, taxes. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the incremental analysis** when two alternatives are mutually exclusive. Examiner expects step-by-step $\Delta\text{NPV}$ or $\Delta \text{IRR}$ beyond just computing individual NPVs.
> 4. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly** — students compute NPV but never write *"Since NPV > 0, ACCEPT"* — Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 5. **Using non-uniform cash flow formula for uniform flows** — rare but happens. Always verify $A$ is uniform before applying $(P/A, i, n)$.
>
> **Pro Tip:** State the decision rule *before* the calculation. Format: *"Decision Rule: Since NPV ≥ 0 ⇒ Accept the proposal."* Examiners award full marks when this is shown.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Glance Recap — Memorize This Before Every KTU Exam**

- ✅ **Simple Payback (SPP)** = $I_0 / A$. Ignores time value and post-payback benefits. Use as a **screen**, not a final decision.
- ✅ **ROI** = $(A/I_0) \times 100$. Use **Incremental ROI** for mutually exclusive projects.
- ✅ **NPV** = $-I_0 + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + \text{SV} \cdot (P/F, i, n)$. **NPV > 0 ⇒ Accept.**
- ✅ **IRR** = solve for $i^*$ giving NPV = 0. Use **interpolation** between two trial rates. **IRR > MARR ⇒ Accept.**
- ✅ **BCR** = $\text{PV(Benefits)} / \text{PV(Costs)}$. **BCR > 1 ⇒ Accept.** Suitable for public-sector and BEE audits.
- ✅ **CSE** = $\frac{I_0 \cdot \text{CRF} - A}{E_{\text{saved}}}$. **CSE < Tariff ⇒ Accept.** Mandatory in BEE PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme.
- ✅ **LCC** = $I_0 + \sum \text{O\&M}_t (1+i)^{-t} - \text{SV} \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$. **Choose lowest LCC.**
- ✅ **CRF** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$. Used for converting $P \to A$ and $I_0 \to$ annualized capital cost.
- ✅ **$(P/A, i, n)$** = $\dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$. Used for converting $A \to P$.
- ✅ **$(P/F, i, n)$** = $(1+i)^{-n}$. Used for converting single future value $F \to P$.
- ✅ **MARR** = Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return, typically equal to the firm's weighted average cost of capital (WACC).
- ✅ **Incremental Analysis** is mandatory for **mutually exclusive** alternatives — never compare NPVs directly if $I_0$ differs.
- ✅ For **unequal lives**, convert to Annual Equivalent (AE) using CRF, or extend to Least Common Multiple of Lives (LCLM).
- ✅ Always **convert percentage to decimal** before substituting into formulas.
- ✅ Always **subtract incremental O&M and labor** from gross savings to get true $A$.
- ✅ **Always end with a decision statement** — *"Accept"* or *"Reject"* with the corresponding rule.
- ✅ **Discounted methods (NPV, IRR, BCR, CSE, LCC) are academically superior** to non-discounted methods (SPP, ROI).
- ✅ **Sensitivity Analysis** is the next step after base-case evaluation — vary tariff, $i$, savings, $n$ by ±10–20% to test robustness.
- ✅ **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India)** recommends CSE and LCC as primary metrics in national energy audits.

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — EVALUATION OF PROPOSALS**
*PEMRT746 — Energy Management & Auditing | KTU 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Payback Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Payback Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Payback Method (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The Payback Method is a fundamental **capital budgeting / investment appraisal technique** used in Energy Management and Auditing to determine the exact length of time (usually expressed in years or months) required for the net cash inflows — generated primarily through **energy cost savings** — to recover the **initial capital investment** made in an energy efficiency project or energy conservation measure (ECM).

In the context of KTU's *PEMRT746* course, this method is one of the simplest and most widely used screening tools by energy managers and certified energy auditors to quickly assess the financial viability of proposed energy conservation projects, such as installing variable frequency drives (VFDs), replacing inefficient motors, retrofitting HVAC systems, or deploying LED lighting retrofits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Lifebuoy" Analogy:**
> Imagine you borrow **₹1,00,000** from a friend to buy an energy-efficient boiler for your factory. You promise to return the money using the monthly savings you make on your electricity bill. If your new boiler saves you exactly **₹25,000 every month**, you will pay back your friend in exactly **4 months**. That "4 months" is your **Payback Period**.

In energy auditing, the "friend" is the bank/investor, the "₹1,00,000" is the **Initial Investment** on the ECM, and the "₹25,000/month" represents the **Annual Energy Cost Savings** flowing back into the company's accounts.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Currency Unit:** INR (₹) for KTU Kerala context; USD ($) for international projects.
- **Time Horizon:** Usually measured in **Years** (occasionally months for very short-term ECMs).
- **Discount Rate (for Discounted Payback):** Typically ranges from **8% to 15%** for Indian industrial projects, depending on the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC).
- **Benchmarks (Industrial Standards):**
  - Payback < **2 years** → Highly Attractive
  - Payback **2 to 4 years** → Acceptable
  - Payback > **4 to 5 years** → Marginal / Risky
  - Payback > **5 years** → Generally Rejected (unless strategic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PEMRT746 explicitly lists the Payback Method as a **financial analysis tool** for evaluating the economics of recommended Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) identified during the energy audit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Payback Methods

There are two primary variants of the Payback Method used in energy management:

#### (A) Simple Payback Method (SPP)
This is the **most basic** form. It ignores the **Time Value of Money (TVM)**. It simply asks: *"In how many years will my savings equal my investment?"*

- **Decision Rule:** If `SPP ≤ Target Payback Period`, accept the project.
- **Best Used For:** Quick screening of low-risk, short-duration projects (e.g., lighting retrofits, insulation, capacitor banks).

#### (B) Discounted Payback Method (DPP)
A more **rigorous** version that accounts for the **Time Value of Money** by discounting future cash flows back to the present using a suitable discount rate (often the company's cost of capital or MARR — Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).

- **Decision Rule:** If `DPP ≤ Target Payback Period`, accept the project.
- **Best Used For:** Long-term ECMs with significant capital outlay and cash flows spread over many years (e.g., cogeneration plants, boiler replacement, renewable energy installations).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Identify the Initial Investment (I₀):** The total capital cost to implement the ECM (equipment + installation + commissioning).
2. **Estimate Annual Cash Inflows (CF):** These come from *energy cost savings* (kWh saved × Tariff) + *maintenance savings* − *added O&M costs*.
3. **Compute Cumulative Cash Flow:** Add annual savings year by year until the cumulative total equals or exceeds the initial investment.
4. **Identify the Payback Year:** The exact year in which the cumulative cash flow turns positive.
5. **Interpolate (if needed):** To find the fractional year for precise payback.
6. **Compare with Target:** If payback is within the company's acceptable limit, the ECM is financially viable.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **High-Yield Formula Sheet** you must memorize for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) questions on this topic.

| **S. No.** | **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | $SPP = \dfrac{I_0}{CF_{annual}}$ | Years | When annual cash flow is constant |
| 2 | Simple Payback (Variable CF) | $SPP = Y + \dfrac{\vert I_0 - C_{Y-1} \vert}{CF_Y}$ | Years | Y = last year with negative cumulative CF; C = cumulative |
| 3 | Present Worth Factor (PWF) | $PWF = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ | Dimensionless | i = discount rate, n = year |
| 4 | Discounted Cash Flow | $DCF_n = \dfrac{CF_n}{(1+i)^n}$ | ₹ / $ | Discounted value of year-n saving |
| 5 | Cumulative Discounted CF | $CDCF_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} DCF_k$ | ₹ / $ | Sum of all discounted savings up to year n |
| 6 | Discounted Payback Period (DPP) | $DPP = Y + \dfrac{\vert I_0 - CDCF_{Y-1} \vert}{DCF_Y}$ | Years | Y = last year CDCF < I₀ |
| 7 | Energy Cost Savings (Annual) | $S = \Delta E \times C_e$ | ₹ / year | ΔE = energy saved (kWh/year); Cₑ = tariff (₹/kWh) |
| 8 | Annual Demand Charge Savings | $S_d = \Delta D \times C_d$ | ₹ / year | ΔD = kVA reduction; C_d = demand charge (₹/kVA) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Markdown:** In the table above, the absolute value notation uses `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to prevent breaking the markdown table structure (a strict KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE formatting rule).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Industrial Energy Auditors:** Use SPP to quickly rank ECMs in the audit report's "Executive Summary" table.
- **Plant Managers / CFOs:** Use DPP to justify capital expenditure (CAPEX) requests to top management and boards.
- **Banking & Project Finance:** Energy projects with shorter payback are considered **lower-risk** and receive easier loan approvals.
- **Government & ESCOs (Energy Service Companies):** Payback period is a key performance indicator (KPI) in **Energy Performance Contracts (EPCs)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Simple Payback Method

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> A textile mill in Kerala invests **₹12,00,000** to install a VFD on its main motor. The annual energy savings amount to **₹3,00,000** per year (constant for 5 years). Determine the Simple Payback Period. Also, find out whether the project is acceptable if the company's target payback is **4 years**.

#### Step 1: List the Given Data

$$I_0 = 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ (Initial Investment)}$$

$$CF_{annual} = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year (Constant Annual Cash Inflow)}$$

$$T_{target} = 4 \text{ years}$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Simple Payback Formula

Since annual cash flow is constant, we use the direct formula:

$$SPP = \frac{I_0}{CF_{annual}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$SPP = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{SPP = 4 \text{ years}}$$

#### Step 3: Decision Rule Application

We compare with the target:

$$SPP = 4 \text{ years} = T_{target} = 4 \text{ years}$$

Since the payback is **exactly equal to** the target, the project is **just acceptable** (marginally). Most auditors would treat it as a borderline case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A payback of exactly 4 years means the project recovers its capital outlay in the 49th month (4 × 12 = 48 months end). After this point, all savings become pure profit for the remaining 1 year of the 5-year life.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Discounted Payback Method (Variable Cash Flows)

> **Problem Statement (KTU Advanced Style):**
> An industrial unit invests **₹10,00,000** in an ECM. The expected cash inflows for 5 years are: Year 1: ₹2,50,000; Year 2: ₹3,00,000; Year 3: ₹3,50,000; Year 4: ₹2,00,000; Year 5: ₹1,50,000. The discount rate is **10% per annum**. Compute the Discounted Payback Period.

#### Step 1: Compute the Present Worth Factor (PWF) for Each Year

The PWF formula is:

$$PWF_n = \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$$

Where `i = 0.10`. Let's compute each PWF:

$$PWF_1 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^1} = 0.9091$$

$$PWF_2 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^2} = 0.8264$$

$$PWF_3 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^3} = 0.7513$$

$$PWF_4 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^4} = 0.6830$$

$$PWF_5 = \frac{1}{(1.10)^5} = 0.6209$$

#### Step 2: Compute the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) for Each Year

Using:

$$DCF_n = CF_n \times PWF_n$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DCF_1 &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 2{,}22{,}727 \text{ ₹} \\
DCF_2 &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 2{,}47{,}934 \text{ ₹} \\
DCF_3 &= 3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 2{,}62{,}965 \text{ ₹} \\
DCF_4 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 1{,}36{,}602 \text{ ₹} \\
DCF_5 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6209 = 93{,}138 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Compute the Cumulative Discounted Cash Flow (CDCF)

We sum the DCFs year by year:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CDCF_1 &= 0 + 2{,}22{,}727 = 2{,}22{,}727 \text{ ₹} \\
CDCF_2 &= 2{,}22{,}727 + 2{,}47{,}934 = 4{,}70{,}661 \text{ ₹} \\
CDCF_3 &= 4{,}70{,}661 + 2{,}62{,}965 = 7{,}33{,}626 \text{ ₹} \\
CDCF_4 &= 7{,}33{,}626 + 1{,}36{,}602 = 8{,}70{,}228 \text{ ₹} \\
CDCF_5 &= 8{,}70{,}228 + 93{,}138 = 9{,}63{,}366 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Identify the Payback Year

Comparing CDCF with the initial investment `I₀ = 10,00,000 ₹`:

| **Year** | **DCF (₹)** | **CDCF (₹)** | **Status vs. I₀ = 10,00,000** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | 2,22,727 | 2,22,727 | Not recovered |
| 2 | 2,47,934 | 4,70,661 | Not recovered |
| 3 | 2,62,965 | 7,33,626 | Not recovered |
| 4 | 1,36,602 | 8,70,228 | Not recovered |
| 5 | 93,138 | 9,63,366 | **Not recovered** (short by ₹36,634) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Even after 5 full years, the cumulative discounted cash flow (₹9,63,366) **does not exceed** the initial investment (₹10,00,000). Therefore, strictly speaking, the **Discounted Payback Period exceeds 5 years**.

#### Step 5: Extrapolation (for partial year estimation)

If we assume the Year 5 DCF (₹93,138) is spread evenly across the year, the additional time needed beyond Year 4 is:

$$Extra = \frac{\vert 10{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}70{,}228 \vert}{93{,}138}$$

$$Extra = \frac{1{,}29{,}772}{93{,}138} = 1.394 \text{ years}$$

Therefore:

$$DPP = 4 + 1.394 = 5.39 \text{ years}$$

#### Step 6: Final Conclusion

$$\boxed{DPP \approx 5.39 \text{ years}}$$

> **Engineering Verdict:** The project has a discounted payback of approximately **5.4 years**. If the company's target payback is 5 years, the ECM is **NOT acceptable** on a discounted basis — even though the simple payback (ignoring time value of money) might have looked attractive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Payback Method Decision Flow

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the end-to-end logic of the Payback Method as applied to an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify ECM from Energy Audit] --> B[Determine Initial Investment I0]
    B --> C[Estimate Annual Energy Cost Savings CF]
    C --> D{Is CF Constant Every Year?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply SPP = I0 divided by CF]
    D -- No --> F[Build Year-wise Cash Flow Table]
    F --> G[Compute Cumulative CF Each Year]
    G --> H[Identify Year Y where Cumulative CF turns positive]
    H --> I[Apply SPP = Y plus Interpolated Fraction]
    E --> J{Time Value of Money Considered?}
    I --> J
    J -- No --> K[Simple Payback Method Result]
    J -- Yes --> L[Compute PWF for Each Year at Discount Rate i]
    L --> M[Compute Discounted Cash Flow DCF per year]
    M --> N[Compute Cumulative Discounted Cash Flow CDCF]
    N --> O[Identify Payback Year using CDCF vs I0]
    O --> P[Apply DPP formula with interpolation]
    P --> Q[Discounted Payback Method Result]
    K --> R{Compare with Target Payback Ttarget}
    Q --> R
    R -- Payback less than or equal to Ttarget --> S[ECM ACCEPTED Proceed to Implementation]
    R -- Payback greater than Ttarget --> T[ECM REJECTED Explore Alternatives]
    S --> U[End: Include ECM in Audit Report]
    T --> U
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Cumulative Cash Flow vs. Time (Conceptual Block Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Timeline
        Y0[Year 0: I0 Negative Outlay]
        Y1[Year 1 Partial Recovery]
        Y2[Year 2 Break Even Point]
        Y3[Year 3 Positive Zone]
        Y4[Year 4 Full Profit Zone]
    end
    Y0 --> Y1 --> Y2 --> Y3 --> Y4
    style Y0 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Y1 fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Y2 fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Y3 fill:#4D96FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Y4 fill:#2EC4B6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Year 0 represents the initial capital outflow (red zone). As years progress, cumulative cash flow climbs from negative to zero. The **break-even point** is the Payback Period. Beyond that, the project operates in the **green profit zone**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Define the **Payback Method** as used in the financial evaluation of energy conservation projects. State its decision rule.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Payback Method is a financial evaluation technique that determines the time period required for the cumulative energy cost savings of an ECM to recover the initial capital investment.
>
> **Decision Rule:** An ECM is considered financially acceptable if its calculated payback period is **less than or equal to** the pre-set target payback period fixed by the organization. *(1 Mark for definition + 1 Mark for formula + 1 Mark for decision rule)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **Simple Payback** and **Discounted Payback** methods.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Simple Payback** | **Discounted Payback** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Time Value of Money | Ignored | Considered |
| Discount Rate | Not used | Required (cost of capital) |
| Accuracy | Approximate | More accurate |
| Computation Effort | Low | Moderate to high |

*(1 Mark per correct distinction × 3 key points)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> A manufacturing plant proposes to install a new energy-efficient compressor. The total capital investment is **₹15,00,000**. The estimated energy savings are:
> - Year 1: ₹4,00,000
> - Year 2: ₹5,00,000
> - Year 3: ₹5,00,000
> - Year 4: ₹3,00,000
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **Simple Payback Period** using the cumulative cash flow method. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** If the company's target payback is **3.5 years**, state whether the project should be accepted. Justify your answer with reasoning. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Computation:**

**Step 1:** Set up the cumulative cash flow table.

| **Year** | **Annual Saving (₹)** | **Cumulative Saving (₹)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 4,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 2 | 5,00,000 | 9,00,000 |
| 3 | 5,00,000 | 14,00,000 |
| 4 | 3,00,000 | 17,00,000 |

**Step 2:** Compare cumulative savings with investment `I₀ = ₹15,00,000`.

- End of Year 2: Cumulative = ₹9,00,000 (not yet recovered)
- End of Year 3: Cumulative = ₹14,00,000 (still short by ₹1,00,000)
- End of Year 4: Cumulative = ₹17,00,000 (fully recovered)

**Step 3:** Identify the last year with negative balance (Y) and interpolate:

$$Y = 3 \text{ (last year where cumulative < I}_0\text{)}$$

$$SPP = Y + \frac{\vert I_0 - C_{Y-1} \vert}{CF_Y}$$

$$SPP = 3 + \frac{\vert 15{,}00{,}000 - 14{,}00{,}000 \vert}{3{,}00{,}000}$$

$$SPP = 3 + \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{SPP = 3 + 0.333 = 3.33 \text{ years}}$$

**Part (b) — Decision Analysis:**

$$SPP_{calculated} = 3.33 \text{ years} \quad ; \quad T_{target} = 3.5 \text{ years}$$

Since:

$$3.33 \text{ years} < 3.5 \text{ years}$$

> **Verdict:** The project **SHOULD BE ACCEPTED**. The compressor installation will recover its ₹15,00,000 investment in approximately 3 years and 4 months, which is within the company's acceptable 3.5-year threshold. The remaining 0.67 years of operation within the 4-year analysis horizon will generate pure profit.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> A hotel building invests **₹8,00,000** in a solar water heating system. Annual uniform savings of **₹1,60,000** are expected for 7 years. The discount rate is **12% per annum**.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **Simple Payback Period**. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the **Discounted Payback Period** and comment on the difference. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a):** Using the direct formula (constant cash flow):

$$SPP = \frac{I_0}{CF_{annual}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1{,}60{,}000} = 5 \text{ years}$$

**Part (b):** Compute DCF for each year and identify payback.

| **Year (n)** | **CF (₹)** | **PWF = 1/(1.12)ⁿ** | **DCF (₹)** | **CDCF (₹)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1,60,000 | 0.8929 | 1,42,857 | 1,42,857 |
| 2 | 1,60,000 | 0.7972 | 1,27,551 | 2,70,408 |
| 3 | 1,60,000 | 0.7118 | 1,13,885 | 3,84,293 |
| 4 | 1,60,000 | 0.6355 | 1,01,683 | 4,85,976 |
| 5 | 1,60,000 | 0.5674 | 90,789 | 5,76,765 |
| 6 | 1,60,000 | 0.5066 | 81,062 | 6,57,827 |
| 7 | 1,60,000 | 0.4523 | 72,378 | 7,30,205 |

Even after Year 7, CDCF = ₹7,30,205 which is **less than** I₀ = ₹8,00,000. So DPP > 7 years.

**Comparison and Comment:**
- **SPP = 5 years** (ignores time value of money)
- **DPP > 7 years** (accounting for the 12% discount rate)

> **Engineering Insight:** The large gap between SPP and DPP indicates that the future savings are heavily eroded by discounting at 12%. This ECM is attractive on a simple basis but **marginal or unattractive** on a discounted, time-value-adjusted basis. A more sophisticated method like **Net Present Value (NPV)** or **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is recommended for final decision-making.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to Interpolate:** When cumulative cash flow crosses the investment in the middle of a year, students often stop at the previous full year. **Always interpolate** to get the fractional year. (Lose 1-2 marks if missed.)
>
> 2. **Using Simple Payback When Time Value of Money is Required:** If the problem mentions a **discount rate** or **cost of capital**, you MUST use the Discounted Payback Method, not Simple Payback. Read the question carefully.
>
> 3. **Units Mismatch:** Tariff may be given in ₹/kWh and demand in ₹/kVA. Always convert energy savings to monetary terms before applying the payback formula.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "Year 0" Outflow:** The initial investment happens at Year 0, not Year 1. Cumulative cash flow at Year 0 is `−I₀`.
>
> 5. **Decision Rule Reversal:** Some students wrongly write "if payback > target, accept." The correct rule is: **shorter payback = better** → Accept if `Payback ≤ Target`.
>
> 6. **Not Showing PWF Calculation:** When solving DPP problems, examiners expect you to show `PWF = 1/(1+i)ⁿ` computation for each year explicitly. Just writing the final DCF without PWF steps will cost 1-2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Payback Method**
>
> - ✅ The **Payback Method** measures how quickly an ECM's energy savings recover its initial capital cost.
> - ✅ **Simple Payback (SPP)** = `I₀ / Annual Cash Inflow` (for constant savings) OR uses cumulative table + interpolation (for variable savings).
> - ✅ **Discounted Payback (DPP)** uses `PWF = 1/(1+i)ⁿ` to convert future savings to present value before checking recovery.
> - ✅ **Decision Rule:** Accept the ECM if `Payback ≤ Target Payback`.
> - ✅ **Simple Payback is a screening tool** — easy but ignores TVM; used for quick ranking of multiple ECMs in an audit report.
> - ✅ **Discounted Payback is more rigorous** — accounts for the opportunity cost of capital; preferred for large/long-term projects.
> - ✅ **Key Industrial Benchmark:** Payback < 3 years is considered excellent; 3-5 years acceptable; > 5 years risky.
> - ✅ **Major Limitation:** Payback ignores the savings that occur **after** the payback period and does not measure total profitability (use NPV or IRR for that).
> - ✅ **Typical KTU Exam Weightage:** Payback Method carries 7-14 marks as a Part B question, often combined with NPV or IRR.
> - ✅ **Always show the cumulative cash flow table** in your answer — it earns easy process marks even if the final interpolation is slightly off.
> - ✅ **Pay attention to the presence/absence of a discount rate** in the question — this is the #1 clue for choosing between SPP and DPP.
> - ✅ **Real-world anchor:** Most ESCO contracts in India are structured around a 3 to 5 year payback guarantee.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Average Rate of Return Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Average Rate of Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Average Rate of Return (ARR) Method** is a classical, non-discounted capital budgeting technique used to evaluate the profitability of an energy conservation project (or any investment proposal) by expressing the average annual accounting profit as a percentage of the average investment made over the project's useful life. It belongs to the family of **traditional methods of investment appraisal** and is extensively applied in **Energy Management & Auditing** to rank mutually exclusive energy-saving projects.

Mathematically, it is defined as:

> $$\text{ARR} = \left( \frac{\text{Average Annual Net Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \right) \times 100\%$$

The ARR is also interchangeably referred to in the engineering economics literature as the **Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)** or the **Return on Investment (ROI)** on an un-discounted (book value) basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Energy Management & Auditing, ARR is most often applied to compare competing energy efficiency projects (e.g., installing a VFD vs. replacing boiler burners). Unlike the Net Present Value (NPV) or Internal Rate of Return (IRR) methods, ARR **ignores the time value of money**, making it a simple yet less precise screening tool.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you invest **₹1,00,000** in a small rooftop solar plant. Each year, the plant generates cash savings of about ₹25,000 on your electricity bill. After deducting yearly maintenance (say ₹5,000), your **net yearly earnings** are ₹20,000. Now, intuitively, you might ask: *"For every rupee I have tied up in the plant, how many rupees do I earn back per year?"*

That intuition is exactly what ARR captures. If the average amount of money you have locked into the plant is **₹60,000** (because it depreciates over 10 years to scrap value), then:

- You earn ₹20,000 on an average locked-in capital of ₹60,000.
- That gives you roughly a **33.3% return** — very attractive!

**Plain-English Meaning:** ARR is simply *"How much annual profit am I making, compared to the average money I have stuck inside this project?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Threshold:** Projects with **ARR ≥ Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)** set by the firm (often called the *hurdle rate*) are accepted. In energy management, the MARR is frequently pegged equal to the firm's after-tax cost of capital (typically 10–15%).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in ARR

The following bolded terms appear repeatedly in KTU examination problems:

- **Average Investment (AI):** The mean book value of the asset over its economic life.
- **Average Annual Profit (AAP):** Mean net profit per year after depreciation and taxes.
- **Initial Investment (I):** Capital outlay at Year 0.
- **Salvage Value (SV):** Realizable worth of the asset at the end of useful life.
- **Useful Life (n):** Economic life of the project in years.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The ARR method works on **accounting profit** rather than cash flow. Here is the structured logic flow used in any KTU numerical problem:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Initial Investment ($I$):** Total capital cost of the energy project (equipment + installation + commissioning).
2. **Step 2 — Estimate the Useful Life ($n$):** Number of years the asset will generate energy savings.
3. **Step 3 — Determine Salvage Value ($SV$):** Expected resale/scrap value at end of life.
4. **Step 4 — Compute Average Investment ($AI$):** The asset loses value linearly (book depreciation), so the *average* amount of capital tied up is:
$$AI = \frac{I + SV}{2}$$
   *Why?* Because at the start you have $I$, at the end you have $SV$; the linearly interpolated mean is exactly $\frac{I+SV}{2}$.
5. **Step 5 — Compute Average Annual Net Profit ($AAP$):** Total life-time profit divided by useful life, or equivalently:
$$AAP = \text{Annual Cash Inflow (Energy Savings)} - \text{Annual Depreciation} - \text{Taxes}$$
6. **Step 6 — Apply the ARR Formula:**
$$\text{ARR (\%)} = \left( \frac{AAP}{AI} \right) \times 100$$
7. **Step 7 — Decision Rule:**
   - If **ARR ≥ MARR (Hurdle Rate)** → **Accept** the project.
   - If **ARR < MARR** → **Reject** the project.
   - For mutually exclusive alternatives → **Choose the project with the highest ARR**.

### 2.2 Why "Average" Investment?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind Averaging:** A capital asset is not fully tied up for all $n$ years at its initial cost. As depreciation occurs, the *book value* shrinks. The firm recovers its capital gradually. So the *effective* capital locked in is somewhere between the initial cost and the salvage value. The midpoint formula $\frac{I+SV}{2}$ is the simplest (linear) approximation of this average tied-up capital.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | LaTeX Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Investment | $AI = \dfrac{I + SV}{2}$ | Mean book value of asset (₹, $) |
| 2 | Annual Straight-Line Depreciation | $D = \dfrac{I - SV}{n}$ | Depreciation per year (₹/yr) |
| 3 | Average Annual Profit | $AAP = \text{Annual Savings} - D - \text{Taxes}$ | Net profit per year (₹/yr) |
| 4 | Average Rate of Return | $\text{ARR} = \dfrac{AAP}{AI} \times 100$ | Expressed as **%** |
| 5 | Decision Criterion | $\text{ARR} \geq \text{MARR}$ | Accept project if satisfied |
| 6 | Alternative Profit Form | $AAP = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Yearly Net Profit})}{n}$ | Used when yearly profits vary |
| 7 | Useful Life | $n$ (years) | Economic life of project |
| 8 | MARR (Hurdle Rate) | $r_h$ | Minimum acceptable ARR (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Safeguard:** Both numerator ($AAP$) and denominator ($AI$) must be in the **same currency** for the ratio to be dimensionless. Always express the final answer in **% (per annum)**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Energy Management & Auditing**, ARR is used as a **first-pass screening filter**:

- *Rank ordering* competing retrofits (e.g., LED lighting vs. HVAC upgrade vs. VFD installation).
- *Quick feasibility* check for small-to-medium energy projects without complex DCF analysis.
- *Communication tool* with non-financial plant managers — easy to understand as "annual % gain on capital."

**Limitation:** Because it ignores the time value of money, ARR tends to **overstate** returns for long-duration energy projects. Hence, for high-value ECMs (Energy Conservation Measures), KTU also expects students to know **NPV / IRR / Payback** as supplementary tools (covered in the same module).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Average Investment Formula

We start with the assumption of **straight-line depreciation**. Let the book value of an asset at year $t$ be $BV(t)$.

**Assumption:** The asset depreciates linearly from $I$ (at $t=0$) to $SV$ (at $t=n$).

The depreciation rate per year is:

$$D = \frac{I - SV}{n}$$

Therefore, the book value at any year $t$ is:

$$BV(t) = I - D \cdot t = I - \left(\frac{I - SV}{n}\right) t$$

The **Average Investment** is defined as the time-averaged book value over the project's life:

$$AI = \frac{1}{n} \int_{0}^{n} BV(t) \, dt$$

Substituting:

$$AI = \frac{1}{n} \int_{0}^{n} \left[ I - \left(\frac{I - SV}{n}\right) t \right] dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AI &= \frac{1}{n} \left[ I \cdot t - \left(\frac{I - SV}{n}\right) \frac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{n} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{n} \left[ I \cdot n - \left(\frac{I - SV}{n}\right) \frac{n^{2}}{2} \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{n} \left[ I \cdot n - \frac{(I - SV) \cdot n}{2} \right] \\[6pt]
&= I - \frac{I - SV}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2I - (I - SV)}{2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{I + SV}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the average investment over the asset's life equals the arithmetic mean of the initial investment and salvage value:

$$\boxed{AI = \frac{I + SV}{2}}$$

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU Style

> **[KTU-Style Numerical Problem]**
> A manufacturing firm is evaluating two energy conservation measures (ECMs):
>
> | Parameter | ECM-A (VFD on Motor) | ECM-B (LED Retrofit) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Initial Investment ($I$) | ₹5,00,000 | ₹2,50,000 |
> | Annual Energy Savings | ₹1,20,000 | ₹70,000 |
> | Annual Maintenance | ₹10,000 | ₹5,000 |
> | Salvage Value ($SV$) | ₹50,000 | ₹25,000 |
> | Useful Life ($n$) | 10 years | 8 years |
> | MARR (Hurdle Rate) | 15% | 15% |
>
> *Assume no taxes. Use the Average Rate of Return Method to recommend which ECM the firm should select.*

---

#### **Solution for ECM-A (VFD on Motor)**

**Step 1 — Annual Depreciation:**
$$D_A = \frac{I_A - SV_A}{n_A} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{10} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{10} = \text{₹}45{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2 — Average Annual Profit:**
Annual Savings = ₹1,20,000
Annual Maintenance = ₹10,000
$$
\begin{aligned}
AAP_A &= \text{Annual Savings} - \text{Maintenance} - D_A \\
&= 1{,}20{,}000 - 10{,}000 - 45{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}65{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Investment:**
$$AI_A = \frac{I_A + SV_A}{2} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000}{2} = \frac{5{,}50{,}000}{2} = \text{₹}2{,}75{,}000$$

**Step 4 — ARR for ECM-A:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARR}_A &= \frac{AAP_A}{AI_A} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{65{,}000}{2{,}75{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 23.64\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:** Since $\text{ARR}_A = 23.64\% > 15\%$, **ECM-A is acceptable.**

---

#### **Solution for ECM-B (LED Retrofit)**

**Step 1 — Annual Depreciation:**
$$D_B = \frac{I_B - SV_B}{n_B} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000 - 25{,}000}{8} = \frac{2{,}25{,}000}{8} = \text{₹}28{,}125 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2 — Average Annual Profit:**
Annual Savings = ₹70,000
Annual Maintenance = ₹5,000
$$
\begin{aligned}
AAP_B &= 70{,}000 - 5{,}000 - 28{,}125 \\
&= \text{₹}36{,}875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Investment:**
$$AI_B = \frac{I_B + SV_B}{2} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000 + 25{,}000}{2} = \frac{2{,}75{,}000}{2} = \text{₹}1{,}37{,}500$$

**Step 4 — ARR for ECM-B:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARR}_B &= \frac{AAP_B}{AI_B} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{36{,}875}{1{,}37{,}500} \times 100 \\
&= 26.82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:** Since $\text{ARR}_B = 26.82\% > 15\%$, **ECM-B is acceptable.**

---

#### **Final Recommendation (Mutually Exclusive Choice)**

Since both projects are individually feasible, the firm must choose the one with the **higher ARR** for capital efficiency:

| ECM | ARR | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| ECM-A (VFD) | 23.64% | Acceptable |
| ECM-B (LED) | **26.82%** | **Recommended** |

> **Recommendation:** The firm should select **ECM-B (LED Retrofit)** because it offers the highest return per rupee of average tied-up capital (26.82% vs. 23.64%).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | ECM-A (VFD) | ECM-B (LED) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | Higher (₹5,00,000) | Lower (₹2,50,000) |
| Annual Net Profit | ₹65,000 | ₹36,875 |
| Average Investment | ₹2,75,000 | ₹1,37,500 |
| **ARR (%)** | **23.64%** | **26.82%** |
| ARR vs. MARR (15%) | ✓ Feasible | ✓ Feasible |
| Ranking | 2nd | **1st** |
| Capital Efficiency | Lower | **Higher** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Logic Flow for ARR Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Energy Project] --> B[Collect Financial Data]
    B --> B1[Initial Investment I]
    B --> B2[Salvage Value SV]
    B --> B3[Useful Life n]
    B --> B4[Annual Energy Savings]
    B --> B5[Annual Maintenance Cost]
    B --> B6[Tax Rate if applicable]

    B1 --> C[Compute Average Investment AI]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[AI = I + SV divided by 2]

    B3 --> D[Compute Annual Depreciation D]
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
    D --> D1[D = I minus SV divided by n]

    B4 --> E[Compute Average Annual Profit AAP]
    B5 --> E
    B6 --> E
    D --> E
    E --> E1[AAP = Savings minus Maintenance minus Depreciation minus Taxes]

    C1 --> F[Compute ARR]
    E1 --> F
    F --> F1[ARR equals AAP divided by AI times 100 percent]

    F1 --> G{ARR greater than or equal to MARR}
    G -->|Yes| H[ACCEPT the Energy Project]
    G -->|No| I[REJECT the Energy Project]

    H --> J[Compare ARR across alternatives]
    J --> K[Select project with HIGHEST ARR]
    K --> L[End Recommendation]

    I --> L
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ARR Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Block]
        I1[Initial Investment I]
        I2[Salvage Value SV]
        I3[Useful Life n]
        I4[Annual Savings]
        I5[Annual Expenses]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Engine]
        M1[Depreciation Module]
        M2[Profit Module]
        M3[Average Capital Module]
        M4[ARR Ratio Module]
    end

    subgraph DECISION[Decision Output]
        O1[ARR Percentage]
        O2[Accept or Reject Flag]
        O3[Ranking for Alternatives]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M1
    I3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2

    I4 --> M2
    I5 --> M2
    M2 --> M4

    I1 --> M3
    I2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4

    M4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Time-Line of Book Value (Depreciation Profile)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Y0[Year 0: Book Value = I] --> Y1[Year 1: Book Value reduces by D]
    Y1 --> Y2[Year 2: Book Value reduces by D]
    Y2 --> Y3[Year 3: Book Value reduces by D]
    Y3 --> Y4[Year n minus 1]
    Y4 --> YN[Year n: Book Value equals SV]
    Y0 -.-> AI[Average Investment equals midpoint]
    YN -.-> AI
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Average Rate of Return (ARR) method. State its decision criterion.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Average Rate of Return (ARR) method is a traditional technique of investment appraisal that evaluates the profitability of a project by expressing the **average annual accounting profit** as a percentage of the **average investment** tied up in the project.

$$\text{ARR} = \left( \frac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \right) \times 100\%$$

**Decision Criterion:** A project is accepted if its **ARR is greater than or equal to the Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)** set by the firm. Otherwise, it is rejected. For mutually exclusive alternatives, the project with the **highest ARR** is selected. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is "average investment" used in the ARR formula instead of the initial investment?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In a capital project, the entire initial investment is **not tied up uniformly** for all $n$ years. Due to depreciation, the book value of the asset **gradually declines** from the initial cost $I$ at Year 0 to the salvage value $SV$ at the end of useful life.

Therefore, the **effective capital locked** into the project lies somewhere between $I$ and $SV$. The **arithmetic mean** $\frac{I + SV}{2}$ provides a simple, linear approximation of this average tied-up capital, giving a more realistic denominator for computing the return. Using the initial investment alone would **understate** the actual rate of return. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

A factory is considering the installation of a **cogeneration system** to recover waste heat. The following data is provided:

- **Initial Investment (I):** ₹8,00,000
- **Estimated Useful Life (n):** 10 years
- **Salvage Value (SV):** ₹1,00,000
- **Annual Revenue from Energy Savings:** ₹2,50,000
- **Annual Operating & Maintenance Cost:** ₹50,000
- **Applicable Tax Rate:** 30%
- **Depreciation Method:** Straight Line
- **MARR (Hurdle Rate):** 18%

**(a)** Calculate the **Average Rate of Return (ARR)** for the project. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Based on the ARR, recommend whether the project should be **accepted or rejected**. Comment on the **feasibility** of the cogeneration system. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Calculation of ARR (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Annual Straight-Line Depreciation:**

$$D = \frac{I - SV}{n} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{10} = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{10} = \text{₹}70{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Profit Before Tax (PBT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PBT} &= \text{Annual Revenue} - \text{O\&M Cost} - D \\
&= 2{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000 - 70{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}1{,}30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Tax @ 30%:**

$$\text{Tax} = 0.30 \times 1{,}30{,}000 = \text{₹}39{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Average Annual Profit After Tax (AAP):**

$$AAP = 1{,}30{,}000 - 39{,}000 = \text{₹}91{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Average Investment:**

$$AI = \frac{I + SV}{2} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{2} = \text{₹}4{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 6 — ARR Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARR} &= \frac{AAP}{AI} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{91{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 20.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\text{ARR} = 20.22\%}$$

**(7 Marks: D=1, PBT=1, Tax=1, AAP=1, AI=1, Final ARR & box=2)**

---

**Part (b) — Recommendation & Feasibility Comment (7 Marks):**

Comparing with the **MARR (Hurdle Rate) of 18%**:

$$\text{ARR} = 20.22\% > \text{MARR} = 18\%$$

- **Decision:** The cogeneration project **should be ACCEPTED**, because its return (20.22%) exceeds the firm's minimum acceptable rate of return (18%).
- **Margin of Safety:** Excess return over MARR = $20.22\% - 18\% = 2.22\%$. This provides a **modest safety cushion**.
- **Feasibility Comment:** The cogeneration system is **financially feasible** under the ARR criterion. The project recovers, on average, 20.22 paise per rupee of capital tied up annually, making it a profitable waste-heat recovery initiative.
- **Caveat:** Since ARR ignores the time value of money, the firm should **cross-verify** with NPV or IRR methods (covered elsewhere in Module 4) before final capital commitment.

**(7 Marks: ARR vs MARR comparison=2, Accept/Reject statement=2, Margin & feasibility=2, Caveat about TVM=1)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

A textile industry wants to replace its existing inefficient motors with **energy-efficient IE3 class motors**. Two proposals are being considered:

| Parameter | Proposal-P | Proposal-Q |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment ($I$) | ₹6,00,000 | ₹4,00,000 |
| Annual Energy Cost Savings | ₹1,80,000 | ₹1,30,000 |
| Annual Maintenance Cost | ₹30,000 | ₹20,000 |
| Useful Life ($n$) | 6 years | 5 years |
| Salvage Value ($SV$) | ₹60,000 | ₹40,000 |

Assume the firm's **MARR is 20%**. Ignore taxes.

**(a)** Compute the **ARR for both proposals** and identify which is more attractive. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If both proposals are **mutually exclusive**, recommend the one to be implemented. Justify your recommendation with a comparative ARR analysis and state any **one limitation** of the ARR method. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — ARR for Both Proposals (7 Marks):**

**Proposal-P:**

Annual Depreciation:
$$D_P = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000}{6} = \frac{5{,}40{,}000}{6} = \text{₹}90{,}000$$

Average Annual Profit:
$$AAP_P = 1{,}80{,}000 - 30{,}000 - 90{,}000 = \text{₹}60{,}000$$

Average Investment:
$$AI_P = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000}{2} = \text{₹}3{,}30{,}000$$

ARR:
$$\text{ARR}_P = \frac{60{,}000}{3{,}30{,}000} \times 100 = 18.18\%$$

**Proposal-Q:**

Annual Depreciation:
$$D_Q = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}000}{5} = \frac{3{,}60{,}000}{5} = \text{₹}72{,}000$$

Average Annual Profit:
$$AAP_Q = 1{,}30{,}000 - 20{,}000 - 72{,}000 = \text{₹}38{,}000$$

Average Investment:
$$AI_Q = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000}{2} = \text{₹}2{,}20{,}000$$

ARR:
$$\text{ARR}_Q = \frac{38{,}000}{2{,}20{,}000} \times 100 = 17.27\%$$

**Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Proposal-P | Proposal-Q |
|---|---|---|
| ARR | **18.18%** | 17.27% |
| MARR | 20% | 20% |

**Comparison:** Proposal-P yields a **higher ARR (18.18%)** than Proposal-Q (17.27%).

**(7 Marks: Each proposal ~3 marks, comparison ~1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — Recommendation & Limitation (7 Marks):**

- **Feasibility Check:** Both proposals have ARR < MARR (20%). So, **individually, neither is acceptable** under strict ARR criterion.
- **Mutually Exclusive Choice:** When both are infeasible but selection is mandatory, the proposal with the **higher ARR** is chosen for **relative capital efficiency**.
- **Recommendation:** Select **Proposal-P (IE3 motors at ₹6,00,000)** because it offers the **higher ARR (18.18% vs. 17.27%)**, indicating superior return per rupee of average invested capital.
- **Caveat:** Since neither meets MARR, the firm should either **revise MARR downward**, **renegotiate the project cost**, or **explore alternative ECMs**.

**One Limitation of ARR:**
*It ignores the time value of money. Cash flows occurring in distant years are treated equal to those in the near term, which can mislead capital budgeting decisions in long-life energy projects.*

**(7 Marks: Feasibility=2, Choice with justification=2, Caveat on MARR=1, Limitation=2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract depreciation** while computing average annual profit → mark loss (1–2).
> 2. **Using initial investment** instead of average investment in the denominator → wrong ARR by a large margin (3+ marks lost).
> 3. **Ignoring taxes** when problem explicitly states tax rate → 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Comparing ARR to MARR incorrectly** (writing "ARR = MARR" instead of "ARR ≥ MARR") → 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the % symbol** in the final answer → small but recurring deduction.
> 6. **Not stating the decision explicitly** (Accept/Reject) → 1–2 marks lost in Part B.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Average Rate of Return Method**

- ✅ **ARR Formula:** $\text{ARR} = \dfrac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **Average Investment:** $\dfrac{I + SV}{2}$ (arithmetic mean of initial and salvage value).
- ✅ **Annual Depreciation (SLM):** $\dfrac{I - SV}{n}$
- ✅ **Average Annual Profit:** Annual Cash Savings − Annual Maintenance − Depreciation − Taxes.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** **Accept if ARR ≥ MARR**; reject if ARR < MARR.
- ✅ **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Select the one with the **highest ARR**.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Simple, easy to compute, uses accounting data, good for ranking small ECMs.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Ignores **Time Value of Money**, ignores cash flow timing, based on accounting profit (not cash flow).
- ✅ **Best Used For:** First-pass screening of energy conservation measures; not recommended as a sole criterion for large investments.
- ✅ **Complementary Methods:** Always cross-verify with **NPV, IRR, and Payback Period** for high-value energy projects.
- ✅ **Threshold Rule of Thumb:** Energy projects typically require **ARR ≥ 15–20%** to be considered attractive in Indian industrial contexts.
- ✅ **Critical Subtraction Order:** Savings → minus Maintenance → minus Depreciation → minus Tax → equals Profit (do not reverse order).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Internal Rate of Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is defined as the discount rate at which the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of all cash flows (both inflows and outflows) from a capital investment project becomes **exactly equal to zero**. In the context of energy management and auditing, IRR represents the **effective annualized rate of return** generated by an energy conservation project over its entire operational life cycle, considering the time value of money.

Mathematically, IRR is the value of the discount rate $r$ that satisfies the fundamental capital recovery equation:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Net Cash Flow in year $t$ (inflows minus outflows)
- $r$ = Internal Rate of Return (the unknown)
- $n$ = Project economic life in years
- $t$ = Time period (year index, starting from 0)

The condition $NPV(r) = 0$ is the defining mathematical signature of the IRR. Any discount rate **lower than IRR yields a positive NPV**, and any rate **higher than IRR yields a negative NPV**. This crossover behavior at zero NPV forms the geometric backbone of every IRR-based decision in KTU board examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Break-Even Interest Rate" Intuition

Imagine you deposit ₹1,00,000 in a **special savings scheme** that pays you back in unequal annual instalments over 5 years (say ₹30,000, ₹35,000, ₹40,000, ₹25,000, ₹20,000). Now, you ask yourself: **"What is the exact interest rate at which the future value of all my instalments equals the principal I deposited?"** That magical interest rate — where the present value of all future cash inflows exactly recovers the initial investment with zero surplus and zero deficit — is precisely the **Internal Rate of Return**.

In simpler words:
> The IRR is the **"break-even discount rate"** — the rate at which the project neither makes money nor loses money in present-value terms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IRR matters in Energy Auditing:** Energy efficiency projects (e.g., replacing old motors with VFDs, installing LED lighting, recovering waste heat) involve significant upfront capital expenditure followed by **annual energy cost savings**. IRR allows the energy auditor to express the attractiveness of such a project as a single percentage figure, which can be directly compared against the company's **Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)** or the **hurdle rate** set by financial management.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Energy Auditing Context

The following bolded standard metrics are universally used in KTU 2024 scheme evaluations:

- **MARR (Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return)** — the threshold below which a project is rejected.
- **NPV (Net Present Value)** — must equal zero at IRR.
- **PBP (Pay Back Period)** — used alongside IRR as a secondary metric.
- **BCR (Benefit-Cost Ratio)** — alternative capital budgeting criterion.
- **WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)** — typical reference for MARR in industrial settings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IRR is a **discounted cash flow (DCF)** technique and is therefore considered a **superior method** compared to non-DCF methods like simple Payback Period because it accounts for the **time value of money**, **entire project life**, and **profitability per unit of capital invested**.

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Logic of IRR — Structured Stepwise Decomposition

The Internal Rate of Return method operates on a series of structured logical steps. Each step is explained with the **'Why'** (justification) and **'How'** (procedure) to maintain KTU 2024 pedagogical clarity:

**Step 1 — Identify all Cash Flows over Project Life**
- *Why:* IRR requires complete enumeration of every monetary benefit and cost stream.
- *How:* List initial investment (negative cash flow at $t=0$) and subsequent annual net cash inflows (savings minus operating costs) for every year of the project's economic life.

**Step 2 — Set up the NPV Equation and Equate to Zero**
- *Why:* The defining mathematical property of IRR is $NPV = 0$.
- *How:* Substitute all identified cash flows into the standard NPV formula with $r$ as the unknown discount rate.

**Step 3 — Solve the Polynomial Equation for $r$**
- *Why:* The NPV equation becomes a polynomial of degree $n$ in $(1+r)$.
- *How:* Since closed-form solutions are impractical beyond $n = 2$ or $3$, the **trial-and-error method with interpolation** (also called the **Secant/Linear Interpolation Method**) is used.

**Step 4 — Compare IRR with MARR (Hurdle Rate)**
- *Why:* A project is only financially viable if its IRR exceeds the company's minimum acceptable return.
- *How:* Apply the decision rule: $IRR \geq MARR \Rightarrow$ **Accept Project**; otherwise **Reject**.

**Step 5 — Perform Sensitivity Analysis (Optional but Recommended)**
- *Why:* Energy savings projections carry uncertainty (tariff changes, load variations).
- *How:* Recompute IRR under pessimistic and optimistic scenarios.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Boundary Conditions** | **Decision Rule / Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| General NPV Equation | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Currency units (₹, $, etc.); $t$ in years | Foundation for IRR definition |
| IRR Defining Equation | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | $IRR$ expressed as **% per annum** | Set $NPV = 0$ and solve for $r$ |
| Linear Interpolation Formula | $IRR = r_1 + \dfrac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (r_2 - r_1)$ | $r_1 < r_2$, $NPV_1 > 0$, $NPV_2 < 0$ | Approximate IRR between two trial rates |
| Decision Criterion | Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$ | $MARR$ usually $= WACC + Risk Premium$ | Reject if $IRR < MARR$ |
| Project Ranking | Higher $IRR \Rightarrow$ More preferred (for **independent projects** at same investment scale) | Percentage scale | For **mutually exclusive** projects, use incremental IRR |
| Incremental IRR | $IRR(\Delta CF_t)$ between two alternatives | Used when initial costs differ | Compare $\Delta IRR$ with MARR |
| Annuity IRR Approximation | $IRR \approx \dfrac{Average\ Annual\ Savings}{Initial\ Investment}$ for uniform cash flows | Only valid as rough check | Quick sanity test |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The interpolation formula provides an **approximation**, not the exact IRR. The accuracy depends on the **closeness** of $r_1$ and $r_2$ and the **linearity** of NPV between these two points. In KTU exams, the interpolation result is accepted unless the question explicitly demands iteration to refine further.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of IRR in Energy Management

The IRR method has direct and substantial applications in energy management and auditing:

1. **Energy Efficient Equipment Replacement Decisions:** When an auditor proposes replacing an old inefficient boiler with a new one, the IRR of the upgrade quantifies whether the energy savings justify the capital outlay.

2. **Renewable Energy Project Feasibility:** Solar PV installations, wind energy systems, and biogas plants have high upfront costs and long-term savings — IRR captures this structure elegantly.

3. **Comparative Evaluation of Alternatives:** When choosing between multiple energy conservation measures (ECMs), IRR provides a unified percentage metric for direct comparison.

4. **Financing Justification to Management:** A single IRR percentage (e.g., "22.3%") is far more persuasive to financial controllers than a stream of NPV numbers over multiple discount rates.

5. **Compliance with ESCO (Energy Service Company) Contracts:** Performance-based contracts often benchmark savings against an agreed IRR floor.

6. **Risk Assessment in Long-Term Energy Projects:** Higher IRR projects imply greater returns per rupee invested; energy managers use this to prioritize capital allocation.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Interpolation Formula (From First Principles)

The interpolation formula used to estimate IRR between two trial discount rates is **not arbitrary** — it is derived from the principle of **linear interpolation** on the NPV-versus-rate curve.

**Starting Point — Two Known Points on the NPV Curve:**

At discount rate $r_1$, the NPV is $NPV_1$ (positive). At a higher discount rate $r_2$, the NPV is $NPV_2$ (negative). The NPV curve crosses the horizontal axis (i.e., $NPV = 0$) at some rate between $r_1$ and $r_2$.

**Linear Interpolation Assumes a Straight Line Between $(r_1, NPV_1)$ and $(r_2, NPV_2)$:**

By the property of similar triangles, the ratio of vertical distance to horizontal distance is preserved along a straight line:

$$\frac{IRR - r_1}{0 - NPV_1} = \frac{r_2 - r_1}{NPV_2 - NPV_1}$$

**Solving for IRR algebraically:**

$$\frac{IRR - r_1}{-NPV_1} = \frac{r_2 - r_1}{NPV_2 - NPV_1}$$

$$IRR - r_1 = \frac{-NPV_1}{NPV_2 - NPV_1} \times (r_2 - r_1)$$

$$IRR = r_1 + \frac{-NPV_1}{NPV_2 - NPV_1} \times (r_2 - r_1)$$

Since $NPV_2 - NPV_1 = -(NPV_1 - NPV_2)$:

$$\boxed{IRR = r_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (r_2 - r_1)}$$

This is the **canonical KTU interpolation formula** for IRR.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Standard KTU Board Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Type 1): Uniform Annual Savings (Annuity Case)**

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing unit invests **₹5,00,000** in an energy efficiency project. The project generates uniform annual energy cost savings of **₹1,20,000** for **6 years**. The salvage value at the end of year 6 is **₹50,000**. Determine the IRR using the trial-and-error (interpolation) method. Assume $r_1 = 10\%$ and $r_2 = 20\%$ as initial trial rates.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the Cash Flows:**

| Year ($t$) | Cash Flow $CF_t$ (₹) | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-5,00,000$ | Initial Investment (Outflow) |
| 1 to 6 | $+1,20,000$ per year | Annual Energy Savings (Inflow) |
| 6 (additional) | $+50,000$ | Salvage Value (Inflow at $t=6$) |

**Step 2 — Set up the NPV Equation and evaluate at $r_1 = 10\%$:**

$$NPV_{10\%} = -5,00,000 + \sum_{t=1}^{6} \frac{1,20,000}{(1.10)^t} + \frac{50,000}{(1.10)^6}$$

Computing the Annuity Present Value Factor (APVF) for 6 years at 10%:

$$\text{APVF}(10\%, 6) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.5645}{0.10} = 4.3553$$

Computing the Single Payment Present Value Factor (SPPVF) for year 6 at 10%:

$$\text{SPPVF}(10\%, 6) = (1.10)^{-6} = 0.5645$$

Substituting:

$$NPV_{10\%} = -5,00,000 + (1,20,000 \times 4.3553) + (50,000 \times 0.5645)$$

$$NPV_{10\%} = -5,00,000 + 5,22,636 + 28,225$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{10\%} = +50,861 \text{ ₹ (Positive)}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate NPV at $r_2 = 20\%$:**

$$\text{APVF}(20\%, 6) = \frac{1 - (1.20)^{-6}}{0.20} = \frac{1 - 0.3349}{0.20} = 3.3255$$

$$\text{SPPVF}(20\%, 6) = (1.20)^{-6} = 0.3349$$

Substituting:

$$NPV_{20\%} = -5,00,000 + (1,20,000 \times 3.3255) + (50,000 \times 0.3349)$$

$$NPV_{20\%} = -5,00,000 + 3,99,060 + 16,745$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{20\%} = -84,195 \text{ ₹ (Negative)}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Linear Interpolation Formula:**

$$IRR = r_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (r_2 - r_1)$$

$$IRR = 10 + \frac{50,861}{50,861 - (-84,195)} \times (20 - 10)$$

$$IRR = 10 + \frac{50,861}{1,35,056} \times 10$$

$$IRR = 10 + (0.3767 \times 10)$$

$$IRR = 10 + 3.767$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 13.77\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 5 — Decision Rule Application:**

If the company's MARR is, say, **12%**, then since $IRR (13.77\%) > MARR (12\%)$, the project is **accepted**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Type 2): Non-Uniform Cash Flows with No Salvage**

**Problem Statement:**
An energy management project requires an initial investment of **₹8,00,000** and yields the following net cash inflows over 5 years:

| Year | Cash Inflow (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 2,50,000 |
| 2 | 3,00,000 |
| 3 | 2,80,000 |
| 4 | 2,20,000 |
| 5 | 1,80,000 |

Compute IRR using trial rates $r_1 = 15\%$ and $r_2 = 25\%$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — NPV at $r_1 = 15\%$:**

$$NPV_{15\%} = -8,00,000 + \frac{2,50,000}{1.15} + \frac{3,00,000}{(1.15)^2} + \frac{2,80,000}{(1.15)^3} + \frac{2,20,000}{(1.15)^4} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.15)^5}$$

Computing each present value term-by-term:

$$\frac{2,50,000}{1.15} = 2,17,391$$

$$\frac{3,00,000}{1.3225} = 2,26,843$$

$$\frac{2,80,000}{1.5209} = 1,84,103$$

$$\frac{2,20,000}{1.7490} = 1,25,786$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{2.0114} = 89,490$$

Summing the present values:

$$NPV_{15\%} = -8,00,000 + 2,17,391 + 2,26,843 + 1,84,103 + 1,25,786 + 89,490$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{15\%} = +43,613 \text{ ₹ (Positive)}}$$

**Step 2 — NPV at $r_2 = 25\%$:**

$$NPV_{25\%} = -8,00,000 + \frac{2,50,000}{1.25} + \frac{3,00,000}{(1.25)^2} + \frac{2,80,000}{(1.25)^3} + \frac{2,20,000}{(1.25)^4} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.25)^5}$$

Computing each present value term-by-term:

$$\frac{2,50,000}{1.25} = 2,00,000$$

$$\frac{3,00,000}{1.5625} = 1,92,000$$

$$\frac{2,80,000}{1.9531} = 1,43,360$$

$$\frac{2,20,000}{2.4414} = 90,113$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{3.0518} = 58,981$$

Summing the present values:

$$NPV_{25\%} = -8,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 1,92,000 + 1,43,360 + 90,113 + 58,981$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{25\%} = -1,15,546 \text{ ₹ (Negative)}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Interpolation Formula:**

$$IRR = 15 + \frac{43,613}{43,613 - (-1,15,546)} \times (25 - 15)$$

$$IRR = 15 + \frac{43,613}{1,59,159} \times 10$$

$$IRR = 15 + (0.2740 \times 10)$$

$$IRR = 15 + 2.740$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 17.74\% \text{ per annum}}$$

If MARR = 15%, then $IRR (17.74\%) > MARR$ → **Project is financially acceptable**.

### 3.3 Advantages and Limitations of IRR Method (Analytical Matrix)

| **Aspect** | **Advantage** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|
| Time Value of Money | Fully accounted for through discounting | Requires iterative numerical solution |
| Project Life | Considers entire life of project | Multiple IRRs possible for non-conventional cash flows |
| Communication | Single percentage figure easily understood by non-financial managers | Assumes reinvestment at IRR itself (often unrealistic) |
| Ranking | Useful for ranking independent projects | Cannot rank mutually exclusive projects of different scale (use Incremental IRR) |
| Decision Rule | Clear cut-off against MARR | Solution requires educated guess of trial rates |
| Mathematical Nature | Self-correcting — error bounded by interpolation gap | Sensitive to estimation errors in cash flow projections |

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Flowchart — IRR Computation Procedure (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Energy Project] --> B[Enumerate All Cash Flows CFt over Project Life]
    B --> C[Construct NPV Equation with Unknown Rate r]
    C --> D[Choose Two Trial Rates: r1 and r2]
    D --> E[Compute NPV at r1]
    E --> F{NPV at r1 > 0?}
    F -- No --> G[Reduce r1 and Recompute]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute NPV at r2 where r2 > r1]
    H --> I{NPV at r2 < 0?}
    I -- No --> J[Increase r2 and Recompute]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Apply Linear Interpolation Formula]
    K --> L[Calculate Approximate IRR]
    L --> M{IRR >= MARR?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Accept the Energy Project]
    M -- No --> O[Reject the Energy Project]
    N --> P[Document in Energy Audit Report]
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — NPV vs Discount Rate Behavior (Graphical Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Discount Rate r] --> B[NPV Curve - Decreasing Function]
    B --> C{NPV = 0?}
    C -- Yes --> D[This rate is IRR]
    C -- No --> E[NW direction]
    E --> F[r less than IRR yields Positive NPV]
    E --> G[r greater than IRR yields Negative NPV]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — IRR-Based Project Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Energy Project Identified] --> Q1{Independent or Mutually Exclusive?}
    Q1 -- Independent --> A1[Compute IRR for each project]
    A1 --> A2[Accept if IRR exceeds MARR]
    Q1 -- Mutually Exclusive --> B1[Rank by Initial Investment]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Incremental Cash Flows]
    B2 --> B3[Compute Incremental IRR]
    B3 --> B4{Incremental IRR exceeds MARR?}
    B4 -- Yes --> B5[Choose Higher Investment Alternative]
    B4 -- No --> B6[Choose Lower Investment Alternative]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Position of IRR on the NPV-Rate Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NPV-axis vertical] --> B[Discount Rate-axis horizontal]
    B --> C[IRR point lies exactly on horizontal axis where NPV equals zero]
    C --> D[Left of IRR: Positive NPV Region]
    C --> E[Right of IRR: Negative NPV Region]
    D --> F[Project Acceptable Zone]
    E --> G[Project Rejection Zone]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The IRR is the precise point where the NPV-versus-rate curve crosses the horizontal axis. All discount rates to the **left** of IRR give **positive NPV** (project gains value), while rates to the **right** give **negative NPV** (project destroys value).
