## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
> Define Internal Rate of Return (IRR). State the decision criterion for accepting an energy project based on IRR.

**Model Answer:**
The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of a project is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value (NPV) of all cash flows over the project life equals zero. Mathematically, IRR is the value of $r$ satisfying $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$. The decision criterion is: **Accept the project if IRR is greater than or equal to the Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR)**; otherwise reject the project.

*(2 marks for definition with equation; 1 mark for decision rule)*

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
> List any three advantages of the IRR method over the simple Payback Period method for evaluating energy conservation projects.

**Model Answer:**
1. IRR considers the **time value of money**, while Payback Period does not discount future cash flows.
2. IRR accounts for **cash flows over the entire project life**, whereas Payback ignores benefits occurring after the payback year.
3. IRR expresses profitability as a **percentage rate**, enabling direct comparison with the company's cost of capital (MARR).

*(1 mark per advantage)*

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the sign convention — initial investment must be entered as a **negative** cash flow, while savings must be **positive**. Reversing this yields NPV values with wrong signs and a completely incorrect IRR.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** When using the interpolation formula, students frequently invert the numerator and denominator or use $r_2 - r_1$ in the wrong direction. Always ensure: **lower rate corresponds to positive NPV; higher rate to negative NPV**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to add the salvage value at the end of the project life. KTU examiners explicitly test this in **Type 1 annuity + salvage** combinations.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to compare the computed IRR against the given MARR and **omit the final accept/reject statement**. This costs 1–2 marks even when the numerical answer is correct.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Internal Rate of Return (IRR) in the context of energy management and auditing. Discuss the significance of IRR in evaluating energy efficiency projects and compare it briefly with the Net Present Value (NPV) method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company is considering the installation of a solar PV system requiring an initial investment of **₹12,00,000**. The expected annual energy savings are **₹3,00,000** per year for **5 years**, with no salvage value at the end. The company's Minimum Acceptable Rate of Return (MARR) is **10%**. Determine the IRR using the trial-and-error interpolation method with $r_1 = 12\%$ and $r_2 = 20\%$. State clearly whether the project should be accepted. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation:**

The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value of a project's cash flows equals zero. In energy management and auditing, IRR represents the effective rate at which an energy conservation project earns returns on the invested capital through energy cost savings. The decision rule is simple: **if IRR ≥ MARR, accept; otherwise reject.**

**Significance in Energy Auditing:**
- Provides a **percentage metric** that directly compares against the firm's cost of capital.
- Considers **complete project lifetime** savings, not just early-year benefits.
- Enables **objective ranking** of multiple competing energy conservation measures.
- Facilitates **communication** with finance teams and top management using a familiar rate-of-return vocabulary.

**Comparison with NPV:**

| **Parameter** | **IRR** | **NPV** |
|---|---|---|
| Output Type | A rate (%) | An absolute monetary value (₹) |
| Reinvestment Assumption | At IRR itself | At MARR (more realistic) |
| Decision Rule | Compare with MARR | Compare with 0 |
| Multiple Solutions | Possible (for non-conventional flows) | Unique solution |
| Scale Sensitivity | Independent of project scale | Reflects absolute profitability |

*(Allocate ~3.5 marks for IRR explanation, ~2 marks for significance in energy auditing, ~1.5 marks for NPV comparison table)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Cash Flows:**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) |
|---|---|
| 0 | $-12,00,000$ |
| 1 to 5 | $+3,00,000$ per year |
| 5 (salvage) | None (₹0) |

**Step 2 — NPV at $r_1 = 12\%$:**

$$\text{APVF}(12\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = 3.6048$$

$$NPV_{12\%} = -12,00,000 + (3,00,000 \times 3.6048)$$

$$NPV_{12\%} = -12,00,000 + 10,81,440$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{12\%} = -1,18,560 \text{ ₹ (Negative)}}$$

**Step 3 — NPV at $r_2 = 20\%$:**

$$\text{APVF}(20\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.20)^{-5}}{0.20} = \frac{1 - 0.4019}{0.20} = 2.9906$$

$$NPV_{20\%} = -12,00,000 + (3,00,000 \times 2.9906)$$

$$NPV_{20\%} = -12,00,000 + 8,97,180$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{20\%} = -3,02,820 \text{ ₹ (Negative)}}$$

**Step 4 — Problem Identified:** Both NPVs are **negative**. The trial rates must be adjusted. Let us try a lower $r_1 = 5\%$:

$$\text{APVF}(5\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.05)^{-5}}{0.05} = \frac{1 - 0.7835}{0.05} = 4.3295$$

$$NPV_{5\%} = -12,00,000 + (3,00,000 \times 4.3295)$$

$$NPV_{5\%} = -12,00,000 + 12,98,850$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{5\%} = +98,850 \text{ ₹ (Positive)}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpolation between $r_1 = 5\%$ and $r_2 = 12\%$:**

$$IRR = 5 + \frac{98,850}{98,850 - (-1,18,560)} \times (12 - 5)$$

$$IRR = 5 + \frac{98,850}{2,17,410} \times 7$$

$$IRR = 5 + (0.4547 \times 7)$$

$$IRR = 5 + 3.183$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 8.18\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $IRR (8.18\%) < MARR (10\%)$, **the solar PV project should be REJECTED**.

*(Marks allocation: 1 mark cash flow tabulation; 1 mark each for NPV at $r_1=12\%$, $r_2=20\%$, $r_1=5\%$; 2 marks for interpolation formula application; 1 mark final IRR with units; 1 mark decision rule and final statement)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)**

**(a)** What are the limitations of the IRR method? Under what circumstances can the IRR method give multiple or no solutions? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial energy audit recommends replacing an old compressor with a high-efficiency model. The data is as follows: Initial cost = **₹6,00,000**, Annual energy savings = **₹1,80,000** for 7 years, Additional maintenance cost savings = **₹20,000** in year 3 and year 5 only. Salvage value at end of year 7 = **₹40,000**. Calculate the IRR using interpolation with trial rates $r_1 = 8\%$ and $r_2 = 18\%$. State your recommendation if MARR = 12%. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Limitations of IRR:**

1. **Multiple IRR Problem:** When cash flows change sign more than once (e.g., negative-positive-negative), the NPV equation becomes a higher-degree polynomial yielding **multiple real roots**, each claiming to be a valid IRR.

2. **No Real Solution:** For certain unconventional cash flow patterns, the polynomial has **no real roots** — IRR simply cannot be computed.

3. **Reinvestment Rate Assumption:** IRR implicitly assumes that all intermediate cash inflows are reinvested **at the IRR itself**, which is often unrealistic. The true opportunity cost is closer to MARR.

4. **Scale Insensitivity for Mutually Exclusive Projects:** A small project with high IRR may be preferred over a larger project with slightly lower IRR, even when the larger project adds more absolute value. The **Incremental IRR analysis** must be used to correct this.

5. **Computational Difficulty:** Requires iterative solution or interpolation; cannot be computed by simple formula for $n > 3$.

6. **Sensitivity to Estimation Errors:** Small errors in projected cash flows can cause large changes in computed IRR.

**Multiple/No Solutions Condition:**
Multiple IRRs arise when the cash flow stream has **more than one sign change**. By Descartes' rule of signs, the number of possible positive real roots equals the number of sign changes or is less by an even number. No real IRR exists if the polynomial has no positive real roots.

*(Allocate ~3 marks for limitations list, ~2 marks for multiple IRR explanation, ~1 mark for sign-change condition, ~1 mark for Descartes' rule mention)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate Cash Flows:**

| Year | Cash Flow (₹) | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-6,00,000$ | Initial Investment |
| 1 | $+1,80,000$ | Energy Savings |
| 2 | $+1,80,000$ | Energy Savings |
| 3 | $+2,00,000$ | Energy + Maintenance Savings |
| 4 | $+1,80,000$ | Energy Savings |
| 5 | $+2,00,000$ | Energy + Maintenance Savings |
| 6 | $+1,80,000$ | Energy Savings |
| 7 | $+2,20,000$ | Energy Savings + Salvage |

**Step 2 — NPV at $r_1 = 8\%$:**

$$NPV_{8\%} = -6,00,000 + \frac{1,80,000}{1.08} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.08)^2} + \frac{2,00,000}{(1.08)^3} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.08)^4} + \frac{2,00,000}{(1.08)^5} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.08)^6} + \frac{2,20,000}{(1.08)^7}$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.08} = 1,66,667$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.1664} = 1,54,321$$

$$\frac{2,00,000}{1.2597} = 1,58,767$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.3605} = 1,32,304$$

$$\frac{2,00,000}{1.4693} = 1,36,120$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.5869} = 1,13,432$$

$$\frac{2,20,000}{1.7138} = 1,28,371$$

Summing present values of inflows:

$$= 1,66,667 + 1,54,321 + 1,58,767 + 1,32,304 + 1,36,120 + 1,13,432 + 1,28,371 = 9,89,982$$

$$NPV_{8\%} = -6,00,000 + 9,89,982$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{8\%} = +3,89,982 \text{ ₹ (Positive)}}$$

**Step 3 — NPV at $r_2 = 18\%$:**

$$NPV_{18\%} = -6,00,000 + \frac{1,80,000}{1.18} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.18)^2} + \frac{2,00,000}{(1.18)^3} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.18)^4} + \frac{2,00,000}{(1.18)^5} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.18)^6} + \frac{2,20,000}{(1.18)^7}$$

Computing each term:

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.18} = 1,52,542$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.3924} = 1,29,272$$

$$\frac{2,00,000}{1.6430} = 1,21,728$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.9388} = 92,840$$

$$\frac{2,00,000}{2.2878} = 87,422$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{2.6996} = 66,679$$

$$\frac{2,20,000}{3.1855} = 69,062$$

Summing present values of inflows:

$$= 1,52,542 + 1,29,272 + 1,21,728 + 92,840 + 87,422 + 66,679 + 69,062 = 7,19,545$$

$$NPV_{18\%} = -6,00,000 + 7,19,545$$

$$\boxed{NPV_{18\%} = +1,19,545 \text{ ₹ (Positive)}}$$

**Step 4 — Both NPVs Positive. Try $r_3 = 25\%$:**

Computing at 25%:

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.25} = 1,44,000$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{1.5625} = 1,15,200$$

$$\frac{2,00,000}{1.9531} = 1,02,400$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{2.4414} = 73,728$$

$$\frac{2,00,000}{3.0518} = 65,536$$

$$\frac{1,80,000}{3.8147} = 47,189$$

$$\frac{2,20,000}{4.7684} = 46,138$$

Sum of inflows at 25%: $1,44,000 + 1,15,200 + 1,02,400 + 73,728 + 65,536 + 47,189 + 46,138 = 5,94,191$

$$NPV_{25\%} = -6,00,000 + 5,94,191 = -5,809 \text{ ₹ (Slightly Negative)}$$

**Step 5 — Interpolation between $r_2 = 18\%$ and $r_3 = 25\%$:**

$$IRR = 18 + \frac{1,19,545}{1,19,545 - (-5,809)} \times (25 - 18)$$

$$IRR = 18 + \frac{1,19,545}{1,25,354} \times 7$$

$$IRR = 18 + (0.9537 \times 7)$$

$$IRR = 18 + 6.676$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 24.68\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $IRR (24.68\%) > MARR (12\%)$, the high-efficiency compressor replacement project is **ACCEPTED**.

*(Marks allocation: 1 mark cash flow tabulation with maintenance savings included correctly; 2 marks each for NPV at 8% and 18%; 1 mark recognizing need to extend trial to 25%; 1.5 marks for correct interpolation; 0.5 mark final IRR with units; 1 mark for accept/reject decision)*

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] IRR is the **discount rate** at which **NPV equals zero**.
- [ ] The fundamental IRR equation is $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$.
- [ ] Decision rule: **Accept** if $IRR \geq MARR$; **Reject** if $IRR < MARR$.
- [ ] Use **Linear Interpolation Formula**: $IRR = r_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2}(r_2 - r_1)$.
- [ ] Always ensure $NPV_1 > 0$ at lower rate $r_1$ and $NPV_2 < 0$ at higher rate $r_2$.
- [ ] Include **salvage value** as an additional positive cash flow in the final year.
- [ ] Use **APVF** (Annuity Present Value Factor) for uniform cash flows; compute term-by-term for **non-uniform** cash flows.
- [ ] For **mutually exclusive projects** of differing scale, compute **Incremental IRR**.
- [ ] IRR assumes **reinvestment at IRR**; NPV assumes reinvestment at MARR.
- [ ] **Multiple IRRs** occur with non-conventional cash flows (multiple sign changes).
- [ ] IRR is a **DCF method** — superior to simple Payback Period for energy audits.
- [ ] Final answer must always include the **percentage unit (%)** and a clear **accept/reject statement**.
- [ ] In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, present cash flows in a **tabulated form** for clarity and easy verification.
- [ ] Always show **trial rate selection reasoning** when both NPVs come out positive or both negative.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** When both trial NPVs come out with the **same sign**, immediately recognize this and adjust the trial rate before proceeding. Examiners often test this diagnostic ability by giving deliberately close trial rates that yield NPVs of the same sign.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Value Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Present Value Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Present Value Method (PVM)**, also widely known as the **Net Present Value (NPV) Method** or **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Method**, is a rigorous time-adjusted economic analysis technique that evaluates the financial worthiness of an energy conservation project (ECP) by discounting all future cash inflows and outflows back to a common baseline — the present day — using a predefined **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** or **Discount Rate (i)**. A project is considered **economically viable if and only if its NPV ≥ 0** at the chosen MARR.

In the context of **Energy Management and Auditing (EMA)**, the Present Value Method is the *gold standard* recommended by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** and the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** for evaluating capital-intensive energy efficiency retrofits — such as replacement of standard-efficiency motors with IE3/IE4 premium-efficiency motors, VFD installation, HVAC retrofits, lighting upgrades, and waste heat recovery systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ice-Cream Money" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine your father gives you **₹100 today** OR **₹100 one year from now**. Which would you pick? Of course, the ₹100 *today* — because:
> 1. You can deposit it in a bank and earn interest (say 8%), making it ₹108 next year.
> 2. Inflation will erode the purchasing power of ₹100 received later (a bar of chocolate costing ₹100 today may cost ₹108 next year).
> 3. There is an inherent *risk* in waiting — what if the promise is not kept?
> 
> **Therefore, money has a TIME VALUE.** A rupee in hand today is worth MORE than a rupee promised in the future.

The Present Value Method simply **mathematically quantifies this intuition**: it asks the question — *"If a future cash flow is worth ₹X after 'n' years, what is its equivalent value in today's rupees?"* The answer is obtained by **discounting** it back through a factor called the **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)**, denoted as $(P/F, i, n) = \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Present Value Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any PV calculation rests on **three mandatory inputs**:
> 
> | # | Input Parameter | Symbol | Typical Engineering Source |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Discount Rate / MARR** | $i$ | Company's cost of capital (typically 10%–15% in Indian industries) |
> | 2 | **Project Economic Life** | $n$ | Useful life of the energy-efficient equipment (years) |
> | 3 | **Net Annual Cash Flow** | $A$ | Annual energy cost savings MINUS additional O&M costs |

## 1.4 Why Simple Payback Method is INSUFFICIENT — The Need for PV

> [!WARNING]
> The **Simple Payback Period Method** (taught in Module 3) ignores:
> - The **time value of money** (₹1 today ≠ ₹1 tomorrow)
> - **Cash flows occurring AFTER the payback period** (it completely ignores them!)
> - **Salvage value** of equipment at end-of-life
> 
> The **Present Value Method corrects all three deficiencies**, making it the most academically rigorous and BEE-recommended approach for KTU University Examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Core Economic Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The Present Value Method operationalizes the following logical chain:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Cash Flow Stream:** Enumerate the initial capital outlay ($P$, a *negative* cash flow at $t=0$) and the series of net annual cash flows ($A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$) over the project's economic life.

- **Step 2 — Choose the Discount Rate ($i$):** Select the **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)**, representing the opportunity cost of capital. For energy projects, this is usually the company's weighted average cost of capital (WACC), typically 10%–15%.

- **Step 3 — Apply the Time Value Adjustment:** Multiply each future cash flow $A_t$ occurring at the end of year $t$ by the discount factor $\frac{1}{(1+i)^t}$ to bring it to "present-day rupees."

- **Step 4 — Sum the Present Values:** Algebraically sum all discounted cash flows along with the initial investment. The result is the **Net Present Value (NPV)**.

- **Step 5 — Apply the Decision Rule:**
  - If $\text{NPV} \geq 0$ → **ACCEPT** the energy project (it earns at least the MARR).
  - If $\text{NPV} < 0$ → **REJECT** the energy project (it earns less than the MARR).

- **Step 6 — Compare Mutually Exclusive Alternatives:** When choosing between competing energy options (e.g., LED vs. CFL, VFD vs. soft-starter), compute the NPV of each and select the one with the **highest NPV**, *provided* the incremental NPV of the higher-cost option is also positive.

## 2.2 The Six Fundamental Discounting Factors (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> These six factors form the **alphabet of engineering economics**. Master them — they appear in nearly every KTU Part-B question.

| # | Factor Name | Symbol | Converts | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $P \rightarrow F$ | $(1+i)^n$ |
| 2 | Single Payment Present Worth | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $F \rightarrow P$ | $\frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$ |
| 3 | Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $A \rightarrow P$ | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ |
| 4 | Capital Recovery (CR) | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $P \rightarrow A$ | $\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ |
| 5 | Sinking Fund | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $F \rightarrow A$ | $\frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ |
| 6 | Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $A \rightarrow F$ | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Pair Rule:** $(P/A, i, n) = \frac{1}{(A/P, i, n)}$ and $(P/F, i, n) = \frac{1}{(F/P, i, n)}$. Always check this for verification during KTU exams.

## 2.3 The Master NPV Equation

For an energy conservation project with initial capital investment $P$, uniform annual net savings $A$ over $n$ years, and salvage value $S$ at the end of year $n$, the NPV at discount rate $i$ is:

$$
\text{NPV} = -P + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + S \cdot (P/F, i, n)
$$

If annual savings are **non-uniform** ($A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ varying year-to-year due to tariff escalation or degradation), the general form is:

$$
\text{NPV} = -P + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{A_t}{(1+i)^t} + \frac{S}{(1+i)^n}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Industrial Sector:** Evaluating boiler retrofit, waste heat recovery, cogeneration, compressed air system optimization.
> - **Commercial Buildings:** HVAC chiller replacement, building management system (BMS) installation, LED retrofit.
> - **Utilities:** Power plant design choices, renewable integration (solar/wind) vs. conventional grid extension.
> - **BEE PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme:** Mandatory for industries to compute NPV of energy-saving projects to claim Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$

This is the most frequently used factor in KTU energy economics problems. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Start with a geometric series of discounted cash flows:**

$$
P = \frac{A_1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A_2}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{A_3}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{A_n}{(1+i)^n}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume uniform annual cash flow** ($A_1 = A_2 = \cdots = A_n = A$):

$$
P = \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $A$ and recognize the geometric series:**

$$
P = A \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Let $r = \frac{1}{1+i}$. The sum of a finite geometric series** $\sum_{k=1}^{n} r^k = \frac{r(1-r^n)}{1-r}$:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{1+i} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{1}{1+i}\right)^n\right]}{1 - \frac{1}{1+i}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the denominator** $1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}$:

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{1+i} \cdot \left[\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i)^n}\right]}{\frac{i}{1+i}}
$$

**Step 6 — Cancel common terms** $\frac{1}{1+i}$:

$$
\boxed{\,P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} = A \cdot (P/A, i, n)\,}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Example 1 — Motor Replacement (KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam Style — 14 Marks Pattern]**

**Problem Statement:** A textile mill is evaluating the replacement of an old standard-efficiency motor with a premium-efficiency IE3 motor. The data is given below:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Motor Rating | 30 kW |
| Old Motor Efficiency ($\eta_{old}$) | 88% |
| New Motor Efficiency ($\eta_{new}$) | 94% |
| Cost of New Motor | ₹1,80,000 |
| Installation & Commissioning | ₹20,000 |
| Annual Operating Hours | 4,500 hours/year |
| Electricity Tariff | ₹8.50 per kWh |
| Project Life | 12 years |
| Discount Rate (MARR) | 12% per annum |
| Salvage Value of New Motor | ₹15,000 |

**Find:** (a) Annual energy & cost savings, (b) Net Present Value (NPV), (c) State whether the project should be accepted.

### Part (a) — Annual Savings Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute input power to old motor:**

$$
P_{in,old} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{old}} = \frac{30}{0.88} = 34.0909 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute input power to new motor:**

$$
P_{in,new} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{new}} = \frac{30}{0.94} = 31.9149 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute annual energy savings in kWh:**

$$
\Delta E = (P_{in,old} - P_{in,new}) \times \text{Operating Hours}
$$

$$
\Delta E = (34.0909 - 31.9149) \times 4500 = 2.1760 \times 4500 = 9,792.0 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual monetary savings (assume no additional O&M):**

$$
A = \Delta E \times \text{Tariff} = 9792.0 \times 8.50 = \text{₹}83,232 \text{ per year}
$$

### Part (b) — NPV Calculation

**Step 5 — Total Initial Investment:**

$$
P = 1,80,000 + 20,000 = \text{₹}2,00,000
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $(P/A, 12\%, 12)$ using the derived formula:**

$$
(1+i)^n = (1.12)^{12}
$$

$$
(1.12)^{12} = 3.8959 \quad \text{(standard tabulated value)}
$$

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 12) = \frac{(1.12)^{12} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{12}} = \frac{3.8959 - 1}{0.12 \times 3.8959} = \frac{2.8959}{0.4675} = 6.1944
$$

**Step 7 — Compute $(P/F, 12\%, 12)$ for the salvage value:**

$$
(P/F, 12\%, 12) = \frac{1}{(1.12)^{12}} = \frac{1}{3.8959} = 0.2567
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute into the master NPV equation:**

$$
\text{NPV} = -P + A \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 12) + S \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 12)
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -2,00,000 + 83,232 \times 6.1944 + 15,000 \times 0.2567
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -2,00,000 + 5,15,572.4 + 3,850.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{NPV} = \text{₹}3,19,422.9\,}
$$

### Part (c) — Decision

Since $\text{NPV} = +\text{₹}3,19,423 > 0$, the motor replacement project is **economically attractive** and **must be ACCEPTED**. The mill earns MORE than its MARR of 12% on this investment.

## 3.3 Numerical Example 2 — Comparison of Two Alternatives

**Problem:** Two lighting retrofit options are available for a factory:

| Option | Initial Cost (₹) | Annual Savings (₹) | Life (years) | Salvage (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A — LED Retrofit | 4,00,000 | 1,20,000 | 8 | 20,000 |
| B — Solar-LED Hybrid | 8,00,000 | 2,80,000 | 8 | 50,000 |

**MARR = 10% p.a. Compute NPV of both options.**

**Step 1 — Compute the discount factors at $i = 10\%$, $n = 8$:**

$$
(1.10)^8 = 2.1436
$$

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 8) = \frac{2.1436 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.1436} = \frac{1.1436}{0.21436} = 5.3349
$$

$$
(P/F, 10\%, 8) = \frac{1}{2.1436} = 0.4665
$$

**Step 2 — NPV of Option A (LED):**

$$
\text{NPV}_A = -4,00,000 + 1,20,000 \times 5.3349 + 20,000 \times 0.4665
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_A = -4,00,000 + 6,40,188 + 9,330 = \text{₹}2,49,518
$$

**Step 3 — NPV of Option B (Solar-LED Hybrid):**

$$
\text{NPV}_B = -8,00,000 + 2,80,000 \times 5.3349 + 50,000 \times 0.4665
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_B = -8,00,000 + 14,93,772 + 23,325 = \text{₹}7,17,097
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** Since $\text{NPV}_B = \text{₹}7,17,097 > \text{NPV}_A = \text{₹}2,49,518$, and both are positive, **Option B (Solar-LED Hybrid) is the superior choice** under the Present Value Method.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Present Value Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Project Identification] --> A2[Data Collection]
    A2 --> A3[Initial Investment P]
    A2 --> A4[Annual Cash Flow A]
    A2 --> A5[Project Life n]
    A2 --> A6[Discount Rate MARR i]
    A2 --> A7[Salvage Value S]
    A3 --> B1[Apply Discounting Factors]
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B1
    A6 --> B1
    A7 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Compute PW of Annual Savings]
    B1 --> C2[Compute PW of Salvage Value]
    C1 --> D1[NPV equals negative P plus PW of A plus PW of S]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1{NPV greater than or equal to 0}
    E1 -- YES --> F1[ACCEPT PROJECT]
    E1 -- NO --> F2[REJECT PROJECT]
    F1 --> G1[Compare with Alternatives]
    F2 --> G3[Re evaluate or Abandon]
    G1 --> E1
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic Flow for Single vs. Mutually Exclusive Projects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start NPV Analysis] --> S2{Single Project or Multiple Alternatives}
    S2 -- Single Project --> S3[Compute NPV at MARR]
    S3 --> S4{NPV positive}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Accept and Implement]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Reject or Seek Subsidy]
    S2 -- Multiple Alternatives --> S7[Compute NPV of EACH]
    S7 --> S8[Rank by NPV descending]
    S8 --> S9[Perform Incremental NPV B minus A]
    S9 --> S10{Incremental NPV positive}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Choose Higher Cost Option]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Choose Lower Cost Option]
```

## 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline Diagram (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Year 0] -->|minus P| T1[Year 1]
    T1 -->|plus A| T2[Year 2]
    T2 -->|plus A| T3[Year 3]
    T3 -->|plus A| T4[Year dots]
    T4 -->|plus A| TN[Year n]
    TN -->|plus S| TEND[End of Life]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Timeline:** At $t = 0$, the firm pays out the initial investment $P$ (outflow). From years 1 to $n$, it receives uniform annual net savings $A$ (inflows). At the end of year $n$, it additionally recovers the salvage value $S$. The Present Value Method mathematically shifts all these future cash flows back to a single point at $t = 0$ using the discount factor $\frac{1}{(1+i)^t}$.

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Role of Present Value in Energy Project Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Energy Audit Findings] --> E2[Identify EE Opportunities]
    E2 --> E3[Estimate Capital Cost P]
    E2 --> E4[Estimate Annual Savings A]
    E2 --> E5[Estimate Life n and Salvage S]
    E3 --> PV[Present Value Method]
    E4 --> PV
    E5 --> PV
    PV --> R1[Compute NPV]
    R1 --> R2[Compare with MARR]
    R2 --> R3{Decision}
    R3 -- Accept --> R4[Implementation and M and V]
    R3 -- Reject --> R5[Document and Archive]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Present Value Method. Why is it considered superior to the Simple Payback Method for evaluating energy conservation projects?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Present Value Method (PVM)** is a time-adjusted economic analysis technique that determines the worth today of all future cash flows associated with an energy project by discounting them at a chosen **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** using the factor $\frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$.

It is **superior to the Simple Payback Method** for the following reasons:
1. It explicitly accounts for the **time value of money** (₹1 today ≠ ₹1 tomorrow).
2. It considers **all cash flows over the entire project life**, not just up to the payback year.
3. It incorporates the **salvage value** of equipment at end-of-life.
4. It provides a **direct measure of profitability** in monetary terms (rupees), making it consistent with the BEE's PAT scheme guidelines.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the significance of the discount rate ($i$) in the Present Value Method. What is the typical range used in Indian industrial projects?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **discount rate ($i$)**, also called the **MARR** or **hurdle rate**, represents the **opportunity cost of capital** — i.e., the minimum return an investor expects from a project. In the Present Value Method, $i$ plays a critical role:

- A **higher discount rate** makes future cash flows worth LESS in present terms, thereby **reducing NPV** and making fewer projects acceptable.
- A **lower discount rate** increases the present value of future cash flows, making **more projects attractive**.

**Typical Range in Indian Industrial Sector:** The MARR generally lies between **10% and 15% per annum**, often set equal to the company's **weighted average cost of capital (WACC)**. For government-sponsored energy efficiency schemes (e.g., BEE, EESL), the social discount rate of **8%–10%** may be used.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Full 14 Marks]`

A process industry plans to replace its existing boiler with a high-efficiency condensing boiler. The technical-economic data are:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Existing Boiler Efficiency | 72% |
| New Boiler Efficiency | 88% |
| Annual Fuel Consumption (existing) | 15,000 tonnes of coal |
| Calorific Value of Coal | 5,500 kcal/kg |
| Cost of New Boiler | ₹85,00,000 |
| Installation Cost | ₹5,00,000 |
| Coal Price | ₹8,000 per tonne |
| Annual O&M Cost Increase | ₹2,50,000 |
| Project Life | 15 years |
| Discount Rate (MARR) | 12% |
| Salvage Value | ₹3,00,000 |

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Compute the annual fuel cost savings and the Net Present Value (NPV) of the project.**

**Step 1 — Calculate the annual fuel saved (in tonnes):**

Heat required per year (constant for both cases):

$$
Q = 15,000 \times 1000 \times 5500 = 8.25 \times 10^{10} \text{ kcal/year}
$$

Fuel required with new boiler (more efficient, so less fuel needed):

$$
\text{Fuel}_{new} = 15,000 \times \frac{0.72}{0.88} = 12,272.73 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

Fuel saved annually:

$$
\Delta F = 15,000 - 12,272.73 = 2,727.27 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual cost savings (gross):**

$$
S_{gross} = 2,727.27 \times 8,000 = \text{₹}2,18,18,160 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Net annual cash flow** (subtract extra O&M):

$$
A = 2,18,18,160 - 2,50,000 = \text{₹}2,15,68,160 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 4 — Total initial investment:**

$$
P = 85,00,000 + 5,00,000 = \text{₹}90,00,000
$$

**Step 5 — Look up discount factors at 12%, n = 15:**

$$
(1.12)^{15} = 5.4736
$$

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 15) = \frac{5.4736 - 1}{0.12 \times 5.4736} = \frac{4.4736}{0.6568} = 6.8109
$$

$$
(P/F, 12\%, 15) = \frac{1}{5.4736} = 0.1827
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the NPV formula:**

$$
\text{NPV} = -90,00,000 + 2,15,68,160 \times 6.8109 + 3,00,000 \times 0.1827
$$

$$
\text{NPV} = -90,00,000 + 14,68,86,938 + 54,810
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{NPV} = \text{₹}13,79,41,748\,}
$$

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Comment on the acceptability. Also, calculate the Discounted Payback Period.**

**Acceptability:** Since NPV is **highly positive (₹13.79 Crore)**, the project is **strongly recommended for implementation**. The investment earns far more than the MARR of 12%.

**Discounted Payback Period:**

Cumulative discounted cash flow must reach ₹90,00,000. Discounted annual cash flow:

$$
D = 2,15,68,160 \times 6.8109 / 15 \quad \text{(approx. average annual PW)}
$$

A simpler approach: compute cumulative PW year-by-year using $(P/A, 12\%, t)$:

| Year ($t$) | $(P/A, 12\%, t)$ | Cumulative PW (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8929 | 1,92,57,168 |
| 2 | 1.6901 | 3,64,53,308 |
| 3 | 2.4018 | 5,18,07,395 |
| 4 | 3.0373 | 6,55,18,672 |
| 5 | 3.6048 | 7,77,60,224 |

The investment of ₹90,00,000 is recovered **between Year 1 and Year 2**.

$$
\text{Discounted Payback} = 1 + \frac{90,00,000 - 1,92,57,168}{3,64,53,308 - 1,92,57,168} \times 1 \approx \text{approx 1.4 years}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract extra O&M cost** from gross savings (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Wrong use of calorific value** — students often multiply incorrectly. Remember: 1 tonne = 1000 kg.
> 3. **Tabulation errors** in looking up $(P/A, 12\%, 15)$ — always cross-verify using the formula.
> 4. **Ignoring salvage value** in the NPV formula — fetches 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the decision explicitly** — always conclude with "ACCEPT" or "REJECT".

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Alternative Choice 14 Marks]`

A hospital is evaluating two HVAC chiller alternatives for its new building. The data are:

| Parameter | Chiller X (Conventional) | Chiller Y (Magnetic Bearing) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | ₹30,00,000 | ₹45,00,000 |
| Annual Energy Cost | ₹9,50,000 | ₹6,20,000 |
| Annual Maintenance | ₹1,20,000 | ₹80,000 |
| Project Life | 20 years | 20 years |
| Salvage Value | ₹1,00,000 | ₹2,00,000 |

**MARR = 15% p.a.** Evaluate both alternatives using the Present Value Method and recommend the better option.

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks] — Compute NPV of both Chillers X and Y.**

**Step 1 — Net annual cash flow (savings relative to do-nothing baseline):**

For Chiller X, the cost is an *outflow*, but in PV method we treat total cost as a *negative* cash flow. NPV of *cost* of X:

Annual cost of X = 9,50,000 + 1,20,000 = ₹10,70,000

Discount factor at 15%, 20 years:

$$
(1.15)^{20} = 16.3665
$$

$$
(P/A, 15\%, 20) = \frac{16.3665 - 1}{0.15 \times 16.3665} = \frac{15.3665}{2.4550} = 6.2593
$$

$$
(P/F, 15\%, 20) = \frac{1}{16.3665} = 0.0611
$$

**Step 2 — Total Present Worth of Cost for Chiller X:**

$$
\text{PW}_X = 30,00,000 + 10,70,000 \times 6.2593 - 1,00,000 \times 0.0611
$$

$$
\text{PW}_X = 30,00,000 + 66,97,451 - 6,110 = \text{₹}96,91,341
$$

**Step 3 — Total Present Worth of Cost for Chiller Y:**

Annual cost of Y = 6,20,000 + 80,000 = ₹7,00,000

$$
\text{PW}_Y = 45,00,000 + 7,00,000 \times 6.2593 - 2,00,000 \times 0.0611
$$

$$
\text{PW}_Y = 45,00,000 + 43,81,510 - 12,220 = \text{₹}88,69,290
$$

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks] — Recommend the better alternative using Incremental NPV analysis.**

**Step 1 — Compute incremental cost (Y − X):**

$$
\Delta P = 45,00,000 - 30,00,000 = \text{₹}15,00,000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute incremental annual savings (X − Y):**

$$
\Delta A = 10,70,000 - 7,00,000 = \text{₹}3,70,000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute incremental salvage value:**

$$
\Delta S = 2,00,000 - 1,00,000 = \text{₹}1,00,000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute incremental NPV (of going from X to Y):**

$$
\Delta \text{NPV} = -15,00,000 + 3,70,000 \times 6.2593 + 1,00,000 \times 0.0611
$$

$$
\Delta \text{NPV} = -15,00,000 + 23,15,941 + 6,110 = \text{₹}8,22,051
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:** Since $\Delta \text{NPV} = +\text{₹}8,22,051 > 0$, the **extra investment of ₹15 lakh in Chiller Y is justified**. Hence, **Chiller Y (Magnetic Bearing)** is the recommended alternative.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing "savings" with "cost":** When computing NPV of *cost*, both operating cost and maintenance are added (as outflows) — students often mistakenly subtract.
> 2. **Skipping incremental analysis:** When both alternatives are acceptable independently, you MUST perform incremental NPV analysis for full marks.
> 3. **Sign errors:** The extra cost of Y over X is a *negative* initial cash flow (−ΔP). Get this right!

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Definition:** Present Value Method discounts all future cash flows to year zero using a chosen MARR.
- **Master Equation:** $\text{NPV} = -P + A \cdot (P/A, i, n) + S \cdot (P/F, i, n)$
- **Decision Rule:** Accept if NPV ≥ 0; Reject if NPV < 0.
- **Most-used Factors:**
  - $(P/F, i, n) = \frac{1}{(1+i)^n}$
  - $(P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$
- **Typical MARR in India:** 10%–15% p.a.
- **Energy Audit Linkage:** Present Value Method is the **BEE-recommended** approach for evaluating energy conservation projects under the **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme**.
- **Comparison Rule:** Use **Incremental NPV analysis** when both alternatives have NPV > 0; choose the higher-cost option only if incremental NPV > 0.
- **Key Advantages over Simple Payback:**
  1. Accounts for time value of money.
  2. Considers entire project life (not just payback).
  3. Incorporates salvage value.
  4. Provides a direct rupee measure of profitability.
- **Limitation:** Requires a reliable estimate of MARR and project life — sensitivity analysis is recommended.
- **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
  1. Sign convention errors (cash outflow = negative).
  2. Forgetting to add installation/commissioning to initial cost.
  3. Confusing P, F, A — always match the factor to the conversion.
  4. Using Simple Payback factors in a PV problem.
  5. Not stating the final decision explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Life Cycle Costing Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Life Cycle Costing Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Life Cycle Costing (LCC)** is a systematic economic evaluation methodology that quantifies the **total ownership cost** of an asset, system, or energy-efficient project over its **entire operational life span**, expressed in terms of Present Value (PV). In the context of Energy Management & Auditing, LCC transforms an energy investment decision from a short-term capital expenditure focus to a **holistic, time-adjusted cost-of-ownership perspective**, enabling auditors to recommend the most economically efficient alternative when comparing competing energy-efficient technologies.

Mathematically, the LCC approach states:

$$
\text{LCC} = C_{ic} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{C_{ot} + C_{mt}}{(1+i)^t} - \frac{S}{(1+i)^N}
$$

Where every term represents a **discounted cash flow** brought back to a common temporal baseline (typically Year 0, i.e., "today").

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Buying a Car Analogy**: Imagine you are choosing between two cars.
> - **Car A (₹8 Lakh)**: Cheap to buy, but burns more fuel (₹15,000/month) and needs frequent servicing (₹20,000/year).
> - **Car B (₹12 Lakh)**: Expensive to buy, but is a hybrid — very low fuel cost (₹4,000/month) and rare servicing (₹5,000/year).
>
> At first glance, Car A looks cheaper. However, if you keep the car for **10 years**, the **total money flowing out of your pocket** (purchase + fuel + service - resale value) will almost certainly make Car B the **smarter buy**. That "total money over 10 years, adjusted for the time-value of money" is exactly what **Life Cycle Costing** calculates.

For an **energy auditor**, this same logic is applied to motors, chillers, boilers, pumps, lighting systems, and transformers.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Standard Parameter | Typical Range / Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Discount Rate (i)** | **8% – 15%** (often taken as 10%) | Reflects cost of capital / opportunity cost |
| **Project Life (N)** | **10 – 25 years** | Useful life of energy equipment |
| **Inflation Rate** | **5% – 7%** | Used to escalate future O&M costs |
| **Real Discount Rate** | **i − f ≈ 3% – 6%** | Used when costs are already escalated |
| **Evaluation Currency** | **₹ (INR)** | All PV calculations in same base year |

### 1.4 Why LCC is Critical in Energy Auditing

- Energy-efficient equipment has **higher First Cost** but **lower Operating Cost**.
- A naive payback-only analysis might reject profitable long-term investments.
- LCC provides a **Board-approved, audit-defensible** metric that captures the *true* cost of ownership.
- Aligns with **ISO 15686-5** (Service Life Planning) and **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** investment appraisal guidelines in India.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the LCC Methodology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LCC methodology follows a **7-step logical ladder**. Master these as KTU frequently asks 7-mark questions testing *procedure* rather than numbers.

1. **Define the Decision Boundary** — Identify the alternatives (e.g., Standard IE2 Motor vs Premium IE4 Motor).
2. **Establish the Study Period (N)** — Must be identical for all alternatives to enable fair comparison.
3. **Identify Cost Categories** — Break every cost into:
   - Initial Costs (C_ic)
   - Annual Operating Costs (C_ot) — energy, fuel, labor
   - Annual Maintenance Costs (C_mt) — spares, downtime, servicing
   - Disposal / Decommissioning Costs
   - Salvage Value (S) at end of life
4. **Select the Discount Rate (i)** — Based on Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) or hurdle rate.
5. **Escalate Costs (if required)** — Apply inflation/escalation rate to convert *real* costs into *nominal* future costs.
6. **Discount to Present Value (PV)** — Bring every future cash flow to Year 0.
7. **Aggregate & Compare** — Sum all PV components → LCC. Select alternative with **minimum LCC**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat-sheet** you must memorize for KTU ESE. Every numerical problem will draw from these identities.

| # | Name of Factor | Mathematical Form | Use Case in LCC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Payment Present Worth (SPPW) | $\text{SPPW} = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^N}$ | Find PV of a single future cost F |
| 2 | Single Payment Future Worth (SPFW) | $\text{SPFW} = P \cdot (1+i)^N$ | Find future value of present cost P |
| 3 | Uniform Series Present Worth (USPW) | $\text{USPW} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N}$ | Find PV of equal annual payments A over N years |
| 4 | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | $\text{CRF} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | Convert a present cost P into equivalent annual cost A |
| 5 | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | $\text{SFF} = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ | Find annual deposit to reach future amount F |
| 6 | Arithmetic Gradient Present Worth | $\text{PV}_{\text{grad}} = G \cdot \left[\dfrac{(1+i)^N - iN - 1}{i^2(1+i)^N}\right]$ | Costs that increase by constant G each year |
| 7 | Master LCC Equation | $\text{LCC} = C_{ic} + (C_o + C_m)\cdot \text{USPW} - S \cdot \text{SPPW}$ | Ultimate objective of the entire analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick**: Always state which factor you are using — examiners allocate **1 mark** just for correctly *naming* the factor. Do not skip this in your solution.

### 2.3 Cost Components in Detail

- **C_ic (Initial Cost):** Purchase price, installation, commissioning, civil works. Occurs at Year 0 → no discounting needed.
- **C_ot (Operating Cost):** Energy cost (kWh × tariff), fuel cost (litres × price), operator wages. Often the **largest** component over the life cycle.
- **C_mt (Maintenance Cost):** Preventive maintenance, spares, breakdown costs, condition-monitoring overheads.
- **S (Salvage Value):** Resale or scrap value at end of life. **Subtracted** from LCC because it is a *negative cost* (inflow).
- **Disposal Cost:** Environmental compliance, dismantling, recycling — added to LCC at end of life.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Sector:** Comparing IE3 vs IE4 induction motors (₹ vs ₹50,000 lifetime energy bill).
- **Buildings:** HVAC system selection — VRF vs Chilled-Water Plant.
- **Renewables:** Solar PV grid-tied vs Diesel Generator backup.
- **Utilities:** Pump selection in water supply schemes — capital vs operating trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why We Discount Money (Time Value of Money)

**Premise:** ₹100 today is *worth more* than ₹100 received one year from now, because today's ₹100 can be invested at interest rate $i$ and grow.

Let $P$ = money today, $F$ = money after 1 year, $i$ = annual interest rate.

$$
F = P + P \cdot i = P(1 + i)
$$

Extending to **N years** (compounding annually):

$$
F = P(1 + i)^N
$$

**Inverting** to find PV from a known future cost:

$$
P = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^N}
$$

This is the **Single Payment Present Worth (SPPW)** factor — the foundation of all LCC mathematics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Uniform Series Present Worth (USPW)

**Premise:** An equal annual amount $A$ is paid at the **end** of each year for $N$ years. Find the equivalent single present sum $P$.

The series of payments is:

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^1}, \quad A_2 = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^2}, \quad \ldots, \quad A_N = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^N}
$$

Summing the geometric series:

$$
P = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^t} = A \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{N} (1+i)^{-t}
$$

Using the closed-form of geometric series:

$$
P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-N}}{i} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N}
$$

This is the **USPW factor**, denoted in textbooks as $(P/A, i, N)$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Format)

**Problem Statement:**
An energy auditor must choose between two motors for a continuous-duty fan application:

| Parameter | Motor X (Standard) | Motor Y (Premium) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | ₹40,000 | ₹65,000 |
| Annual Energy Cost | ₹32,000 | ₹22,000 |
| Annual Maintenance | ₹3,000 | ₹1,500 |
| Salvage Value | ₹4,000 | ₹8,000 |
| Useful Life | 12 years | 12 years |

Discount rate **i = 10%**. Compute LCC for both and recommend the best alternative.

---

**Step 1: Compute the USPW factor at i = 10%, N = 12**

$$
\text{USPW} = \dfrac{(1+0.10)^{12} - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1+0.10)^{12}}
$$

$$
(1.10)^{12} = 3.1384 \quad (\text{standard table value})
$$

$$
\text{USPW} = \dfrac{3.1384 - 1}{0.10 \times 3.1384} = \dfrac{2.1384}{0.31384} \approx 6.8137
$$

---

**Step 2: Compute the SPPW factor at i = 10%, N = 12**

$$
\text{SPPW} = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^{12}} = \dfrac{1}{3.1384} \approx 0.3186
$$

---

**Step 3: LCC of Motor X**

$$
\text{LCC}_X = C_{ic} + (C_o + C_m) \cdot \text{USPW} - S \cdot \text{SPPW}
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_X = 40{,}000 + (32{,}000 + 3{,}000)(6.8137) - 4{,}000(0.3186)
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_X = 40{,}000 + (35{,}000)(6.8137) - 1{,}274.40
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_X = 40{,}000 + 238{,}479.50 - 1{,}274.40
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LCC}_X \approx \text{₹ } 2{,}77{,}205}
$$

---

**Step 4: LCC of Motor Y**

$$
\text{LCC}_Y = 65{,}000 + (22{,}000 + 1{,}500)(6.8137) - 8{,}000(0.3186)
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_Y = 65{,}000 + (23{,}500)(6.8137) - 2{,}548.80
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_Y = 65{,}000 + 160{,}121.95 - 2{,}548.80
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LCC}_Y \approx \text{₹ } 2{,}22{,}573}
$$

---

**Step 5: Decision**

Since **LCC_Y (₹2,22,573) < LCC_X (₹2,77,205)**, the **Premium Motor Y** is the economically superior choice, saving approximately **₹54,632** over its 12-year life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity Insight**: The decision is sensitive to the discount rate. If the management's hurdle rate drops to 5%, the USPW factor grows to ~8.863, amplifying the savings of Motor Y even further. Always conduct a **sensitivity analysis** in your KTU answer for full marks.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Decision Criterion | Best Method | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Quick screening | Simple Payback Period | Ignores time-value of money |
| Intermediate | Discounted Payback | Still ignores post-payback cash flows |
| **Comprehensive (KTU preferred)** | **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** | **Captures entire life & discount rate** |
| Comparative ranking | Net Present Value (NPV) | Used when benefits ≠ costs (e.g., revenue projects) |
| Equivalent annual comparison | Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) | Useful when alternatives have **different lives** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse **LCC** with **NPV**. Remember:
> - **LCC** is used when comparing **costs only** (typical energy-efficiency investment).
> - **NPV** is used when comparing **costs vs. revenues** (profit-generating projects).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The LCC Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Energy Project] --> B[Identify Alternatives A and B]
    B --> C[Set Study Period N]
    C --> D[Enumerate Cost Categories]
    D --> D1[Initial Cost C_ic]
    D --> D2[Annual Operating Cost C_ot]
    D --> D3[Annual Maintenance Cost C_mt]
    D --> D4[Salvage Value S]
    D1 --> E[Select Discount Rate i]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Escalate Future Costs if Needed]
    F --> G[Compute USPW and SPPW Factors]
    G --> H[Calculate LCC for Alternative A]
    G --> I[Calculate LCC for Alternative B]
    H --> J{Compare LCC_A vs LCC_B}
    I --> J
    J -->|LCC_A less| K[Select Alternative A]
    J -->|LCC_B less| L[Select Alternative B]
    K --> M[Generate Sensitivity Report]
    L --> M
    M --> N[End: Recommendation to Management]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an LCC Calculation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP1[Cost Data Input Module] --> ENG[Energy Cost Calculator]
    INP2[Equipment Specification] --> ENG
    ENG --> PV[Present Value Engine]
    INP3[Discount Rate and Life] --> PV
    PV --> SUM[Cost Aggregator]
    INP4[Salvage and Disposal Data] --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[LCC Output and Ranking]
    OUT --> SENS[Sensitivity Analysis Module]
    SENS --> RPT[Final Audit Report]
```

### 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline (Schematic Representation)

```
Year:    0           1       2       3    ...        N
         |           |       |       |              |
         |-----------A-------A-------A--------------A----->
         |  C_ic                             (C_o + C_m each year)
         |
         |                                              |
         |                                              v
         |                                              S (Salvage inflow at Year N)
```

| Visual Element | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **C_ic** (large downward arrow at Year 0) | Initial Capital Investment |
| **A, A, A, ... A** (small downward arrows Years 1–N) | Annual Operating + Maintenance Costs |
| **S** (upward arrow at Year N) | Salvage Value (cash inflow, hence subtracted) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

**Q1. Define Life Cycle Costing (LCC). Mention any two of its cost components.**
*(Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand | CO Mapping: CO4)*

**Model Answer:**
Life Cycle Costing (LCC) is an economic evaluation technique that sums the **Present Value (PV)** of all costs incurred over the entire useful life of an asset — from acquisition to disposal.

**Two Cost Components:**
1. **Initial Cost (C_ic):** Purchase, installation, and commissioning cost incurred at Year 0.
2. **Annual Operating Cost (C_ot):** Recurring cost of energy, fuel, and consumables over the asset life.

*(Any other valid component — Maintenance, Salvage, Disposal — also acceptable. 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per component.)*

---

**Q2. What is the role of the discount rate (i) in LCC analysis?**
*(Cognitive Level: Understand | CO Mapping: CO4)*

**Model Answer:**
The discount rate $i$ represents the **opportunity cost of capital** — the return that money could earn if invested elsewhere. Its role in LCC is to:
- Convert future costs (Years 1 to N) into **equivalent present-day values**.
- A **higher discount rate** de-emphasizes distant future costs, favoring alternatives with low upfront cost.
- A **lower discount rate** gives more weight to long-term operating savings, favoring energy-efficient alternatives with higher initial cost.

*(3 marks: 1 mark concept, 2 marks for the two implications.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Important:** Both Question A and Question B carry equal marks. Only **one** is to be attempted.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**A factory is evaluating two lighting options for a 10,000 sq. ft. production floor:**

| Parameter | Option P (LED) | Option Q (Conventional) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | ₹8,00,000 | ₹3,50,000 |
| Annual Energy Cost | ₹1,20,000 | ₹3,00,000 |
| Annual Maintenance | ₹20,000 | ₹60,000 |
| Salvage Value | ₹50,000 | ₹10,000 |
| Useful Life | 10 years | 10 years |

**Discount rate i = 10% per annum.**

**(a)** Compute the Life Cycle Cost (LCC) of each option using the Present Worth method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Recommend the economically superior alternative and justify your decision with the calculated savings. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step 1 — Compute factors at i = 10%, N = 10:**

$$
\text{USPW} = \dfrac{(1.10)^{10} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}} = \dfrac{1.5937}{0.25937} \approx 6.1446
$$

$$
\text{SPPW} = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^{10}} \approx 0.3855
$$

**Step 2 — LCC of Option P (LED):**

$$
\text{LCC}_P = 8{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}20{,}000 + 20{,}000)(6.1446) - 50{,}000(0.3855)
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_P = 8{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000(6.1446) - 19{,}275
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_P = 8{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}60{,}244 - 19{,}275 = \text{₹ } 16{,}40{,}969
$$

**Step 3 — LCC of Option Q (Conventional):**

$$
\text{LCC}_Q = 3{,}50{,}000 + (3{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000)(6.1446) - 10{,}000(0.3855)
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_Q = 3{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000(6.1446) - 3{,}855
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_Q = 3{,}50{,}000 + 22{,}12{,}056 - 3{,}855 = \text{₹ } 25{,}58{,}201
$$

**(b) Recommendation:**

Since **LCC_P (₹16,40,969) < LCC_Q (₹25,58,201)**, **Option P (LED)** is the economically superior alternative.

**Net Savings over 10 years:**

$$
\text{Savings} = \text{LCC}_Q - \text{LCC}_P = 25{,}58{,}201 - 16{,}40{,}969 = \text{₹ } 9{,}17{,}232
$$

**Justification:** Although Option P requires a higher initial investment (₹4.5 lakh more), its drastically lower annual energy and maintenance costs — when discounted to present value — more than offset the upfront premium. The investment is recommended despite the longer payback period (~2.5 years), because LCC captures the full life-cycle economic advantage.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**A pump manufacturer offers two pump models for a continuous cooling-water circulation duty:**

| Parameter | Pump R | Pump S |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | ₹2,00,000 | ₹3,20,000 |
| Annual Energy Cost | ₹85,000 | ₹45,000 |
| Annual Maintenance | ₹15,000 | ₹8,000 |
| Salvage Value | ₹10,000 | ₹25,000 |
| Useful Life | 8 years | 8 years |

**Discount rate i = 12% per annum.**

**(a)** Compute the LCC of both pumps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the company insists on a 4-year simple payback, does Pump S still qualify? Compare with your LCC-based recommendation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step 1 — Factors at i = 12%, N = 8:**

$$
\text{USPW} = \dfrac{(1.12)^{8} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{8}}
$$

$$
(1.12)^{8} = 2.4760
$$

$$
\text{USPW} = \dfrac{2.4760 - 1}{0.12 \times 2.4760} = \dfrac{1.4760}{0.29712} \approx 4.9676
$$

$$
\text{SPPW} = \dfrac{1}{2.4760} \approx 0.4039
$$

**Step 2 — LCC of Pump R:**

$$
\text{LCC}_R = 2{,}00{,}000 + (85{,}000 + 15{,}000)(4.9676) - 10{,}000(0.4039)
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_R = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000(4.9676) - 4{,}039
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_R = 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}96{,}760 - 4{,}039 = \text{₹ } 6{,}92{,}721
$$

**Step 3 — LCC of Pump S:**

$$
\text{LCC}_S = 3{,}20{,}000 + (45{,}000 + 8{,}000)(4.9676) - 25{,}000(0.4039)
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_S = 3{,}20{,}000 + 53{,}000(4.9676) - 10{,}097.50
$$

$$
\text{LCC}_S = 3{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}63{,}283 - 10{,}098 = \text{₹ } 5{,}73{,}185
$$

**(b) Payback Analysis for Pump S:**

Incremental investment = ₹3,20,000 − ₹2,00,000 = ₹1,20,000
Annual savings (energy + maintenance) = (85,000 + 15,000) − (45,000 + 8,000) = ₹47,000

$$
\text{Simple Payback} = \dfrac{1{,}20{,}000}{47{,}000} \approx 2.55 \text{ years}
$$

**Conclusion:**
- **Payback Criterion:** Pump S pays back in **~2.55 years < 4 years** → ✅ Qualifies.
- **LCC Criterion:** LCC_S < LCC_R by ₹1,19,536 → ✅ Pump S is superior.

Both criteria agree in this case, but **LCC provides the stronger, time-value-aware justification** for the auditor's recommendation. If the discount rate had been very high (e.g., 20%), payback might favor Pump R, but LCC would still correctly identify Pump S as the long-term winner.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Valuation Key Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to name the factor** — When using USPW or CRF, explicitly write *"Using Uniform Series Present Worth factor (USPW) at i = 10%, N = 12"*. Examiners allocate **1 mark** for factor identification alone.
> 2. **Mixing real and nominal rates** — If energy costs are escalated by inflation, the discount rate used must be the **nominal** rate. If costs are *not* escalated, use the **real** discount rate $i_r = (1+i)/(1+f) - 1$. Mixing them gives wrong LCC.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract salvage value** — Salvage is a *negative cost* (inflow). Missing this sign flips the entire recommendation.
> 4. **Comparing alternatives with different lives** — When lives differ, use the **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** method, not raw LCC. KTU has asked this as a 14-mark question previously.
> 5. **No sensitivity analysis** — Always run the LCC calculation for at least one alternative discount rate (e.g., 8% and 15%) to demonstrate robustness. Examiners reward this with **bonus marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **LCC = Sum of Present Values of ALL costs** over the asset's life minus discounted salvage value.
- [x] Master equation: $\text{LCC} = C_{ic} + (C_o + C_m)\cdot \text{USPW} - S \cdot \text{SPPW}$
- [x] **USPW factor formula:** $\dfrac{(1+i)^N - 1}{i(1+i)^N}$
- [x] **SPPW factor formula:** $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^N}$
- [x] **CRF formula:** $\dfrac{i(1+i)^N}{(1+i)^N - 1}$ — used when lives of alternatives **differ**.
- [x] Always state **factor name, i, and N** before plugging values.
- [x] Salvage value is **subtracted** (cash inflow at end of life).
- [x] Operating cost (energy + fuel) is usually the **largest** LCC component — hence energy-efficient investments look expensive initially but **dominate** over time.
- [x] LCC ≠ NPV. Use LCC for **cost-only** energy projects; use NPV when **revenues** are involved.
- [x] Sensitivity analysis on **discount rate** is a KTU bonus-mark move.
- [x] Aligned with **BEE (India)** investment appraisal guidelines and **ISO 15686-5**.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4, Topic: Life Cycle Costing Approach**

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT746 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT & AUDITING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Case Study** in Energy Management & Auditing (EMA) refers to the systematic, comprehensive, and detailed investigation of energy consumption, conversion, and conservation practices within a specific industrial plant, commercial facility, or utility system. It serves as a real-world validation framework for the theoretical principles of energy auditing (ISO 50002), pinch analysis, and economic feasibility assessment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMRT746 - Module 4):**
> "Case studies covering energy audit of at least five industries — Sugar, Pulp & Paper, Cement, Steel, Textile, Electrical Utility (T&D), and building sector including hospitals and commercial complexes."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Medical Diagnosis" Framework:**
> Think of an energy case study exactly like a doctor examining a patient. The patient (industry) reports symptoms (high electricity bills). The doctor (energy auditor) orders diagnostic tests (energy audit), identifies the disease (inefficient boiler / oversized motor / uninsulated steam line), prescribes medicine (Energy Conservation Measure — ECM), and finally estimates the recovery time (Payback Period). A **case study** is the documented clinical record of this entire doctor-patient interaction — it shows what was found, what was prescribed, and how the patient recovered financially through energy savings.

### 1.3 Purpose of Industrial Case Studies in KTU Context

1. **Validation of Audit Methodology** — Applies the 3-tier audit (Walk-through, Detailed, Investment-grade) to real plants.
2. **Sector Benchmarking** — Establishes Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) baselines.
3. **ECM Identification** — Surfaces engineering interventions like waste heat recovery (WHR), cogeneration, and VFD retrofits.
4. **Economic Translation** — Converts kWh savings into ₹/$ payback using Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR).
5. **Technology Mapping** — Demonstrates deployment of high-efficiency motors, soft starters, and trigeneration in different process layouts.

### 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics Used in Case Studies

The following **bold** metrics are universally accepted indicators:

- **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption)** — *Energy used per unit of product* (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/kg).
- **PEC (Plant Energy Consumption)** — *Total energy input to plant boundary*.
- **ECR (Energy Cost of Rejection)** — *Cost of waste heat dumped to environment*.
- **η (Thermal / Electrical Efficiency)** — *Useful energy output ÷ Energy input × 100%*.
- **kWh/₹ (Energy Productivity)** — *Value added per unit energy consumed*.
- **Sankey Width** — *Visualizes proportional energy flows in a process*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generic Workflow of an Industrial Energy Case Study

Every KTU case study follows this structured analytical pipeline:

1. **Plant Boundary Definition** — Mark the physical limits (Battery Limits) for the energy balance.
2. **Process Flow Mapping** — Document unit operations (crushing, grinding, drying, baking, etc.).
3. **Data Acquisition** — Collect utility bills, log-sheet data, and instrumentation readings.
4. **Baseline Energy Modeling** — Construct the **Energy Balance Equation**:

$$E_{in} = E_{product} + E_{waste} + E_{losses}$$

   Where $E_{in}$ = total input energy (fuel + electricity), $E_{product}$ = useful energy in output stream, $E_{waste}$ = rejected energy (flue gas, hot condensate, blow-down), $E_{losses}$ = radiation, conduction, and unaccounted losses.

5. **Inefficiency Diagnosis** — Compare actual SEC with **BAT (Best Available Technology)** benchmarks.
6. **ECM Portfolio Development** — Generate a list of recommended Energy Conservation Measures.
7. **Economic Prioritization** — Rank ECMs using payback, NPV, and IRR.
8. **Implementation Roadmap** — Schedule ECMs across short-term (<1 yr), medium-term (1–3 yr), and long-term (>3 yr) horizons.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for Case Study numerical problems in KTU ESE. All equations are mathematically verified and align with BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) national standards.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{product}}$ | GJ/tonne or kWh/kg |
| 2 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_f \times GCV_f} \times 100$ | %; $m_s$ = steam mass, $m_f$ = fuel mass, $h_g$ = enthalpy of steam, $h_{fw}$ = feedwater enthalpy, $GCV_f$ = Gross Calorific Value |
| 3 | Boiler Efficiency (Indirect) | $\eta_{boiler} = 100 - (L_{flue} + L_{rad} + L_{moist} + L_{CO} + L_{unacc})$ | Sum of all losses in % |
| 4 | Cogeneration Power Output | $P_{cogen} = \eta_{gen} \times m_f \times GCV_f$ | kW; $\eta_{gen}$ ≈ 30–40% |
| 5 | Annual Energy Savings | $AES = (Q_{saved} \times Hours_{annual})$ | kWh/yr or MJ/yr |
| 6 | Annual Monetary Savings | $AMS = AES \times Rate_{unit}$ | ₹/yr or \$/yr |
| 7 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{CapEx}{AMS}$ | Years |
| 8 | Discounted Payback | $DPP = \dfrac{\ln\left(\dfrac{AMS}{AMS - r \cdot CapEx}\right)}{\ln(1+r)}$ | Years; $r$ = discount rate |
| 9 | Net Present Value | $NPV = -CapEx + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹; CF = cash flow |
| 10 | Internal Rate of Return | $0 = -CapEx + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | %; IRR > hurdle rate → accept |
| 11 | Heat Recovery Savings | $Q_{rec} = m \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | kW; $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temperature drop |
| 12 | Power Factor Correction | $Q_c = P \times (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)$ | kVAr; $\phi_1$ = old PF angle, $\phi_2$ = target PF angle |
| 13 | Cogeneration Heat-to-Power Ratio | $HPR = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{W_{electric}}$ | Dimensionless; 1–10 typical |
| 14 | Energy Cost of Rejection | $ECR = \dfrac{E_{waste} \times C_{fuel}}{\eta_{boiler}}$ | ₹/GJ wasted |
| 15 | Carbon Footprint | $CO_{2,emit} = E_{fuel} \times EF_{fuel}$ | tCO₂/yr; $EF$ = emission factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Boundary Condition:** In all KTU numerical problems, assume **8760 operating hours/year** for continuous plants, **6000 hours/year** for 2-shift operations, and **3000 hours/year** for 1-shift operations, unless stated otherwise.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar Industry**: Drives the case for bagasse-based cogeneration and condensate recovery.
- **Cement Industry**: Highlights pre-heater and pre-calciner kiln efficiency.
- **Steel Industry**: Validates top-pressure recovery turbine (TRT) and coke dry quenching (CDQ).
- **Pulp & Paper**: Demonstrates black liquor evaporation economics.
- **Textile**: Anchors steam optimization and process heat integration.
- **T&D Utility**: Anchors distribution loss reduction and capacitor bank sizing.
- **Hospitals**: Validates HVAC, steam sterilizer, and lighting retrofits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Case Study Problems

### 3.1 Worked Case Study — Sugar Industry (Bagasse Cogeneration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):**
> A sugar mill processes **2500 tonnes of cane per day** during a 160-day crushing season. The mill generates **12 tonnes of bagasse per hour** (moisture 50%, GCV = 7.5 MJ/kg). Existing conventional boilers operate at **65% efficiency** with no cogeneration. The plant currently purchases **4.5 MW** of grid electricity at ₹7.50/kWh. Propose a **back-pressure cogeneration (5 bar, 220 °C)** scheme and determine:
> (a) Available thermal energy from bagasse.
> (b) Steam generation rate.
> (c) Electrical power generated via turbine (assume $\eta_{gen}$ = 30%, mechanical $\eta_{m}$ = 95%).
> (d) Annual monetary savings.
> (e) Simple payback if CapEx = ₹22 crore.

---

#### Step 1 — Bagasse Mass Flow Rate Calculation

We must first convert cane processing rate to bagasse generation rate.

$$Cane = 2500 \text{ tonnes/day} = \dfrac{2500 \times 1000}{24} \text{ kg/hr} = 104{,}166.67 \text{ kg/hr}$$

The bagasse yield per tonne of cane in a typical Indian sugar mill is **30%**.

$$m_{bagasse} = 0.30 \times 104{,}166.67 = 31{,}250 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**However**, the problem explicitly states bagasse generation = 12 tonnes/hr (likely from the boiler-feed perspective post-extraction). We use the given data:

$$m_{bagasse} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

Since bagasse is 50% moist, dry solid content = 50% × 12,000 = 6000 kg/hr.

---

#### Step 2 — Thermal Energy Released by Combustion

We compute energy based on dry bagasse GCV at 7.5 MJ/kg (note: GCV is usually quoted on dry basis for biomass).

$$Q_{in} = m_{bagasse,dry} \times GCV_{bagasse}$$

$$Q_{in} = 6000 \times 7.5 = 45{,}000 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

Convert to kW:

$$Q_{in} = \dfrac{45{,}000}{3.6} = 12{,}500 \text{ kW (thermal input)}$$

---

#### Step 3 — Useful Steam Energy Output (with 65% boiler efficiency)

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{steam}}{Q_{in}} \implies Q_{steam} = 0.65 \times 12{,}500$$

$$Q_{steam} = 8125 \text{ kW (thermal)}$$

Equivalent steam generation rate using $h_g - h_{fw} \approx 2200$ kJ/kg at 5 bar saturated steam:

$$m_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{steam} \times 3600}{h_g - h_{fw}} = \dfrac{8125 \times 3600}{2200}$$

$$m_{steam} = 13{,}295.45 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 13.3 \text{ tonnes/hr}$$

---

#### Step 4 — Cogeneration Electrical Output

For a **back-pressure turbine**, assume 75% of the steam enthalpy drop is converted to mechanical work, then to electrical.

$$P_{gross,mech} = m_{steam} \times (h_{in} - h_{out}) \times \eta_{turb}$$

Steam inlet at 5 bar saturated: $h_g \approx 2748$ kJ/kg. Back-pressure exhaust at 1.5 bar (process steam): $h_g \approx 2693$ kJ/kg — but typical approach uses isentropic enthalpy drop:

$$(h_{in} - h_{out}) \approx 350 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$P_{gross,mech} = 13{,}295.45 \times 350 \times 10^{-3} = 4653.4 \text{ kW (mechanical)}$$

Apply generator + mechanical efficiencies:

$$P_{electric} = P_{gross,mech} \times \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{m}$$

$$P_{electric} = 4653.4 \times 0.30 \times 0.95 = 1326.2 \text{ kW}$$

Therefore, **cogeneration produces ≈ 1.33 MW**, which offsets part of the 4.5 MW grid import.

---

#### Step 5 — Annual Monetary Savings Calculation

Net grid offset = 1.326 MW continuously across the 160-day season.

Operating hours per season = $160 \times 24 = 3840$ hours.

$$AES = 1.326 \times 1000 \times 3840 = 5{,}091{,}840 \text{ kWh/season}$$

Convert to annual equivalent (assuming sugar mill operates 8 months equivalent, but for single season as KTU norm):

$$AMS = 5{,}091{,}840 \times 7.50 = ₹3{,}81{,}88{,}800 / \text{season}$$

$$AMS \approx ₹3.82 \text{ crore/season}$$

---

#### Step 6 — Simple Payback Period

$$SPP = \dfrac{CapEx}{AMS} = \dfrac{22}{3.82}$$

$$SPP \approx 5.76 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The payback exceeds 5 years, indicating bagasse cogeneration needs **PLG/REC (Renewable Energy Certificate)** revenue and **accelerated depreciation** benefits to be financially attractive — a recurring KTU exam insight.

---

### 3.2 Worked Case Study — Cement Industry (Pre-Heater Waste Heat Recovery)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A cement plant with a **4-stage pre-heater kiln** vents **220,000 Nm³/hr** of flue gas at **380 °C** (ambient 30 °C, $c_p$ = 1.34 kJ/Nm³·K). Design a **waste heat recovery (WHR) boiler** producing 15 bar saturated steam, and compute: (a) Recoverable thermal power, (b) Steam generation rate, (c) Annual monetary savings at ₹6.50/kWh of equivalent process heat, (d) Payback if CapEx = ₹14 crore.

---

#### Step 1 — Recoverable Thermal Power

$$Q_{rec} = \dot{V} \times c_p \times \Delta T$$

$$Q_{rec} = 220{,}000 \times 1.34 \times (380 - 30)$$

$$Q_{rec} = 220{,}000 \times 1.34 \times 350$$

$$Q_{rec} = 103{,}180{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

Convert to kW:

$$Q_{rec} = \dfrac{103{,}180{,}000}{3600} = 28{,}661.11 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### Step 2 — Useful Heat After WHR Boiler Efficiency (Assume 70% net recovery)

$$Q_{useful} = 0.70 \times 28{,}661.11 = 20{,}062.78 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### Step 3 — Steam Generation Rate at 15 bar

At 15 bar saturated steam: $h_g - h_{fw} \approx 2025$ kJ/kg (using 105 °C feedwater).

$$m_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{useful} \times 3600}{2025} = \dfrac{20{,}062.78 \times 3600}{2025}$$

$$m_{steam} = 35{,}667 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 35.67 \text{ tonnes/hr}$$

---

#### Step 4 — Annual Monetary Savings

Convert thermal kW to equivalent process heat kWh per hour:

$$AES = 20{,}062.78 \times 8760 \text{ hr/yr} = 175{,}749{,}953 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

$$AMS = 175.75 \times 10^6 \times 6.50 = ₹1{,}142.37 \times 10^6 / \text{yr}$$

$$AMS \approx ₹114.24 \text{ crore/yr}$$

---

#### Step 5 — Payback Period

$$SPP = \dfrac{14}{114.24} = 0.123 \text{ years}$$

$$SPP \approx 45 \text{ days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Massive Payback Insight:** Cement WHR is one of the most lucrative ECMs in industry — payback often under 6 months, making it a board-favorite KTU 14-mark question.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sector-Wise Case Study Snapshot

| Industry | Primary Energy Source | Key ECM | Typical SEC Range | Typical Payback |
|----------|----------------------|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Sugar** | Bagasse + Grid | Cogeneration, Condensate recovery | 3.0–5.0 GJ/tonne cane | 4–6 yr |
| **Cement** | Coal + Pet-coke | WHR, VRM (Vertical Roller Mill) | 3.0–4.5 GJ/tonne clinker | 0.5–2 yr |
| **Steel** | Coke oven gas + Grid | TRT, CDQ, BF gas recovery | 18–22 GJ/tonne crude steel | 2–4 yr |
| **Pulp & Paper** | Coal + Biomass | Black liquor evaporator, digester heat | 7–10 GJ/tonne paper | 3–5 yr |
| **Textile** | Grid + Furnace oil | Steam optimization, VFDs | 1.8–2.5 kWh/kg fabric | 1–3 yr |
| **T&D Utility** | Grid distribution | HT/LT line reconductoring, capacitor banks | — (loss reduction 12% → 6%) | 2–4 yr |
| **Hospitals** | Grid + Diesel | HVAC retrofits, LED, steam traps | 200–300 kWh/bed-day | 1–2 yr |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Energy Audit Case Study Methodology Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture flow** of an industrial energy case study, applicable to any sector (Sugar, Cement, Steel, etc.).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Selection & Boundary Definition] --> B[Process Flow Diagram PFD Mapping]
    B --> C[Data Acquisition: Utility Bills, Log Sheets]
    C --> D[Baseline Energy Modeling: SEC and Sankey]
    D --> E{Energy Inefficiency Identified?}
    E -- Yes --> F[ECM Portfolio Generation]
    E -- No --> G[Reporting and Benchmarking]
    F --> H[Technical Feasibility Analysis]
    H --> I[Economic Evaluation: NPV, IRR, Payback]
    I --> J{Project Acceptable?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Implementation Roadmap]
    J -- No --> L[Re-engineer or Reject ECM]
    K --> M[Post-Implementation M&V Verification]
    M --> N[Final Case Study Report]
    G --> N
    L --> F
```

### 4.2 Cogeneration System Architecture (Sugar Industry Use Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FUEL_SYSTEM [Fuel Supply Subsystem]
        A1[Bagasse Conveyor] --> A2[Boiler Furnace]
    end

    subgraph BOILER [Steam Generation Subsystem]
        A2 --> B1[Water Wall Tubes]
        B1 --> B2[Steam Drum 65 bar 450 C]
    end

    subgraph TURBINE [Power Generation Subsystem]
        B2 --> C1[Back Pressure Turbine]
        C1 --> C2[Generator 30 percent eta]
        C2 --> C3[Plant Electrical Bus 11 kV]
        C2 -.-> C4[Process Steam 5 bar 220 C]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS [Plant Process Load]
        C4 --> D1[Evaporator]
        C4 --> D2[Pan Boiling]
        C3 --> E1[Mills and Centrifuges]
    end

    C3 --> F1[Grid Export or Import]
```

### 4.3 Cement Plant Waste Heat Recovery Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pre-heater Kiln Flue Gas 380 C] --> B[Dust Precipitator ESP]
    B --> C[WHR Boiler Shell Side]
    C --> D[Steam Drum 15 bar]
    D --> E[Turbine Generator]
    D --> F[Process Steam Consumers]
    D --> G[Feedwater Preheater]
    G --> H[Deaerator]
    H --> I[Boiler Feed Pump]
    I --> D
    C --> J[Stack 150 C Exhaust]
```

### 4.4 T&D Loss Reduction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Generating Station 220 kV] --> B[EHV Transmission]
    B --> C[Bulk Power Transformer]
    C --> D[Primary Distribution 11 kV]
    D --> E[Distribution Transformer]
    E --> F[Secondary Distribution LT 415 V]
    F --> G[Industrial Consumer]

    A -.-> H[Technical Loss 4 percent]
    C -.-> I[Technical Loss 2 percent]
    D -.-> J[Technical Loss 5 percent]
    E -.-> K[Technical Loss 3 percent]
    F -.-> L[Commercial Loss 6 to 12 percent]

    H --> M[ECM HVDS Reconductoring]
    I --> N[ECM AMR Smart Metering]
    J --> O[ECM Capacitor Banks]
    K --> P[ECM HVDS LT Conversion]
    L --> Q[ECM Theft Control]
```
