## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **"List the major energy conservation opportunities in a sugar industry and identify the most economically attractive one."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The major energy conservation opportunities in a sugar industry are:
1. **Bagasse-based cogeneration** — Combines fuel saving with electricity generation.
2. **Condensate recovery and flash steam utilization** — Reduces makeup water and fuel.
3. **VFD retrofit on cane crushers and centrifuges** — Reduces peak motor loads.
4. **Boiler efficiency improvement** — Through economizers and FD/ID fan optimization.
5. **Insulation upgrades on steam lines and evaporator bodies**.

Among these, **bagasse-based cogeneration** is the most economically attractive due to its dual-fuel displacement and renewable energy revenue stream (REC/PLG mechanism).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **"Why is Waste Heat Recovery (WHR) from cement kilns considered a high-payback investment?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Cement kiln WHR is high-payback because:
1. The kiln flue gas exits at **350–400 °C**, carrying massive enthalpy (≈ 1.4 GJ/tonne clinker).
2. The continuous 8760-hour operation maximizes **annual energy capture**.
3. The recovered steam directly substitutes fossil-fuel-fired boiler steam, yielding monetary savings at ₹6.5–7.5/kWh equivalent.
4. Typical **simple payback is 0.5–2 years**, often under 6 months.
5. It also earns **carbon credits** under CDM/Verra schemes, enhancing IRR further.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — May 2023 / Carry-over]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the energy audit methodology applied to the **Pulp & Paper Industry**, highlighting the major energy-consuming unit operations and their typical energy-saving opportunities.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A paper mill generates **40 tonnes/hr of black liquor solids at 65% moisture** with GCV = 14 MJ/kg on dry basis. The existing recovery boiler operates at **62% efficiency**. A new **high-pressure recovery boiler (85% efficiency)** is proposed at a CapEx of **₹85 crore**. Calculate (i) useful steam energy generated annually by the new boiler (8400 operating hours), (ii) annual monetary savings at ₹5.50/kWh equivalent, and (iii) simple payback period.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Energy Audit Methodology in Pulp & Paper:**
1. **Boundary Setting** — Defines the digester, evaporator train, recovery boiler, dryer section, and stock preparation as the battery limit.
2. **Walk-through Audit** — Identifies steam leaks, malfunctioning traps, and uninsulated digesters.
3. **Detailed Audit** — Uses pin-ch analysis on evaporator trains for heat integration.
4. **Major Energy-Consuming Units**:
   - Wood chip digester (≈ 30% plant thermal load).
   - Multiple-effect evaporators (≈ 25%).
   - Recovery boiler (≈ 20%).
   - Paper machine dryers (≈ 15%).
   - Refining and pumping (≈ 10%).
5. **ECMs**: Black liquor concentration increase, falling-film evaporator retrofit, dryer hood ventilation optimization, and VFDs on refining motors.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Dry solids mass rate:**
$$m_{dry} = 40{,}000 \times 0.35 = 14{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$$

Thermal input:
$$Q_{in} = 14{,}000 \times 14 = 196{,}000 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

Convert to kW:
$$Q_{in} = 54{,}444.4 \text{ kW}$$

Useful steam energy from **new** boiler (85%):
$$Q_{new} = 0.85 \times 54{,}444.4 = 46{,}277.8 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Annual Savings:**
Difference between new and old useful output:
$$\Delta Q = (0.85 - 0.62) \times 54{,}444.4 = 12{,}522.2 \text{ kW}$$

$$AES = 12{,}522.2 \times 8400 = 105{,}186{,}480 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

$$AMS = 105.19 \times 10^6 \times 5.50 = ₹578.5 \text{ million/yr} = ₹57.85 \text{ crore/yr}$$

**(iii) Payback:**
$$SPP = \dfrac{85}{57.85} = 1.47 \text{ years} \approx 17.6 \text{ months}$$

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the energy audit procedure for a **Textile Industry** (composite mill) with reference to its process flow and major energy-consuming equipment. List at least 5 ECMs with their payback ranges.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A textile composite mill consumes **8.5 million kWh/yr** at an average power factor of **0.78 lag**. The electricity tariff has a **kVArh penalty of ₹0.45 per lagging kVArh** for PF below 0.95. Calculate: (i) Required kVAr compensation to raise PF to 0.96, (ii) Annual kVAr penalty currently paid, (iii) Annual penalty after PF correction, and (iv) CapEx justification if CapEx of capacitor bank = ₹28 lakh and assumed annual demand charge saving = ₹6.2 lakh/yr.

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Textile Mill Energy Audit Procedure:**
1. Map the process: Spinning → Knitting/Weaving → Processing (Desizing, Bleaching, Dyeing, Printing) → Finishing → Garmenting.
2. Quantify energy per process: Spinning = 35%, Wet processing = 40%, Utilities = 25%.
3. Perform detailed audit on **stenter frames, dyeing machines, boilers, and thermic fluid heaters**.
4. Benchmark against BEE PAT scheme norms.
5. **ECMs**:
   - Heat recovery from dyeing effluent → preheat incoming water → SPP = 1.5 yr.
   - VFD retrofit on ring frames and looms → SPP = 2 yr.
   - Steam trap audit & replacement → SPP = 0.5 yr.
   - LED retrofit in loom shed → SPP = 1 yr.
   - Thermic fluid heater insulation upgrade → SPP = 1.2 yr.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Required kVAr:**
Active power $P = 8.5 \times 10^6$ kWh, assuming avg kW load = $8.5 \times 10^6 / 6000 = 1416.67$ kW (2-shift).

Original reactive power at $\cos \phi_1 = 0.78$:
$$\tan \phi_1 = 0.8023$$
$$Q_1 = 1416.67 \times 0.8023 = 1136.6 \text{ kVAr}$$

Target reactive power at $\cos \phi_2 = 0.96$:
$$\tan \phi_2 = 0.2917$$
$$Q_2 = 1416.67 \times 0.2917 = 413.2 \text{ kVAr}$$

Capacitor bank size:
$$Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = 1136.6 - 413.2 = 723.4 \text{ kVAr}$$

**(ii) Annual Penalty Currently Paid:**
Penalty applies to kVArh above the threshold at PF = 0.95:
$$\tan(0.95) = 0.3287$$
Threshold kVArh = $1416.67 \times 0.3287 \times 6000 = 2{,}794{,}910$ kVArh/yr.

Actual kVArh consumed = $1136.6 \times 6000 = 6{,}819{,}600$ kVArh/yr.

Penalizable kVArh = $6{,}819{,}600 - 2{,}794{,}910 = 4{,}024{,}690$ kVArh/yr.

Current penalty = $4{,}024{,}690 \times 0.45 = ₹18.11 \text{ lakh/yr}$.

**(iii) Annual Penalty After PF Correction:**
After correction, $Q_2 = 413.2$ kVAr < threshold kVAr (465.7 kVAr equivalent at PF 0.95). Since $Q_2 = 413.2$ kVAr < 465.7 kVAr threshold, the penalty = **₹0**.

**(iv) Justification:**
Annual savings = Penalty saved + Demand charge saved = $18.11 + 6.2 = ₹24.31$ lakh/yr.
$$SPP = \dfrac{28 \text{ lakh}}{24.31 \text{ lakh/yr}} = 1.15 \text{ years} \approx 14 \text{ months}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common pitfall in Part B numerical problems — students often forget to:
> 1. Convert kWh to kW using **operating hours** before calculating reactive power.
> 2. Apply the **threshold PF = 0.95** correctly for penalty calculations (penalty is on kVArh **above** threshold, not total).
> 3. In payback questions, **include all monetary benefits** (penalty + demand + energy savings) — missing any one component costs 1 mark.
> 4. State assumptions for **GCV, ambient temperature, and operating hours** explicitly — examiners reward assumption clarity with 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PEMRT746 Module 4 — Case Studies:**

- [ ] **Sugar Industry** → Bagasse cogeneration (5 bar, 220 °C back-pressure turbine); typical SEC = 3–5 GJ/tonne cane.
- [ ] **Cement Industry** → WHR boiler from pre-heater exhaust (350–400 °C); payback often < 1 year.
- [ ] **Steel Industry** → Top Recovery Turbine (TRT), Coke Dry Quenching (CDQ), BF gas recovery.
- [ ] **Pulp & Paper** → Black liquor concentration, falling-film evaporators, dryer hood optimization.
- [ ] **Textile Industry** → Heat recovery from dyeing effluent, VFDs, steam trap audit.
- [ ] **T&D Utility** → HT/LT loss reduction (technical + commercial), capacitor banks, HVDS reconductoring.
- [ ] **Hospital/Building** → HVAC retrofits, LED lighting, sterilizer steam trap management.
- [ ] **Default Operating Hours** → 8760 (continuous), 6000 (2-shift), 3000 (1-shift).
- [ ] **Payback Benchmarks** → < 2 yr (highly attractive), 2–5 yr (acceptable), > 5 yr (defer or reject).
- [ ] **Key Formulas** → SEC, boiler efficiency (direct + indirect), SPP, NPV, IRR, PF compensation, WHR heat recovery.
- [ ] **Always state** → plant boundary, GCV, ambient T, operating hours, and discount rate (typically 10–12% in KTU problems).
- [ ] **Tip for writing Part B** → Use tables to list ECMs vs. SEC savings vs. payback — examiners allocate 1 mark each for structured presentation.
- [ ] **Common units to remember** → 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 toe (tonne oil equivalent) = 42 GJ; 1 MMBTU = 1.055 GJ.

> **Suggested Reading (Non-Textbook):**
> Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India — *Energy Audit Reports of PAT Industries*, Ministry of Power, Government of India (publicly available at beeindia.gov.in). These reports are the authentic source of sector-wise SEC benchmarks cited in KTU question papers.
